model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "When evaluating an author's worth and determining his place among our literary icons, we are entitled to view him from our own perspective and judge him by our own criteria. However, when assessing the strength demonstrated in his creations, we must be guided by his personal intentions, comparing his work against his own ideal to determine what might be missing. We diverge from Mr. Poe regarding the purpose of art. He considers its goal to be the generation of Beauty, and perhaps our disagreement lies solely in how that term is defined. Nevertheless, in our commentary on his literature, we shall utilize his own standard as our compass. The sanctuary of the muse is reachable from every direction, and there is ample space within for everyone who offers tributes or consults the oracle.

In his short stories, Mr. Poe has elected to demonstrate his capabilities primarily within that obscure territory extending from the furthest edges of probability into the strange borders of superstition and fantasy. He blends in a striking fashion two talents rarely seen together: an ability to sway the reader's consciousness through the intangible veils of mystery, and a precision of detail that overlooks not even the smallest object. Both are, in reality, the inevitable outcomes of the primary trait of his intellect, which we have previously mentioned: analysis. This is what sets the artist apart. His mind immediately anticipates the intended result. Having decided to evoke specific feelings in the reader, he ensures all secondary elements contribute directly to the central theme. Even his mystery is, to his own mind, mathematical. For him, X is a known variable throughout. In every scene he depicts, he comprehends the chemical nature of all his pigments. Regardless of how indistinct some of his figures might appear, or how shapeless the shadows, the outline remains as sharp and precise to him as a geometric figure. For this reason, Mr. Poe lacks any affinity for Mysticism. The Mystic resides within the mystery, wrapped in it; it tints all his reflections; it impacts his vision specifically, causing mundane objects to acquire a prismatic fringe. Mr. Poe, conversely, is an external observer. He examines, he dissects, he watches

“with an eye serene,
The very pulse of the machine,”

for that is essentially what it is to him, with gears, cogs, and pistons, all functioning to achieve a particular outcome.

This analytical inclination of his intellect counterbalances his poetic side, and by granting him the endurance to be meticulous, allows him to infuse a remarkable sense of reality into his most fantastical visions. He portrays monomania with great intensity. He enjoys dissecting these mental afflictions, tracing the intricate spread of their roots. In evoking images of terror, too, he finds peculiar success, occasionally conveying through a dark suggestion some profound doubt that serves as the core of all horror. He entrusts the imagination with the duty of completing the scene, a task only it can perform.

“For much imaginary work was there;
Conceit deceitful, so compact, so kind,
That for Achilles’ image stood his spear
Grasped in an armed hand; himself behind
Was left unseen, save to the eye of mind.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_001",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "When evaluating an author's merit and assigning them a place among our literary icons, we have the right to view them from our own perspective and measure them by our own standards. However, when estimating the power displayed in their work, we must be guided by their own intentions, comparing their creations to their own ideals to see what might be missing. We differ from Mr. Poe in his opinions regarding the purpose of art. He believes that purpose is the creation of Beauty, and perhaps it is only in the definition of that word that we disagree. But in our discussion of his writings, we will use his own standard as our guide. The temple of the god of song is equally accessible from every side, and there is enough room within it for everyone who brings offerings or seeks an oracle.

In his stories, Mr. Poe has chosen to demonstrate his power primarily in that dim region stretching from the outer limits of the probable into the strange borders of superstition and unreality. He combines, in a remarkable way, two faculties rarely found together: the ability to influence a reader's mind through the intangible shadows of mystery, and a precision of detail that overlooks nothing, not even a pin or a button. Both are, in truth, the natural results of the dominant quality of his mind, which we have mentioned before: analysis. This is what distinguishes the artist. His mind immediately reaches toward the intended effect. Having decided to evoke specific emotions in the reader, he ensures all subordinate parts contribute strictly to that central goal. Even his mystery is mathematical to him. To him, X is a known quantity from the start. In any picture he paints, he understands the chemical properties of all his colors. However vague some of his figures may seem, or however formless the shadows, the outline remains as clear and distinct to him as a geometrical diagram. For this reason, Mr. Poe has no sympathy for Mysticism. The Mystic dwells within the mystery, enveloped by it; it colors all their thoughts, affects their vision, and gives the most common objects a rainbow edge. Mr. Poe, on the other hand, is an outside observer. He analyzes, he dissects, he watches

“with an eye serene,
The very pulse of the machine,”

for that is what it practically is to him, with wheels, cogs, and piston-rods, all working to produce a specific result.

This analytical tendency of his mind balances his poetic side, and by giving him the patience to be detailed, it enables him to inject a wonderful sense of reality into his most unreal fantasies. He paints monomania with great power. He loves to dissect these mental cancers and trace all the subtle ramifications of their roots. In evoking images of horror, he also finds strange success, sometimes conveying a terrible doubt through a dark hint, which is the secret of all horror. He leaves the task of finishing the picture to the imagination, a task only it is capable of performing.

“For much imaginary work was there;
Conceit deceitful, so compact, so kind,
That for Achilles’ image stood his spear
Grasped in an armed hand; himself behind
Was left unseen, save to the eye of mind.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“My primary objective is to launch a magazine titled ‘The Stylus,’ yet it would be of no value to me, even once founded, if it remained under a publisher’s authority. Consequently, I intend to create a periodical that is entirely my own in every respect. To achieve this, I must secure a minimum of five hundred subscribers to start; I have nearly two hundred already. I plan, however, to travel South and West to visit my personal and literary associates—former college and West Point companions—to see what I can accomplish. To obtain the funds necessary for the initial phase, I intend to deliver a lecture at the Society Library on Thursday, February 3rd, and to avoid any potential for disagreement, my topic will not be literary in nature. I have selected a wide-ranging subject: ‘The Universe.’

“Having provided you with the facts of the situation, I leave the remainder to your own discretion and kindness. Gratefully, most gratefully,

“Your friend always,
“EDGAR A. POE.”

Brief and incidental as these letters may be, we believe they adequately demonstrate the presence of the very traits often denied to Mr. Poe—humility, a readiness to persist, faith in the friendship of others, and the capacity for sincere and thankful companionship! Such he certainly was when of sound mind. Such he has consistently appeared to us, throughout all our personal experiences with him during a friendship spanning five or six years. Since it is far simpler to trust what we have witnessed and experienced than what we merely hear, we recall him only with admiration and respect; these accounts of him, when he was morally unstable, seem to us like portraits of a man we knew only in health, painted during a time of illness.

Yet there exists another, more poignant, and significantly more compelling proof that there was goodness in Edgar A. Poe. To disclose it, we must risk drawing back the curtain that reverently hides the sorrow and refinement found in poverty; however, we believe it may be justified if it helps to illuminate the poet’s memory, even if there were not a more urgent and immediate purpose it might serve for the closest person affected by his passing.

Our initial awareness of Mr. Poe’s relocation to this city came through a visit from a lady who introduced herself as his wife’s mother. She was seeking work on his behalf, and she justified her request by noting that he was unwell, that her daughter was a chronic invalid, and that their situation forced her to take this task upon herself. The face of this woman, rendered beautiful and saintly by a life clearly devoted to hardship and sorrowful affection, her soft and melancholy voice pressing her request, her long-neglected but naturally and instinctively refined demeanor, and her pleading yet appreciative remarks regarding the merits and talents of her son, revealed immediately the presence of one of those earthly angels that women in adversity can become. It was a difficult destiny she was tending to. Mr. Poe composed with meticulous effort, and in a manner too elevated above the common taste to be adequately compensated."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_002",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“My main goal is to launch a magazine called ‘The Stylus,’ but it would be useless to me, even once established, if it remained under a publisher’s control. I intend, therefore, to create a journal that is entirely my own in every respect. To achieve this, I need to secure at least five hundred subscribers to start; I already have nearly two hundred. I plan, however, to travel South and West to visit my personal and literary friends—former college and West Point acquaintances—to see what I can accomplish. To fund the initial steps, I plan to lecture at the Society Library on Thursday, February 3rd, and to avoid any potential arguments, my topic will not be literary at all. I have chosen a broad subject: ‘The Universe.’

“Having provided you with the facts of the situation, I leave the rest to your own tact and generosity. Gratefully, most gratefully,

“Your friend always,
“EDGAR A. POE.”

Brief and informal as these letters are, we believe they sufficiently prove the existence of the very qualities often denied to Mr. Poe—humility, a willingness to persevere, faith in the friendship of others, and the capacity for warm, grateful companionship. This is certainly who he was when he was of sound mind. This is the only version of him we have consistently known throughout our five or six years of friendship. Because it is so much easier to believe what we have personally witnessed than what we only hear about, we remember him with nothing but admiration and respect. These descriptions of him during his periods of moral instability seem to us like portraits of a healthy man painted while he was ill.

Yet there is another, more touching, and far more compelling piece of evidence that there was goodness in Edgar A. Poe. To reveal it, we must venture to lift the veil that sacredly covers the grief and refinement of those living in poverty; we believe this may be excused if it helps brighten the poet’s memory, even if there were not a more urgent and immediate service it might provide to the person most affected by his death.

Our first knowledge of Mr. Poe’s move to this city came through a visit from a lady who introduced herself as his wife’s mother. She was looking for work for him, and she justified her errand by mentioning that he was ill, that her daughter was a chronic invalid, and that their circumstances forced her to take this matter into her own hands. The woman’s face, made beautiful and saintly by her evident devotion to a life of hardship and sorrowful tenderness, her gentle and mournful voice as she made her plea, her long-forgotten but habitually refined manners, and her appealing yet appreciative mention of her son-in-law’s talents, immediately revealed the presence of one of those earthly angels that women in adversity can become. It was a harsh fate she was watching over. Mr. Poe wrote with fastidious difficulty, and in a style too elevated above the popular taste to be well compensated."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I leaned, nevertheless, toward the latter view. The icy border toward the north was becoming increasingly visible. The temperature was not nearly as severe. Nothing significant took place, and I spent the day reading, having made sure to bring a supply of books along.

“April 5th. Witnessed the strange occurrence of the sun ascending while almost the entire observable surface of the globe remained shrouded in shadow. Eventually, however, the illumination extended everywhere, and I once more observed the icy boundary to the north. It was now quite clear, appearing significantly darker in shade than the surrounding sea. I was clearly drawing nearer to it, and with significant speed. I imagined I could once again make out a stretch of land to the east, and another to the west, though I could not be sure. The weather was mild. Nothing of note transpired throughout the day. I retired to bed early.

“April 6th. I was astonished to discover the icy rim at a quite reasonable distance, with a vast expanse of the same substance extending toward the northern horizon. It was clear that if the balloon maintained its current path, it would soon reach the Frozen Ocean, and I now felt little doubt that I would eventually behold the Pole. Throughout the day, I continued to approach the ice. As night fell, the extent of my horizon expanded suddenly and significantly, surely because the earth is an oblate spheroid and I was reaching the flattened areas near the Arctic circle. When darkness finally descended, I went to bed feeling quite anxious, worried I might drift over the object of my intense curiosity while lacking the chance to study it.

“April 7th. I woke early, and, to my immense delight, finally saw what I could only conclude was the northern Pole itself. It was there, without question, directly below me; but, unfortunately, I had ascended to such a great height that nothing could be observed with precision. Indeed, judging by the sequence of figures representing my various altitudes at different times between six A.M. on April 2nd and twenty minutes before nine A.M. that same day (when the barometer ceased functioning), one might reasonably conclude that the balloon had, by four A.M. on April 7th, attained an altitude of at least 7,254 miles above sea level. This height might seem vast, but the calculation used likely produced a result far lower than the reality. Regardless, I certainly viewed the entire major diameter of the earth; the whole northern hemisphere was spread out below me like an orthographic map, and the great circle of the equator served as the edge of my horizon. Your Excellencies can, however, easily grasp that the restricted, previously uncharted regions within the Arctic circle, though located directly beneath me and thus seen without any foreshortening, were still, in themselves, relatively too small and too far from my vantage point to allow for any very detailed inspection."
}

  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I leaned, nevertheless, toward the latter view. The icy border toward the north was becoming increasingly visible. The temperature was not nearly as severe. Nothing significant took place, and I spent the day reading, having made sure to bring a supply of books along.

“April 5th. Witnessed the strange occurrence of the sun ascending while almost the entire observable surface of the globe remained shrouded in shadow. Eventually, however, the illumination extended everywhere, and I once more observed the icy boundary to the north. It was now quite clear, appearing significantly darker in shade than the surrounding sea. I was clearly drawing nearer to it, and with significant speed. I imagined I could once again make out a stretch of land to the east, and another to the west, though I could not be sure. The weather was mild. Nothing of note transpired throughout the day. I retired to bed early.

“April 6th. I was astonished to discover the icy rim at a quite reasonable distance, with a vast expanse of the same substance extending toward the northern horizon. It was clear that if the balloon maintained its current path, it would soon reach the Frozen Ocean, and I now felt little doubt that I would eventually behold the Pole. Throughout the day, I continued to approach the ice. As night fell, the extent of my horizon expanded suddenly and significantly, surely because the earth is an oblate spheroid and I was reaching the flattened areas near the Arctic circle. When darkness finally descended, I went to bed feeling quite anxious, worried I might drift over the object of my intense curiosity while lacking the chance to study it.

“April 7th. I woke early, and, to my immense delight, finally saw what I could only conclude was the northern Pole itself. It was there, without question, directly below me; but, unfortunately, I had ascended to such a great height that nothing could be observed with precision. Indeed, judging by the sequence of figures representing my various altitudes at different times between six A.M. on April 2nd and twenty minutes before nine A.M. that same day (when the barometer ceased functioning), one might reasonably conclude that the balloon had, by four A.M. on April 7th, attained an altitude of at least 7,254 miles above sea level. This height might seem vast, but the calculation used likely produced a result far lower than the reality. Regardless, I certainly viewed the entire major diameter of the earth; the whole northern hemisphere was spread out below me like an orthographic map, and the great circle of the equator served as the edge of my horizon. Your Excellencies can, however, easily grasp that the restricted, previously uncharted regions within the Arctic circle, though located directly beneath me and thus seen without any foreshortening, were still, in themselves, relatively too small and too far from my vantage point to allow for any very detailed inspection."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "This is indeed the situation. I possess a great deal—a vast amount—that I would be delighted to share. I have much to relate regarding the planet’s climate; its remarkable shifts between heat and cold, with two weeks of intense, scorching sun followed by a fortnight of arctic freezing; the continuous movement of moisture, distilled as if in a vacuum, from the area directly under the sun to the point furthest away; the shifting belt of flowing water; the inhabitants themselves; their habits, traditions, and government; their unique physical makeup; their lack of beauty; their missing ears, which are unnecessary in such a distinct atmosphere; their resulting inability to use or understand speech; their unique way of communicating without it; the mysterious link between every lunar individual and a specific person on Earth—a connection similar to, and reliant upon, the relationship between the planet and its moons, through which the fates of those on one world are tied to the lives of those on the other; and, most importantly, if it pleases your Excellencies, the dark and terrifying secrets hidden in the moon’s outer regions—areas that, due to the near-perfect synchronization of the satellite’s rotation with its orbit around the Earth, have never been, and by divine grace never will be, exposed to human telescopes. I would gladly provide all this and much more. However, to be concise, I require compensation. I am longing to return to my home and family; therefore, in exchange for further information—and considering the insight I can provide into various vital areas of physical and metaphysical study—I must request, through your honorable assembly, a pardon for the crime I committed by killing my creditors when I left Rotterdam. This is the purpose of this letter. The bearer, a lunar resident whom I have convinced and trained to act as my messenger to Earth, will wait for your Excellencies’ decision and bring the pardon back to me, should it be granted.

“I have the honor to remain, etc., your Excellencies’ most humble servant,

“HANS PFAALL.”

It is reported that upon reading this highly unusual letter, Professor Rub-a-dub was so shocked he dropped his pipe, while Mynheer Superbus Von Underduk removed his glasses, cleaned them, and put them away, momentarily forgetting his own dignity to spin around on his heels three times in sheer amazement and wonder. There was no question about it—the pardon would be secured. At least, that is what Professor Rub-a-dub vowed with a firm oath, and it was the eventual conclusion of the distinguished Von Underduk as he took his colleague’s arm and, remaining silent, hurried home to consider the necessary steps."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "When I reached the cabin, I knocked as I usually did. Receiving no answer, I located the hidden key, opened the door, and stepped inside. A pleasant fire burned in the hearth, which was an unexpected and welcome sight. I removed my overcoat, settled into an armchair near the popping logs, and waited for my hosts to return.

They returned shortly after nightfall and greeted me warmly. Jupiter, wearing a wide grin, hurried to cook some marsh-hens for our meal. Legrand was in one of his typical states of intense excitement. He had discovered a previously unknown bivalve, representing a new genus, and had also captured, with Jupiter’s help, a beetle he believed to be entirely unique, though he wanted my assessment of it the following day.

“Why not tonight?” I inquired, warming my hands by the flames and wishing the entire species of beetles were elsewhere.

“If only I had known you were coming!” Legrand exclaimed. “It has been so long since we met; how could I have guessed you would visit tonight of all nights? On my way home, I ran into Lieutenant G— from the fort and foolishly lent him the insect. You won't be able to see it until morning. Stay the night, and I will have Jup fetch it at sunrise. It is the most beautiful thing in existence!”

“What? The sunrise?”

“Don't be silly! No, the beetle. It is a striking golden color, roughly the size of a large hickory nut, with two jet-black spots near one end of its back and a longer one at the other. The antennae are—”

“There ain't no tin in him, Massa Will, I keep telling you,” Jupiter interrupted. “The bug is a gold bug, solid, every bit of him, inside and out, except for his wings. I’ve never felt a bug half so heavy in my life.”

“Well, suppose it is, Jup,” Legrand answered, with more intensity than I thought necessary. “Does that mean you should let the birds burn? The color,” he said, turning to me, “is truly enough to justify Jupiter’s theory. You have never seen a more brilliant metallic shine than these scales produce, though you cannot judge for yourself until tomorrow. In the meantime, I can describe its form.” He sat at a small table holding a pen and ink, but no paper. He searched a drawer, finding nothing.

“It doesn't matter,” he finally said. “This will do.” He pulled a piece of what looked like grimy foolscap from his vest pocket and sketched a rough drawing with his pen. I remained by the fire, still feeling cold, while he worked. Once finished, he handed the sketch to me without standing. As I took it, a deep growl sounded, followed by scratching at the door. Jupiter opened it, and Legrand’s large Newfoundland dog bounded in, jumped onto my shoulders, and covered me with affection, as I had been kind to him during my earlier visits."
}

  "rewritten_text": "When I reached the cabin, I knocked as I usually did. Receiving no answer, I located the hidden key, opened the door, and stepped inside. A pleasant fire burned in the hearth, which was an unexpected and welcome sight. I removed my overcoat, settled into an armchair near the popping logs, and waited for my hosts to return.

They returned shortly after nightfall and greeted me warmly. Jupiter, wearing a wide grin, hurried to cook some marsh-hens for our meal. Legrand was in one of his typical states of intense excitement. He had discovered a previously unknown bivalve, representing a new genus, and had also captured, with Jupiter’s help, a beetle he believed to be entirely unique, though he wanted my assessment of it the following day.

“Why not tonight?” I inquired, warming my hands by the flames and wishing the entire species of beetles were elsewhere.

“If only I had known you were coming!” Legrand exclaimed. “It has been so long since we met; how could I have guessed you would visit tonight of all nights? On my way home, I ran into Lieutenant G— from the fort and foolishly lent him the insect. You won't be able to see it until morning. Stay the night, and I will have Jup fetch it at sunrise. It is the most beautiful thing in existence!”

“What? The sunrise?”

“Don't be silly! No, the beetle. It is a striking golden color, roughly the size of a large hickory nut, with two jet-black spots near one end of its back and a longer one at the other. The antennae are—”

“There ain't no tin in him, Massa Will, I keep telling you,” Jupiter interrupted. “The bug is a gold bug, solid, every bit of him, inside and out, except for his wings. I’ve never felt a bug half so heavy in my life.”

“Well, suppose it is, Jup,” Legrand answered, with more intensity than I thought necessary. “Does that mean you should let the birds burn? The color,” he said, turning to me, “is truly enough to justify Jupiter’s theory. You have never seen a more brilliant metallic shine than these scales produce, though you cannot judge for yourself until tomorrow. In the meantime, I can describe its form.” He sat at a small table holding a pen and ink, but no paper. He searched a drawer, finding nothing.

“It doesn't matter,” he finally said. “This will do.” He pulled a piece of what looked like grimy foolscap from his vest pocket and sketched a rough drawing with his pen. I remained by the fire, still feeling cold, while he worked. Once finished, he handed the sketch to me without standing. As I took it, a deep growl sounded, followed by scratching at the door. Jupiter opened it, and Legrand’s large Newfoundland dog bounded in, jumped onto my shoulders, and covered me with affection, as I had been kind to him during my earlier visits."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "This thought implied a certain significance—a relevance—to the skull. I also noticed the shape of the paper. Even though one corner had been damaged by some mishap, it was evident that its original shape was rectangular. It was precisely the kind of scrap that might have been selected for a note—for a record of something intended to be kept in mind and guarded with care.

“However,” I interrupted, “you stated that the skull was not visible on the paper when you sketched the beetle. How then do you establish a link between the boat and the skull—since the latter, by your own account, must have been created (heaven only knows how or by whom) at a time after you drew the scarab?”

“Ah, that is where the entire enigma hinges; though the secret, at this juncture, was not very hard for me to unravel. My logic was sound and could lead to only one conclusion. I reasoned, for instance, in this way: When I sketched the scarab, no skull was visible on the parchment. Once I finished the drawing, I handed it to you and watched you closely until you gave it back. Therefore, you did not draw the skull, and no one else was there to do it. Thus, it was not created by human hands. And yet, it was created.

“At this point in my thinking, I tried to recall, and did recall, with perfect clarity, every event that took place around that time. The air was cold (a fortunate and rare coincidence!), and a fire was burning in the fireplace. I was warm from exertion and sat by the table. You, however, had pulled a chair near the hearth. Just as I put the parchment into your hand, and as you were examining it, Wolf, the Newfoundland dog, came in and jumped onto your shoulders. With your left hand you petted him and held him back, while your right hand, holding the paper, dropped loosely between your knees, very near the flames. For a second, I thought the fire had ignited it and was about to warn you, but before I could say anything, you had pulled it away and were busy looking at it. When I weighed all these details, I did not doubt for an instant that heat had been the force that revealed the skull I saw drawn upon the paper. You know well that chemical substances exist, and have existed for ages, that allow one to write on paper or vellum so that the markings appear only when exposed to heat. Zaffre, dissolved in aqua regia and thinned with four times its weight in water, is sometimes used; it produces a green hue. The regulus of cobalt, dissolved in spirit of nitre, creates a red. These colors fade after the material cools, but reappear when heat is applied once more.

“I then examined the skull closely. Its outer boundaries—the parts of the drawing closest to the edge of the vellum—were much sharper than the rest. It was obvious that the heat had acted in an incomplete or uneven manner.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "My initial attempt involved separating the sentence into the logical segments the code-maker had in mind.

“You mean, to add punctuation?”

“Something similar to that.”

“But how could you accomplish this?”

“I considered that the author had made it a priority to join his words without spaces to make solving it harder. Now, a person of average intelligence, while attempting this, would almost certainly go too far. Whenever he reached a shift in his topic that would normally call for a pause or a mark, he would be very likely to press his characters together more tightly than usual at that spot. If you examine the manuscript here, you will readily spot five instances of such abnormal crowding. Following this clue, I divided it this way: ‘A good glass in the Bishop’s hostel in the Devil’s seat—forty-one degrees and thirteen minutes—northeast and by north—main branch seventh limb east side—shoot from the left eye of the death’s-head—a bee-line from the tree through the shot fifty feet out.’”

“Even with this division,” I remarked, “I remain confused.”

“It kept me in the dark as well,” Legrand answered, “for several days, during which I searched carefully around Sullivan’s Island for any structure known as the ‘Bishop’s Hotel,’ since I had naturally discarded the archaic term ‘hostel.’ Finding nothing, I was about to broaden my search and proceed more methodically when it suddenly occurred to me one morning that ‘Bishop’s Hostel’ might relate to an old family named Bessop, who had long owned an ancient manor house about four miles north of the island. I traveled to the plantation and questioned the elderly Black residents. Eventually, one of the oldest women mentioned she had heard of a place called Bessop’s Castle and believed she could lead me there, though she noted it was neither a castle nor an inn, but a tall rock.

“I promised her a generous payment for her assistance, and after some hesitation, she agreed to take me to the location. We reached it easily, and after sending her away, I began to inspect the area. The ‘castle’ was a jagged collection of cliffs and rocks, one of which was quite striking due to its height and its isolated, man-made look. I climbed to the top, then felt uncertain about my next move.

“While I was deep in thought, I noticed a narrow shelf on the eastern side of the rock, perhaps a yard beneath the peak where I stood. This ledge stuck out about eighteen inches and was no more than a foot wide, while a hollow in the cliff directly above it made it look somewhat like the high-backed chairs of our forefathers. I was certain this was the ‘devil’s seat’ mentioned in the manuscript.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I apologize; I had overlooked the fact that Shakespeare will not emerge for another seventeen centuries and a half. Yet, does the look of Epidaphne not warrant my labeling it grotesque?

“It is strongly defended; in this regard, it owes as much to nature as to human craft.”

Quite correct.

“There is a vast quantity of grand palaces.”

There are.

“And the many temples, opulent and grand, might stand alongside the most praised structures of old.”

I must concede all this. Nevertheless, there is an endless supply of mud shacks and wretched hovels. We cannot avoid noticing the wealth of grime in every gutter, and, were it not for the overwhelming scents of pagan incense, I am certain we would encounter a truly unbearable odor. Have you ever seen streets so painfully cramped, or buildings so impossibly high? What a gloom their shadows throw upon the earth! It is fortunate that the hanging lanterns in those perpetual colonnades remain lit all day; otherwise, we would suffer the darkness of Egypt during her period of ruin.

“It is surely a bizarre location! What is the purpose of that peculiar structure over there? Look! It rises higher than all the rest and sits to the east of what I assume is the king’s residence!”

That is the new Temple of the Sun, who is worshipped in Syria by the name Elah Gabalah. In time, a highly infamous Roman Emperor will establish this cult in Rome, and from it take the name Heliogabalus. I imagine you would enjoy a glimpse of the temple’s god. You need not gaze at the sky; his Sunship is not located there—at least, not the Sunship the Syrians revere. That deity resides inside that building. He is honored in the form of a massive stone pillar ending at the top in a cone or pyramid, which serves to represent Fire.

“Listen—look!—who could those absurd creatures be, partially unclothed, with their faces colored, yelling and waving at the crowd?”

A few are charlatans. Others belong more specifically to the class of philosophers. The majority, however—especially those striking the commoners with sticks—are the chief courtiers of the palace, performing, as they are obligated to do, some praiseworthy jest of the monarch’s.

“But what do we have here? Goodness! The city is crawling with wild animals! What a frightening sight!—what a hazardous trait!”

Frightening, if you insist; but not in the slightest bit hazardous. Each creature, if you care to notice, is trailing quite calmly behind its owner. A few, admittedly, are guided by a cord around the neck, but these are mostly the smaller or more fearful types. The lion, the tiger, and the leopard are completely free. They have been easily broken into their current roles and serve their various masters as personal valets. It is true that there are times when Nature reclaims her offended territory; yet, the consumption of a soldier or the strangling of a sacred bull is a matter of too little importance to be more than whispered about in Epidaphne.

“But what strange uproar do I hear? Surely this is a deafening sound even for Antioch! It suggests some disturbance of uncommon significance.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_012",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I apologize; I had forgotten that Shakespeare will not appear for another seventeen hundred and fifty years. But does the appearance of Epidaphne not justify me in calling it grotesque?

“It is well fortified; and in this regard, it owes as much to nature as to art.”

That is very true.

“There is a staggering number of stately palaces.”

There are.

“And the numerous temples, sumptuous and magnificent, could hold their own against the most praised structures of antiquity.”

I must acknowledge all of that. Still, there is an endless supply of mud huts and abominable hovels. We cannot help but notice an abundance of filth in every gutter, and were it not for the overpowering fumes of idolatrous incense, I have no doubt we would find the stench intolerable. Have you ever seen streets so insufferably narrow, or houses so miraculously tall? What gloom their shadows cast upon the ground! It is a good thing the swinging lamps in those endless colonnades are kept burning throughout the day; otherwise, we would have the darkness of Egypt during the time of her desolation.

“It is certainly a strange place! What is the meaning of that singular building over there? Look! It towers above all the others and lies to the east of what I assume is the royal palace!”

That is the new Temple of the Sun, who is adored in Syria under the title of Elah Gabalah. In the future, a very notorious Roman Emperor will establish this worship in Rome and derive a surname from it: Heliogabalus. I imagine you would like to take a look at the divinity of the temple. You need not look up at the heavens; his Sunship is not there—at least not the Sunship adored by the Syrians. That deity will be found inside that building. He is worshipped in the form of a large stone pillar that ends at the top in a cone or pyramid, which represents Fire.

“Listen—look!—who could those ridiculous beings be, half-naked, with their faces painted, shouting and gesturing to the crowd?”

Some are charlatans. Others belong more specifically to the class of philosophers. The majority, however—especially those who beat the populace with clubs—are the primary courtiers of the palace, performing, as is their duty, some commendable comedy for the king.

“But what do we have here? Heavens! The town is swarming with wild beasts! What a terrible spectacle! What a dangerous peculiarity!”

Terrible, if you like; but not in the least bit dangerous. Each animal, if you take the trouble to observe, is following its master quite quietly. A few, to be sure, are led by a rope around the neck, but these are mostly the smaller or timid species. The lion, the tiger, and the leopard are entirely unrestrained. They have been trained without difficulty for their current profession and attend their respective owners in the capacity of valets. It is true, there are occasions when nature asserts her violated authority; but then, the devouring of a soldier or the throttling of a consecrated bull is a circumstance of too little importance to be more than hinted at in Epidaphne.

“But what extraordinary tumult do I hear? Surely this is a loud noise even for Antioch! It suggests some commotion of unusual interest.”"
}

It is fortunate that the swinging lamps in those endless colonnades are kept burning throughout the day; otherwise, we would have the darkness of Egypt during its time of desolation.

“It is certainly a strange place! What is the meaning of that unique building over there? Look! It towers above all the others and lies to the east of what I assume is the royal palace!”

That is the new Temple of the Sun, who is worshipped in Syria under the name Elah Gabalah. In the future, a notorious Roman Emperor will bring this worship to Rome and take the name Heliogabalus from it. I imagine you would like to take a look at the deity of the temple. You do not need to look up at the sky; his Sunship is not there—at least not the Sunship the Syrians worship. That deity is found inside that building. He is worshipped in the form of a large stone pillar that ends in a cone or pyramid at the top, which represents Fire.

“Listen—look!—who could those ridiculous people be, half-naked, with their faces painted, shouting and gesturing to the crowd?”

Some are street performers. Others belong more specifically to the class of philosophers. However, the majority—especially those who strike the crowd with clubs—are the main courtiers of the palace, performing some amusing task for the king as their duty requires.

“But what do we have here? Heavens! The town is swarming with wild beasts! What a terrible sight! What a dangerous feature!”

Terrible, if you like; but not in the least bit dangerous. If you take the time to observe, each animal is following its master quite quietly. A few are led by a rope around the neck, but these are mostly the smaller or timid species. The lion, the tiger, and the leopard are completely unrestrained. They have been trained easily for their current roles and serve their owners as personal servants. It is true that there are times when nature reclaims its violated territory; but the devouring of a soldier or the killing of a sacred bull is a matter of too little importance to be more than mentioned in Epidaphne.

“But what extraordinary noise do I hear? Surely this is loud even for Antioch! It suggests some commotion of unusual interest.”"
}

(*1) Flavius Vospicus notes that the chant mentioned here was performed by the common people when Aurelian, during the Sarmatic conflict, killed nine hundred and fifty foes with his own blade.

THE MURDERS IN THE RUE MORGUE

What melody the Syrens performed, or what identity Achilles took when concealing himself among the women, while perplexing, are not entirely beyond speculation.

—Sir Thomas Browne.

The intellectual traits described as analytical are, in their own nature, difficult to analyze. We recognize them solely through their consequences. We understand, among other facts, that they provide their owner, when possessed to a high degree, with the most intense satisfaction. Just as a powerful man takes pride in his bodily strength, finding joy in activities that engage his muscles, so the analyst takes pride in the mental vigor that unravels complexities. He finds gratification in even the most minor tasks that utilize his skill. He enjoys puzzles, riddles, and secret symbols, demonstrating in his answers a level of sharpness that seems supernatural to the average observer. His conclusions, reached through the very heart and spirit of systematic thought, possess the distinct appearance of pure intuition.

The power of resolution is perhaps greatly strengthened by the study of mathematics, particularly that supreme branch which, unfairly and solely because of its reverse procedures, has been labeled analysis as if it were the only form. Yet, to perform calculations is not the same as to analyze. A chess player, for instance, performs the former without attempting the latter. Consequently, the game of chess, regarding its influence on the mind, is frequently misinterpreted. I am not composing a formal essay, but merely introducing a rather strange story with some scattered remarks; I will, therefore, take the opportunity to claim that the superior faculties of the reflective mind are more effectively and usefully engaged by the modest game of draughts than by the intricate triviality of chess. In the latter, where the pieces possess strange and varied movements, along with differing and shifting values, what is merely complicated is often wrongly assumed to be deep—a common mistake. The attention is here engaged with great intensity. If it wavers for a moment, a mistake occurs that leads to damage or loss. Since the potential moves are not only numerous but intricate, the likelihood of such errors increases; in nine instances out of ten, it is the player with greater focus rather than the one with more sharpness who triumphs. In draughts, conversely, where the moves are singular and possess little variety, the chances for carelessness are reduced, and since focus is left relatively free, any advantages gained by either side are won through superior insight. To be less theoretical, consider a game of draughts where the pieces are limited to four kings, and where, naturally, no mistakes are anticipated. It is clear that in this case, victory can be determined (assuming the players are matched) only by some sophisticated maneuver, the product of a significant mental effort. Lacking standard options, the analyst adopts the mindset of his rival, aligns himself with that perspective, and frequently perceives in an instant the only ways—sometimes indeed quite simple ones—by which he might lure the opponent into a mistake or force a miscalculation."
}

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Footnotes—Four Beasts

(*1) Flavius Vospicus states that the hymn mentioned here was sung by the common people when Aurelian, during the Sarmatian war, killed nine hundred and fifty of the enemy with his own hand.

THE MURDERS IN THE RUE MORGUE

What song the Sirens sang, or what name Achilles took when he hid himself among women, while puzzling, are not beyond all conjecture.

—Sir Thomas Browne.

The mental traits described as analytical are, in themselves, difficult to analyze. We only recognize them through their effects. We know, among other things, that they are always a source of intense enjoyment to those who possess them in abundance. Just as a strong person takes pride in their physical ability, delighting in exercises that engage their muscles, the analyst takes pride in the mental activity that disentangles problems. They find pleasure in even the most trivial tasks that put their talent to use. They are fond of enigmas, riddles, and hieroglyphics, displaying in their solutions a level of insight that seems supernatural to the average observer. Their results, achieved through the very essence of method, truly appear to be the product of intuition.

The faculty of resolution is perhaps strengthened by mathematical study, especially by that highest branch which, unfairly and merely because of its reverse operations, has been called analysis by way of distinction. Yet, to calculate is not the same as to analyze. A chess player, for instance, does the former without attempting the latter. It follows that the game of chess, in its effect on the mind, is widely misunderstood. I am not writing a formal essay here, but simply prefacing a somewhat unusual story with some random observations. I will, therefore, take this opportunity to assert that the higher powers of the reflective intellect are more effectively and usefully challenged by the modest game of checkers than by all the elaborate frivolity of chess. In the latter, where the pieces have different and strange movements with various, changing values, complexity is often mistaken for depth—a common error. Attention is called into play quite forcefully here. If it flags for an instant, a mistake is made that leads to damage or defeat. Because the possible moves are not only numerous but intricate, the chances for such oversights are multiplied; in nine cases out of ten, it is the more focused player, rather than the more acute one, who wins. In checkers, by contrast, where the moves are uniform and have little variation, the chances of oversight are reduced. Since mere attention is left relatively unemployed, any advantages gained by either party are the result of superior insight. To be less abstract, let us imagine a game of checkers where the pieces are reduced to four kings, and where, naturally, no oversight is expected. It is obvious that here, victory can be decided—assuming the players are equal—only by some sophisticated move, the result of a strong exertion of the intellect. Deprived of ordinary resources, the analyst enters into the spirit of their opponent, identifies with them, and frequently sees at a glance the only methods—sometimes indeed absurdly simple ones—by which they may lure the opponent into error or force them into a miscalculation."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Regarding this ghastly enigma, we remain convinced that there is not the slightest lead.”

The following day, the newspaper provided these supplementary details.

“The Rue Morgue Tragedy.—Numerous individuals have been questioned concerning this highly unusual and terrifying event” [The term ‘affaire’ does not yet carry the trivial connotation in France that it does for us], “yet nothing has emerged to clarify the situation. We present here all the significant evidence gathered.

“Pauline Dubourg, a laundress, testifies that she has been acquainted with both victims for three years, having performed their laundry throughout that time. The elderly woman and her daughter appeared to have a harmonious relationship, showing great affection for one another. They were reliable in their payments. She could not comment on their lifestyle or financial status. She suspected that Madame L. earned money by fortune-telling. It was rumored that she had savings. She never encountered any visitors at the residence when she arrived to collect or return their garments. She was certain they employed no domestic help. There seemed to be no furnishings in any section of the residence aside from the fourth floor.

“Pierre Moreau, a tobacconist, testifies that he has regularly sold small amounts of tobacco and snuff to Madame L’Espanaye for nearly four years. He was born in the vicinity and has always lived there. The deceased and her daughter had resided in the building where the bodies were discovered for over six years. It was previously inhabited by a jeweler, who sublet the upper chambers to various tenants. The property belonged to Madame L. She grew unhappy with how the tenant mistreated the building, so she moved in herself and declined to rent out any space. The elderly woman was senile. The witness had seen the daughter perhaps five or six times over the course of six years. The pair led an extremely secluded existence and were rumored to possess wealth. He had heard neighbors claim that Madame L. practiced fortune-telling, though he did not credit the rumor. He had never observed anyone enter the premises except for the old woman, her daughter, a porter on one or two occasions, and a doctor perhaps eight or ten times.

“Numerous other neighbors provided testimony to a similar effect. No one was mentioned as a frequent visitor to the home. It was unknown if Madame L. and her daughter had any surviving relatives. The front window shutters were rarely opened. Those at the rear remained permanently shut, save for the large back room on the fourth floor. The building was in good condition and not particularly aged.

“Isidore Musèt, a gendarme, testifies that he was summoned to the residence around three o’clock in the morning and discovered twenty or thirty people at the entrance, attempting to enter. He eventually forced it open using a bayonet, not a crowbar. He encountered minimal resistance in opening it, as it was a double gate and was not bolted at the top or bottom. The cries persisted until the gate was breached, at which point they abruptly stopped. They sounded like the screams of one or more individuals in intense pain; they were loud and sustained, rather than brief and rapid. The witness led the ascent up the stairs. Upon arriving at the first landing, he heard two voices in a heated and hostile argument—one a harsh, gruff voice, the other much higher in pitch—a very peculiar voice.”"
}

He had never seen anyone enter the door except for the old lady and her daughter, a porter once or twice, and a doctor about eight or ten times.

“Many other neighbors provided similar testimony. No one was mentioned as a frequent visitor to the house. It was unknown whether Madame L. and her daughter had any living relatives. The front window shutters were rarely opened. The rear ones were always closed, except for those in the large back room on the fourth floor. The house was in good condition and not very old.

“Isidore Musèt, a police officer, testifies that he was called to the house around three o’clock in the morning and found twenty or thirty people at the gate, trying to get in. He eventually forced it open with a bayonet, not a crowbar. He had little trouble opening it because it was a double gate and was not bolted at the top or bottom. The shrieking continued until the gate was forced, at which point it suddenly stopped. The sounds seemed to be the screams of someone—or several people—in great agony; they were loud and prolonged, not short and sharp. The witness led the way upstairs. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I could only concur with the rest of Paris in viewing them as a riddle that defied solution. I perceived no way to track down the killer.

“We must not evaluate the methods,” Dupin remarked, “by this superficial investigation. The police of Paris, though highly praised for their sharpness, are merely sly. Their actions lack any system beyond the needs of the immediate moment. They put on a grand display of effort, yet these steps are often so poorly suited to their goals that they remind one of Monsieur Jourdain asking for his dressing gown to better hear the music. Their successes are sometimes startling, but they usually stem from basic hard work and energy. When those traits fail, their plans fall apart. Vidocq, for instance, was a persistent man who made good guesses. Yet, lacking disciplined thought, he constantly blundered through the sheer intensity of his focus. He blurred his sight by pressing the object too near. He might perceive one or two details with extreme clarity, but in doing so, he inevitably lost sight of the entire picture. Therefore, it is possible to be overly profound. Truth does not always reside in a deep well. Indeed, regarding the most significant knowledge, I suspect it is consistently found on the surface. The depth exists in the valleys where we search, rather than on the mountain peaks where it is actually discovered. The nature and origins of this mistake are well illustrated by observing the stars. To view a star by glancing at it—to look at it indirectly by turning the outer edges of the retina toward it, which are more sensitive to faint light than the center—is to see the star clearly and appreciate its brilliance, a brilliance that fades the more directly we stare at it. More light rays strike the eye in the latter instance, but the former allows for a more refined perception. Through excessive depth, we confuse and weaken our reasoning; it is even possible to make Venus disappear from the sky by staring at it too intently, too steadily, or too directly.

“Regarding these killings, let us conduct our own investigation before forming a judgment. The inquiry will provide us with some entertainment,” [I considered this a strange word to use, but I remained silent] “and furthermore, Le Bon once did me a favor for which I am grateful. We shall visit the scene ourselves. I am acquainted with G——, the Prefect of Police, and will easily secure the required authorization.”

The authorization was granted, and we traveled immediately to the Rue Morgue. It is one of those dreary streets situated between the Rue Richelieu and the Rue St. Roch. We arrived late in the day, as that district was quite far from our own. The residence was easily located, as a crowd of people remained on the opposite side of the street, staring at the shut windows with aimless curiosity."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "We have no intention of harming you. I give you my word as a gentleman and a Frenchman that we mean you no injury. I am fully aware that you did not commit the horrors in the Rue Morgue. Nevertheless, it is impossible to deny that you are involved to some degree. Based on my previous remarks, you must realize that I possess sources of information regarding this case that you could never have imagined. The situation is this: you have done nothing that you could have prevented, and certainly nothing that makes you guilty. You did not even commit robbery, even though you could have done so without consequence. You have nothing to hide, and no reason to keep secrets. Conversely, you are obligated by every standard of honor to reveal everything you know. An innocent person is currently jailed, accused of a crime for which you can identify the true culprit.

The sailor had largely regained his composure while Dupin spoke, yet his initial bold demeanor had vanished entirely.

"So help me God!" he exclaimed after a short silence, "I will tell you everything I know about this matter, though I do not expect you to believe half of it; I would be a fool to think otherwise. Still, I am innocent, and I will confess everything even if it costs me my life."

His account was essentially as follows. He had recently traveled to the Indian Archipelago. A group he belonged to went ashore at Borneo and ventured into the interior for a pleasure trip. He and a companion had caught the orangutan. When his companion passed away, the creature became his sole property. After significant difficulty caused by the animal's stubborn aggression during the journey home, he finally managed to secure it in his Paris residence. To avoid drawing unwanted attention from his neighbors, he kept it hidden until it could heal from a foot injury caused by a ship splinter. His final plan was to sell the beast.

Returning home from a night of drinking on the morning of the murders, he discovered the animal in his bedroom, having escaped from an adjacent closet where it had been kept, as he believed, securely locked. Holding a razor and covered in lather, it sat before a mirror, trying to shave, having surely observed its owner performing the task through the closet keyhole. Frightened by the sight of such a dangerous tool held by a ferocious animal capable of using it, the man was momentarily paralyzed. However, he was used to calming the creature, even during its most violent outbursts, by using a whip, and he turned to this now. Upon seeing the whip, the orangutan immediately leaped through the bedroom door, ran down the stairs, and escaped into the street through an unfortunately open window.

The Frenchman pursued it in despair; the ape, still clutching the razor, would stop now and then to look back and gesture at its pursuer until the man had almost caught up to it."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The official notice announcing this bounty guaranteed a complete pardon to any partner in the crime who might testify against his associates. Furthermore, a private notice from a group of citizens was attached to these posters, promising an extra ten thousand francs beyond the sum pledged by the Prefecture. Consequently, the total reward reached thirty thousand francs, a truly remarkable amount given the victim's modest status and the common occurrence of such brutal acts in major urban centers.

Everyone assumed the mystery surrounding this homicide would be solved at once. However, while a few arrests were made that seemed promising, no evidence emerged to connect the suspects to the crime, and they were quickly released. Surprisingly, three weeks had elapsed since the body was found without any clarity, and even the rumors of these events, which had so disturbed the public, had not yet reached Dupin or me. We were occupied with studies that required our total focus, and nearly a month had passed since we had ventured out, welcomed a guest, or done more than skim the main political stories in a daily journal. The initial news of the killing was delivered to us by G— himself. He visited us on the afternoon of July 13, 18—, and stayed until deep into the night. He was frustrated by his inability to track down the killers. His standing—as he remarked with a distinctly Parisian manner—was in jeopardy. His very reputation was involved. The public was watching him, and he claimed there was no sacrifice he would not make to solve the enigma. He finished a rather odd address by praising what he called Dupin's intuition, and he offered him a straightforward and undoubtedly generous proposal, the specific details of which I cannot reveal, though they are irrelevant to my story.

My friend deflected the praise as best he could, yet he immediately agreed to the offer, even though its benefits were entirely conditional. With this decided, the Prefect began detailing his own theories, frequently pausing to offer lengthy remarks on the evidence, which we had not yet seen. He spoke at length and, without question, with great knowledge, while I offered a few ideas as the night dragged on slowly. Dupin, remaining motionless in his usual chair, was the picture of attentive courtesy. He wore his spectacles for the entire meeting, and an occasional look beneath those green lenses was enough to assure me that he was sleeping quite soundly, if quietly, during the seven or eight sluggish hours that passed before the Prefect finally left.

At dawn, I obtained a complete record of all the testimony gathered at the Prefecture, along with copies of every newspaper that had printed any significant information regarding this tragic matter."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "No one crossed over. Within the Rue Pavée St. Andrée, nothing regarding the affair was spoken or overheard, even by those living in the same residence. M. St. Eustache, Marie’s suitor and fiancé who resided with her mother, testified that he remained unaware of the body’s discovery until the following morning, when M. Beauvais entered his room to inform him. Such a significant piece of news, it seems to us, was met with remarkable indifference.

In this fashion, the newspaper attempted to suggest a lack of concern among Marie’s kin, which contradicted the idea that they truly believed the remains were hers. The paper implied that Marie, aided by her associates, had left the city due to concerns regarding her reputation, and that these friends, upon finding a body in the Seine that looked somewhat like the girl, seized the chance to convince the public she had died. However, L’Etoile acted with premature haste. It was clearly demonstrated that no such indifference existed; the elderly woman was quite frail and so distressed that she could handle no responsibilities. Furthermore, St. Eustache, far from reacting calmly, was overwhelmed by sorrow and acted so wildly that M. Beauvais had to ask a relative to watch over him, keeping him from the disinterment proceedings. Additionally, while L’Etoile claimed the body was buried at public expense—asserting that the family rejected a generous offer for a private tomb and that no relatives attended the service—all these claims were effectively refuted. In a later edition, the paper tried to cast doubt upon Beauvais himself. The editor wrote:

“Now, the situation shifts. We are informed that once, while a Madame B. was visiting Madame Rogêt’s home, M. Beauvais, before departing, told her that a gendarme was expected, and that she must remain silent until he returned, leaving the matter to him. As things stand, M. Beauvais seems to hold the entire affair within his own control. One cannot move without encountering M. Beauvais; no matter where you turn, you run into him. For some reason, he decided that no one else should be involved in the investigation, and he has, by all accounts, pushed the male relatives aside in a very peculiar fashion. He appears to have been quite unwilling to let the family view the corpse.”

This suspicion directed at Beauvais gained some credibility from the following detail: a visitor to his office, shortly before the girl vanished and while he was away, noticed a rose placed in the door’s keyhole and the name “Marie” written upon a nearby slate."
}

Beauvais came into his room and told him. For a piece of news like this, it strikes us that it was received very calmly.

In this way, the newspaper tried to create the impression that Marie’s relatives were indifferent, which was inconsistent with the idea that these relatives believed the corpse was hers. Its insinuations amounted to this: that Marie, with the help of her friends, had left the city for reasons involving her reputation, and that these friends, upon finding a corpse in the Seine that somewhat resembled the girl, had taken the opportunity to convince the public that she was dead. But L’Etoile was once again too hasty. It was clearly proven that no such indifference existed; the old lady was extremely frail and so upset that she was unable to attend to any duties. St. Eustache, far from receiving the news calmly, was distracted by grief and acted so frantically that M. Beauvais convinced a friend and relative to look after him and keep him from attending the examination at the exhumation. Furthermore, although L’Etoile stated that the corpse was reburied at public expense—that a generous offer for a private burial was flatly refused by the family—and that no family member attended the ceremony—although, I say, all this was claimed by L’Etoile to support the impression it wanted to convey—all of it was satisfactorily disproven. In a later issue of the paper, an attempt was made to cast suspicion on Beauvais himself. The editor says:

“Now, then, the situation changes. We are told that on one occasion, while a Madame B. was at Madame Rogêt’s house, M. Beauvais, who was heading out, told her that a police officer was expected there, and that she, Madame B., must not say anything to the officer until he returned, but leave the matter to him.... In the current state of affairs, M. Beauvais seems to have the entire matter locked away in his head. A single step cannot be taken without M. Beauvais, for no matter which way you turn, you run into him.... For some reason, he decided that no one else should have anything to do with the proceedings but himself, and he has pushed the male relatives aside, according to their accounts, in a very strange manner. He seems to have been very much against letting the relatives see the body.”

Some credibility was given to the suspicion cast upon Beauvais by the following fact. A visitor at his office, a few days before the girl’s disappearance and while the office was empty, had noticed a rose in the keyhole of the door and the name “Marie” written on a slate hanging nearby."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "In my view, this piece demonstrates little more than the fervor of its author. We must remember that newspapers typically aim to stir up excitement or score a point rather than advance the truth. The latter is only sought when it aligns with the former. A publication that simply echoes common opinion, no matter how valid, gains no prestige with the crowd. The public considers only those who offer sharp contradictions to conventional wisdom to be deep thinkers. In logic, just as in literature, the epigram is the most instantly and widely valued, yet in both fields, it represents the lowest level of quality.

“My point is that the blend of wit and drama in the notion that Marie Rogêt survives, rather than any genuine credibility in the theory, is what prompted L’Etoile to propose it and ensured its warm public reception. Let us review the main points of this journal’s reasoning, while attempting to rectify the confusion with which it was initially presented.

“The author’s primary goal is to demonstrate, based on the short time between Marie’s vanishing and the discovery of the drifting body, that the corpse cannot be hers. Consequently, minimizing this duration becomes the reasoner’s immediate priority. In the reckless pursuit of this goal, he jumps to an unsupported assumption at the start. ‘It is absurd to imagine,’ he claims, ‘that the murder, if one occurred, could have been finished quickly enough for the killers to cast the body into the river before midnight.’ We naturally ask, why? Why is it absurd to think the murder happened within five minutes of the girl leaving her mother’s home? Why is it absurd to think it occurred at any other time of day? Murders have taken place at every hour. Yet, had the killing occurred at any moment between nine o’clock Sunday morning and a quarter to midnight, there would have been sufficient time to cast the body into the river before midnight. This premise essentially claims that the murder did not happen on Sunday at all; if we grant L’Etoile this assumption, we might as well allow it any freedom it desires. The paragraph starting ‘It is absurd to imagine,’ despite how it appears in L’Etoile, likely existed in the writer’s mind as: ‘It is absurd to imagine that the murder, if committed, could have been finished in time for the killers to cast the body into the river before midnight; it is absurd, we argue, to imagine all this, while simultaneously imagining (as we are determined to do) that the body was not cast in until after midnight’—a statement that is illogical on its own, though not as entirely ridiculous as the one that was printed.

“If my intention,” Dupin continued, “were simply to build a case against this portion of L’Etoile’s argument, I could safely leave it as it stands.”"
}

  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "If that is the case, did she possess any when she was discovered? These are significant queries that the testimony has completely ignored; furthermore, there are additional points of equal importance that have received no notice. We must strive to find answers through our own investigation. The matter of St. Eustache requires a fresh look. I do not suspect this individual, yet we must act in an orderly fashion. We shall verify the truth of the sworn statements concerning his location on that Sunday. Documents of this nature are easily manipulated to create confusion. If everything proves accurate, however, we will remove St. Eustache from our list of suspects. His self-inflicted death, while potentially supporting suspicion if the affidavits were fraudulent, is not an inexplicable event or one that should divert us from our standard analytical path if no such deception exists.

“In the plan I now suggest, we will set aside the central elements of this tragedy and focus our attention on its periphery. A common mistake in inquiries like this is restricting the search to the immediate facts while ignoring secondary or circumstantial occurrences. It is a flaw in legal proceedings to limit testimony and debate to what seems directly pertinent. Nevertheless, experience and sound reasoning demonstrate that a vast, perhaps even the greater, part of the truth emerges from what appears irrelevant. It is by following the spirit, if not the exact letter, of this principle that modern science has learned to account for the unexpected. Perhaps you do not follow me. The record of human understanding has consistently shown that we owe our most numerous and significant breakthroughs to secondary, incidental, or accidental events; consequently, it has become essential, when looking toward future progress, to make substantial—even primary—allowances for discoveries that emerge by chance, far beyond the scope of typical expectations. It is no longer logical to base our vision of the future solely on the past. Chance is now accepted as part of the foundation. We treat luck as a subject for precise calculation. We apply the mathematical formulas of academia to the unforeseen and the unimagined.

“I reiterate that it is a fact that the majority of all truth has originated from the collateral; it is in keeping with the spirit of this principle that I wish to shift our investigation, in this instance, away from the well-worn and unproductive path of the event itself toward the surrounding circumstances. While you verify the authenticity of the affidavits, I will review the newspapers more broadly than you have managed so far. We have only scouted the area of inquiry thus far; however, it would be truly remarkable if a thorough examination of the public journals, as I intend, does not provide us with small details that will guide our search.”

Following Dupin’s advice, I conducted a careful review of the affidavit situation. The outcome was a solid belief in their accuracy and the resulting innocence of St. Eustache. Meanwhile, my companion busied himself with what appeared to me to be an entirely pointless examination of various newspaper archives."
}

It is no longer philosophical to base a vision of the future solely on what has happened in the past. Accident is now accepted as part of the foundation. We make chance a matter of precise calculation. We subject the unexpected and the unimagined to the mathematical formulas of the academy.

“I repeat that it is a fact that the greater portion of all truth has sprung from the collateral; and it is in accordance with the spirit of the principle behind this fact that I would divert the inquiry in this case from the well-trodden and so far fruitless ground of the event itself to the surrounding circumstances. While you verify the validity of the affidavits, I will examine the newspapers more broadly than you have done so far. We have only scouted the field of investigation, but it would be very strange if a comprehensive survey of the public press, as I propose, does not provide us with some small details that will establish a direction for our inquiry.”

Following Dupin’s suggestion, I made a careful examination of the matter of the affidavits. The result was a firm conviction of their validity and the subsequent innocence of St. Eustache. In the meantime, my friend occupied himself, with what seemed to me a completely pointless level of detail, in a scrutiny of the various newspaper files."
}

  "rewritten_text": "In this account, while ostensibly describing the destiny of a Parisian working girl, the writer has meticulously tracked the core elements while merely mirroring the peripheral details of the actual homicide of Mary Rogers. "The Mystery of Marie Roget" was written far from the site of the crime, utilizing only information provided by newspapers. As a result, the author missed details that could have been utilized had he been present at the location. It is worth noting, however, that the admissions of two individuals—including the Madame Deluc mentioned in the story—provided long after the work was published, fully validated not only the overarching deduction but every significant theoretical point used to reach that conclusion.

(*2) The pseudonym of Von Hardenburg.

(*3) Nassau Street.

(*4) Anderson.

(*5) The Hudson.

(*6) Weehawken.

(*7) Payne.

(*8) Crommelin.

(*9) The New York “Mercury.”

(*10) The New York “Brother Jonathan,” edited by H. Hastings Weld, Esq.

(*11) New York “Journal of Commerce.”

(*12) Philadelphia “Saturday Evening Post,” edited by C. I. Peterson, Esq.

(*13) Adam

(*14) Refer to “Murders in the Rue Morgue.”

(*15) The New York “Commercial Advertiser,” edited by Col. Stone.

(*16) “A theory founded upon an object's attributes will hinder its development according to its purposes; and one who organizes subjects based on their causes will stop valuing them by their outcomes. Thus, the legal systems of all nations demonstrate that when law evolves into a rigid science, it stops being justice. The mistakes caused by a blind adherence to classification principles in common law are evident when observing how frequently the legislature must intervene to recover the fairness its own structure had forfeited.”—Landor.

(*17) New York “Express”

(*18) New York “Herald.”

(*19) New York “Courier and Inquirer.”

(*20) Mennais was among those initially suspected and detained, but he was released due to a complete absence of proof.

(*21) New York “Courier and Inquirer.”

(*22) New York “Evening Post.”

(*23) Regarding the periodical where the piece first appeared.

THE BALLOON-HOAX

[Incredible News via Express from Norfolk!—The Atlantic traversed in three days! A major victory for Mr. Monck Mason’s aerial craft!—Mr. Mason, Mr. Robert Holland, Mr. Henson, Mr. Harrison Ainsworth, and four companions have arrived at Sullivan’s Island, near Charleston, South Carolina, aboard the steerable balloon "Victoria," following a seventy-five-hour journey from shore to shore! Complete details of the trip! The following witticism, featuring the preceding headline in bold capital letters and filled with exclamation points, was first printed as a factual report in the "New York Sun" newspaper. It successfully served to provide sensational fodder for the curious public during the brief interval between Charleston mail deliveries. The demand for the "only paper with the story" was truly extraordinary; and, in truth, if some claim the "Victoria" did not actually complete the voyage described, it is hard to explain why she could not have done so.]"
}
