model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The people living on the island and at the fort naturally crowded out to view the airship, yet it was extremely hard to convince anyone that the journey—the crossing of the Atlantic—had truly occurred. The anchor hooked at exactly 2:00 P.M., meaning the entire trip was finished in seventy-five hours, or slightly less, measuring from coast to coast. No major mishaps took place. No genuine peril was felt at any point. The balloon was deflated and fastened without difficulty; when the manuscript used for this account was sent from Charleston, the group remained at Fort Moultrie. Their future plans were not discovered, but we can confidently assure our audience that further details will be provided by Monday or, at the latest, the following day.

This is without doubt the most astonishing, captivating, and significant feat ever achieved or even tried by humanity. Speculating on what grand developments might follow would be pointless at this time.

(*1) Note.—Mr. Ainsworth has not tried to explain this occurrence, which is, however, entirely capable of being understood. A cord lowered from a height of 25,000 feet, straight down to the earth or sea, would create the vertical side of a right triangle, where the base would stretch from the right angle to the horizon, and the hypotenuse from the horizon to the balloon. Yet, 25,000 feet of height is negligible when measured against the breadth of the view. Stated differently, the base and hypotenuse of this theoretical triangle would be so vast relative to the vertical side that the two could be seen as nearly parallel. Consequently, the horizon of the traveler would seem to be even with the basket. However, because the spot directly under him appears, and is, far below him, it naturally seems to be far below the horizon as well. This creates the sense of concavity, and this perception must persist until the height reaches such a ratio to the breadth of the view that the apparent parallelism of the base and hypotenuse vanishes, at which point the actual curvature of the earth must become visible.

MS. FOUND IN A BOTTLE

Qui n’a plus qu’un moment a vivre
N’a plus rien a dissimuler.

—Quinault—Atys.

I have little to share regarding my homeland or my kin. Mistreatment and the passage of time have pushed me from the former and alienated me from the latter. Inherited riches provided me with an exceptional education, and a reflective disposition allowed me to organize the knowledge that my youthful studies had carefully accumulated. Above all, I found great pleasure in the works of German moralists, not because of any misguided praise for their passionate insanity, but because my disciplined way of thinking allowed me to easily spot their inaccuracies. I have frequently been criticized for the dryness of my intellect; a lack of imagination has been blamed on me as a fault, and my skeptical viewpoints have always made me well-known. In truth, a deep fondness for physical science has, I suspect, colored my thinking with a very typical mistake of this era—I refer to the tendency to attribute events, even those least suited to such an explanation, to the laws of that discipline."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The vessel’s massive frame was a somber, murky black, lacking any of the typical decorative carvings found on ships. A solitary line of brass cannons extended from her uncovered ports, reflecting the glare of countless battle-lanterns that swayed throughout the rigging. Yet, what filled us with the greatest dread and wonder was that she maintained a full spread of canvas directly against that unnatural ocean and that uncontrollable gale. Upon our initial sighting, only her prow was visible as she ascended sluggishly from the dark, terrifying abyss ahead. For a heartbeat of profound dread, she hung upon the dizzying crest, seemingly reflecting on her own grandeur, before she shook, swayed, and descended.

In that moment, an unexpected composure settled over my mind. Reeling as far toward the stern as possible, I awaited the impending destruction without fear. Our own ship had finally stopped fighting and was foundering, bow-first into the waves. The impact of the falling vessel struck her, therefore, in the section of her structure already submerged, and the unavoidable consequence was to cast me, with unstoppable force, onto the rigging of the foreign craft.

As I landed, the ship tacked and turned; I credited the resulting chaos for my remaining unseen by the sailors. With minimal effort, I crept unnoticed to the main hatch, which stood slightly ajar, and quickly discovered a chance to hide within the hold. My reasons for doing so are difficult to explain. A vague sense of dread, which had gripped me upon first observing the ship’s mariners, was likely the primary motive for my concealment. I was reluctant to expose myself to a group of beings who had presented, during my brief observation, so many elements of strange unfamiliarity, uncertainty, and alarm. I therefore decided it was wise to arrange a hiding spot in the hold. I accomplished this by shifting a few planks, creating a suitable refuge tucked between the vessel’s massive beams.

I had barely finished when footsteps in the hold compelled me to utilize my shelter. A man walked past my hiding place with a weak and wavering stride. Though his face remained hidden, I could discern his general demeanor. He displayed signs of extreme age and frailty. His knees shook under the weight of many years, and his entire body trembled beneath the burden. He mumbled to himself in a low, fractured voice, using a tongue I could not comprehend, while rummaging in a corner among a collection of odd-looking tools and weathered navigational charts. His behavior was a strange blend of the irritability of extreme old age and the grave majesty of a deity. Eventually, he returned to the deck, and I did not see him again.

A sentiment, for which I possess no label, has seized my spirit—an emotion that defies explanation, for which the teachings of the past are insufficient, and for which I fear the future will provide no answer."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_1_029",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Her massive hull was a deep, dingy black, lacking any of the usual ship carvings. A single row of brass cannons poked out from her open ports, reflecting the light from countless battle-lanterns swinging back and forth in the rigging. But what truly filled us with horror and amazement was that she held her course under full sail directly into the teeth of that supernatural sea and uncontrollable hurricane. When we first spotted her, only her bow was visible as she rose slowly from the dark, terrifying abyss behind her. For a moment of intense terror, she paused at the dizzying peak, as if contemplating her own grandeur, then she trembled, teetered, and came crashing down.

At that moment, I don’t know what sudden calm came over me. Staggering as far toward the stern as I could, I fearlessly awaited the destruction that was about to overwhelm us. Our own ship was finally giving up the fight, sinking bow-first into the sea. The impact of the descending mass struck our vessel in the section already submerged, and the inevitable result was that I was hurled with irresistible force onto the rigging of the stranger.

As I fell, the ship tacked and turned; I credited the resulting confusion for my escape from the crew’s notice. With little difficulty, I made my way unseen to the main hatch, which was partially open, and soon found a chance to hide in the hold. Why I did so, I can hardly say. A vague sense of awe, which had gripped my mind the moment I saw the ship’s navigators, was perhaps the reason for my hiding. I was unwilling to trust myself among a group of people who, in the brief glance I had caught, presented so many points of strange novelty, doubt, and apprehension. I therefore thought it best to arrange a hiding spot in the hold. I did this by removing a small section of the shifting-boards, creating a convenient retreat between the ship’s massive timbers.

I had barely finished when a footstep in the hold forced me to use it. A man walked past my hiding place with a weak, unsteady gait. I couldn't see his face, but I had the chance to observe his general appearance. There was evidence of extreme age and frailty about him. His knees shook under the weight of his years, and his entire frame trembled under the burden. He muttered to himself in a low, broken tone, using words from a language I didn't understand, and fumbled in a corner among a pile of strange-looking instruments and rotted navigation charts. His manner was a wild mix of the irritability of second childhood and the solemn dignity of a god. Eventually, he went up to the deck, and I saw him no more.

A feeling for which I have no name has taken hold of my soul—a sensation that defies analysis, for which the lessons of the past are inadequate, and for which I fear the future itself will offer no explanation."
}

I had barely finished when a footstep in the hold forced me to use my hiding spot. A man walked past my place of concealment with a weak and unsteady gait. I could not see his face, but I had the chance to observe his general appearance. He showed clear signs of great age and frailty. His knees shook under the weight of his years, and his entire body trembled. He muttered to himself in a low, broken tone, using words from a language I could not understand, and he searched in a corner among a pile of strange-looking instruments and rotted navigation charts. His manner was a wild mix of the irritability of second childhood and the solemn dignity of a god. Eventually, he went up to the deck, and I saw him no more.

A feeling for which I have no name has taken possession of my soul—a sensation that cannot be analyzed, for which the lessons of the past are insufficient, and for which I fear the future will offer no explanation."
}

NOTE.—“MS. Found in a Bottle” first appeared in 1831, and it was only many years later that I discovered Mercator’s maps, which depict the ocean pouring through four openings into the (northern) Polar Gulf to be swallowed by the earth's core; the Pole itself is shown as a black rock of immense height.

THE OVAL PORTRAIT

The castle my servant had broken into, rather than let me spend the night outdoors in my severely injured state, was one of those structures of mixed melancholy and majesty that have long loomed over the Apennines, both in reality and in the imagination of Mrs. Radcliffe. It seemed to have been deserted quite recently. We settled into one of the smallest, least opulent rooms, located in a distant turret. Its furnishings were ornate but worn and aged. The walls were covered in tapestries and adorned with various heraldic displays, along with a large collection of vibrant modern paintings in elaborate golden frames. These artworks, hanging not only on the main walls but also in the many alcoves created by the castle's strange design—these paintings, perhaps due to my early fever, captured my intense interest; I instructed Pedro to shut the heavy window shutters—as night had fallen—to ignite the candles of a tall candelabrum beside my bed, and to pull back the fringed black velvet curtains surrounding the bed. I desired this so I could surrender myself, if not to slumber, then to alternating between studying these images and reading a small book found on the pillow, which claimed to critique and explain them.

I read for a very long time, gazing with devotion and intensity. The hours passed swiftly and brilliantly until deep midnight arrived. The placement of the candelabrum bothered me, and reaching out with effort, rather than waking my sleeping servant, I adjusted it to cast its light more directly onto the book.

However, this movement had an entirely unexpected result. The light from the many candles now illuminated a niche in the room that had previously been cast into deep shadow by a bedpost. I thus beheld in clear light a portrait I had not noticed before. It was the likeness of a young woman transitioning into adulthood. I looked at the painting briefly, then shut my eyes. The reason for this was not immediately clear to me. Yet, while my eyes were closed, I reviewed my motive for doing so. It was an instinctive reaction to allow myself time to reflect—to confirm my sight had not misled me—to quiet and restrain my imagination for a more composed and certain look."
}

NOTE.—“MS. Found in a Bottle” was originally published in 1831, and it was not until many years later that I became familiar with Mercator’s maps, in which the ocean is depicted as rushing through four openings into the (northern) Polar Gulf, to be swallowed into the earth’s core; the Pole itself is represented by a black rock, rising to a staggering height.

THE OVAL PORTRAIT

The château that my valet had forced his way into, rather than let me spend the night outdoors in my severely wounded state, was one of those structures of mixed gloom and grandeur that have long loomed over the Apennines, just as much in reality as in the imagination of Mrs. Radcliffe. To all appearances, it had been abandoned only recently and temporarily. We settled into one of the smallest and least lavishly furnished rooms. It was located in a remote turret of the building. Its decor was rich, yet worn and antique. The walls were covered with tapestry and adorned with various armorial trophies, along with an unusually large number of spirited modern paintings in ornate golden frames. In these paintings, which hung not only on the main walls but also in many nooks created by the château’s strange architecture—in these paintings, my early delirium perhaps caused me to take a deep interest; so I told Pedro to close the heavy shutters—since it was already night—to light the candles of a tall candelabra standing by my bed, and to pull back the fringed black velvet curtains that surrounded the bed itself. I wanted this done so I could surrender myself, if not to sleep, then at least to alternating between studying these pictures and reading a small book found on the pillow, which claimed to critique and describe them.

I read for a long, long time—and I gazed with devotion. The hours flew by rapidly and gloriously, and deep midnight arrived. The position of the candelabra bothered me, and reaching out with difficulty, rather than waking my sleeping valet, I moved it to cast its light more directly onto the book.

But the movement produced an entirely unexpected effect. The light from the many candles now fell into a niche in the room that had previously been cast into deep shadow by one of the bedposts. I thus saw in vivid light a painting I had not noticed before. It was the portrait of a young girl just blossoming into womanhood. I glanced at the painting quickly, then closed my eyes. Why I did this was not immediately clear even to me. But while my eyes remained shut, I reviewed in my mind my reason for closing them. It was an impulsive move to gain time for thought—to ensure my vision had not deceived me—to calm and steady my imagination for a more sober and certain look."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "This official, however, has been completely bewildered; the root of his failure stems from the assumption that the Minister must be a simpleton because he has gained fame as a poet. All fools are poets; the Prefect senses this, and he simply commits a logical fallacy by concluding that all poets are fools.

“But is he truly a poet?” I inquired. “I am aware of two brothers, both of whom have achieved literary acclaim. I believe the Minister has authored scholarly works on the Differential Calculus. He is a mathematician, not a poet.”

“You are in error; I am well acquainted with him; he is both. As a poet and a mathematician, he would reason effectively; as a mere mathematician, he would have been unable to reason at all, and would have been entirely vulnerable to the Prefect.”

“Your views surprise me,” I remarked, “as they contradict the consensus of the world. You surely do not intend to disregard the established wisdom of centuries. Mathematical reasoning has long been considered the pinnacle of intellect.”

“‘Il y a à parièr,’” Dupin replied, citing Chamfort, “‘that every public notion, every accepted convention, is a folly, because it has suited the majority.’ The mathematicians, I admit, have endeavored to spread the popular misconception you mention, which remains an error despite its acceptance as truth. With a skill deserving a better purpose, for instance, they have applied the label ‘analysis’ to algebra. The French initiated this specific deception; yet if terminology holds any significance—if words gain meaning from their usage—then ‘analysis’ relates to ‘algebra’ about as much as, in Latin, ‘ambitus’ signifies ‘ambition,’ ‘religio’ means ‘religion,’ or ‘homines honesti’ describes a group of honorable men.”

“I perceive you have a dispute,” I said, “with certain Parisian algebraists; but please continue.”

“I challenge the utility, and therefore the merit, of reasoning developed in any mode other than purely abstract logic. Specifically, I contest the reasoning derived from mathematical study. Mathematics is the science of form and quantity; mathematical reasoning is simply logic applied to observations of form and quantity. The major mistake is assuming that even the truths of so-called pure algebra are abstract or universal truths. This error is so profound that I am amazed by how universally it has been accepted. Mathematical axioms are not axioms of universal truth. What holds true for relations—of form and quantity—is frequently entirely false regarding, for instance, morality. In that field, it is rarely true that the sum of the parts equals the whole. This axiom also fails in chemistry. It fails when considering motives; two motives, each of a specific value, do not necessarily possess a combined value equal to the sum of their individual parts. There are many other mathematical truths that are only valid within the constraints of relation. Yet, the mathematician argues from his limited truths, out of habit, as if they possessed absolute, universal application—just as the world indeed assumes they do. Bryant, in his scholarly ‘Mythology,’ notes a similar source of error, stating that ‘although the Pagan fables are not believed, yet we continually forget ourselves, and draw inferences from them as if they were existing realities.’”"
}

In ethics, it is rarely true that the sum of the parts equals the whole. This axiom also fails in chemistry. It fails when considering human motives, because two motives, each with a specific value, do not necessarily equal the sum of their values when combined. There are many other mathematical truths that are only true within the limits of their own field. However, the mathematician, out of habit, argues from these limited truths as if they had universal application—just as the world imagines they do. Bryant, in his scholarly ‘Mythology,’ notes a similar source of error when he says that ‘although we do not believe in pagan fables, we constantly forget ourselves and make inferences from them as if they were real.’”"
}

  "rewritten_text": "This official, however, has been completely confused. The root of his failure is the assumption that the Minister must be a fool because he is famous as a poet. The Prefect believes that all fools are poets, and he makes the logical error of assuming that all poets are fools.

“But is he really a poet?” I asked. “I know there are two brothers, and both are well-known writers. I believe the Minister has written scholarly works on differential calculus. He is a mathematician, not a poet.”

“You are mistaken. I know him well; he is both. As a poet and a mathematician, he can reason effectively. If he were only a mathematician, he would not have been able to reason at all, and he would have been easily defeated by the Prefect.”

“You surprise me,” I said, “with these opinions, which contradict what the rest of the world believes. You surely do not mean to ignore the established wisdom of centuries. Mathematical reasoning has long been considered the highest form of logic.”

“‘I would bet,’” Dupin replied, quoting Chamfort, “‘that any popular idea or accepted convention is foolish, because it has been agreed upon by the majority.’ I admit that mathematicians have done their best to spread this popular error, which remains an error even though it is presented as truth. For example, they have used their skill to apply the term ‘analysis’ to algebra. The French started this particular deception. But if words have any real meaning, then ‘analysis’ describes ‘algebra’ about as accurately as the Latin word ‘ambitus’ implies ‘ambition,’ ‘religio’ implies ‘religion,’ or ‘homines honesti’ implies a group of ‘honorable’ men.”

“I see you have a disagreement with some of the algebraists of Paris,” I said, “but please continue.”

“I question the usefulness, and therefore the value, of any reasoning that is limited to a specific field rather than being purely logical. Specifically, I question the reasoning developed through mathematical study. Mathematics is the science of form and quantity; mathematical reasoning is simply logic applied to observations of form and quantity. The great mistake is assuming that the truths of pure algebra are abstract or universal truths. This error is so significant that I am amazed at how widely it is accepted. Mathematical axioms are not universal truths. What is true regarding form and quantity is often completely false in areas like ethics. Bryant, in his scholarly ‘Mythology,’ notes a similar source of error when he says that ‘although we do not believe in pagan fables, we constantly forget ourselves and make inferences from them as if they were real.’”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_002",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "As you may recall, in the standard version of these tales, a certain king, having valid reason to be jealous of his queen, not only executes her but swears an oath, by his beard and the prophet, to marry the most beautiful maiden in his kingdom each night, only to hand her over to the executioner the following morning.

Having carried out this vow for many years with a religious punctuality and methodical approach that earned him a reputation as a man of devout character and sound judgment, he was interrupted one afternoon—presumably while praying—by a visit from his grand vizier, whose daughter had come up with an idea.

Her name was Scheherazade, and her plan was to either save the land from this tax on its beauty or die, as all heroines are expected to, in the attempt. 

Accordingly, even though it was not a leap year—which makes her sacrifice even more commendable—she instructs her father, the grand vizier, to offer her hand in marriage to the king. The king eagerly accepts this offer—he had intended to take her anyway, having only delayed the matter out of fear of the vizier—but in accepting, he makes it perfectly clear to everyone that, regardless of the vizier’s status, he has no intention of abandoning his vow or his privileges. Therefore, when the beautiful Scheherazade insisted on marrying the king, and actually did so despite her father’s wise advice to the contrary—when she insisted on marrying him, whether he liked it or not—she did so with her beautiful black eyes as wide open as the situation allowed.

It seems, however, that this clever young woman (who had undoubtedly been reading Machiavelli) had a very ingenious plan in mind. On the night of the wedding, she managed, under some plausible pretext, to have her sister sleep on a couch close enough to the royal bed for them to converse easily. Shortly before dawn, she made sure to wake the king, her husband (who held no grudge against her, even though he planned to break her neck the next day). She managed to wake him—even though he slept soundly due to a clear conscience and good digestion—by telling a story (about a rat and a black cat, I believe) in a low voice to her sister. When day broke, the story was not quite finished, and naturally, Scheherazade could not complete it then, as it was time for her to get up and be strangled—a fate slightly less pleasant than hanging, though perhaps a bit more refined.

The king’s curiosity, however, unfortunately outweighed his religious principles, leading him to postpone his vow for one day, with the hope of hearing that night how the black cat (I believe it was a black cat) and the rat fared in the end."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Suspended from the rooftops of these grand structures were countless jewels, resembling diamonds yet exceeding the size of a man; meanwhile, through the thoroughfares lined with spires, pyramids, and shrines, ran massive rivers as dark as ebony, teeming with eyeless fish.”

“Hmm,” the king remarked.

“We then navigated into a maritime region where we encountered a towering mountain, down the slopes of which poured torrents of molten metal, some reaching twelve miles in breadth and sixty miles in length; furthermore, from a crater at the peak, such a colossal volume of ash erupted that the sun was completely obscured from the sky, plunging everything into a darkness deeper than the blackest midnight. Consequently, even when we were a hundred and fifty miles away from the peak, it was impossible to discern the whitest item, no matter how near we held it to our sight.”

“Hmm,” the king remarked.

“Upon leaving that shore, the creature persisted in its journey until we reached a territory where the natural order appeared inverted—for there we observed a vast lake, at the floor of which, over a hundred feet below the water’s surface, a forest of tall, lush trees thrived in full foliage.”

“Hoo!” the king exclaimed.

“A hundred miles further on, we reached a climate where the air was sufficiently thick to support iron or steel, just as our own atmosphere supports a feather.”

“Fiddle-dee-dee,” the king replied.

“Continuing in that same path, we soon reached the most splendid region on earth. A magnificent river wound through it for several thousand miles. This river was of unfathomable depth and possessed a clarity more opulent than amber. It spanned between three and six miles in width, and its banks, which rose on both sides to a vertical height of twelve hundred feet, were adorned with ever-blooming trees and constant, fragrant flowers, rendering the entire domain a breathtaking garden; yet this lush land was known as the Kingdom of Horror, and entry meant certain death.”

“Humph!” the king grunted.

“We departed this realm with great urgency, and after several days, arrived at another, where we were startled to see swarms of monstrous creatures with horns shaped like scythes upon their heads. These grotesque beasts excavate massive, funnel-shaped caverns in the earth, lining the walls with stones arranged so precariously that they collapse the moment they are stepped upon by other creatures, plunging them into the monster’s lair, where their blood is instantly drained and their remains are later tossed disdainfully to a great distance from the ‘caverns of death.’”

“Pooh!” the king scoffed.

“Proceeding further, we observed a region containing vegetation that grew not in the earth but in the air. There were others that sprouted from the matter of different plants; others that drew their nourishment from the bodies of living animals; and then again, there were others that shimmered with intense fire; others that shifted from location to location at will, and, most remarkably, we found flowers that lived, breathed, and moved their limbs as they pleased, possessing, furthermore, the loathsome human inclination to enslave other beings and lock them in grim, isolated dungeons until they completed assigned labors.”"
}

“We then swam into a region of the sea where we found a towering mountain, down whose sides streamed torrents of molten metal, some twelve miles wide and sixty miles long; while from an abyss at the summit issued such a vast quantity of ash that the sun was completely blotted out from the sky, and it became darker than the deepest midnight. Even at a distance of a hundred and fifty miles from the mountain, it was impossible to see the whitest object, no matter how close we held it to our eyes.”

“Hmm!” said the king.

“After leaving this coast, the beast continued its voyage until we encountered a land where the nature of things seemed reversed—for we saw a great lake, at the bottom of which, more than a hundred feet beneath the surface, a forest of tall, lush trees flourished in full leaf.”

“Whoa!” said the king.

“A hundred miles further on brought us to a climate where the atmosphere was so dense that it could support iron or steel, just as our own supports a feather.”

“Nonsense,” said the king.

“Proceeding in the same direction, we soon arrived at the most magnificent region in the entire world. A glorious river meandered through it for several thousand miles. This river was of incredible depth and possessed a transparency richer than amber. It was between three and six miles wide, and its banks, which rose on either side to twelve hundred feet in perpendicular height, were crowned with ever-blooming trees and perpetual, sweet-scented flowers, making the entire territory one gorgeous garden. Yet, the name of this lush land was the Kingdom of Horror, and to enter it meant certain death.”

“Humph!” said the king.

“We left this kingdom in great haste, and after some days, arrived at another, where we were astonished to see countless monstrous animals with horns like scythes upon their heads. These hideous beasts dig vast, funnel-shaped caverns in the soil and line the sides with rocks, arranged so that they fall instantly when stepped upon by other animals, plunging them into the monster’s dens. There, their blood is immediately drained, and their carcasses are later contemptuously hurled a great distance away from the ‘caverns of death.’”

“Pooh!” said the king.

“Continuing our journey, we observed a district with plants that grew not in the soil, but in the air. There were others that sprouted from the substance of other plants; others that drew their sustenance from the bodies of living animals; and then again, there were others that glowed with intense fire; others that moved from place to place at will; and, what was even more wonderful, we discovered flowers that lived, breathed, and moved their limbs as they pleased, possessing, moreover, the detestable human passion for enslaving other creatures and confining them in horrid, solitary prisons until they finished their assigned tasks.”"
}

  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The sea, situated between the far-off island and the coast, possessed a highly peculiar quality. Even though a powerful gale was pushing toward the land at that moment—forcing a brig in the far distance to heave to under a double-reefed trysail, where it repeatedly vanished from view—there was no sign of a standard swell here. Instead, there was merely a brief, sharp, and turbulent clashing of water moving in every direction, regardless of the wind's orientation. Aside from the area right by the cliffs, there was very little foam.

“The Norwegians refer to that distant island as Vurrgh,” the elderly man continued. “The one in the middle is Moskoe. The island a mile to the north is Ambaaren. Over there are Islesen, Hotholm, Keildhelm, Suarven, and Buckholm. Further away, located between Moskoe and Vurrgh, are Otterholm, Flimen, Sandflesen, and Stockholm. Those are the actual names of these locations, though why anyone felt the need to name them at all is beyond both of our understandings. Do you perceive any sound? Do you notice any shift in the water?”

We had spent roughly ten minutes atop Helseggen, having climbed there from the inland part of Lofoden, meaning we had not seen the ocean until it suddenly appeared before us at the peak. As the man spoke, I began to hear a loud, steadily intensifying noise, reminiscent of the lowing of a massive herd of buffalo on an American prairie. Simultaneously, I noticed that the choppy nature of the ocean below us was quickly transforming into a current flowing toward the east. As I watched, this current gained incredible speed, its momentum growing more reckless with every passing second. Within five minutes, the entire sea, stretching as far as Vurrgh, was whipped into an uncontrollable rage; however, the primary disturbance was concentrated between Moskoe and the shore. There, the immense expanse of water, divided into countless clashing channels, erupted into a frantic state—heaving, boiling, hissing, and spinning into massive, countless whirlpools, all rushing eastward with a swiftness rarely seen in water unless it is falling down a steep cliff.

After a few more minutes, the scene underwent another drastic transformation. The overall surface became somewhat calmer, and the whirlpools vanished one by one, while massive lines of foam emerged where there had previously been none. Eventually, these streaks expanded over a wide area, merged together, adopted the spinning motion of the vanished vortices, and appeared to create the foundation for a much larger one. All at once, this formation took on a clear and precise shape, spanning a circle over a mile wide. The perimeter of the vortex was marked by a wide band of bright spray, yet not a single drop fell into the opening of the terrifying funnel. Its interior, as deep as the eye could see, consisted of a smooth, glistening, jet-black wall of water, tilted at a forty-five-degree angle to the horizon, spinning dizzily with a swaying, churning motion, and emitting a horrifying sound—part shriek, part roar—that even the great Niagara Falls never produces in its own desperate struggle toward the sky."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“It is impossible for anyone to comprehend the emotions I experienced at that instant. My entire body trembled as though I were suffering from a severe bout of fever. I understood his meaning behind that single word perfectly; I grasped exactly what he intended to convey. Carried by the gale that propelled us forward, we were heading directly toward the vortex of the Ström, and there was no escape for us.

“You must understand that when traversing the Ström channel, we typically steered far above the whirlpool, even during tranquil conditions, waiting patiently for the slack tide—yet now we were rushing straight toward the center of the abyss, and in the midst of such a tempest! ‘Perhaps,’ I reasoned, ‘we might arrive precisely at the slack—there is a sliver of hope in that thought’—but immediately I condemned myself for being foolish enough to entertain any optimism. I was fully aware that we were lost, even if we had been aboard a massive ninety-gun vessel.

“By this point, the initial intensity of the storm had subsided, or perhaps we simply felt it less while running before the wind; regardless, the waves, which had previously been suppressed by the gale and appeared flat and foamy, now rose into immense mountains. A remarkable transformation had also occurred in the sky. While the horizon remained pitch black in every direction, a circular opening of clear sky suddenly appeared directly above—as transparent as I have ever witnessed and of a vivid, deep blue—and through it, the full moon shone with a brilliance I had never before seen. It illuminated our surroundings with startling clarity—but, merciful God, what a sight it revealed!

“I attempted several times to address my brother, but for reasons I could not fathom, the roar had intensified to such a degree that he could not hear a single syllable, even though I shouted at the top of my lungs into his ear. Eventually, he shook his head, his face deathly pale, and raised a finger as if to command me to ‘listen!’

“At first, I failed to grasp his meaning, but then a terrifying realization struck me. I pulled my watch from my pocket. It had stopped. I examined its face by the moonlight, then wept as I cast it far into the sea. It had ceased running at seven o’clock! We had missed the slack tide, and the Ström’s whirlpool was raging at its peak intensity!

“When a vessel is sturdily constructed, balanced correctly, and not overburdened, the waves during a heavy storm, while running with the wind, seem to slide away beneath her—a phenomenon that appears quite odd to those unfamiliar with the sea—and this is termed ‘riding’ in nautical parlance.

“We had managed to ride the swells quite skillfully thus far; however, a massive wave suddenly caught us beneath the stern, lifting us upward—higher and higher—as if we were being carried into the heavens."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_005",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“No one will ever understand how I felt at that moment. I shook from head to toe as if I were suffering from a violent fever. I understood perfectly what he meant by that single word—I knew exactly what he wanted me to realize. With the wind currently driving us forward, we were headed straight for the vortex of the Maelström, and nothing could save us!

“You understand that when crossing the Maelström channel, we always traveled far above the whirlpool, even in the calmest weather, and then had to wait and watch carefully for the slack tide—but now we were being driven directly into the pool itself, and in a hurricane like this! ‘Surely,’ I thought, ‘we will arrive right around the slack—there is some small hope in that’—but the next moment I cursed myself for being such a fool as to dream of hope at all. I knew very well that we were doomed, even if we had been a massive ninety-gun warship.

“By this time the initial fury of the storm had faded, or perhaps we simply felt it less as we raced before it, but regardless, the seas, which had initially been flattened and frothing from the wind, now rose into absolute mountains. A strange change had also come over the sky. In every direction it remained pitch black, but almost directly overhead, a circular rift of clear sky suddenly opened—as clear as I have ever seen—a deep, bright blue—and through it, the full moon blazed with a brilliance I had never seen her wear before. She lit up everything around us with perfect clarity—but, oh God, what a scene it was to illuminate!

“I made a few attempts to speak to my brother—but, in a way I could not understand, the roar had increased so much that I could not make him hear a single word, even though I screamed at the top of my lungs into his ear. Presently, he shook his head, looking pale as death, and held up one of his fingers, as if to say ‘listen!’

“At first I could not understand what he meant—but soon a hideous thought flashed upon me. I pulled my watch from my pocket. It had stopped. I glanced at its face by the moonlight, and then burst into tears as I flung it far away into the ocean. It had stopped at seven o’clock! We had missed the time of the slack, and the whirlpool was in full fury!

“When a boat is well built, properly balanced, and not heavily loaded, the waves in a strong gale, when she is running with the wind, always seem to slip from beneath her—which seems very strange to a landsman—and this is what is called riding, in nautical terms.

“Well, so far we had ridden the swells quite skillfully; but presently a gigantic wave happened to catch us right under the stern, and carried us with it as it rose—up—up—as if into the sky."
}

Now, however, we were being driven directly into the pool during a hurricane. I thought that we might reach it just as the tide turned, which offered a glimmer of hope, but I immediately cursed myself for being foolish enough to dream of survival. I knew we were doomed, even if we had been in a massive warship.

By this time, the initial fury of the storm had faded, or perhaps we simply felt it less as we raced before it. Regardless, the waves, which had been flattened by the wind, now rose into absolute mountains. A strange change had also come over the sky. While it remained pitch black in every direction, a circular opening of clear, bright blue sky suddenly appeared directly overhead. Through it, the full moon shone with a brilliance I had never seen before. It illuminated everything around us with perfect clarity, but, God, what a terrifying scene it revealed.

I tried to speak to my brother once or twice, but the noise had increased so much that I could not make him hear a word, even though I screamed at the top of my lungs into his ear. He shook his head, looking pale as death, and held up a finger as if to say, "Listen!"

At first, I did not understand, but then a horrifying thought struck me. I pulled my watch from my pocket. It had stopped. I looked at the face by the moonlight and burst into tears as I threw it into the ocean. It had stopped at seven o’clock! We had missed the time of the slack tide, and the whirlpool was now in full fury.

When a boat is well-built, balanced, and not overloaded, the waves in a strong gale seem to slip beneath her as she moves with the wind—a process sailors call "riding."

We had been riding the swells quite well so far, but suddenly a gigantic wave caught us under the stern and lifted us up, higher and higher, as if we were being carried into the sky."
}

This discovery asserts that beyond its inherent utility for manufacturing, gold is now, or will soon be—assuming Von Kempelen cannot keep his secret indefinitely—no more valuable than lead and significantly less valuable than silver. It is truly challenging to predict the future outcomes of this finding, yet one point is certain: had this news been revealed six months earlier, it would have significantly impacted the colonization of California. MESMERIC REVELATION

Whatever uncertainty may still surround the underlying principles of mesmerism, its remarkable phenomena are now almost globally accepted. In this state, the subject uses their external senses only with great effort and weakness, yet perceives matters beyond physical reach through unknown channels with heightened clarity. Furthermore, their mental faculties are remarkably sharpened, their connection to the mesmerizer is deep, and their responsiveness grows with repeated sessions, making the resulting phenomena more frequent and distinct.

I maintain that these general laws of mesmerism require no further proof, and I will not burden my readers with such a redundant demonstration today. My current objective is quite different. He had been suffering from advanced tuberculosis for many months, though my treatments had alleviated the most painful symptoms. On the night of Wednesday, the fifteenth, I was called to his bedside. The patient was experiencing severe cardiac pain and struggled to breathe, exhibiting all the standard signs of asthma."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The final form remains hidden from our basic senses, leaving us to observe only the outer casing that sheds away during decomposition; we do not see the core itself, though that core, like the shell, is perceptible to those who have attained the final state of existence.

_P._ You have frequently remarked that the mesmeric condition is quite similar to death. Why is that?

_V._ By saying it resembles death, I mean it resembles the final existence; when I am in a trance, the senses belonging to my basic life are suspended, and I experience the external world directly, without the need for physical organs, utilizing a medium that I will eventually use in my final, unorganized state.

_P._ Unorganized?

_V._ Indeed; organs are mechanisms that connect an individual to specific types and shapes of matter, while excluding others. Human organs are suited solely for our basic state; our final state, being unorganized, possesses boundless understanding in every respect except for one—the nature of God’s will—which is to say, the movement of unparticled matter. You can form a clear notion of the final body by imagining it as pure brain. It is not actually that, but such a thought will help you grasp its true nature. A glowing object causes vibrations in the luminiferous ether. These vibrations create corresponding ones within the retina, which then pass them to the optic nerve. The nerve transmits them to the brain, and the brain passes them to the unparticled matter that flows through it. The movement of this matter constitutes thought, with perception being its initial ripple. This is how the mind in our basic life interacts with the outside world, and this world is restricted for the basic life by the unique limitations of its organs. However, in the final, unorganized life, the external world reaches the entire body—which consists of a substance similar to brain, as I noted—with no interference other than an ether far more refined than the luminiferous one; the entire body vibrates in harmony with this ether, triggering the unparticled matter within it. Therefore, we must credit the nearly infinite perception of the final life to the lack of specialized organs. For basic beings, organs serve as the cages required to hold them until they are ready to fly.

_P._ You mention basic “beings.” Are there other thinking creatures besides humans?

_V._ The vast gathering of rare matter into nebulae, planets, suns, and other bodies that are neither nebulae, suns, nor planets, exists solely to provide sustenance for the specific organs of an infinite number of basic beings. Without the requirement for a basic stage preceding the final life, such bodies would not exist. Each one is inhabited by a unique type of organic, basic, thinking creature. In every case, the organs differ according to the characteristics of the environment. Upon death, or transformation, these creatures enter the final life—immortality—and, aware of all mysteries except for the one, they move and act everywhere through simple will: dwelling not within the stars, which appear to us as the only tangible things and for which we foolishly believe space was created, but within SPACE itself—that infinity whose truly substantial vastness consumes the shadows of the stars, erasing them as non-existent from the view of the angels."
}

 P. You have often said that the mesmeric state very closely resembles death. How is this?

 V. When I say that it resembles death, I mean that it resembles the ultimate life; for when I am entranced, the senses of my basic life are suspended, and I perceive external things directly, without organs, through a medium that I shall use in the ultimate, unorganized life.

 P. Unorganized?

 V. Yes; organs are mechanisms by which the individual is brought into sensory contact with specific classes and forms of matter, to the exclusion of other classes and forms. The organs of man are adapted to his basic condition, and to that only; his ultimate condition, being unorganized, is of unlimited comprehension in all points but one—the nature of the will of God—that is to say, the motion of the unparticled matter. You will have a clear idea of the ultimate body by imagining it to be entirely brain. This it is not; but a conception of this nature will bring you near a comprehension of what it is. A luminous body imparts vibration to the luminiferous ether. The vibrations generate similar ones within the retina; these again communicate similar ones to the optic nerve. The nerve conveys similar ones to the brain; the brain, also, similar ones to the unparticled matter that permeates it. The motion of this latter is thought, of which perception is the first ripple. This is the mode by which the mind of the basic life communicates with the external world; and this external world is, to the basic life, limited, through the unique nature of its organs. But in the ultimate, unorganized life, the external world reaches the whole body, (which is of a substance having affinity to brain, as I have said,) with no other intervention than that of an infinitely rarer ether than even the luminiferous; and to this ether—in unison with it—the whole body vibrates, setting in motion the unparticled matter that permeates it. It is to the absence of unique organs, therefore, that we must attribute the nearly unlimited perception of the ultimate life. To basic beings, organs are the cages necessary to confine them until they are ready to fly.

 P. You speak of basic “beings.” Are there other basic thinking beings than man?

 V. The vast conglomeration of rare matter into nebulae, planets, suns, and other bodies that are neither nebulae, suns, nor planets, is for the sole purpose of supplying nourishment for the unique nature of the organs of an infinity of basic beings. But for the necessity of the basic, prior to the ultimate life, there would have been no bodies such as these. Each of these is inhabited by a distinct variety of organic, basic, thinking creatures. In all, the organs vary with the features of the place inhabited. At death, or metamorphosis, these creatures, enjoying the ultimate life—immortality—and aware of all secrets but the one, act all things and pass everywhere by mere will:—dwelling, not in the stars, which to us seem the only tangible things, and for the accommodation of which we blindly deem space created—but in that SPACE itself—that infinity of which the truly substantive vastness swallows up the star-shadows—blotting them out as non-entities from the perception of the angels."
}

  "rewritten_text": "The ultimate body remains hidden from our basic senses, and we only perceive the outer shell that falls away as it decays. We do not see the inner form itself, though that inner form, like the shell, is visible to those who have already attained the ultimate life.

P. You have often said that the mesmeric state is very similar to death. Why is that?

V. When I say it resembles death, I mean it resembles the ultimate life. When I am in a trance, the senses of my basic life are suspended. I perceive external things directly, without using physical organs, through a medium that I will use in my ultimate, unorganized life.

P. Unorganized?

V. Yes. Organs are tools that connect an individual to specific types of matter while excluding others. Human organs are designed only for our basic condition. Our ultimate condition, being unorganized, has unlimited understanding in every area except one: the nature of God’s will, which is the movement of unparticled matter. You can get a clear idea of the ultimate body by imagining it as entirely brain. It is not actually a brain, but thinking of it that way will help you understand what it is. A glowing object causes vibrations in the light-carrying ether. These vibrations create similar ones in the retina, which then pass them to the optic nerve. The nerve carries them to the brain, and the brain passes them to the unparticled matter that flows through it. The movement of this matter is thought, and perception is its first ripple. This is how the mind in our basic life connects with the external world, which is limited by the specific nature of our organs. However, in the ultimate, unorganized life, the external world reaches the entire body—which is made of a substance similar to brain—without any intervention other than an ether much rarer than light. The whole body vibrates in harmony with this ether, setting the unparticled matter in motion. Therefore, we owe the nearly unlimited perception of the ultimate life to the absence of specialized organs. For basic beings, organs are like cages that hold them until they are ready to fly.

P. You mention basic “beings.” Are there other thinking beings besides humans?

V. The vast collection of rare matter into nebulae, planets, suns, and other bodies exists solely to provide nourishment for the specific organs of an infinite number of basic beings. If not for the necessity of this basic stage before the ultimate life, these bodies would not exist. Each one is home to a unique variety of organic, thinking creatures. In every case, their organs vary based on the environment they inhabit. At death, or metamorphosis, these creatures enter the ultimate life—immortality—and become aware of all secrets except the one. They act and travel everywhere simply by willing it. They do not dwell in the stars, which we mistakenly think are the only solid things and the reason space was created. Instead, they dwell in SPACE itself—that infinity whose true, substantial vastness swallows up the shadows of the stars, erasing them as if they were nothing in the eyes of the angels."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He maintained that he was still quite prepared and even eager for the procedure to be performed, pressing me to begin immediately. Although a male and a female nurse were present, I did not feel comfortable undertaking such a task with only these individuals as witnesses, fearing they might prove unreliable should a sudden emergency arise. Consequently, I delayed the work until approximately eight o'clock the following evening, at which point the arrival of a medical student I knew, Mr. Theodore L—l, spared me from further difficulty. My initial plan had been to wait for the doctors, but I was persuaded to move forward, first by M. Valdemar’s insistent requests, and second by my certainty that time was critical, as he was clearly fading rapidly.

Mr. L—l kindly agreed to my request that he record everything that transpired, and it is from his notes that the account I now provide is largely summarized or transcribed word for word.

It was roughly five minutes before eight when I took the patient’s hand and asked him to confirm, as clearly as possible, to Mr. L—l whether he, M. Valdemar, was fully prepared for me to attempt to mesmerize him in his current state.

He answered weakly, yet clearly enough to be heard, “Yes, I wish to be. I fear you have mesmerized”—adding immediately after: “I fear you have waited too long.”

As he spoke, I began the motions that I had previously discovered were most successful in calming him. He was clearly affected by the initial sideways sweep of my hand across his brow; however, despite my greatest efforts, no further visible change occurred until shortly after ten o'clock, when Doctors D—— and F—— arrived as scheduled. I briefly described my intentions to them, and since they offered no resistance, noting that the patient was already in the throes of death, I continued without hesitation—though I switched from lateral movements to downward ones, focusing my gaze entirely on the sufferer’s right eye.

By then, his pulse could not be detected, and his respiration was labored and rattling, occurring at thirty-second intervals.

This state remained largely unchanged for fifteen minutes. At the end of that time, however, a natural yet very profound sigh emerged from the dying man’s chest, and the rattling quality of his breathing vanished, though the pauses between breaths remained the same. The patient’s limbs were cold as ice.

At five minutes to eleven, I observed clear evidence of the mesmeric effect. The vacant, rolling gaze of his eyes shifted to that look of restless internal focus seen only in cases of somnambulism, which is impossible to misinterpret. With several quick sideways passes, I caused his eyelids to tremble as if in the early stages of sleep, and with a few more, I shut them entirely. I was not content with this, however, and persisted with the movements energetically, applying the full force of my will, until I had entirely rigidified the limbs of the sleeper after arranging them in a seemingly comfortable posture."
}

My original plan had been to wait for the doctors, but I was persuaded to proceed, first by M. Valdemar’s urgent requests, and second by my conviction that I had no time to waste, as he was clearly fading rapidly.

Mr. L—l kindly agreed to my request that he record everything that happened, and it is from his notes that the following account is, for the most part, either summarized or transcribed word for word.

It was about five minutes before eight when I took the patient’s hand and asked him to state as clearly as possible to Mr. L—l whether he, M. Valdemar, was fully willing for me to attempt to mesmerize him in his current state.

He replied weakly, yet quite audibly, “Yes, I want to be. I fear you have mesmerized”—adding immediately afterward, “I fear you have waited too long.”

As he spoke, I began the hand passes that I had previously found most effective in subduing him. I briefly explained my intentions to them, and since they offered no objection, noting that the patient was already in the throes of death, I proceeded without hesitation—switching, however, from sideways passes to downward ones, while focusing my gaze entirely on the sufferer’s right eye.

By this time, his pulse was undetectable and his breathing was heavy, labored, and occurred at half-minute intervals.

This state remained largely unchanged for fifteen minutes. At the end of this period, however, a natural but very deep sigh escaped the dying man’s chest, and the labored breathing stopped—that is to say, the harshness was no longer apparent, though the intervals remained the same. The patient’s limbs were ice cold.

At five minutes to eleven, I noticed clear signs of the mesmeric influence. The glassy, rolling look of his eyes shifted to that expression of uneasy internal focus seen only in cases of sleep-walking, which is impossible to mistake. With a few quick sideways passes, I made his eyelids quiver as if he were drifting into sleep, and with a few more, I closed them completely. I was not satisfied with this, however, and continued the movements vigorously, exerting my full will until I had completely stiffened the limbs of the sleeper after arranging them in a seemingly comfortable position."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Pluto—that was the feline’s name—was my preferred companion and playfellow. I alone provided his meals, and he accompanied me throughout the residence. It was actually a struggle to keep him from trailing me along the public thoroughfares.

Our bond persisted in this fashion for a number of years, throughout which my overall disposition and nature—by way of the Demon Intemperance—had (I am ashamed to admit) undergone a fundamental shift for the worse. I became, as time passed, increasingly sullen, short-tempered, and indifferent to the emotions of those around me. I permitted myself to speak harshly to my spouse. Eventually, I even resorted to physical abuse against her. My other animals, naturally, were forced to endure the shift in my temperament. I not only ignored but mistreated them. Regarding Pluto, however, I still held enough affection to keep me from abusing him, as I felt no hesitation in harming the rabbits, the monkey, or even the dog whenever they happened to cross my path. Yet my malady intensified—for what sickness compares to Alcohol!—and eventually even Pluto, who was by then aging and thus somewhat irritable, began to suffer the consequences of my hostility.

One evening, arriving home heavily inebriated from one of my usual spots in town, I imagined the cat was shunning me. I grabbed him; when, startled by my aggression, he gave my hand a minor scratch with his fangs. A demonic rage immediately seized me. I was no longer myself. My true spirit seemed to vanish from my body at once, and a malevolence more than diabolical, fueled by gin, surged through every nerve of my being. I pulled a pen-knife from my vest, unfolded it, gripped the wretched creature by the neck, and intentionally sliced one of its eyes from the socket! I flush, I burn, I tremble as I record this wretched act.

As sanity returned with the dawn—once I had slept off the effects of the previous night’s indulgence—I felt a sensation of mixed horror and regret for the transgression I had committed; yet it was, at best, a weak and ambiguous emotion, and my soul remained unmoved. I sank back into debauchery and quickly submerged all recollection of the act in wine.

Meanwhile, the cat gradually healed. The empty eye socket certainly looked ghastly, but he no longer seemed to be in any pain. He wandered the house as he always had, but, as one might anticipate, he darted away in sheer panic whenever I drew near. I retained enough of my former self to feel initially saddened by this clear aversion from a creature that had once adored me. Yet this sentiment soon yielded to annoyance. And then arrived, as if to ensure my ultimate and permanent ruin, the spirit of PERVERSITY. Philosophy ignores this impulse. Yet I am no more certain that my soul exists than I am that perversity is a fundamental drive of the human spirit—one of the core, inseparable faculties or feelings that shape the character of Man. Who has not, countless times, caught themselves performing a wicked or foolish deed for no reason other than the knowledge that they ought not to?"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_010",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Pluto—that was the cat’s name—was my favorite pet and playmate. I was the only one who fed him, and he followed me everywhere I went around the house. It was even difficult to keep him from trailing me through the streets.

Our friendship continued this way for several years, during which my general temperament and character—due to the influence of the demon Alcohol—had (I blush to admit it) undergone a radical change for the worse. I grew, day by day, moodier, more irritable, and more indifferent to the feelings of others. I allowed myself to use harsh language toward my wife. Eventually, I even became physically violent toward her. My pets, naturally, were made to feel the change in my disposition. I not only neglected them but mistreated them. For Pluto, however, I still held enough regard to keep me from abusing him, as I had no hesitation in abusing the rabbits, the monkey, or even the dog when they accidentally or affectionately got in my way. But my disease grew upon me—for what disease is like alcohol!—and eventually even Pluto, who was now becoming old and consequently somewhat irritable, began to experience the effects of my bad temper.

One night, returning home heavily intoxicated from one of my usual spots in town, I imagined that the cat was avoiding me. I grabbed him; when, frightened by my violence, he inflicted a slight wound on my hand with his teeth. The fury of a demon instantly possessed me. I was no longer myself. My original soul seemed to flee my body at once, and a more than fiendish malice, fueled by gin, thrilled through every fiber of my frame. I took a penknife from my waistcoat pocket, opened it, grabbed the poor beast by the throat, and deliberately cut one of its eyes from the socket! I blush, I burn, I shudder as I write down this damnable atrocity.

When my reason returned in the morning—once I had slept off the effects of the night’s drinking—I felt a sentiment half of horror, half of remorse, for the crime I had committed; but it was, at best, a weak and ambiguous feeling, and my soul remained untouched. I plunged back into excess and soon drowned all memory of the deed in wine.

In the meantime, the cat slowly recovered. The empty eye socket, it is true, looked frightful, but he no longer seemed to suffer any pain. He went about the house as usual, but, as might be expected, fled in extreme terror whenever I approached. I still had enough of my old heart left to be initially saddened by this clear dislike from a creature that had once loved me so much. But this feeling soon gave way to irritation. And then came, as if to ensure my final and irrevocable downfall, the spirit of PERVERSITY. Philosophy takes no account of this spirit. Yet I am no more certain that my soul exists than I am that perversity is one of the primitive impulses of the human heart—one of the indivisible primary faculties, or sentiments, that shape human character. Who has not, a hundred times, found himself committing a vile or foolish act for no other reason than the knowledge that he should not?"
}

  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_010",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "Pluto—that was the cat’s name—was my favorite pet and playmate. I was the only one who fed him, and he followed me everywhere around the house. It was even difficult to keep him from following me out into the streets.

Our friendship continued this way for several years, during which my personality and character—due to the influence of the demon Alcohol—had (I am ashamed to admit) changed for the worse. Day by day, I became moodier, more irritable, and less concerned with the feelings of others. I allowed myself to speak harshly to my wife. Eventually, I even became physically violent toward her. Naturally, my pets felt the change in my temperament. I not only neglected them but mistreated them. However, I still had enough regard for Pluto to keep from abusing him, even though I did not hesitate to mistreat the rabbits, the monkey, or even the dog when they happened to get in my way. But my sickness grew worse—for what sickness is like Alcohol!—and eventually even Pluto, who was becoming old and therefore somewhat irritable, began to suffer from my bad temper.

One night, returning home quite drunk from one of my usual spots in town, I imagined that the cat was avoiding me. I grabbed him, and in his fear of my violence, he bit my hand, causing a slight wound. A demonic fury instantly took hold of me. I was no longer myself. My original soul seemed to leave my body, and a more than evil malice, fueled by gin, surged through every fiber of my being. I took a penknife from my vest pocket, opened it, grabbed the poor animal by the throat, and deliberately cut one of its eyes from its socket! I blush, I burn, and I shudder as I write about this terrible act.

When my reason returned in the morning—after I had slept off the effects of the night’s drinking—I felt a mix of horror and remorse for the crime I had committed. But it was, at best, a weak and uncertain feeling, and my soul remained untouched. I plunged back into excess and soon drowned all memory of the deed in wine.

Meanwhile, the cat slowly recovered. The empty eye socket looked frightening, it is true, but he no longer seemed to be in pain. He walked around the house as usual, but, as one might expect, he fled in terror whenever I approached. I still had enough of my old heart left to be saddened at first by this clear dislike from a creature that had once loved me so much. But this feeling soon turned to irritation. And then came, as if to ensure my final and total downfall, the spirit of perverseness. Philosophy does not account for this spirit. Yet I am no more certain that my soul exists than I am that perverseness is one of the basic impulses of the human heart—one of the fundamental faculties or feelings that shape human character. Who has not, a hundred times, found themselves doing something vile or foolish for no other reason than the knowledge that they should not?"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Confident, nonetheless, in the hidden nature of my hiding spot, I experienced no discomfort at all. The officers invited me to join them during their investigation. They searched every single cranny and crevice. Eventually, for the third or fourth occasion, they went down into the basement. I did not tremble in the slightest. My pulse remained as steady as that of a person sleeping in purity. I paced the cellar from one side to the other. I crossed my arms across my chest and strolled about with ease. The police were completely convinced and readied themselves to leave. The joy within me was too intense to be contained. I was desperate to utter at least a single remark to celebrate, and to make their belief in my innocence doubly certain.

“Gentlemen,” I finally stated, as the group climbed the stairs, “I am pleased to have quieted your doubts. I wish you all good health, and perhaps a bit more politeness. By the way, gentlemen, this—this is a very sturdily built residence.” (In my frantic urge to speak casually, I hardly understood what I was saying at all.)—“I might say an exceptionally well-built residence. These walls—are you leaving, gentlemen?—these walls are put together quite solidly;” and here, driven by a sheer madness of defiance, I struck hard with a walking stick I carried upon that exact section of the brickwork behind which rested the body of my beloved wife.

But may God protect and save me from the clutches of the Devil! No sooner had the echo of my strikes faded into quiet, than I was met with a sound from inside the grave!—a cry, initially stifled and fragmented, like the weeping of a youngster, and then rapidly growing into one prolonged, intense, and steady shriek, completely unnatural and monstrous—a howl—a wailing scream, part terror and part victory, such as could have emerged only from hell, combined from the throats of the damned in their suffering and the devils who rejoice in their ruin.

It is useless to describe my own reflections. Fainting, I reeled toward the far wall. For a single moment, the group on the stairs stood frozen, overcome by extreme fear and wonder. The next instant, a dozen strong men were laboring at the wall. It collapsed entirely. The body, already significantly decomposed and covered in blood, stood upright before the eyes of the onlookers. Upon its head, with a red open mouth and a single fiery eye, sat the repulsive creature whose cunning had lured me into homicide, and whose revealing cry had delivered me to the executioner. I had entombed the beast within the grave!

THE FALL OF THE HOUSE OF USHER

Son cœur est un luth suspendu;
Sitôt qu’on le touche il résonne..

—De Béranger.

Throughout the entirety of a dreary, somber, and silent autumn day, when the clouds pressed heavily upon the sky, I had been traveling by myself, on horseback, across a remarkably bleak region; and finally arrived, as the evening shadows approached, within sight of the sorrowful House of Usher. I cannot explain why—but, upon the initial view of the structure, a feeling of unbearable sadness filled my soul."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_011",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Confident, however, in the secrecy of my hiding spot, I felt no embarrassment at all. The officers asked me to join them in their search. They left no nook or corner unexplored. Eventually, for the third or fourth time, they went down into the cellar. I did not twitch a muscle. My heart beat as calmly as that of someone sleeping in innocence. I walked the cellar from end to end. I folded my arms across my chest and paced back and forth easily. The police were completely satisfied and prepared to leave. The joy in my heart was too strong to contain. I was burning to say just one word to celebrate my triumph and to make their belief in my innocence doubly certain.

“Gentlemen,” I said at last, as the group climbed the stairs, “I am pleased to have put your suspicions to rest. I wish you all good health and a little more courtesy. By the way, gentlemen, this—this is a very well-built house.” (In my frantic desire to say something casual, I barely knew what I was saying.)—“I might say an excellently well-built house. These walls—are you leaving, gentlemen?—these walls are solidly put together;” and here, through sheer reckless bravado, I rapped heavily with a cane I held in my hand against the very section of brickwork behind which stood the corpse of my wife.

But may God protect and deliver me from the fangs of the devil! No sooner had the echo of my blows faded into silence than I was answered by a voice from within the tomb! It was a cry, at first muffled and broken, like the sobbing of a child, and then quickly swelling into one long, loud, and continuous scream, completely unnatural and inhuman—a howl—a wailing shriek, half of horror and half of triumph, such as might have risen only from hell, coming together from the throats of the damned in their agony and the demons that rejoice in their damnation.

It is foolish to speak of my own thoughts. Fainting, I staggered to the opposite wall. For one instant, the group on the stairs remained motionless, paralyzed by extreme terror and awe. In the next, a dozen strong arms were tearing at the wall. It collapsed entirely. The corpse, already badly decayed and clotted with blood, stood upright before the eyes of the spectators. Upon its head, with a red, open mouth and a single eye of fire, sat the hideous beast whose cunning had seduced me into murder, and whose revealing voice had condemned me to the hangman. I had walled the monster up inside the tomb!

THE FALL OF THE HOUSE OF USHER

His heart is a suspended lute;
As soon as it is touched, it resonates.

—De Béranger.

During the entirety of a dull, dark, and silent day in the autumn of the year, when the clouds hung oppressively low in the sky, I had been traveling alone on horseback through a remarkably dreary stretch of country; and eventually, as the shadows of evening approached, I found myself within view of the melancholy House of Usher. I do not know how it was—but with the first glimpse of the building, a sense of unbearable gloom filled my spirit."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The extreme intensification of his familiar features and their customary expression was so profound that I questioned the identity of the man before me. The deathly whiteness of his complexion and the extraordinary brilliance of his eyes particularly shocked and intimidated me. Furthermore, his silky hair had been left to grow without care, and as it drifted around his face in a wild, delicate web, I found it impossible to associate its exotic, intricate appearance with any ordinary human being.

I was immediately struck by a lack of coherence in my friend’s behavior, which I soon realized stemmed from his vain, weak attempts to suppress a chronic state of nervous trembling. I had been somewhat expecting this, given his correspondence, my memories of his childhood habits, and my own deductions regarding his unique physical and temperamental makeup. His movements shifted between sudden animation and gloomy withdrawal. His tone fluctuated rapidly from a shaky uncertainty—when his vitality seemed entirely suppressed—to that specific style of forceful brevity: a sudden, heavy, deliberate, and hollow-sounding delivery, much like the leaden, controlled, and perfectly measured guttural speech heard from a hopeless alcoholic or a chronic opium user during their moments of peak agitation.

He spoke in this manner about the purpose of my arrival, his sincere wish to see me, and the comfort he hoped I would provide. He discussed his illness at length, describing it as a hereditary, constitutional affliction for which he saw no cure, though he quickly qualified it as a mere nervous condition that would surely vanish soon. It manifested through a variety of strange sensations. Some of these, as he described them, both intrigued and confused me, though perhaps his choice of words and his narrative style played a role. He suffered from a morbid sensitivity of the senses; he could only tolerate the blandest food, could only wear clothing of a specific fabric, found the scent of all flowers stifling, felt tortured by even dim light, and could only endure certain sounds produced by stringed instruments without feeling intense dread.

I discovered he was a captive to a strange, singular form of fear. “I will die,” he declared, “I must perish in this wretched madness. This is how I shall be destroyed. I do not fear future events themselves, but rather their consequences. I tremble at the thought of any incident, however minor, that might affect this unbearable agitation of my soul. I do not fear danger itself, only its ultimate result—terror. In this weak, pathetic state, I know the time will come when I must lose both my life and my sanity in a battle against the grim specter of FEAR.”

Additionally, through fragmented and vague suggestions, I gathered another peculiar aspect of his mental state. He was bound by superstitious beliefs regarding the house he occupied, which he had not left for many years. He spoke of an influence whose supposed power was described in terms too indistinct to repeat—an influence that the specific architecture and composition of his ancestral home had, through long endurance, exerted over his spirit. He claimed the physical appearance of the gray walls and towers, along with the dark lake they overlooked, had eventually shaped the very essence of his existence."
}
