model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Occasionally, I was forced to attribute his behavior to the inexplicable whims of insanity, for I would observe him staring into empty space for extended periods with the deepest concentration, as though he were hearing some phantom noise. It was not surprising that his state frightened me—that it spread to me. I felt the wild effects of his strange yet compelling superstitions gradually taking hold of me.

It was particularly when I went to bed late on the seventh or eighth night after Lady Madeline had been placed in the vault that I felt the full weight of these emotions. Sleep avoided my bed as the hours slowly slipped away. I fought to reason away the anxiety that controlled me. I tried to convince myself that much, if not all, of my feeling was caused by the unsettling effect of the room's somber furnishings—the dark, ragged curtains, which, stirred by the breath of a gathering storm, swayed erratically against the walls and rustled restlessly around the bed's ornaments. Yet my attempts were in vain. An uncontrollable shivering slowly spread through my body, and eventually, a weight of groundless dread settled upon my heart. Shaking this off with a gasp and a struggle, I sat up on my pillows and, staring intently into the deep gloom of the room, listened—I know not why, except that an instinct urged me—to certain faint and vague noises that arrived, during the lulls in the storm, at long intervals, from an unknown source. Overwhelmed by a profound sense of horror, inexplicable yet unbearable, I dressed quickly (for I knew I would not sleep again that night) and tried to rouse myself from the pathetic state I had fallen into by pacing rapidly back and forth across the room.

I had only walked a few times when a soft footfall on a nearby staircase caught my notice. I soon identified it as Usher's. A moment later, he knocked softly at my door and walked in, carrying a lamp. His face was, as always, deathly pale—but, in addition, there was a kind of frantic joy in his eyes—a clearly suppressed hysteria in his entire manner. His appearance terrified me—but anything was better than the isolation I had endured for so long, and I actually greeted his arrival as a comfort.

“And you have not seen it?” he asked suddenly, after gazing around the room for a few moments in silence—“you have not seen it?—but, wait! you shall.” Saying this, and having carefully shielded his lamp, he rushed to one of the windows and flung it wide open to the storm.

The violent force of the incoming gale nearly knocked us off our feet. It was, in truth, a stormy yet strikingly beautiful night, one uniquely singular in its terror and its splendor. A whirlwind had seemingly gathered its strength nearby; for there were frequent and fierce shifts in the wind's direction; and the extreme thickness of the clouds (which hung so low they seemed to press against the house's turrets) did not stop us from seeing the lifelike speed with which they raced from every direction against one another, without ever drifting away into the distance."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "His forehead was high with contemplation, and his gaze was frantic with anxiety; in the few lines etched upon his face, I discerned tales of grief, exhaustion, disdain for humanity, and a yearning for isolation.

“The man rested upon the stone, supporting his head with his hand, and gazed out at the wasteland. He peered down at the low, restless brush, up at the towering ancient trees, further toward the whispering sky, and into the blood-red moon. I remained hidden near the lilies, watching the man’s movements. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“The man shifted his focus from the heavens to the bleak Zaire River, the sickly yellow currents, and the ghostly ranks of water-lilies. He hearkened to the soft moans of the lilies and the low murmurs rising from them. I stayed concealed in my shelter, observing him. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“I then descended into the depths of the marsh, wading deep into the thicket of lilies, and summoned the hippopotami that lived in the swampy recesses. Hearing my cry, the hippopotami arrived with the behemoth at the base of the rock, roaring with terrifying intensity beneath the moonlight. I stayed concealed in my shelter, observing him. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“I then cast a curse of chaos upon the elements; a terrifying storm brewed in the sky where previously there had been no breeze. The heavens turned bruised and livid from the storm’s fury; rain lashed the man’s head, the river surged, the waters were churned into froth, the lilies wailed in their beds, the woods collapsed under the gale, thunder crashed, lightning struck, and the rock swayed to its base. I stayed concealed in my shelter, observing him. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“Enraged, I cast a curse of silence upon the river, the lilies, the wind, the woods, the sky, the thunder, and the sighs of the lilies. They became cursed and fell silent. The moon stopped its ascent, the thunder vanished, the lightning ceased, the clouds stood frozen, the waters leveled and stilled, the trees stopped swaying, the lilies stopped sighing, and no sound remained in the vast, infinite desert. I examined the inscriptions on the rock, and they had transformed; the characters now read SILENCE.

“My gaze turned to the man’s face, and it was pale with dread.”"
}

The man trembled in his isolation, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“He turned his gaze from the sky to the dreary Zaire River, the ghastly yellow waters, and the pale legions of water lilies. He listened to the sighs of the lilies and the murmurs rising from them. I stayed in my hiding place, observing him. The man trembled in his isolation, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“I then descended into the depths of the swamp, waded deep into the wilderness of lilies, and called to the hippopotami that lived in the marshy recesses. They heard my call and came, along with the behemoth, to the base of the rock, roaring loudly and terrifyingly beneath the moon. I stayed in my hiding place, observing him. The man trembled in his isolation, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“I then cursed the elements with a curse of chaos, and a frightening storm gathered in the sky where there had been no wind before. The heavens turned livid with the violence of the tempest. Rain lashed the man’s head, the river flooded, the water was whipped into foam, the lilies shrieked in their beds, the forest collapsed in the wind, thunder rolled, lightning struck, and the rock shook to its foundation. “I grew angry and cursed the river, the lilies, the wind, the forest, the sky, the thunder, and the sighs of the lilies with a curse of silence. They became cursed and fell still. “My eyes fell upon the man’s face, and it was pale with terror.”"

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "It was at that moment that Prince Prospero, driven to madness by fury and the humiliation of his brief moment of fear, dashed quickly through the six rooms, though no one dared follow him due to the paralyzing dread that had overcome them all. He held a naked blade high and had charged, with fierce haste, to within a few feet of the retreating figure, when the stranger, having reached the far end of the velvet-draped room, spun around to face his pursuer. A piercing cry rang out—the dagger fell, shining, onto the black rug, and immediately afterward, Prince Prospero collapsed, dead. Then, gathering the frantic bravery of desperation, a crowd of the partygoers surged into the dark chamber and grabbed the masked figure, whose tall form stood still and upright in the shadow of the ebony clock, only to gasp in absolute terror upon discovering that the burial shrouds and corpse-like mask they had handled so roughly contained no solid body.

And then the presence of the Red Death was recognized. He had arrived like a burglar in the night. One by one, the revelers collapsed in the blood-stained halls of their celebration, each expiring in the hopeless position in which they fell. The life of the ebony clock flickered out along with the last of the merrymakers. The flames in the tripods died away. And Darkness, Decay, and the Red Death maintained absolute control over everything.

THE CASK OF AMONTILLADO.

I had endured the thousand slights of Fortunato as best I could; but when he dared to insult me, I swore to have my revenge. You, who understand my character so well, will not imagine, however, that I voiced any threat. Eventually, I would have my vengeance; this was a matter firmly decided—but the very certainty of my resolve ruled out any possibility of danger. I had to punish, but I had to punish without suffering consequences. A wrong remains unavenged when the retribution strikes the person seeking it. It is also unavenged if the avenger fails to make his identity known to the one who committed the offense.

It must be clear that I had never given Fortunato any reason to question my friendship, either through my words or my actions. I continued to smile at him as I always had, and he failed to notice that my smile was now inspired by the thought of his destruction.

Fortunato possessed a vulnerability—though in other respects he was a man to be both respected and feared. He took great pride in his expertise regarding wine. Few Italians truly possess the spirit of a connoisseur. Mostly, their passion is feigned to fit the occasion—to deceive British and Austrian millionaires. In the arts of painting and jewelry, Fortunato, like his peers, was a fraud—but regarding vintage wines, he was genuine. In this area, I was much like him: I was quite knowledgeable about Italian wines myself and purchased them in large quantities whenever possible.

It was around twilight, one evening during the peak of the carnival madness, that I met my friend. He greeted me with great enthusiasm, as he had been drinking heavily. The man was dressed in motley. He wore a tight, multi-colored suit, and his head was topped with a conical cap adorned with bells."
}

A sharp cry rang out—and the dagger fell, gleaming, onto the black carpet, upon which Prince Prospero immediately collapsed, dead. Then, summoning the desperate courage of the hopeless, a crowd of the revelers threw themselves into the black room and seized the masked figure, whose tall form stood still and upright within the shadow of the ebony clock. They gasped in absolute horror to find that the burial shroud and corpse-like mask, which they had handled so roughly, contained no solid body.

And now, the presence of the Red Death was acknowledged. He had arrived like a thief in the night. One by one, the revelers fell in the blood-stained halls of their party, dying in the same desperate positions in which they collapsed. The life of the ebony clock flickered out along with that of the last party-goer. The flames in the tripods died. And Darkness, Decay, and the Red Death held total dominion over everything.

THE CASK OF AMONTILLADO.

I had endured the thousand injuries of Fortunato as best I could; but when he dared to insult me, I vowed revenge. You, who know my character so well, will not assume, however, that I voiced any threat. Eventually, I would have my revenge; this was a settled matter—but the very certainty of my plan ruled out any risk. I had to not only punish, but punish without consequence. A wrong is not truly addressed if the retribution catches up to the person seeking it. It is also not addressed if the avenger fails to make his identity known to the one who committed the wrong.

It must be understood that I had given Fortunato no reason, through word or deed, to doubt my goodwill. I continued, as was my habit, to smile in his face, and he did not realize that my smile was now caused by the thought of his destruction.

Fortunato had a weakness, though in other respects he was a man to be respected and even feared. He took great pride in his expertise regarding wine. Few Italians possess the true spirit of a connoisseur. For the most part, their enthusiasm is merely a performance to suit the occasion—a way to deceive British and Austrian millionaires. In painting and gems, Fortunato was a fraud like his countrymen, but regarding old wines, he was genuine. In this, I was much like him: I was skilled in Italian vintages myself and bought them whenever I could.

It was about dusk one evening during the height of the carnival season when I ran into my friend. He greeted me with excessive warmth, for he had been drinking heavily. The man was wearing a jester’s costume. He had on a tight-fitting, multi-colored outfit, and his head was topped with a conical cap and bells."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_016",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I discarded countless plans, as their execution carried a risk of being discovered. Eventually, while perusing some French memoirs, I encountered a story regarding a nearly lethal sickness that befell Madame Pilau, caused by a candle that had been inadvertently poisoned. The concept immediately captured my imagination. I was aware of my victim’s custom of reading while in bed. I also knew that his room was cramped and lacked proper ventilation. However, I will not bore you with unnecessary particulars. I need not recount the simple tricks through which I replaced the candle in his bedroom holder with a wax light of my own manufacture. The following morning, he was found deceased in his bed, and the coroner’s official finding was “Death by the visitation of God.”

Having come into his property, everything proceeded smoothly for me for several years. The possibility of being found out never crossed my mind. I had personally and meticulously destroyed the remnants of the lethal candle. I had left no trace of evidence that could lead to my conviction, or even to any suspicion of my guilt. It is impossible to describe the profound sense of contentment that filled my heart as I contemplated my total safety. For a very long time, I took pleasure in this feeling. It provided me with more genuine joy than all the material benefits I gained from my transgression. Yet, eventually, a time came when this pleasant sensation transformed, by almost invisible degrees, into a persistent and troubling thought. It was distressing because it was persistent. I could hardly banish it for a moment. It is quite typical to be bothered by the repetition in our minds of the chorus of a mundane tune or some unremarkable fragments from an opera. We are no less tormented even if the song itself is pleasant or the operatic melody is excellent. In this way, I eventually found myself constantly dwelling on my security, whispering to myself the phrase, “I am safe.”

One day, while walking through the streets, I caught myself muttering these familiar words aloud. In a moment of irritation, I altered them to: “I am safe—I am safe—yes—provided I am not foolish enough to confess!”

As soon as I uttered these words, I felt a freezing sensation spread to my heart. I had encountered these bouts of perversity before (the nature of which I have tried to clarify), and I recalled clearly that I had never managed to overcome their influence. Now, my own impulsive suggestion that I might be foolish enough to admit to the murder I had committed stood before me like the phantom of the man I had killed, gesturing toward my own destruction.

Initially, I attempted to cast off this mental nightmare. I walked briskly—then faster—and finally, I began to run. I felt an uncontrollable urge to scream. Each subsequent wave of thought filled me with fresh dread, for, unfortunately, I understood all too well that to think, in my circumstances, was to be ruined. I continued to increase my speed."
}

Having come into his property, everything proceeded smoothly for me for several years. The possibility of being found out never crossed my mind. I had personally and meticulously destroyed the remnants of the lethal candle. I had left no trace of evidence that could lead to my conviction, or even to any suspicion of my guilt. It is impossible to describe the profound sense of contentment that filled my heart as I contemplated my total safety. For a very long time, I took pleasure in this feeling. It provided me with more genuine joy than all the material benefits I gained from my transgression. Yet, eventually, a time came when this pleasant sensation transformed, by almost invisible degrees, into a persistent and troubling thought. It was distressing because it was persistent. I could hardly banish it for a moment. It is quite typical to be bothered by the repetition in our minds of the chorus of a mundane tune or some unremarkable fragments from an opera. We are no less tormented even if the song itself is pleasant or the operatic melody is excellent. In this way, I eventually found myself constantly dwelling on my security, whispering to myself the phrase, “I am safe.”

One day, while walking through the streets, I caught myself muttering these familiar words aloud. In a moment of irritation, I altered them to: “I am safe—I am safe—yes—provided I am not foolish enough to confess!”

As soon as I uttered these words, I felt a freezing sensation spread to my heart. I had encountered these bouts of perversity before (the nature of which I have tried to clarify), and I recalled clearly that I had never managed to overcome their influence. Now, my own impulsive suggestion that I might be foolish enough to admit to the murder I had committed stood before me like the phantom of the man I had killed, gesturing toward my own destruction.

Initially, I attempted to cast off this mental nightmare. I walked briskly—then faster—and finally, I began to run. I felt an uncontrollable urge to scream. Each subsequent wave of thought filled me with fresh dread, for, unfortunately, I understood all too well that to think, in my circumstances, was to be ruined. I continued to increase my speed."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Every attempt was unsuccessful. Numerous individuals who had been most vigorous in the hunt were beginning to slacken their efforts, succumbing to a dark despair. There appeared to be minimal hope for the infant—and even less for the mother. Yet, from the depths of that shadowy alcove previously noted as a segment of the Old Republican jail, situated opposite the Marchesa’s lattice, a person wrapped in a mantle emerged into the light. Pausing briefly at the edge of the dizzying drop, he dove straight into the canal. A moment later, as he stood upon the marble paving beside the Marchesa holding the still-living child, his cloak, weighed down by the soaking water, came loose. It fell in layers around his feet, revealing to the astonished onlookers the elegant figure of a very young man whose name was then famous throughout most of Europe.

The rescuer uttered no sound. But the Marchesa! She would surely take her child now—clasp it to her breast—embrace its small body and cover it with kisses. Sadly, someone else’s arms took the infant from the stranger—someone else’s arms carried it away, unnoticed, into the palace. And the Marchesa! Her lip—her lovely lip quivers; tears well up in her eyes—those eyes which, like the acanthus of Pliny, are soft and nearly liquid. Yes, tears gather in those eyes, and look! The entire woman vibrates to her core, and the statue has awakened to life. The whiteness of her marble face, the rising of her marble chest, and the very purity of her marble feet are suddenly flooded with a wave of uncontrollable crimson. A faint tremor runs through her delicate frame, like a soft breeze in Naples moving over rich silver lilies in the meadow.

Why should that lady flush? There is no reply to this question, other than the fact that, in the frantic haste and panic of a mother’s heart, she had departed the privacy of her boudoir without putting on her slippers and had completely failed to drape her Venetian shoulders as was proper. What other explanation could exist for her blushing, for the look in those wild, pleading eyes, for the strange agitation of that heaving chest, or for the convulsive squeeze of that shaking hand—the hand that, as Mentoni entered the palace, brushed by chance against the stranger’s hand? What reason could there be for the low, the remarkably low pitch of those hollow words the lady spoke hastily while saying goodbye? “You have won,” she said, or perhaps the murmuring water misled me, “you have won—one hour past sunrise—we shall meet—so be it!”

The commotion had faded, the palace lights had vanished, and the stranger, whom I now identified, remained alone on the stones. He trembled with immense agitation, his gaze scanning the area for a gondola. I felt compelled to offer him the use of mine, and he accepted the courtesy. After securing an oar at the water-gate, we traveled together to his home, during which time he quickly regained his composure and discussed our previous brief acquaintance with apparent warmth."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Come,” he finally remarked, pivoting toward a table of heavy, ornate silver, which held a few strangely colored goblets and two large Etruscan vases, crafted in the same unusual style as the one in the painting’s foreground, and containing what I took to be Johannisberger. “Come,” he said suddenly, “let us drink! It is early—but let us drink. It is truly early,” he added, lost in thought, as a golden cherub with a heavy hammer caused the room to echo with the first hour past dawn: “It is truly early—but what does it signify? Let us drink! Let us offer a libation to that solemn sun which these bright lamps and incense burners are so desperate to suppress!” After making me toast him with a full glass, he quickly downed several goblets of the wine in a row.

“To dream,” he went on, returning to his rambling manner of speaking, while holding one of the grand vases up to the brilliant glow of an incense burner, “to dream has been my life’s work. I have therefore constructed for myself, as you observe, a sanctuary of dreams. Could I have built a better one in the center of Venice? You see around you, it is true, a chaotic mix of architectural styles. The purity of Ionia is insulted by ancient designs, and Egyptian sphinxes lie stretched across golden rugs. Yet the result only disturbs the faint-hearted. Rules of location, and particularly of time, are the phantoms that keep people from appreciating the magnificent. I was once a decorator myself; but that height of absurdity has grown tiresome to my spirit. All this is now better suited to my needs. Like these arabesque incense burners, my soul is twisting in flames, and the madness of this setting is preparing me for the more intense visions of that realm of true dreams toward which I am now swiftly traveling.” He stopped suddenly, lowered his head to his chest, and appeared to be listening to a noise I could not detect. Finally, straightening his body, he gazed upward and recited the verses of the Bishop of Chichester:

“Stay for me there! I will not fail
To meet thee in that hollow vale.”

Immediately after, yielding to the wine’s influence, he collapsed at full length onto a couch.

A rapid footfall sounded on the stairs, followed quickly by a loud knock at the door. I was hurrying to prevent another interruption when a servant from Mentoni’s house rushed into the room, stammering in a voice thick with distress the disjointed words, “My mistress!—my mistress!—Poisoned!—poisoned! Oh, beautiful—oh, beautiful Aphrodite!”

Confused, I rushed to the couch and tried to wake the sleeper to the shocking news. But his body was stiff—his lips were pale—his once-bright eyes were fixed in death. I reeled back toward the table—my hand struck a shattered and scorched goblet—and the full, horrifying reality dawned upon me."
}

It is early—but let us drink. It is truly early,” he added, lost in thought, as a golden cherub with a heavy hammer caused the room to echo with the first hour past dawn: “It is truly early—but what does it signify? Let us drink! Let us offer a libation to that solemn sun which these bright lamps and incense burners are so desperate to suppress!” After making me toast him with a full glass, he quickly downed several goblets of the wine in a row.

“To dream,” he went on, returning to his rambling manner of speaking, while holding one of the grand vases up to the brilliant glow of an incense burner, “to dream has been my life’s work. I have therefore constructed for myself, as you observe, a sanctuary of dreams. Could I have built a better one in the center of Venice? You see around you, it is true, a chaotic mix of architectural styles. The purity of Ionia is insulted by ancient designs, and Egyptian sphinxes lie stretched across golden rugs. I was once a decorator myself; but that height of absurdity has grown tiresome to my spirit. All this is now better suited to my needs. Like these arabesque incense burners, my soul is twisting in flames, and the madness of this setting is preparing me for the more intense visions of that realm of true dreams toward which I am now swiftly traveling.” He stopped suddenly, lowered his head to his chest, and appeared to be listening to a noise I could not detect. Finally, straightening his body, he gazed upward and recited the verses of the Bishop of Chichester:

“Stay for me there! I will not fail
To meet thee in that hollow vale.”

Immediately after, yielding to the wine’s influence, he collapsed at full length onto a couch.

A rapid footfall sounded on the stairs, followed quickly by a loud knock at the door. }

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_019",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I had been saved for the latter. Through prolonged suffering, my nerves had frayed until I trembled at the sound of my own voice, making me in every way a suitable candidate for the kind of torture that lay ahead.

Shaking in every limb, I felt my way back to the wall, deciding to die there rather than risk the terrors of the pits, which my imagination now pictured in various spots around the dungeon. In a different state of mind, I might have had the courage to end my misery at once by jumping into one of these abysses, but now I was the greatest of cowards. Furthermore, I could not forget what I had read about these pits—that a quick death was not part of their most horrific design.

Agitation kept me awake for many long hours, but eventually, I fell asleep again. Upon waking, I found a loaf of bread and a pitcher of water beside me, just as before. A burning thirst consumed me, and I drained the vessel in one gulp. It must have been drugged, for no sooner had I finished than I became irresistibly drowsy. A deep sleep fell over me—a sleep like death. I have no idea how long it lasted, but when I opened my eyes again, the objects around me were visible. A wild, sulphurous glow, the source of which I could not initially identify, allowed me to see the size and appearance of the prison.

I had been greatly mistaken about its size. The entire perimeter of the walls did not exceed twenty-five yards. For several minutes, this fact caused me a great deal of useless concern; useless indeed, for what could be less important, given the terrible circumstances surrounding me, than the mere dimensions of my dungeon? Yet my mind took a frantic interest in trifles, and I busied myself trying to explain the error I had made in my measurements. The truth finally dawned on me. In my first attempt at exploration, I had counted fifty-two paces up to the moment I fell; I must have been within a pace or two of the scrap of fabric, having nearly completed the circuit of the vault. I then slept, and upon waking, I must have retraced my steps, thus assuming the circuit was nearly double what it actually was. My mental confusion prevented me from noticing that I had begun my tour with the wall to my left and ended it with the wall to my right.

I had also been deceived regarding the shape of the enclosure. While feeling my way, I had encountered many angles and thus concluded it was highly irregular; such is the powerful effect of total darkness on someone waking from lethargy or sleep! The angles were simply those of a few slight depressions or niches at odd intervals. The general shape of the prison was square. What I had taken for masonry now appeared to be iron or some other metal in huge plates, whose seams or joints caused the depressions. The entire surface of this metallic enclosure was crudely painted with all the hideous and repulsive images that the morbid superstitions of the monks have inspired."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I took this lesson deeply to heart. My every movement was surely under observation. Free!—I had merely avoided one agonizing death only to be handed over to something far worse. With that realization, I cast my eyes anxiously across the iron boundaries enclosing me. It was clear that something unusual—a transformation I could not initially grasp—had occurred within the room. For several minutes of hazy, shaking distraction, I occupied myself with useless, disjointed guesses. During this time, I first noticed the source of the sulfurous glow lighting the chamber. It came from a crack, roughly half an inch wide, running entirely around the prison at the bottom of the walls, which appeared, and were, entirely detached from the floor. I tried, though fruitlessly, to peer through the opening.

As I stood up from this effort, the secret of the room's change suddenly dawned on me. I had noted that while the outlines of the wall figures were clear enough, their colors seemed hazy and vague. These hues had now taken on, and were steadily gaining, a shocking and intense brightness, giving the ghostly, hellish images a look that might have shaken even stronger nerves than mine. Demon eyes, possessing a wild and ghastly life, stared at me from countless spots where none had been seen before, shining with the lurid gleam of a fire I could not force myself to believe was fake.

Fake!—Even as I inhaled, the scent of heated iron reached my nose! A stifling smell filled the cell! A deeper radiance settled with every second into the eyes watching my suffering! A more vivid shade of red spread across the painted scenes of blood. I panted! I struggled for air! There was no mistaking the intent of my torturers—oh! most cruel! oh! most devilish of men! I recoiled from the burning metal toward the middle of the room. Amidst thoughts of the fiery ruin that loomed, the memory of the well's coolness felt like a soothing balm. I ran to its lethal edge. I cast my straining gaze downward. The light from the glowing ceiling illuminated its deepest depths. Yet, for one frantic moment, my mind refused to grasp the meaning of what I saw. Finally, it forced—it fought its way into my consciousness—it seared itself into my trembling intellect. Oh! for a voice to cry out!—oh! horror!—oh! any horror but this! With a scream, I fled from the brink and hid my face in my hands—sobbing uncontrollably.

The heat rose quickly, and I looked up once more, shivering as if with a fever. A second transformation had occurred in the cell—and now the shift was clearly in its shape. As before, I tried in vain at first to grasp or comprehend what was happening. But I was not left in uncertainty for long. The Inquisitorial vengeance had been accelerated by my double escape, and there was to be no more playing with the King of Terrors."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_020",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "This was a lesson I took desperately to heart. My every move was undoubtedly watched. Free!—I had only escaped death in one agonizing form, to be delivered into something worse than death in another. With that thought, I rolled my eyes nervously around the iron barriers that hemmed me in. Something unusual—some change that, at first, I could not clearly grasp—had obviously taken place in the room. For many minutes of dreamy, trembling abstraction, I busied myself with vain, unconnected guesses. During this time, I became aware, for the first time, of the source of the sulfurous light illuminating the cell. It came from a crack, about half an inch wide, extending entirely around the prison at the base of the walls, which thus appeared, and were, completely separated from the floor. I tried, but of course in vain, to look through the opening.

As I rose from the attempt, the mystery of the change in the chamber suddenly broke upon my understanding. I had observed that, although the outlines of the figures on the walls were distinct enough, the colors seemed blurred and indefinite. These colors had now assumed, and were momentarily assuming, a startling and most intense brilliance, giving the spectral and fiendish portraits an aspect that might have thrilled even firmer nerves than my own. Demon eyes, of a wild and ghastly vivacity, glared at me from a thousand directions where none had been visible before, gleaming with the lurid luster of a fire that I could not force my imagination to regard as unreal.

Unreal!—Even as I breathed, the scent of heated iron reached my nostrils! A suffocating odor filled the prison! A deeper glow settled each moment into the eyes that glared at my agony! A richer shade of crimson spread over the pictured horrors of blood. I panted! I gasped for air! There could be no doubt of the design of my tormentors—oh! most unrelenting! oh! most demonic of men! I shrank from the glowing metal toward the center of the cell. Amid the thought of the fiery destruction that loomed, the idea of the coolness of the well came over my soul like balm. I rushed to its deadly brink. I threw my straining gaze below. The glare from the ignited roof illuminated its deepest recesses. Yet, for a wild moment, my spirit refused to comprehend the meaning of what I saw. At length, it forced—it wrestled its way into my soul—it burned itself into my shuddering reason. Oh! for a voice to speak!—oh! horror!—oh! any horror but this! With a shriek, I rushed from the edge and buried my face in my hands—weeping bitterly.

The heat increased rapidly, and once again I looked up, shuddering as if with a fever. There had been a second change in the cell—and now the change was obviously in its shape. As before, it was in vain that I first tried to appreciate or understand what was taking place. But I was not left in doubt for long. The Inquisitorial vengeance had been accelerated by my two-fold escape, and there was to be no more dallying with the King of Terrors."
}

I rushed to its deadly brink. I threw my straining gaze below. The glare from the ignited roof illuminated its deepest recesses. Yet, for a wild moment, my spirit refused to comprehend the meaning of what I saw. At length, it forced—it wrestled its way into my soul—it burned itself into my shuddering reason. Oh! for a voice to speak!—oh! horror!—oh! any horror but this! With a shriek, I rushed from the edge and buried my face in my hands—weeping bitterly.

The heat increased rapidly, and once again I looked up, shuddering as if with a fever. There had been a second change in the cell—and now the change was obviously in its shape. As before, it was in vain that I first tried to appreciate or understand what was taking place. But I was not left in doubt for long. The Inquisitorial vengeance had been accelerated by my two-fold escape, and there was to be no more dallying with the King of Terrors."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Though his utterance was impossible to interpret, he did speak; the syllables were clear. Once he had finished, he collapsed onto the ground with a heavy thud.

For a short time, everyone stood frozen in shock, yet the pressing nature of the situation soon brought back their composure. It became clear that Mr. Stapleton still lived, though he had fainted. After being given ether, he recovered and quickly returned to health and his companions—though they were kept ignorant of his revival until there was no longer any risk of him falling ill again. One can imagine their amazement and overwhelming joy.

The most chilling aspect of this event, however, lies in Mr. S.’s own claims. He maintains that he was never entirely unconscious; he insists that, in a dim and muddled way, he perceived everything that occurred from the time his doctors declared him dead until he collapsed in the hospital. “I am alive” were the words he had struggled to speak in his desperation upon realizing he was in the dissecting room.

It would be simple to provide many more such accounts, but I will refrain, as we do not require them to prove that people are sometimes buried while still alive. When we consider how seldom we are able to uncover such cases, we must concede that they likely happen often without our knowledge. In fact, whenever a cemetery is disturbed for any significant reason, skeletons are almost always discovered in positions that provoke the most terrifying suspicions.

The suspicion is indeed dreadful, but the fate itself is far worse! It can be stated with certainty that no situation is better suited to cause the ultimate physical and mental agony than being interred while still breathing. The suffocating pressure on the chest, the foul odors rising from the moist soil, the shroud clinging to the skin, the tight confinement of the coffin, the total darkness, the crushing silence, and the unseen but felt presence of the grave worm—these horrors, combined with thoughts of the world above and the memory of loved ones who would rescue us if they only knew, yet who will never be informed of our plight, create a level of unbearable terror that even the boldest mind must shrink from. We know of no greater agony on Earth, and we can imagine nothing more hideous in the depths of Hell. Consequently, all stories on this subject possess a deep interest, which relies appropriately on our belief in their truth. What I am about to relate comes from my own direct and personal experience.

For many years, I have suffered from a strange condition that doctors, for lack of a better name, have labeled catalepsy. While the underlying causes and the precise nature of this ailment remain obscure, its outward symptoms are well recognized."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Evening drew near, and a tempest caught us. We found our only refuge in the cabin of a small vessel anchored in the current, which was filled with garden soil. We made do with the situation and stayed the night on the boat. I rested in one of the two bunks—and the bunks of a sloop of sixty or twenty tons hardly require a description. The one I used lacked any bedding. It was exactly eighteen inches wide, and the space between its base and the deck above was identical. I struggled immensely to wedge myself inside. Even so, I slept deeply, and my entire vision—for it was neither a dream nor a nightmare—sprang naturally from my physical surroundings, my usual patterns of thought, and the struggle I mentioned to gather my wits and recover my memory long after waking. The men who roused me were the sloop’s crew and some workers hired to unload it. The earthy scent originated from the cargo itself. The cloth around my jaw was a silk scarf I had wrapped around my head, lacking my usual nightcap.

The suffering I endured, however, was certainly, for that duration, equal to the experience of being buried alive. It was terrifying and unimaginably ghastly; yet, out of this evil came good, as the sheer intensity of the experience forced a necessary shift in my spirit. My soul gained strength and resilience. I ventured out. I engaged in active exercise. I inhaled the open air of the heavens. I focused on things other than death. I threw away my medical texts. I burned my copy of Buchan. I stopped reading Night Thoughts, pretentious nonsense about graveyards, or frightening stories like this one. In essence, I transformed into a new person and began living a real life. From that significant night, I cast off my fears of the grave forever, and with them disappeared the cataleptic condition, which perhaps had been less a result of those fears than their primary cause.

There are times when, even to the rational mind, the world of our sorrowful humanity can appear like a hell—but the human imagination is no Carathis, capable of exploring every cavern without consequence. Sadly, the dark army of burial terrors cannot be dismissed as entirely imaginary—but, like the demons who accompanied Afrasiab on his journey down the Oxus, they must be left to sleep, or they will consume us; they must be allowed to rest, or we are lost.

THE DOMAIN OF ARNHEIM

The garden like a lady fair was cut,
That lay as if she slumbered in delight,
And to the open skies her eyes did shut.
The azure fields of Heaven were ’sembled right
In a large round, set with the flowers of light.
The flowers de luce, and the round sparks of dew
That hung upon their azure leaves did shew
Like twinkling stars that sparkle in the evening blue.
—Giles Fletcher.

From birth until death, a wind of good fortune carried my friend Ellison. I do not use the term prosperity in a strictly material sense. I mean it as a synonym for happiness. The man I describe seemed destined to illustrate the philosophies of Turgot, Price, Priestley, and Condorcet—to prove through a single life what has been considered the impossible dream of those who believe in human perfection."
}

Alas, the grim legion of sepulchral terrors cannot be considered entirely imaginary—but, like the demons who accompanied Afrasiab on his voyage down the Oxus, they must sleep, or they will devour us; they must be allowed to slumber, or we perish.

THE DOMAIN OF ARNHEIM

The garden like a lady fair was cut,
That lay as if she slumbered in delight,
And to the open skies her eyes did shut.
The azure fields of Heaven were ’sembled right
In a large round, set with the flowers of light.
The flowers de luce, and the round sparks of dew
That hung upon their azure leaves did shew
Like twinkling stars that sparkle in the evening blue.
—Giles Fletcher.

From his cradle to his grave, a gale of prosperity carried my friend Ellison along. Nor do I use the word prosperity in its merely worldly sense. I mean it as synonymous with happiness. The person I speak of seemed born to foreshadow the doctrines of Turgot, Price, Priestley, and Condorcet—to exemplify through an individual instance what has been considered the fantasy of the perfectionists."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_024",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Further back within the estate, the view is blocked by an impenetrable screen of foliage. These details are observed as the canoe gradually approaches what I have called the gate of the vista. As I draw closer, however, its chasm-like appearance vanishes; a new outlet from the bay is discovered to the left, in which direction the wall is also seen to sweep, still following the general course of the stream. The eye cannot see very far down this new opening, for the stream, accompanied by the wall, continues to bend to the left until both are swallowed up by the leaves.

The boat, nevertheless, glides magically into the winding channel; here, the shore opposite the wall is found to resemble the one opposite the wall in the straight vista. Lofty hills, rising occasionally into mountains and covered with wild, lush vegetation, still enclose the scene.

Floating gently onward, but with slightly increased speed, the voyager, after many short turns, finds his progress apparently blocked by a gigantic gate, or rather a door of polished gold, elaborately carved and decorated, reflecting the direct rays of the now fast-sinking sun with a brilliance that seems to wreath the entire surrounding forest in flames. This gate is set into the lofty wall, which here appears to cross the river at a right angle. In a few moments, however, it becomes clear that the main body of the water still sweeps in a gentle and extensive curve to the left, with the wall following it as before, while a stream of considerable volume, diverging from the main one, makes its way with a slight ripple under the door and is thus hidden from sight. The canoe enters the smaller channel and approaches the gate. Its heavy wings are slowly and musically opened. The boat glides between them and begins a rapid descent into a vast amphitheater entirely surrounded by purple mountains, whose bases are washed by a gleaming river throughout the full extent of their circuit. Meanwhile, the entire Paradise of Arnheim bursts into view. There is a gush of entrancing melody; there is an overwhelming sense of strange, sweet odor; there is a dream-like intermingling to the eye of tall, slender Eastern trees, dense shrubberies, flocks of golden and crimson birds, lily-fringed lakes, meadows of violets, tulips, poppies, hyacinths, and tuberoses, long intertangled lines of silver streamlets, and, rising confusedly from amid all, a mass of semi-Gothic, semi-Saracenic architecture, appearing to sustain itself by miracle in mid-air, glittering in the red sunlight with a hundred oriels, minarets, and pinnacles, and seeming the phantom handiwork of the Sylphs, Fairies, Genii, and Gnomes.

LANDOR’S COTTAGE

A Pendant to “The Domain of Arnheim”

During a walking trip last summer through one or two of the river counties of New York, I found myself, as the day declined, somewhat confused about the road I was following. The land undulated very remarkably, and my path, for the last hour, had wound about so confusedly in its effort to stay in the valleys that I no longer knew in what direction lay the sweet village of B——, where I had decided to stop for the night. The sun had scarcely shone—strictly speaking—during the day, which had nevertheless been unpleasantly warm."
}

As I draw closer, however, its chasm-like appearance disappears; a new exit from the bay is revealed to the left, where the wall also curves, following the general path of the stream. The eye cannot see far down this new opening, as the stream and the wall both bend to the left until they are hidden by the foliage.

The boat, however, glides smoothly into the winding channel. Here, the shore opposite the wall looks just like the shore in the straight section of the vista. Tall hills, sometimes rising into mountains and covered in wild, lush vegetation, continue to enclose the scene.

Floating gently forward, but moving slightly faster, the traveler makes several short turns before his path seems blocked by a massive gate, or rather a door of polished gold. It is intricately carved and reflects the rays of the setting sun with a brilliance that makes the surrounding forest look as if it is on fire. This gate is set into the high wall, which appears to cross the river at a right angle. In a few moments, however, it becomes clear that the main body of water continues in a wide, gentle curve to the left, with the wall following it as before. Meanwhile, a smaller, significant stream branches off from the main one and flows with a light ripple under the door, disappearing from sight. The canoe enters this smaller channel and approaches the gate. Its heavy wings slowly open with a musical sound. The boat glides between them and begins a rapid descent into a vast amphitheater surrounded by purple mountains, whose bases are washed by a gleaming river. Suddenly, the entire Paradise of Arnheim appears. There is a burst of enchanting music and an overwhelming sense of strange, sweet scents. The scene is a dream-like mix of tall, slender Eastern trees, thick shrubberies, flocks of golden and crimson birds, lily-fringed lakes, and meadows filled with violets, tulips, poppies, hyacinths, and tuberoses. Long, tangled lines of silver streams run through it all, and rising from the center is a mass of semi-Gothic, semi-Saracenic architecture that seems to hang miraculously in mid-air. It glitters in the red sunlight with a hundred windows, minarets, and pinnacles, appearing as the phantom creation of Sylphs, Fairies, Genii, and Gnomes.

LANDOR’S COTTAGE

A Companion to “The Domain of Arnheim”

During a walking trip last summer through a few river counties in New York, I found myself, as the day ended, somewhat confused about the road I was taking. The land was remarkably hilly, and my path for the last hour had wound back and forth so confusingly in its attempt to stay in the valleys that I no longer knew which way to go to reach the pleasant village of B—, where I had planned to stay for the night. The sun had barely shone all day, which had nevertheless been unpleasantly warm."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "From that point on, my word became absolute within the home; while still at an age when most youngsters have barely left their infancy, I was granted the freedom to follow my own desires, effectively becoming the sovereign of my own conduct.

My primary memories of schooling are linked to a sprawling, sprawling Elizabethan estate situated in a hazy English hamlet, a place filled with immense, twisted trees and buildings of extreme antiquity. Truly, that aged town was a dreamlike and tranquil setting. Even now, in my imagination, I sense the invigorating coolness of its shaded paths, breathe in the scent of its countless gardens, and feel a renewed, inexplicable joy at the low, resonant tolling of the church bell, which would shatter the quiet of the dim air surrounding the sleeping, intricate Gothic steeple every hour.

It provides me with as much satisfaction as I can currently hope to find to linger on the smallest details of that school and its affairs. Drowning in sorrow—sorrow that is, unfortunately, all too genuine—I hope to be forgiven for finding solace, however brief or slight, in these wandering recollections. These memories, though entirely mundane and perhaps even absurd, take on a special significance in my mind because they are tied to a time and place where I first sensed the vague warnings of the fate that would eventually consume me. Let me, therefore, recall them.

The building, as I noted, was ancient and asymmetrical. The property was vast, surrounded by a tall, sturdy brick wall topped with mortar and jagged glass. This fortress-like barrier defined our boundaries; we only saw what lay beyond it three times a week—once on Saturday afternoons, when we were escorted by two teachers for short, group walks through nearby fields, and twice on Sundays, when we were marched in the same rigid fashion to attend morning and evening services at the village's single church. The head of our school served as the minister of this church. With what profound amazement and confusion I used to watch him from our distant gallery pew as he climbed the pulpit with slow, grave steps! This holy man, with his expression of calm kindness, his sleek and flowing clerical vestments, and his wig, so precisely powdered, stiff, and enormous—could this truly be the same person who, just before, had wielded a rod with a sour expression and in stained clothing to enforce the harsh rules of our school? Oh, a massive contradiction, far too strange to ever resolve!

At one corner of the heavy wall stood an even heavier gate. It was reinforced with iron studs and topped with sharp metal spikes. How deeply it filled us with dread! It was only opened for those three scheduled departures and returns I mentioned; each time its massive hinges groaned, we found a wealth of mystery—a subject for serious discussion or even deeper contemplation.

The large grounds were uneven in shape, featuring several deep alcoves. Three or four of the largest of these served as our playground. It was flat and surfaced with fine, packed gravel."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_025",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "From that point on, my word was law in the house; and at an age when few children have stopped needing supervision, I was left to follow my own desires, becoming, in every way but name, the master of my own behavior.

My earliest memories of school life are tied to a large, sprawling Elizabethan house in a misty English village, where there were countless massive, twisted trees and where every building was incredibly old. Truly, that venerable town was a dreamlike and soothing place. Even now, in my imagination, I feel the refreshing chill of its deeply shaded avenues, breathe in the scent of its many shrubberies, and feel a fresh, indefinable thrill at the deep, hollow sound of the church bell, which broke the stillness of the dim air every hour with a sudden, sullen roar, right where the intricate Gothic steeple sat embedded and asleep.

It gives me perhaps as much pleasure as I can currently experience to dwell on the minute details of that school and its affairs. Steeped in misery as I am—misery that is, unfortunately, all too real—I hope to be forgiven for seeking relief, however slight and temporary, in the indulgence of a few rambling memories. These details, though utterly trivial and even ridiculous on their own, take on an added importance in my mind because they are connected to a time and place where I recognize the first vague warnings of the destiny that later overshadowed me so completely. Let me remember, then.

The house, as I have said, was old and irregular. The grounds were vast, and a high, solid brick wall, topped with mortar and broken glass, surrounded the entire property. This prison-like barrier marked the edge of our world; beyond it, we saw nothing except three times a week—once every Saturday afternoon, when, accompanied by two teachers, we were allowed to take short group walks through the nearby fields, and twice on Sunday, when we were marched in the same formal way to the morning and evening services at the village's only church. The head of our school was the pastor of this church. With what deep wonder and confusion I used to watch him from our distant pew in the gallery as he climbed the pulpit with slow, solemn steps! This reverend man, with his demurely kind face, his glossy, flowing clerical robes, and his wig, so precisely powdered, so rigid, and so vast—could this really be the same person who, just a short time before, had been administering the school’s harsh laws with a sour expression, dirty clothes, and a ruler in his hand? Oh, what a massive paradox, far too monstrous to solve!

At a corner of that heavy wall stood an even heavier gate. It was reinforced and studded with iron bolts and topped with jagged iron spikes. What a deep sense of awe it inspired! It was never opened except for the three scheduled departures and arrivals I already mentioned; then, in every creak of its mighty hinges, we found a wealth of mystery—a world of subject matter for serious conversation or even more serious reflection.

The large enclosure was irregular in shape, with many spacious alcoves. Three or four of the largest ones made up the playground. It was flat and covered with fine, hard gravel."
}

  "rewritten_text": "From that point on, my word was law in the house; and at an age when few children have stopped needing supervision, I was left to follow my own desires, becoming, in every way but name, the master of my own behavior.

My earliest memories of school life are tied to a large, sprawling Elizabethan house in a misty English village, where there were countless massive, twisted trees and where every building was incredibly old. Truly, that venerable town was a dreamlike and soothing place. Even now, in my imagination, I feel the refreshing chill of its deeply shaded avenues, breathe in the scent of its many shrubberies, and feel a fresh, indefinable thrill at the deep, hollow sound of the church bell, which broke the stillness of the dim air every hour with a sudden, sullen roar, right where the intricate Gothic steeple sat embedded and asleep.

It gives me perhaps as much pleasure as I can currently experience to dwell on the minute details of that school and its affairs. Steeped in misery as I am—misery that is, unfortunately, all too real—I hope to be forgiven for seeking relief, however slight and temporary, in the indulgence of a few rambling memories. These details, though utterly trivial and even ridiculous on their own, take on an added importance in my mind because they are connected to a time and place where I recognize the first vague warnings of the destiny that later overshadowed me so completely. Let me remember, then.

The house, as I have said, was old and irregular. The grounds were vast, and a high, solid brick wall, topped with mortar and broken glass, surrounded the entire property. This prison-like barrier marked the edge of our world; beyond it, we saw nothing except three times a week—once every Saturday afternoon, when, accompanied by two teachers, we were allowed to take short group walks through the nearby fields, and twice on Sunday, when we were marched in the same formal way to the morning and evening services at the village's only church. The head of our school was the pastor of this church. With what deep wonder and confusion I used to watch him from our distant pew in the gallery as he climbed the pulpit with slow, solemn steps! This reverend man, with his demurely kind face, his glossy, flowing clerical robes, and his wig, so precisely powdered, so rigid, and so vast—could this really be the same person who, just a short time before, had been administering the school’s harsh laws with a sour expression, dirty clothes, and a ruler in his hand? Oh, what a massive paradox, far too monstrous to solve!

At a corner of that heavy wall stood an even heavier gate. It was reinforced and studded with iron bolts and topped with jagged iron spikes. What a deep sense of awe it inspired! It was never opened except for the three scheduled departures and arrivals I already mentioned; then, in every creak of its mighty hinges, we found a wealth of mystery—a world of subject matter for serious conversation or even more serious reflection.

The large enclosure was irregular in shape, with many spacious alcoves. Three or four of the largest ones made up the playground. It was flat and covered with fine, hard gravel."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I could never determine how his keen insight first realized that such a trivial matter would irritate me, yet once he knew, he made a habit of tormenting me with it. I had long harbored a dislike for my unrefined surname and its common, perhaps even vulgar, first name. These sounds were poison to me, and when another William Wilson arrived at the school on my first day, I resented him for carrying the name, and felt even more repulsed by the name itself because a stranger now possessed it. This meant the name would be spoken twice as often, he would be constantly near me, and our daily school affairs would inevitably become confused due to this detestable coincidence.

This sense of annoyance intensified whenever any detail highlighted a physical or moral likeness between my rival and me. While I had not yet realized we were the same age, I noticed we shared the same height and possessed a striking similarity in our physical build and facial features. I was also irritated by rumors of a family connection that had begun to circulate among the older students. In short, nothing disturbed me more—though I carefully hid my agitation—than any suggestion that we were alike in mind, appearance, or status. In reality, I had no reason to think that anyone other than Wilson himself had noticed or commented on this resemblance. It was clear that he observed it as closely as I did, and his ability to turn such a situation into a source of torment could only be credited, as I previously noted, to his exceptional perceptiveness.

His strategy, which involved perfecting an imitation of me, extended to both his speech and his behavior; he performed this role with remarkable skill. Copying my clothing was simple, and he adopted my walk and general demeanor without trouble. Even my voice did not escape him, despite his natural vocal limitation. He naturally avoided my louder tones, but the pitch was identical, and his peculiar whisper became an exact reflection of my own.

I will not attempt to explain how deeply this precise portrayal troubled me, as it could hardly be called a mere caricature. My only comfort was that the imitation seemed apparent to me alone, leaving me to suffer only the knowing, mocking smiles of my namesake. Content with having achieved his goal, he seemed to relish his secret triumph, showing no interest in the public recognition his clever efforts might have easily earned. For many months, I was baffled as to why the rest of the school failed to notice his design or join in his mockery. Perhaps the subtle nature of his mimicry made it difficult to detect, or more likely, I was spared further embarrassment by the skill of the copyist, who ignored the superficial details that the dull-witted might notice, choosing instead to present the very essence of his original for my private frustration."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_026",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "How he managed to figure out that such a trivial thing would irritate me is a mystery I could never solve; but once he knew, he made a habit of it. I had always hated my unrefined surname and its common, if not low-class, first name. The words were poison to me, and when a second William Wilson arrived at the academy on my first day, I felt angry at him for having the name, and doubly disgusted with the name itself because a stranger now shared it. He would cause the name to be repeated constantly, he would always be around me, and our affairs in the daily school routine would inevitably get mixed up because of this hateful coincidence.

This sense of annoyance grew stronger with every detail that suggested a resemblance, whether moral or physical, between my rival and me. I had not yet discovered the remarkable fact that we were the same age, but I saw that we were the same height, and I noticed we were even strangely similar in our general build and facial features. I was also irritated by rumors of a family connection that had started circulating among the older students. In short, nothing could disturb me more—though I carefully hid my agitation—than any mention of a similarity in our minds, appearances, or circumstances. In truth, I had no reason to believe that anyone else, aside from Wilson himself, had ever commented on or even noticed this similarity. It was clear that he observed it in every detail as closely as I did, but that he could find such a fertile source of irritation in these circumstances can only be attributed, as I said before, to his extraordinary insight.

His strategy, which was to perfect an imitation of me, involved both words and actions, and he played his part brilliantly. My style of dress was easy to copy; my walk and general manner were adopted without difficulty; and despite his natural vocal limitation, even my voice did not escape him. He did not attempt my louder tones, but the pitch was identical, and his strange whisper became the exact echo of my own.

I will not attempt to describe how much this perfect portrait—for it could not fairly be called a caricature—harassed me. I had only one consolation: the fact that the imitation was apparently noticed by me alone, and that I only had to endure the knowing, strangely sarcastic smiles of my namesake. Satisfied with having produced the intended effect in my heart, he seemed to chuckle in secret over the sting he had inflicted, characteristically indifferent to the public praise his clever efforts could have easily earned. The fact that the rest of the school did not sense his design, notice its success, or join in his mockery was, for many anxious months, a riddle I could not solve. Perhaps the gradual nature of his imitation made it less obvious, or, more likely, I owed my privacy to the masterly skill of the copyist, who, disdaining the literal details that only the dull-witted notice in a painting, captured the very spirit of his original for my own private contemplation and frustration."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The fleeting instant I turned my gaze away proved enough to cause a significant shift in the layout at the far side of the chamber. A tall glass—or so I initially perceived in my bewilderment—now occupied a space where nothing had been visible previously; as I approached it in a state of intense fright, my own reflection, yet with a face deathly white and stained with gore, moved toward me with a weak and unsteady stride.

So it seemed, I maintain, but that was not the truth. It was my foe—it was Wilson, who stood before me in the throes of his final moments. His disguise and mantle rested on the ground where he had cast them aside. Not a single fiber of his clothing, nor a single contour of his distinct and peculiar facial features, failed to be, in every absolute detail, my own!

It was Wilson; yet he no longer spoke in a hushed tone, and I might have imagined that I was the one speaking as he uttered: 

“You have triumphed, and I surrender. Yet, from this moment on, you are likewise dead—dead to the world, to heaven, and to hope! You existed within me—and, in my passing, observe through this reflection, which is your own, how completely you have destroyed yourself.”

THE TELL-TALE HEART.

True!—anxious—I have been and remain dreadfully anxious; but why do you insist that I am insane? The malady had honed my perceptions—not ruined—not blunted them. Above all, my sense of hearing was sharp. I perceived every sound in the heavens and on the earth. I perceived many things in hell. How, then, could I be mad? Listen! And notice how soundly—how composedly I can relate the entire tale to you.

It is not possible to explain how the notion first took root in my mind; but once it was formed, it pursued me night and day. There was no specific motive. There was no anger. I held the old man dear. He had never harmed me. He had never offered me an insult. I had no craving for his wealth. I believe it was his eye! Yes, that was it! He possessed the eye of a vulture—a light blue gaze, obscured by a film. Whenever it rested upon me, my blood turned to ice; and so, by increments—very slowly—I resolved to end the old man’s life, and thereby free myself from that eye for good.

Now, here is the crux. You believe me insane. Madmen understand nothing. But you ought to have witnessed me. You should have seen how sagely I acted—with what prudence—with what foresight—with what deceit I carried out my plan! I was never more affectionate toward the old man than during the entire week leading up to his murder. And every night, near midnight, I lifted the latch of his door and swung it open—oh, so carefully! And then, once I had created an opening wide enough for my head, I inserted a dark lantern, completely shut, so that no beam escaped, and then I pushed my head inside. Oh, you would have chuckled to observe how craftily I inserted it! I moved it gradually—very, very gradually, so as not to interrupt the old man’s slumber."
}

That is how it appeared, I say, but it was not the truth. It was my antagonist—it was Wilson, who stood before me in the agony of his final moments. His mask and cloak lay on the floor where he had discarded them. Not a single thread of his clothing, nor a line in the distinct and unique features of his face, failed to be an exact duplicate of my own.

It was Wilson, but he no longer spoke in a whisper, and I could have imagined that I was speaking myself as he said:

“You have conquered, and I surrender. Yet, from now on, you are also dead—dead to the world, to heaven, and to hope! You existed in me, and in my death, see by this image, which is your own, how completely you have murdered yourself.”

THE TELL-TALE HEART.

True! I have been and still am nervous—very, very dreadfully nervous; but why do you claim that I am mad? The illness had sharpened my senses, not destroyed or dulled them. Above all, my sense of hearing was acute. I heard everything in heaven and on earth. I heard many things in hell. How, then, am I mad? Listen! And observe how sanely and calmly I can tell you the whole story.

It is impossible to say how the idea first entered my brain, but once conceived, it haunted me day and night. There was no motive. There was no passion. I loved the old man. He had never wronged me. He had never insulted me. I had no desire for his gold. I think it was his eye! Yes, it was this! He had the eye of a vulture—a pale blue eye with a film over it. Whenever it fell upon me, my blood ran cold; and so, by degrees—very gradually—I decided to take the old man’s life and thus rid myself of the eye forever.

Now, this is the point. You think I am mad. Madmen know nothing. But you should have seen me. You should have seen how wisely I proceeded—with what caution, with what foresight, and with what cunning I went to work! I was never kinder to the old man than during the entire week before I killed him. And every night, around midnight, I turned the latch of his door and opened it—oh, so gently! And then, when I had made an opening wide enough for my head, I inserted a dark lantern, completely closed so that no light shone out, and then I pushed my head in. Oh, you would have laughed to see how cunningly I pushed it in! I moved it slowly—very, very slowly—so that I would not disturb the old man’s sleep."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Alas! Her thinness was extreme, and not a trace of her former self remained in any feature of her outline. My intense gaze finally settled upon her face.

The forehead was lofty, extremely pale, and remarkably calm; the once black hair hung partially across it, shading the sunken temples with countless curls, now a bright yellow, clashing strangely in their whimsical style with the pervasive sadness of her expression. Her eyes were dull, devoid of light, and appeared to lack pupils, causing me to recoil instinctively from their glassy gaze to examine her thin, withered lips. They parted, and in a smile of strange significance, the teeth of the transformed Berenice were revealed to me slowly. I wish to God I had never seen them, or that, having seen them, I had perished!

The sound of a closing door startled me, and looking up, I realized my cousin had left the room. Yet, from the chaotic chamber of my mind, the white and spectral image of those teeth had not departed, nor could it be banished. Not a spot on their surface, not a blemish on their enamel, not a notch in their edges escaped being branded into my memory by that brief moment of her smile. I perceived them now even more clearly than I had then. The teeth! The teeth! They were present everywhere, visible and tangible before me; long, slender, and strikingly white, with pale lips twisting around them, just as they had appeared during that first horrific revelation. Then the full intensity of my obsession seized me, and I fought fruitlessly against its bizarre and overpowering force. Amidst the many objects of the outside world, I could think of nothing but the teeth. I craved them with a frantic intensity. All other concerns and interests were swallowed up by their singular contemplation. They alone occupied my mind, and in their solitary existence, they became the core of my mental life. I examined them in every light. I rotated them into every position. I scrutinized their features. I focused on their distinct traits. I reflected on their structure. I pondered the change in their nature. I trembled as I imagined them possessing a sensitive and sentient quality, and even when separated from the lips, a capacity for moral expression. It has been aptly remarked of Mademoiselle Salle that “all her steps were sentiments,” and I believed even more earnestly of Berenice that all her teeth were ideas. Ideas! Ah, that was the foolish notion that ruined me! Ideas! Ah, that was why I desired them so desperately! I felt that only by possessing them could I regain my peace and return to sanity.

Thus the evening ended for me—and then the darkness arrived, lingered, and passed—and the day broke once more—and the mists of a second night were now collecting—yet I remained sitting still in that lonely room—still lost in thought—and still the phantom of the teeth held its terrifying dominance, as it drifted with vivid, hideous clarity amidst the shifting lights and shadows of the room."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "POE_WORKS_VOLUME_2_030",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Schouw supports the existence of a category of plants that grow on living animals, known as Plantae Epizoae. This group includes various types of seaweed and algae.

Mr. J. B. Williams, of Salem, Massachusetts, gave the National Institute an insect from New Zealand, accompanied by this description: “The Hotte, a distinct caterpillar or worm, is found chewing the roots of the Rota tree, with a plant sprouting from its head. This highly unusual insect climbs both the Rota and Ferriri trees, enters the top, and bores through the trunk until it reaches the root, where it either dies or becomes dormant. The plant then propagates from its head, while the body remains perfectly intact, having hardened significantly since the insect was alive. The local people use this insect to create pigment for tattooing.”

In mines and natural caves, we discover a type of flowerless fungus that produces a bright, glowing light.

The orchis, scabious, and valisneria.

The corolla of this flower (Aristolochia Clematitis), which is tubular but ends in a strap-shaped limb, is inflated into a globe at the base. The interior of the tube is lined with stiff, downward-pointing hairs. The globular section holds the pistil, which consists only of a germen and stigma, surrounded by stamens. Because the stamens are shorter than the germen, they cannot release pollen onto the stigma while the flower remains upright before fertilization. Therefore, without some specific external help, the pollen would simply fall to the bottom of the flower. The aid nature provides in this instance is the Tiputa Pennicornis, a small insect that enters the corolla tube looking for nectar. It descends to the bottom and moves around until it is completely covered in pollen. Unable to force its way back out due to the downward-pointing hairs—which converge like the wires of a mousetrap—the insect becomes restless. It brushes back and forth, exploring every corner, and in doing so, repeatedly traverses the stigma, coating it with enough pollen for fertilization. As a result, the flower soon begins to wilt, and the hairs shrink against the sides of the tube, allowing the insect to escape easily.” —Rev. P. Keith, System of Physiological Botany.

Bees have always built their cells with the exact sides, numbers, and angles that have been proven, through complex mathematical principles, to provide the most space while ensuring the greatest structural stability.

During the late eighteenth century, mathematicians faced the challenge of determining the optimal shape for windmill sails, based on their varying distances from the revolving vanes and the centers of rotation. This is an incredibly complex problem, as it essentially requires finding the best possible position across an infinite number of distances and points along the arm."
}

The interior of the tube is lined with stiff, downward-pointing hairs. The globular section holds the pistil, which consists only of a germen and stigma, surrounded by stamens. Because the stamens are shorter than the germen, they cannot release pollen onto the stigma while the flower remains upright before fertilization. Therefore, without some specific external help, the pollen would simply fall to the bottom of the flower. The aid nature provides in this instance is the Tiputa Pennicornis, a small insect that enters the corolla tube looking for nectar. It descends to the bottom and moves around until it is completely covered in pollen. Unable to force its way back out due to the downward-pointing hairs—which converge like the wires of a mousetrap—the insect becomes restless. It brushes back and forth, exploring every corner, and in doing so, repeatedly traverses the stigma, coating it with enough pollen for fertilization. As a result, the flower soon begins to wilt, and the hairs shrink against the sides of the tube, allowing the insect to escape easily.” —Rev. P. Keith, System of Physiological Botany.

Bees have always built their cells with the exact sides, numbers, and angles that have been proven, through complex mathematical principles, to provide the most space while ensuring the greatest structural stability.

During the late eighteenth century, mathematicians faced the challenge of determining the optimal shape for windmill sails, based on their varying distances from the revolving vanes and the centers of rotation. This is an incredibly complex problem, as it essentially requires finding the best possible position across an infinite number of distances and points along the arm."
}
