model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Still, despite his unhappiness, he is not so consumed by his own sorrow that he fails to take a keen interest in the plans of others. He has often spoken with me about my own, which I have shared with him quite openly. He listened carefully to all my reasoning regarding my potential success and to every small detail of the steps I had taken to ensure it. Encouraged by the empathy he showed, I was easily persuaded to speak from the heart, to express the intense passion of my spirit, and to declare, with all the fervor that moved me, how willingly I would give up my wealth, my life, and all my hopes to advance my undertaking. The life or death of a single man would be a trivial cost for the knowledge I pursued, and for the mastery I would gain and pass on over the natural forces that oppose our kind. As I talked, a dark shadow fell across my listener’s face. At first, I noticed him trying to hide his feelings; he covered his eyes with his hands, and my voice faltered and died away as I watched tears stream from between his fingers; a sob escaped his heaving chest. I stopped; eventually, he spoke in fragmented tones: “Wretched man! Are you afflicted with my same madness? Have you also tasted that intoxicating potion? Listen to me; let me tell you my story, and you will cast that cup away from your lips!”

These words, as you might suppose, greatly stirred my interest; however, the fit of sorrow that had gripped the stranger exhausted his fragile strength, and many hours of rest and quiet talk were required to help him regain his calm.

Having mastered the intensity of his emotions, he seemed to look down on himself for being controlled by such passion; and, suppressing the grim rule of despair, he guided me back to talking about my own life. He inquired about the history of my youth. The story was brief, yet it stirred up many lines of thought. I talked of my longing to find a friend, of my craving for a deeper connection with another mind than I had ever experienced, and stated my belief that a person could claim little joy without enjoying such a gift.

“I concur,” the stranger answered; “we are unfinished beings, only partially formed, if someone wiser, better, and more precious than ourselves—as a true friend should be—does not offer his help to refine our weak and flawed natures. I once possessed a friend, the most noble of all people, and am therefore qualified to judge the nature of friendship. You have hope, the world lies ahead of you, and you have no reason for despair. But I—I have lost everything and cannot start my life over.”

As he spoke, his face took on an expression of quiet, permanent sadness that moved me deeply. Yet he remained silent and soon withdrew to his cabin.

Even with his broken spirit, no one can appreciate the wonders of nature more profoundly than he does. The starry heavens, the ocean, and every view provided by these marvelous lands seem to retain the ability to lift his soul above the earth. Such a man possesses a dual existence: he may endure suffering and be crushed by setbacks, yet when he retreats into his own mind, he resembles a heavenly being surrounded by a halo, within which no sorrow or foolishness dares to enter."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_002",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Deeply aware of their responsibility toward the life they had created, and driven by the active spirit of tenderness that motivated them both, it is easy to imagine that while every hour of my childhood provided a lesson in patience, charity, and self-control, I was guided so gently that my life felt like one continuous experience of joy.

For a long time, I was their sole focus. My mother had very much wanted a daughter, but I remained their only child. When I was about five years old, while traveling beyond the borders of Italy, they spent a week on the shores of Lake Como. Their kind nature often led them to visit the homes of the poor. For my mother, this was more than a duty; it was a necessity, a passion—remembering her own past suffering and how she had been helped, she felt compelled to act as a guardian angel to those in distress. During one of their walks, a poor cottage tucked away in a valley caught their attention because it looked particularly miserable, and the group of half-clothed children gathered outside signaled the worst kind of poverty. One day, while my father had gone to Milan alone, my mother took me to visit this home. She found a hardworking peasant couple, worn down by worry and labor, sharing a meager meal with five hungry children. Among them was one who captured my mother’s attention far more than the others. She seemed to be of a different background. The other four were dark-eyed, rugged little wanderers, but this child was thin and very fair. Her hair was the brightest golden color, and despite her ragged clothing, it seemed to crown her with distinction. Her forehead was clear and broad, her blue eyes were bright, and her lips and facial features were so expressive of sensitivity and sweetness that anyone who saw her would view her as a unique creature, sent from heaven and bearing a divine quality in her appearance.

The peasant woman, noticing that my mother looked at this lovely girl with wonder and admiration, eagerly shared her story. She was not her own child, but the daughter of a Milanese nobleman. Her mother was German and had died giving birth to her. The infant had been placed with these good people to be nursed when they were better off. They had not been married long, and their oldest child was just an infant. The girl’s father was one of those Italians raised on the memory of Italy’s ancient glory—one of the restless, ever-fuming slaves who fought for his country’s freedom. He became a victim of its instability. Whether he had died or was still languishing in an Austrian prison was unknown. His property was seized, and his child became an orphan and a beggar. She remained with her foster parents and blossomed in their rough home, fairer than a garden rose among dark-leaved brambles.

When my father returned from Milan, he found a child playing with me in the hall of our villa who was fairer than any painted angel—a creature who seemed to radiate light, whose form and movements were more graceful than a mountain chamois."
}

She seemed different from the rest. While the four other children were dark-eyed and rugged, this girl was thin and very fair. Her hair was a bright, golden color, and despite her ragged clothes, she seemed to wear a crown of distinction. Her forehead was clear and broad, her blue eyes were bright, and her face was so sweet and sensitive that anyone who saw her would think she was a special, heaven-sent being with a divine quality in her features.

The peasant woman noticed my mother’s wonder and admiration for the lovely girl and eagerly shared her story. The child was not her own, but the daughter of a Milanese nobleman. Her mother was German and had died giving birth to her. The infant had been left with these kind people to be nursed when they were better off financially. They had been married for a short time, and their oldest child was just a baby. The girl’s father was an Italian who dreamed of his country’s former glory and fought for its freedom. He eventually became a victim of the country’s political instability. Whether he had died or was still suffering in an Austrian prison was unknown. His property had been seized, leaving his daughter an orphan and a beggar. She remained with her foster parents, growing up in their humble home like a beautiful garden rose surrounded by dark, thorny bushes.

When my father returned from Milan, he found a child playing with me in the hall of our villa. She was more beautiful than any painting of an angel—a creature who seemed to glow with light, whose movements were as graceful and light as a mountain goat."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "A sense of rare peace and inner joy signaled her triumph, arriving once I abandoned those long-held and recently agonizing pursuits. In this way, I was meant to learn that pursuing such studies brought harm, while ignoring them fostered contentment.

This was a powerful exertion of the spirit of good, yet it proved futile. Fate was too strong, and its unchangeable decrees had ordained my complete and horrific ruin.

Chapter 3

Upon reaching seventeen, my parents decided I should enroll at the University of Ingolstadt. I had previously studied in Geneva, but my father deemed it essential for my development that I experience traditions beyond those of my homeland. My departure was scheduled soon, but before that day arrived, the initial tragedy of my existence took place—a harbinger, in a sense, of my coming wretchedness.

Elizabeth had contracted scarlet fever; her condition was grave, and she faced extreme peril. Throughout her sickness, many urged my mother to avoid nursing her. She initially bowed to our pleas, but upon learning that her favorite’s life was threatened, she could no longer suppress her worry. She tended to the sickbed; her vigilant care overcame the virulence of the disease—Elizabeth was rescued, yet the results of this rashness proved deadly for her savior. By the third day, my mother fell ill; her fever displayed the most frightening signs, and the expressions of her doctors signaled the worst outcome. On her deathbed, the courage and kindness of this finest of women did not waver. She placed Elizabeth’s hand in mine. “My children,” she declared, “my greatest hopes for future joy rested on the vision of your marriage. This expectation will now serve as comfort for your father. Elizabeth, my dear, you must take my place for my younger children. Oh, I lament that I am torn from you; and, blessed and cherished as I have been, is it not difficult to leave you all? Yet these thoughts are unworthy of me; I will strive to accept death with grace and will hold to the hope of reuniting with you in another realm.”

She passed away peacefully, her face showing love even in death. I need not detail the emotions of those whose closest bonds are severed by that most permanent of evils, the emptiness that fills the soul, and the hopelessness visible on the face. It takes so long for the mind to accept that someone seen daily, whose very life seemed woven into our own, has vanished forever—that the radiance of a cherished eye has dimmed and the tone of a voice so well-known and precious to the ear has fallen silent, never to be heard again. These are the musings of the initial days; but when the passage of time confirms the truth of the loss, then the true sharpness of sorrow begins. Yet who has not had some dear relationship torn away by that harsh hand? And why should I recount a grief that everyone has experienced, and must experience?"
}

{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_003",

  "rewritten_text": "Her victory was signaled by an unusual peace and joy of spirit that followed my abandonment of those old, recently tormenting studies. In this way, I was taught to link evil with their pursuit and happiness with their neglect.

It was a powerful exertion of the spirit of good, yet it proved ineffective. Destiny was too strong, and its unchangeable laws had decreed my complete and terrible ruin.

Chapter 3

When I reached the age of seventeen, my parents decided I should become a student at the University of Ingolstadt. I had previously attended schools in Geneva, but my father believed it necessary for the completion of my education that I become familiar with customs other than those of my native country. My departure was therefore scheduled for an early date, but before the appointed day could arrive, the first misfortune of my life occurred—a sign, as it were, of my future misery.

Elizabeth had contracted scarlet fever; her illness was severe, and she was in great danger. During her sickness, many arguments were used to persuade my mother to refrain from nursing her. She had initially yielded to our pleas, but when she heard that the life of her favorite was threatened, she could no longer control her anxiety. She attended her sickbed; her watchful care triumphed over the severity of the disease—Elizabeth was saved, but the consequences of this imprudence were fatal to her savior. On the third day, my mother fell ill; her fever was accompanied by the most alarming symptoms, and the expressions of her doctors predicted the worst outcome. On her deathbed, the strength and kindness of this best of women did not desert her. She joined the hands of Elizabeth and myself. “My children,” she said, “my firmest hopes for future happiness were placed on the prospect of your marriage. This expectation will now be the consolation of your father. Elizabeth, my love, you must take my place for my younger children. Alas! I regret that I am taken from you; and, happy and beloved as I have been, is it not hard to leave you all? But these are not thoughts befitting me; I will try to resign myself cheerfully to death and will hold onto the hope of meeting you in another world.”

She died calmly, and her face expressed affection even in death. I need not describe the feelings of those whose dearest bonds are torn by that most irreparable evil, the void that presents itself to the soul, and the despair shown on the face. It takes so long before the mind can convince itself that she whom we saw every day and whose very existence seemed a part of our own can have departed forever—that the brightness of a beloved eye can have been extinguished and the sound of a voice so familiar and dear to the ear can be hushed, never to be heard again. These are the reflections of the first days; but when the passage of time proves the reality of the loss, then the true bitterness of grief begins. Yet from whom has that harsh hand not torn away some dear connection? And why should I describe a sorrow which all have felt, and must feel?"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "How much has been accomplished, the spirit of Frankenstein cried—I shall achieve even more; following the paths already established, I will blaze a fresh trail, investigate hidden forces, and reveal the most profound secrets of existence to mankind.

I did not sleep that night. My mind was in a state of chaotic rebellion; I sensed that harmony would eventually emerge, yet I lacked the ability to bring it about. Gradually, as day broke, slumber arrived. Upon waking, the thoughts of the previous night seemed like a mere dream. All that persisted was a firm decision to resume my former studies and dedicate myself to a discipline for which I felt I had an innate aptitude. That same day, I called upon M. Waldman. His private demeanor was even gentler and more engaging than his public one, for the air of gravity he maintained during his lectures was replaced at home by immense warmth and friendliness. I provided him with much the same summary of my past interests as I had offered his colleague. He listened intently to my brief account of my studies and smiled at the mention of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, yet he showed none of the disdain that M. Krempe had displayed. He remarked that these were individuals whose tireless dedication provided modern scholars with the bedrock of their understanding. They had left us the simpler duty of renaming and organizing the facts they had largely helped to uncover. The efforts of brilliant men, even when misguided, rarely fail to eventually benefit humanity. I heard his words, which were spoken without arrogance or pretense, and then replied that his talk had dispelled my biases against modern chemists; I spoke with the careful restraint and respect a student owes his teacher, taking care not to reveal—as my youthful inexperience would have made me embarrassed to do—the fervor that drove my planned endeavors. I asked for his guidance regarding which texts I should acquire.

I am pleased, M. Waldman stated, to have found a student; provided your diligence matches your talent, I have no doubt you will succeed. Chemistry is the field of natural science where the most significant progress has occurred and will continue to occur; that is why I have made it my primary focus, though I have not ignored other scientific disciplines. A person would be a poor chemist if they focused solely on that area of study. If you truly desire to become a man of science rather than a mere dabbler in experiments, I suggest you study every aspect of natural philosophy, mathematics included.

He then led me into his laboratory, described the functions of his various instruments, advised me on what equipment I should obtain, and pledged that I might use his own once I had progressed sufficiently to avoid damaging them. He also provided the reading list I had asked for, and I departed."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_004",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "So much has been accomplished, exclaimed the spirit of Frankenstein—more, far more, I will achieve; following the paths already marked, I will blaze a new trail, explore unknown powers, and reveal to the world the deepest mysteries of creation.

I did not close my eyes that night. My inner self was in a state of rebellion and turmoil; I felt that order would eventually emerge, but I lacked the power to create it. Gradually, after the morning dawn, sleep arrived. I awoke, and my thoughts from the previous night felt like a dream. There remained only a resolve to return to my old studies and to dedicate myself to a science for which I believed I possessed a natural talent. That same day, I visited Mr. Waldman. His manner in private was even more gentle and engaging than in public, for the dignity he displayed during his lecture was replaced at home by the greatest friendliness and kindness. I gave him nearly the same account of my previous pursuits as I had given his colleague. He listened with attention to my brief summary of my studies and smiled at the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, but without the contempt Mr. Krempe had shown. He remarked that, “These were men to whose tireless zeal modern scientists owe most of the foundations of their knowledge. They left us the easier task of giving new names and organizing into connected systems the facts they were largely responsible for bringing to light. The efforts of men of genius, however misguided, rarely fail to ultimately provide solid benefits to humanity.” I listened to his statement, which was delivered without any arrogance or pretense, and then added that his lecture had removed my prejudices against modern chemists; I spoke in measured terms, with the modesty and respect due from a student to his instructor, without letting slip—as my lack of life experience would have made me ashamed to do—any of the enthusiasm that fueled my intended work. I requested his advice regarding the books I should acquire.

“I am happy,” said Mr. Waldman, “to have gained a student; and if your dedication matches your ability, I have no doubt of your success. Chemistry is the branch of natural science in which the greatest improvements have been and may yet be made; that is why I have made it my primary study; but at the same time, I have not neglected other branches of science. A man would make a very poor chemist if he focused on that field of knowledge alone. If your wish is to become a true man of science and not merely a minor experimentalist, I advise you to apply yourself to every branch of natural philosophy, including mathematics.”

He then took me into his laboratory and explained the uses of his various machines, instructing me on what I should acquire and promising me the use of his own once I had advanced far enough in the science not to disrupt their mechanisms. He also provided the list of books I had requested, and I took my leave."
}

  "rewritten_text": "So much has been accomplished, exclaimed the soul of Frankenstein—more, far more, will I achieve. Following the paths already marked, I will pioneer a new way, explore unknown powers, and reveal to the world the deepest mysteries of creation.

I did not close my eyes that night. My mind was in a state of rebellion and turmoil; I felt that order would eventually arise from it, but I had no power to create it myself. Gradually, after the morning dawn, sleep arrived. I awoke, and my thoughts from the night before felt like a dream. There only remained a firm decision to return to my old studies and to dedicate myself to a science for which I believed I had a natural talent. That same day, I visited M. Waldman. His manners in private were even more gentle and appealing than in public, for the dignity he showed during his lecture was replaced at home by the greatest friendliness and kindness. I gave him nearly the same account of my previous studies as I had given to his fellow professor. He listened with attention to my story and smiled at the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, but without the contempt that M. Krempe had shown. He said, “These were men whose tireless dedication provided modern philosophers with most of the foundations of their knowledge. They left us the easier task of giving new names and organizing into connected systems the facts they were largely responsible for discovering. The work of men of genius, however wrongly directed, rarely fails to eventually benefit mankind.” I listened to his statement, which was delivered without any arrogance or pretense, and then added that his lecture had removed my prejudices against modern chemists. I spoke carefully, with the modesty and respect due from a student to his teacher, without letting any of the enthusiasm that drove my intended work escape. I asked for his advice regarding the books I should obtain.

“I am happy,” said M. Waldman, “to have gained a student; and if your hard work matches your ability, I have no doubt of your success. Chemistry is the branch of natural science where the greatest improvements have been and can still be made; that is why I have made it my special study. At the same time, I have not neglected other branches of science. A man would make a very poor chemist if he focused only on that one area. If you wish to become a true man of science and not merely a minor experimenter, I advise you to study every branch of natural philosophy, including mathematics.”

He then took me into his laboratory and explained the uses of his various machines, instructing me on what I should buy and promising me the use of his own equipment once I had advanced enough in the science not to damage their mechanisms. He also gave me the list of books I had requested, and I took my leave."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "My father offered no criticism in his correspondence, merely noting my lack of communication by asking more specifically about my daily activities. Winter, spring, and summer elapsed while I labored; yet I failed to observe the blooming flowers or unfolding foliage—sights that previously brought me immense joy—so completely was I absorbed in my task. The year's leaves had faded before my project neared completion, and each day revealed more clearly the extent of my success. However, my fervor was tempered by apprehension, and I resembled a man condemned to forced labor in the mines or some other unhealthy profession rather than an artist engaged in his preferred work. Each evening, a lingering fever weighed upon me, and I grew painfully sensitive; the sound of a falling leaf frightened me, and I avoided other people as if I had committed a transgression. At times, I felt terrified by the physical ruin I had become; only the intensity of my goal kept me going: my efforts would soon conclude, and I trusted that physical activity and recreation would then dispel the onset of illness; I vowed to pursue both once my creation was finished.

Chapter 5

It was on a bleak November night that I witnessed the fruition of my efforts. With an intensity bordering on agony, I gathered the tools of life about me, hoping to spark existence into the inanimate object resting at my feet. It was already one o'clock in the morning; rain tapped gloomily against the glass, and my candle was nearly spent when, by the dim glow of the dying flame, I watched the creature's dull yellow eye open; it gasped, and a spasmodic movement shook its body.

How might I articulate my feelings regarding this disaster, or how depict the miserable being I had labored with such immense effort to construct? His limbs were well-proportioned, and I had chosen features I considered beautiful. Beautiful! Good heavens! His sallow skin barely concealed the underlying muscles and veins; his hair was a glossy, flowing black, and his teeth were pearly white; yet these details only created a more grotesque contrast with his watery eyes, which appeared nearly the same shade as the murky white sockets holding them, his withered skin, and his straight, dark lips.

The various circumstances of existence are not as fickle as the human heart. I had toiled for nearly two years with the singular goal of breathing life into a corpse. For this, I had sacrificed my sleep and well-being. I had pursued it with a passion that far surpassed reason; but now that I had succeeded, the charm of my vision evaporated, and breathless dread and revulsion overwhelmed me. Unable to bear the sight of the creature I had brought to life, I fled the room and spent a long time pacing my bedroom, unable to calm my mind enough to rest. Eventually, exhaustion replaced the turmoil I had previously felt, and I collapsed onto the bed in my clothes, attempting to find a brief period of oblivion."
}

Chapter 5

It was on a dreary November night that I witnessed the completion of my labors. With an anxiety that almost reached agony, I gathered the instruments of life around me so that I might infuse a spark of existence into the lifeless thing lying at my feet. It was already one in the morning; rain tapped dismally against the windowpanes, and my candle was nearly burnt out when, by the glimmer of the dying light, I saw the creature’s dull yellow eye open. It breathed heavily, and a convulsive motion shook its limbs.

How can I describe my emotions at this disaster, or how can I portray the wretch whom I had tried so painstakingly to create? His limbs were proportional, and I had chosen his features to be beautiful. Beautiful! Good God! His yellow skin barely covered the muscles and arteries beneath; his hair was a lustrous, flowing black, and his teeth were pearly white. But these details only created a more horrific contrast with his watery eyes, which seemed almost the same color as the dull-white sockets they were set in, his shriveled complexion, and his straight black lips.

The circumstances of life are not as changeable as human emotions. I had worked hard for nearly two years for the sole purpose of infusing life into an inanimate body. For this, I had deprived myself of rest and health. I had desired it with an intensity that far exceeded moderation, but now that I had finished, the beauty of the dream vanished, and breathless horror and disgust filled my heart. Unable to endure the sight of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room and spent a long time pacing my bedroom, unable to calm my mind enough to sleep. Eventually, exhaustion replaced the turmoil I had felt, and I threw myself onto the bed in my clothes, trying to find a few moments of forgetfulness."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I am worried he will turn into a loafer if we do not concede and allow him to pursue the career he has chosen.

“Hardly anything has shifted, aside from the growth of our beloved children, since your departure. The azure lake and the peaks covered in snow remain constant; I believe our peaceful residence and our satisfied spirits are governed by those same unalterable principles. My small tasks occupy my hours and provide entertainment, and I find my efforts repaid by the sight of only cheerful, gentle countenances nearby. Since you went away, only one alteration has occurred within our small domestic circle. Do you recall the circumstances under which Justine Moritz joined our household? You likely do not; I shall therefore recount her background briefly. Madame Moritz, her mother, was a widow with four offspring, Justine being the third. This girl had always been her father’s darling, yet through an odd stubbornness, her mother could not tolerate her and, following M. Moritz’s passing, mistreated her severely. My aunt noticed this, and when Justine reached the age of twelve, she convinced the mother to permit the girl to reside with us. The democratic systems of our nation have fostered more modest and joyful customs than those found in the vast kingdoms surrounding us. Consequently, there is a smaller divide between the various ranks of its citizens; as the working classes are neither so destitute nor so scorned, their behavior is more polished and virtuous. A domestic worker in Geneva does not signify the same thing as one in France or England. Justine, welcomed into our home in this manner, acquired the responsibilities of a servant, a status which, in our lucky land, does not imply a lack of knowledge or a surrender of one’s human worth.

“Justine, you might recall, was a great favorite of yours; I remember you once noted that if you were in a foul mood, a single look from Justine could banish it, for the same reason Ariosto provides regarding the beauty of Angelica—she appeared so sincere and joyful. My aunt developed a deep fondness for her, which prompted her to provide an education better than what she had originally planned. This kindness was entirely returned; Justine was the most appreciative little soul imaginable: I do not suggest she made any verbal declarations—I never heard one escape her—but you could perceive by her gaze that she nearly worshipped her guardian. Although her nature was lighthearted and in many ways impulsive, she nonetheless observed every movement of my aunt with the utmost care. She viewed her as the standard of all virtue and strove to copy her way of speaking and her habits, so that even today she frequently brings her to my mind.

“When my dearest aunt passed away, everyone was too consumed by their own sorrow to observe poor Justine, who had cared for her throughout her sickness with the most worried devotion. Poor Justine was quite unwell; yet further hardships were in store for her.

“One after another, her siblings passed away; and her mother, save for her disregarded daughter, was left without children. The woman’s conscience was burdened; she began to suspect that the passing of her favorites was a divine punishment to rebuke her favoritism."
}

This girl had always been her father’s favorite, but through a strange bitterness, her mother could not stand her and treated her very poorly after M. Moritz died. Justine, having been welcomed into our family, learned the duties of a servant—a role that, in our fortunate country, does not imply ignorance or the loss of human dignity.

“You might remember that Justine was a great favorite of yours; I recall you once remarking that if you were in a bad mood, a single look from Justine could lift it, for the same reason Ariosto describes the beauty of Angelica—she looked so sincere and happy. My aunt grew very fond of her, which led her to provide Justine with a better education than she had originally planned. This kindness was fully repaid; Justine was the most grateful little person imaginable. I do not mean she made grand declarations—I never heard one pass her lips—but you could see in her eyes that she practically adored her guardian. Although her nature was cheerful and in some ways impulsive, she paid close attention to every move my aunt made. She considered her the model of perfection and tried to imitate her way of speaking and acting, so that even now, she often reminds me of her.

“When my dearest aunt passed away, everyone was too consumed by their own grief to notice poor Justine, who had cared for her during her illness with the most devoted affection. Justine was very ill herself, but further hardships were in store for her.

“One by one, her brothers and sister died, and her mother, aside from her neglected daughter, was left without children. The woman’s conscience was troubled; she began to believe that the deaths of her favorites were a divine judgment meant to punish her for her favoritism.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "My native land, my cherished home! Only one born here could describe the joy I felt in seeing your rivers, your peaks, and above all, your beautiful lake once more.

However, as I neared my destination, sorrow and dread returned to haunt me. Night descended; as the dark mountains faded from view, my spirits sank further. The landscape seemed a sprawling, shadowy theater of malice, and I sensed vaguely that I was fated to be the most miserable of men. How right my premonition was, though I failed in one regard: the suffering I feared was not even a fraction of the agony I was truly meant to bear.

It was pitch black when I reached the outskirts of Geneva. The city gates were locked, forcing me to spend the night in Secheron, a village half a league away. The sky was clear, and since I could not sleep, I decided to visit the site of poor William’s murder. Unable to enter the city, I took a boat across the lake to reach Plainpalais. During the crossing, I watched lightning dance across the peak of Mont Blanc in exquisite patterns. The tempest seemed to draw near quickly, and upon reaching the shore, I climbed a small hill to watch its advance. It moved closer; the clouds gathered, and I felt the first heavy drops of rain, which soon turned into a torrential downpour.

I left my position and continued walking, even as the darkness and the gale intensified, and thunder roared with terrifying force above me. The sound reverberated off the Salêve, the Juras, and the Savoy Alps. Brilliant lightning blinded me, turning the lake into a vast sheet of flame, followed by moments of absolute blackness until my vision adjusted. The storm, as frequently happens in Switzerland, seemed to strike from several directions at once. The most intense activity centered north of the city, over the water between the Belrive promontory and the village of Copêt. Another storm lit up the Jura with pale flickers, while a third obscured and occasionally revealed the Môle, a sharp mountain to the east.

As I observed this beautiful yet frightening spectacle, I hurried forward. This magnificent celestial conflict lifted my mood; I wrung my hands and cried out, “William, sweet angel! This is your funeral, this is your requiem!” As I spoke, I noticed a shape in the shadows slipping from behind a cluster of trees nearby. I stood motionless, staring hard; I could not be wrong. A bolt of lightning lit up the figure, revealing its form clearly. Its immense size and the grotesque, inhuman distortion of its features told me instantly that it was the wretch, the foul demon, to whom I had granted life. Why was he there? Could he be—I trembled at the thought—the killer of my brother?"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Everyone else is convinced of her culpability, which left me miserable, as I was certain of her innocence; witnessing such fatal prejudice against her left me utterly hopeless and despondent.” She sobbed.

“My dear niece,” my father remarked, “cease your weeping. If she is truly innocent, as you maintain, trust in the fairness of our legal system and the diligence with which I shall ensure there is no hint of bias.”

Chapter 8

We endured a few mournful hours until eleven, the scheduled start of the trial. Since my father and the others were required to testify, I went with them to the courthouse. Throughout this miserable travesty of justice, I endured agonizing torment. It was to be determined whether the consequences of my own reckless experiments would lead to the deaths of two human beings: one a cheerful, innocent infant, the other a victim of a brutal, infamous murder that would be remembered for its horror. Justine was a young woman of great merit whose life held promise; now, all of that was to be extinguished in a shameful grave, and I was the reason! I would have gladly admitted to the crime blamed on Justine, but I was away when it occurred, and such a claim would have been dismissed as the ramblings of a lunatic, failing to save the one who suffered because of me.

Justine appeared composed. She wore mourning clothes, and her face, naturally charming, was made strikingly beautiful by the gravity of her emotions. She seemed secure in her innocence and did not shake, even while being stared at and cursed by thousands, as any sympathy her beauty might have inspired was erased by the public’s belief in the enormity of her alleged crime. She was calm, though her composure was clearly forced; because her earlier agitation had been used as evidence of guilt, she steeled herself to project bravery. Upon entering the courtroom, she scanned the room and quickly spotted where we sat. A tear clouded her vision upon seeing us, but she regained her composure, and a gaze of mournful affection seemed to confirm her total lack of guilt.

The proceedings commenced, and after the prosecutor outlined the accusations, various witnesses were summoned. A series of peculiar facts were presented against her, which might have shaken anyone lacking the certainty of her innocence that I possessed. She had been away all night when the murder occurred and was spotted near dawn by a market-woman not far from where the child’s body was later discovered. The woman questioned her presence there, but she appeared quite odd and offered only a muddled, incoherent reply. She arrived back at the house around eight, and when asked where she had spent the night, she claimed she had been searching for the boy and asked anxiously if there was any news of him. Upon being presented with the corpse, she suffered severe hysterics and remained bedridden for several days."
}

“Dearest niece,” my father said, “dry your tears. If she is innocent, as you believe, trust in the fairness of our legal system and the diligence with which I will ensure there is not the slightest hint of bias.”

Chapter 8

We spent a few sorrowful hours until eleven o’clock, when the trial was set to begin. Since my father and the rest of the family had to attend as witnesses, I went with them to the courthouse. Throughout this miserable mockery of justice, I endured absolute torture. It was about to be decided whether the consequences of my curiosity and reckless experiments would cause the deaths of two fellow human beings: one a smiling, innocent baby, and the other someone murdered in a way so horrific it would be remembered for its infamy. Justine was also a girl of merit with qualities that promised a happy life; now, all of that was to be erased in a shameful grave, and I was the cause! I would have a thousand times preferred to confess to the crime attributed to Justine, but I was away when it happened, and such a statement would have been dismissed as the ravings of a madman and would not have cleared the person suffering because of me.

Justine appeared calm. She wore mourning clothes, and her face, always charming, was made exquisitely beautiful by the gravity of her emotions. Yet she seemed confident in her innocence and did not tremble, even though she was stared at and cursed by thousands. Any sympathy her beauty might have inspired was erased from the spectators' minds by the perceived enormity of her supposed crime. She was tranquil, though her composure was clearly forced; and because her earlier confusion had been used as proof of her guilt, she steeled herself to appear courageous. When she entered the courtroom, she scanned the room and quickly spotted where we were sitting. A tear seemed to cloud her eye when she saw us, but she quickly regained her composure, and a look of sorrowful affection seemed to confirm her complete innocence.

The trial began, and after the prosecutor stated the charges, several witnesses were called. A number of strange facts were presented against her, which might have shaken anyone who did not have the proof of her innocence that I possessed. She had been out all night on the night of the murder, and toward morning, she had been spotted by a market woman not far from where the murdered child’s body was later discovered. The woman asked her what she was doing there, but she looked very strange and gave only a confused, unintelligible answer. She returned to the house around eight o’clock, and when someone asked where she had spent the night, she replied that she had been searching for the child and asked urgently if anything had been heard about him. When shown the body, she fell into violent hysterics and remained bedridden for several days."
}

Chapter 8

We spent a few sorrowful hours until eleven o’clock, when the trial was set to begin. Since my father and the rest of the family had to attend as witnesses, I went with them to the courthouse. Throughout this miserable mockery of justice, I endured absolute torture. A tear seemed to cloud her eye when she saw us, but she quickly regained her composure, and a look of sorrowful affection seemed to confirm her complete innocence.

The trial began, and after the prosecutor stated the charges, several witnesses were called. A number of strange facts were presented against her, which might have shaken anyone who did not have the proof of her innocence that I possessed. She had been out all night on the night of the murder, and toward morning, she had been spotted by a market woman not far from where the murdered child’s body was later discovered. The woman asked her what she was doing there, but she looked very strange and gave only a confused, unintelligible answer. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“I admitted to the crime, but it was a fabrication. I gave that confession to receive forgiveness, yet that untruth now weighs more heavily upon my soul than any other transgression. May the Almighty pardon me! Ever since my sentencing, my priest has pressured me; he intimidated and frightened me until I nearly started believing I was the beast he claimed. He warned of being cast out and eternal damnation if I remained stubborn. Kind lady, I had no one to defend me; everyone viewed me as a creature destined for disgrace and ruin. What choice remained? In a moment of weakness, I agreed to a falsehood, and only now am I truly wretched.”

She stopped, sobbing, then resumed, “I was terrified, my dear lady, that you would think your Justine—whom your saintly aunt cherished and whom you loved—was capable of an act that only the devil could commit. Beloved William! Dearest, blessed boy! I will soon reunite with you in heaven, where we shall all find joy; that thought brings me comfort, even as I prepare to face shame and execution.”

“Oh, Justine! Pardon me for doubting you even for an instant. Why did you admit to it? But do not grieve, dear girl. Do not be afraid. I will declare, I will demonstrate your innocence. I will soften the hardened hearts of your accusers with my weeping and pleas. You shall not perish! You, my childhood friend, my companion, my sister, die upon the gallows? Never! I could never endure such a dreadful tragedy.”

Justine moved her head sadly. “I do not dread death,” she remarked; “that fear has vanished. God strengthens my frailty and grants me the fortitude to bear the worst. I depart from a sorrowful and harsh world; if you recall me and view me as one wrongly accused, I am content with my destiny. Take this lesson from me, dear lady: accept the will of heaven with patience!”

Throughout this exchange, I had withdrawn to a remote part of the jail cell, where I might hide the terrible torment consuming me. Despair! Who had the right to speak of such things? The unfortunate victim, who by the next day would cross the final threshold between existence and the afterlife, did not experience the profound and stinging agony that I felt. I clenched my teeth and ground them, releasing a moan that originated from the depths of my being. Justine jumped. Upon seeing me, she walked over and said, “Kind sir, it is very generous of you to see me; I trust you do not believe I am guilty?”

I was unable to speak. “No, Justine,” Elizabeth replied; “he is even more certain of your innocence than I was, for even upon hearing of your confession, he refused to believe it.”

“I am truly grateful to him. In these final moments, I feel genuine appreciation for those who regard me with compassion. How precious is the kindness of others to a wretch like me! It eases more than half of my misery, and I feel as though I can pass away in tranquility now that my innocence is recognized by you, dear lady, and your cousin.”

In this manner, the unfortunate woman attempted to console both others and herself."
}

{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_009",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“I did confess, but it was a lie. I confessed to receive absolution, but now that falsehood weighs more heavily on my heart than all my other sins. May God in heaven forgive me! Ever since I was condemned, my confessor has pressured me; he threatened and intimidated me until I almost started to believe I was the monster he claimed I was. He threatened me with excommunication and hellfire in my final moments if I remained stubborn. Dear lady, I had no one to support me; everyone looked at me as a wretch destined for disgrace and damnation. What could I do? In a moment of weakness, I agreed to a lie, and only now am I truly miserable.”

She paused, weeping, and then continued, “I thought with horror, my sweet lady, that you might believe your Justine—whom your blessed aunt honored so highly and whom you loved—was capable of a crime that only the devil himself could have committed. Dear William! Dearest, blessed child! I will soon see you again in heaven, where we will all be happy; that thought consoles me as I prepare to face disgrace and death.”

“Oh, Justine! Forgive me for doubting you even for a moment. Why did you confess? But do not mourn, dear girl. Do not be afraid. I will proclaim and prove your innocence. I will soften the stony hearts of your enemies with my tears and prayers. You shall not die! You, my playmate, my companion, my sister, perish on the scaffold? No! No! I could never survive such a horrible tragedy.”

Justine shook her head mournfully. “I do not fear death,” she said; “that pain has passed. God strengthens my weakness and gives me the courage to endure the worst. I am leaving a sad and bitter world, and if you remember me as someone unjustly condemned, I am resigned to the fate awaiting me. Learn from me, dear lady, to submit patiently to the will of heaven!”

During this conversation, I had retreated to a corner of the prison cell, where I could hide the horrific anguish that consumed me. Despair! Who dared speak of that? The poor victim, who was to cross the terrifying boundary between life and death the next day, did not feel the deep, bitter agony that I did. I gnashed my teeth and ground them together, letting out a groan from the depths of my soul. Justine jumped. When she saw who it was, she approached me and said, “Dear sir, you are very kind to visit me; I hope you do not believe I am guilty?”

I could not answer. “No, Justine,” Elizabeth said; “he is more convinced of your innocence than I was, for even when he heard you had confessed, he did not believe it.”

“I truly thank him. In these final moments, I feel the sincerest gratitude toward those who think of me with kindness. How sweet the affection of others is to a wretch like me! It removes more than half my misfortune, and I feel as if I could die in peace now that my innocence is acknowledged by you, dear lady, and your cousin.”

Thus, the poor sufferer tried to comfort others and herself."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I could never agree to the execution of any person, yet I would surely have deemed such a monster unworthy of living among mankind. Nevertheless, she was guiltless. I am certain, I feel in my heart that she was innocent; your agreement only strengthens my conviction. Oh, Victor, when lies can appear so much like the truth, who can ever be certain of their own joy? I feel as though I am treading upon the brink of a cliff, where a multitude of people are pushing and striving to cast me into the depths. William and Justine were murdered, yet the killer remains at large; he roams the earth unhindered, and perhaps even held in high regard. Yet, even if I were sentenced to die on the gallows for those same deeds, I would never trade my fate for that of such a villain.

I heard this speech with the deepest torment. I was the actual killer, if not by my own hand, then by the consequences of my actions. Elizabeth perceived my distress in my face, and gently grasping my hand, she said, “My dearest friend, you must compose yourself. These tragedies have moved me, heaven knows how profoundly; yet I am not as miserable as you. There is a look of hopelessness, and at times of vengeance, in your eyes that frightens me. Dear Victor, cast aside these gloomy emotions. Think of the loved ones nearby, who place all their expectations in you. Have we lost the ability to bring you joy? Oh, as long as we cherish one another, as long as we remain loyal, here in this region of serenity and splendor, your own homeland, we might enjoy every quiet comfort—what could possibly disrupt our harmony?”

And could not such pleas from the one I cherished above all other worldly blessings be enough to banish the demon hiding within my breast? Even as she uttered these words, I moved closer to her, as if in fear, dreading that the destroyer might be lurking nearby to steal her away from me at that very instant.

Thus, neither the warmth of companionship, nor the majesty of the world or the heavens, could rescue my spirit from misery; even the gentle tones of affection were powerless. I was surrounded by a gloom that no positive influence could pierce. The injured stag dragging its weary body to some hidden thicket, only to stare at the shaft that had struck it and perish, served as a perfect image of my own state.

At times I could withstand the heavy gloom that crushed me, but occasionally the tempestuous emotions of my spirit compelled me to pursue, through physical exertion and travel, some escape from my unbearable feelings. It was during one of these fits that I abruptly departed from my home, and turning my path toward the nearby Alpine valleys, I attempted, within the grandeur and timelessness of those landscapes, to lose sight of myself and my fleeting, because mortal, griefs. My travels led me toward the valley of Chamounix. I had gone there often in my youth. Six years had elapsed since then: I was a ruin, yet nothing had altered in those wild and permanent vistas.

I completed the initial stage of my travel on horseback. Afterward, I rented a mule, as it was more reliable and less prone to harm on those treacherous paths. The conditions were pleasant; it was roughly the middle of August, nearly two months following the passing of Justine, that wretched point in time from which I measured all my suffering."
}

Oh, as long as we cherish one another, as long as we remain loyal, here in this region of serenity and splendor, your own homeland, we might enjoy every quiet comfort—what could possibly disrupt our harmony?”

And could not such pleas from the one I cherished above all other worldly blessings be enough to banish the demon hiding within my breast? Even as she uttered these words, I moved closer to her, as if in fear, dreading that the destroyer might be lurking nearby to steal her away from me at that very instant.

Thus, neither the warmth of companionship, nor the majesty of the world or the heavens, could rescue my spirit from misery; even the gentle tones of affection were powerless. I was surrounded by a gloom that no positive influence could pierce. The injured stag dragging its weary body to some hidden thicket, only to stare at the shaft that had struck it and perish, served as a perfect image of my own state.

At times I could withstand the heavy gloom that crushed me, but occasionally the tempestuous emotions of my spirit compelled me to pursue, through physical exertion and travel, some escape from my unbearable feelings. It was during one of these fits that I abruptly departed from my home, and turning my path toward the nearby Alpine valleys, I attempted, within the grandeur and timelessness of those landscapes, to lose sight of myself and my fleeting, because mortal, griefs. My travels led me toward the valley of Chamounix. I had gone there often in my youth. Six years had elapsed since then: I was a ruin, yet nothing had altered in those wild and permanent vistas.

I completed the initial stage of my travel on horseback. Afterward, I rented a mule, as it was more reliable and less prone to harm on those treacherous paths. The conditions were pleasant; it was roughly the middle of August, nearly two months following the passing of Justine, that wretched point in time from which I measured all my suffering."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Fiend,” I cried, “how dare you come near me? Are you not afraid of the violent retribution my hand might visit upon your wretched skull? Get away, you loathsome creature! Or better yet, remain, so that I might crush you into the dirt! And, oh! If only I could, by ending your pathetic life, bring back those souls you have so wickedly slaughtered!”

“I anticipated this greeting,” the demon replied. “All people despise the unfortunate; how much more must I be loathed, being the most miserable of all living beings! Yet you, my maker, despise and reject me, your creation, to whom you are linked by bonds that can only be broken by the destruction of one of us. You intend to murder me. How can you play so recklessly with life? Fulfill your obligations to me, and I will fulfill mine to you and the rest of humanity. If you agree to my terms, I will leave you and them in peace; but if you refuse, I will feed the hunger of death until it is gorged with the blood of your surviving friends.”

“Detestable monster! You are a demon! The torments of the underworld are too light a punishment for your sins. Miserable devil! You blame me for your existence; come forward, then, so I may snuff out the life I so carelessly granted.”

My fury was limitless; I lunged at him, driven by every emotion that can turn one being against the life of another.

He avoided me effortlessly and remarked,

“Stay your temper! I beg you to listen before you unleash your malice upon my head. Have I not endured enough, that you wish to add to my suffering? Life, even if it is merely a collection of pain, is precious to me, and I will protect it. Bear in mind, you have crafted me stronger than yourself; I am taller than you, and my limbs are more agile. Yet I will not be provoked into fighting you. I am your creation, and I will be gentle and obedient to my rightful master if you will also fulfill the duties you owe me. Oh, Frankenstein, do not be fair to everyone else while trampling only on me, to whom your justice, and even your mercy and kindness, are most owed. Remember that I am your creation; I should be your Adam, but instead I am the fallen angel, whom you cast out from happiness without cause. Everywhere I look, I see joy, from which I alone am permanently barred. I was kind and good; suffering turned me into a monster. Grant me happiness, and I shall be virtuous once more.”

“Leave! I will not listen to you. There can be no fellowship between us; we are foes. Go away, or let us test our power in a battle, where one of us must perish.”

“How can I sway you? Will no pleas make you look kindly upon your creation, who begs for your goodness and pity? Believe me, Frankenstein, I was once kind; my heart burned with love and compassion; but am I not solitary, wretchedly solitary? You, my creator, loathe me; what expectation can I have from your fellow humans, who owe me nothing? They reject and hate me. The desolate mountains and bleak glaciers are my sanctuary. I have roamed here for many days; the ice caverns, which are the only things I do not fear, serve as my home, and the only one that mankind does not begrudge me.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_011",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Monster,” I shouted, “how dare you come near me? Are you not afraid of the fierce vengeance I will unleash upon your miserable head? Get out of here, you vile insect! Or rather, stay, so I can crush you into the dirt! Oh, if only I could end your wretched existence and bring back the victims you have so diabolically murdered!”

“I expected this reaction,” the demon replied. “All people hate the miserable; how much more must I be hated, being more miserable than any other living thing! Yet you, my creator, despise and reject me, your creature, to whom you are bound by ties that can only be broken by the death of one of us. You intend to kill me. How dare you play with life like this? Do your duty to me, and I will do mine to you and the rest of humanity. If you agree to my terms, I will leave you and them in peace; but if you refuse, I will feed the maw of death until it is satisfied with the blood of your remaining friends.”

“Abhorrent monster! You fiend! The tortures of hell are too mild a punishment for your crimes. Wretched devil! You blame me for your creation—come on, then, so I can extinguish the spark I so carelessly gave you.”

My rage was limitless; I lunged at him, driven by every instinct that can turn one being against another.

He easily dodged me and said,

“Calm down! I beg you to listen before you vent your hatred on my head. Have I not suffered enough, that you seek to increase my misery? Life, even if it is just a collection of pain, is precious to me, and I will defend it. Remember, you have made me stronger than yourself; I am taller than you, and my joints are more flexible. But I will not be tempted to fight you. I am your creature, and I will be gentle and obedient to my natural master if you will also do your part, which you owe me. Oh, Frankenstein, do not be fair to everyone else while trampling only on me, to whom your justice, and even your mercy and affection, are most due. Remember that I am your creature; I should be your Adam, but instead I am the fallen angel, whom you drive away from joy for no wrongdoing. Everywhere I see happiness, from which I alone am permanently excluded. I was benevolent and good; misery made me a fiend. Make me happy, and I will be virtuous again.”

“Get out! I will not listen to you. There can be no connection between us; we are enemies. Leave, or let us test our strength in a fight, where one of us must fall.”

“How can I move you? Will no pleas cause you to look kindly upon your creature, who begs for your goodness and compassion? Believe me, Frankenstein, I was benevolent; my soul glowed with love and humanity; but am I not alone, miserably alone? You, my creator, loathe me; what hope can I expect from your fellow humans, who owe me nothing? They reject and hate me. The desert mountains and dreary glaciers are my refuge. I have wandered here for many days; the ice caves, which are the only things I do not fear, are my home, and the only one that humans do not begrudge me.”"
}

But I will not be tempted to fight you. I am your creature, and I will be gentle and obedient to my natural master if you will also do your part, which you owe me. Oh, Frankenstein, do not be fair to everyone else while trampling only on me, to whom your justice, and even your kindness and affection, are most due. Remember that I am your creature; I should be your Adam, but instead I am the fallen angel, whom you drive away from joy for no reason. Everywhere I see happiness, from which I alone am permanently excluded. I was kind and good; misery turned me into a fiend. Make me happy, and I will be virtuous again.”

“Get away! I will not listen to you. There can be no connection between you and me; we are enemies. Leave, or let us test our strength in a fight, where one of us must fall.”

“How can I move you? Will no pleas make you look kindly upon your creature, who begs for your goodness and compassion? Believe me, Frankenstein, I was kind; my soul glowed with love and humanity; but am I not alone, miserably alone? You, my creator, hate me; what hope can I expect from your fellow humans, who owe me nothing? They reject and hate me. The desert mountains and dreary glaciers are my refuge. I have wandered here for many days; the ice caves, which are the only things I do not fear, are my home, and the only one that humans do not begrudge me.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I attempted to prepare my meals similarly, laying them upon the glowing coals. I discovered that the berries were ruined by this process, while the roots and nuts were significantly better.

“Provisions, however, grew rare, and I frequently passed entire days hunting fruitlessly for a handful of acorns to quiet my hunger. Once I realized this, I decided to abandon the location I had occupied until then, hoping to find a place where my modest needs might be more readily met. During this migration, I deeply mourned the loss of the fire I had acquired by chance, as I possessed no method to recreate it. I dedicated several hours to contemplating this problem, but I eventually had to abandon any hope of replacing it; wrapping myself in my cloak, I headed through the forest toward the setting sun. I spent three days wandering before finally reaching open land. A heavy snowfall had occurred the previous night, leaving the fields a uniform white; the scene was bleak, and my feet grew numb from the cold, wet substance blanketing the earth.

“It was roughly seven in the morning, and I desperately sought sustenance and protection; eventually, I spotted a small cabin on a hill, clearly constructed for a shepherd’s use. This was a novel sight, and I inspected the building with intense interest. Seeing the door ajar, I stepped inside. An elderly man sat nearby, tending a fire while cooking his morning meal. Hearing a sound, he turned, saw me, and let out a piercing scream before bolting from the hut, sprinting across the fields with a swiftness his frail body seemed unable to sustain. His appearance, unlike any I had encountered, and his sudden flight left me somewhat bewildered. Yet, I was captivated by the cabin; here, the rain and snow could not reach me; the floor was dry; and it seemed to me as wonderful and heavenly a sanctuary as Pandemonium must have appeared to the demons of hell following their torments in the lake of fire. I ravenously consumed the leftovers of the shepherd’s meal, which included bread, cheese, milk, and wine—though I disliked the latter. Then, exhausted, I curled up in some straw and drifted off to sleep.

“It was midday when I woke, and drawn by the warmth of the sun shining brilliantly on the white landscape, I resolved to resume my journey. Stowing the remainder of the peasant’s food in a bag I discovered, I walked across the fields for several hours until I reached a village at dusk. How wondrous this seemed! The huts, the tidy cottages, and the grand houses captured my admiration in turn. The garden vegetables, along with the milk and cheese visible in cottage windows, tempted my hunger. I stepped into one of the finer dwellings, but I had barely crossed the threshold when the children began to scream and a woman collapsed in a faint. The entire village was stirred; some people ran, while others assaulted me, until, badly battered by stones and various projectiles, I fled to the open fields and timidly sought shelter in a small, empty shed, which looked miserable compared to the grand structures I had just witnessed in the village."
}

During this migration, I deeply regretted the loss of the fire I had obtained by accident, as I did not know how to recreate it. I spent three days wandering and finally discovered open country. A heavy snowfall had occurred the night before, and the fields were a uniform white; the sight was bleak, and I found my feet chilled by the cold, damp substance covering the ground.

“It was about seven in the morning, and I longed for food and shelter; eventually, I spotted a small hut on a hill, which had clearly been built for the convenience of a shepherd. This was a new sight to me, and I examined the structure with great curiosity. Finding the door open, I went inside. An old man sat near a fire, preparing his breakfast. He turned when he heard a noise, and upon seeing me, he shrieked loudly. Leaving the hut, he ran across the fields with a speed his frail body hardly seemed capable of. His appearance, unlike anything I had ever seen before, and his flight surprised me somewhat. But I was enchanted by the hut; here, the snow and rain could not get in; the ground was dry; and it seemed to me as exquisite and divine a retreat as Pandemonium appeared to the demons of hell after their suffering in the lake of fire. I greedily devoured the remnants of the shepherd’s breakfast, which consisted of bread, cheese, milk, and wine; I did not like the wine, however. Then, overcome by fatigue, I lay down in some straw and fell asleep.

“It was noon when I woke up, and drawn by the warmth of the sun, which shone brightly on the white ground, I decided to resume my travels. Placing the remains of the peasant’s breakfast in a bag I found, I walked across the fields for several hours until I arrived at a village at sunset. How miraculous this seemed! }

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "A significant amount of time passed before I identified a primary source of the distress felt by this kind family: they were living in a state of severe poverty. Their diet was limited to garden vegetables and the milk from a single cow, which produced very little in the winter months when they could barely afford to feed the animal. I believe they frequently endured the sharp pains of hunger, particularly the two younger residents, who would often set meals before the elderly man while keeping nothing for themselves.

This display of affection touched me deeply. I had previously made a habit of stealing from their supplies at night to feed myself, but upon realizing that my actions caused them suffering, I stopped and instead relied on berries, nuts, and roots gathered from the nearby forest.

I also found another way to lighten their burdens. I noticed the young man spent much of his day gathering firewood, so I began taking his tools at night—having quickly learned how to operate them—to provide enough fuel to last them for several days.

I recall that the first time I did this, the young woman seemed quite amazed when she opened the door to find a large stack of wood waiting. She exclaimed in a loud voice, and the young man came to join her, showing equal astonishment. I was pleased to see that he did not head to the woods that day, choosing instead to spend his time fixing the cottage and working in the garden.

Eventually, I made a discovery of even greater importance. I realized these people had a way of sharing their thoughts and emotions through spoken language. I observed that their words could evoke joy or sorrow, smiles or tears, in those who heard them. This seemed like a divine ability, and I was eager to master it. Yet, every effort I made was met with failure. Their speech was rapid, and because the words seemed unrelated to physical objects, I could find no way to decode their meaning. Through intense focus, however, and after remaining in my shelter for several months, I identified the terms for common items; I learned to use the words fire, milk, bread, and wood. I also discovered the names of the cottagers. The young man and his companion had multiple names, while the old man was simply called father. The girl was known as sister or Agatha, and the youth as Felix, brother, or son. I cannot express the joy I felt when I grasped the meaning of these sounds and could speak them myself. I identified several other words, such as good, dearest, and unhappy, though I could not yet fully understand or utilize them.

I passed the winter in this fashion. The gentle nature and beauty of the family made me feel very close to them; when they were sad, I felt downcast, and when they were happy, I shared in their delight."
}

I also learned the names of the cottagers. The young man and the young woman had several names, but the old man had only one, which was father. The girl was called sister or Agatha, and the young man was called Felix, brother, or son. I cannot describe the joy I felt when I understood what each of these sounds meant and was able to say them myself. I identified several other words without yet understanding how to use them, such as good, dearest, and unhappy.

I spent the winter this way. The gentle behavior and beauty of the cottagers made me feel very close to them; when they were sad, I felt depressed, and when they were happy, I shared in their joy."
}

I believe they often felt the sharp pains of hunger, especially the two younger people, who frequently gave their own portions of food to the old man while keeping none for themselves.

This act of kindness touched me deeply. I had been in the habit of stealing some of their food at night for myself, but once I realized that I was causing them pain, I stopped. Instead, I satisfied my hunger with berries, nuts, and roots that I gathered from a nearby wood.

I also found another way to help them with their work. I noticed that the young man spent much of his day gathering firewood. During the night, I would take his tools—which I quickly learned how to use—and bring home enough wood to last them for several days.

I remember the first time I did this: when the young woman opened the door the next morning, she was very surprised to see a large pile of wood outside. She called out in a loud voice, and the young man joined her, also expressing his amazement. I was pleased to see that he did not go to the forest that day, but instead spent his time repairing the cottage and working in the garden.

Gradually, I made an even more important discovery. I realized that these people had a way of sharing their thoughts and feelings through spoken words. I noticed that the sounds they made could cause pleasure or pain, smiles or sadness, in the people listening. This seemed like a godlike ability, and I desperately wanted to learn it. {
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "They gestured in ways I could not grasp, yet I observed that her arrival brought cheer to the home, lifting their gloom just as the sun clears away the dawn fog. Felix appeared especially joyful, greeting his Arabian visitor with radiant smiles. Agatha, ever kind, kissed the beautiful newcomer’s hands and gestured toward her brother, which I interpreted as a sign that he had been miserable before her arrival. Several hours went by in this manner, with their faces showing a happiness I could not fathom. Soon, I realized from the repeated sounds the stranger mimicked that she was attempting to acquire their tongue; I immediately resolved to utilize these same lessons for my own benefit. The visitor mastered about twenty words during that initial session; while I already knew most of them, I gained knowledge from the remainder.

“As evening fell, Agatha and the Arabian went to bed. Upon parting, Felix kissed the stranger’s hand and bid her, ‘Good night, sweet Safie.’ He remained awake much longer to talk with his father, and from the frequent mention of her name, I surmised that their charming guest was the topic of their discussion. I desperately longed to grasp their meaning, focusing all my mental energy toward that goal, yet I found it entirely beyond my reach.

“The following morning, Felix departed for his labor, and once Agatha had completed her customary chores, the Arabian sat at the old man’s feet. She took up his guitar and played melodies so captivatingly lovely that they immediately brought tears of both sadness and joy to my eyes. She sang, her voice rising and falling in a melodic flow, much like a woodland nightingale.

“Upon finishing, she handed the instrument to Agatha, who initially hesitated. She played a modest tune, singing along in gentle tones, though lacking the stranger’s extraordinary skill. The old man seemed enchanted, speaking words that Agatha tried to interpret for Safie, seemingly conveying that her music provided him with immense pleasure.

“The days continued as tranquilly as before, with the only change being that joy had replaced the sorrow on my friends’ faces. Safie remained constantly cheerful; she and I progressed quickly in our linguistic studies, and within two months, I could understand the majority of what my protectors said.

“Meanwhile, the dark earth became blanketed in grass, and the verdant banks were dotted with countless flowers, delightful to both smell and sight, appearing like pale, glowing stars amidst the moonlit trees. The sun grew hotter, the nights turned clear and mild, and my nightly wanderings became a source of great joy, though they were significantly curtailed by the sun’s late setting and early rising. I never dared to venture out in daylight, terrified of encountering the same hostility I had faced in the first village I visited.

“I dedicated my days to intense study so that I might learn the language more quickly. I can claim that I advanced faster than the Arabian, who understood very little and spoke in fragmented phrases, whereas I could grasp and replicate nearly every word spoken.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Safie explained that her mother was a Christian Arab captured and enslaved by the Turks; her striking beauty captured the heart of Safie’s father, who eventually wed her. The young woman described her mother with great fervor, noting that, having been born free, she despised the servitude she now endured. She educated her daughter in her own faith and encouraged her to seek intellectual growth and a spirit of autonomy denied to women under the laws of Muhammad. Though her mother passed away, her teachings remained fixed in Safie’s mind, who felt repulsed by the idea of returning to Asia to be confined within a harem, restricted to childish diversions that did not suit a spirit now shaped by lofty thoughts and a desire for virtue. The possibility of wedding a Christian and living in a land where women held a recognized place in society delighted her.

The date for the Turk’s execution was set, yet the night before, he escaped his cell and was many miles from Paris by dawn. Felix had obtained travel documents for his father, his sister, and himself. He had earlier shared his scheme with his father, who assisted in the ruse by leaving their home under the guise of traveling and hiding with his daughter in a secluded section of Paris.

Felix guided the escapees across France to Lyons and over Mont Cenis to Leghorn, where the merchant intended to wait for a good chance to travel into Turkish territory.

Safie decided to stay with her father until he left, and before that time, the Turk reaffirmed his vow that she would marry her rescuer; Felix stayed with them, awaiting that moment, and meanwhile cherished the company of the Arabian, who showed him the most sincere and gentle devotion. They communicated through an interpreter, and occasionally through glances, while Safie sang the beautiful melodies of her homeland for him.

The Turk permitted this closeness and fostered the young lovers' hopes, though he secretly harbored different intentions. He detested the thought of his daughter marrying a Christian, yet he dreaded Felix’s anger should he seem unenthusiastic, knowing he remained vulnerable to his rescuer if he chose to report him to the Italian authorities. He considered countless schemes to maintain the deception until it was no longer required, intending to secretly take his daughter away upon his departure. His designs were aided by reports arriving from Paris.

The French government was furious at the escape of their prisoner and worked tirelessly to find and punish his benefactor. Felix’s conspiracy was soon uncovered, leading to the imprisonment of De Lacey and Agatha. This news reached Felix, shattering his blissful state. His elderly, sightless father and kind sister were suffering in a foul prison while he remained in the open air, enjoying the company of the woman he loved."
}
