The idea of marrying a Christian and living in a country where women held a respected place in society was a wonderful prospect to her.

The date for the Turk’s execution was set, but on the night before, he escaped from prison and was many miles away from Paris by morning. Felix had obtained passports for his father, his sister, and himself. He had already shared his plan with his father, who helped with the deception by leaving his home under the guise of taking a trip, hiding instead with his daughter in a quiet part of Paris.

Felix led the fugitives through France to Lyons and across the Mont Cenis pass to Leghorn. There, the merchant decided to wait for a good chance to travel into Turkish territory.

Safie decided to stay with her father until he was ready to leave. Before that time, the Turk repeated his promise that she would marry the man who had saved him. Felix stayed with them, waiting for that moment, and in the meantime, he enjoyed the company of the Arabian woman, who showed him simple and tender affection. They spoke to each other through an interpreter, and sometimes just through glances, while Safie sang him the beautiful songs of her homeland.

The Turk allowed this closeness and encouraged the young lovers’ hopes, even though he had very different plans in his heart. He hated the idea of his daughter marrying a Christian, but he feared how Felix might react if he seemed unenthusiastic. He knew he was still in the power of his rescuer, who could betray him to the Italian authorities. He considered many ways to keep up the deception until it was no longer needed, intending to secretly take his daughter with him when he left. His plans were made easier by news that arrived from Paris.

The French government was furious about the escape of their prisoner and worked hard to find and punish the person who had helped him. Felix’s plot was quickly uncovered, and De Lacey and Agatha were thrown into prison. When this news reached Felix, it woke him from his happy dream. His blind, elderly father and his kind sister were suffering in a filthy dungeon while he enjoyed his freedom and the company of the woman he loved."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_016",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The indigent individuals who paused at their threshold were never turned away. It is accurate that I sought more significant riches than mere sustenance or repose: I craved compassion and understanding; yet, I did not consider myself entirely undeserving of such things.

“As winter progressed, a full cycle of the seasons had transpired since I first gained consciousness. My focus during this period was entirely centered on my strategy for presenting myself to the inhabitants of the cottage. I contemplated numerous schemes, but the one I ultimately settled upon was to gain entry to the home while the sightless elderly man was by himself. I possessed sufficient insight to realize that the unnatural repulsiveness of my appearance was the primary source of terror for those who had previously seen me. My tone of voice, while grating, held nothing inherently frightening; I reasoned, therefore, that if I could secure the favor and intercession of the aged De Lacey while his offspring were away, I might, through his influence, be accepted by the younger residents.

“One afternoon, while the sun cast light upon the crimson foliage covering the earth and spread a sense of brightness, despite providing little heat, Safie, Agatha, and Felix set out for an extended walk in the countryside, and the old man, by his own request, remained solitary in the house. Once his children had left, he picked up his guitar and performed several melancholy yet melodic tunes, more beautiful and sorrowful than any I had previously heard him play. Initially, his face glowed with delight, but as he played on, contemplation and melancholy took over; eventually, setting the instrument aside, he sat lost in deep thought.

“My pulse raced; this was the critical moment of testing that would either confirm my aspirations or confirm my dreads. The attendants had departed for a nearby festival. Everything was quiet within and surrounding the cottage; it was a perfect chance; nevertheless, as I moved to carry out my intention, my legs gave way and I collapsed to the earth. I stood once more, and summoning all the resolve I could muster, I shifted the boards I had positioned in front of my shelter to hide my dwelling. The crisp air invigorated me, and with fresh resolve, I drew near the cottage door.

“I tapped. ‘Who is there?’ the old man inquired. ‘Come in.’

“I stepped inside. ‘Forgive this interruption,’ I stated; ‘I am a wanderer in need of brief respite; you would do me a great kindness if you permitted me to stay for a few moments by the hearth.’

“‘Come in,’ De Lacey replied, ‘and I shall see how I might assist your needs; however, regrettably, my children are away, and since I lack sight, I fear I shall struggle to provide you with nourishment.’

“‘Do not concern yourself, my generous host; I possess provisions; it is only warmth and rest that I require.’

“I took a seat, and a quiet interval followed. I understood that every second was valuable to me, yet I hesitated regarding how to begin the conversation, until the old man spoke to me.

‘Judging by your speech, traveler, I assume you are a fellow countryman; are you French?’

“‘No; but I was raised by a French household and am familiar only with that tongue. I am currently on my way to seek the aid of some companions, whom I deeply cherish, and whose goodwill I have some reason to hope for.’"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_016",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The poor who stopped at their door were never turned away. It is true that I asked for greater treasures than a little food or rest: I required kindness and sympathy, and I did not believe myself entirely unworthy of them.

“Winter approached, and a full cycle of the seasons had passed since I first awoke to life. My focus at this time was entirely on my plan to introduce myself to the cottage of my protectors. I considered many projects, but I finally decided to enter the home when the blind old man was alone. I was perceptive enough to realize that the unnatural hideousness of my appearance was the primary source of horror for those who had seen me before. My voice, though harsh, was not terrifying; I thought, therefore, that if I could gain the goodwill and mediation of old De Lacey while his children were away, I might be tolerated by my younger protectors through his influence.

“One day, when the sun shone on the red leaves covering the ground and spread cheerfulness, even if it lacked warmth, Safie, Agatha, and Felix left for a long walk in the country, and the old man, at his own request, was left alone in the cottage. Once his children had departed, he picked up his guitar and played several mournful but sweet melodies, more sweet and sorrowful than I had ever heard him play before. At first, his face was bright with pleasure, but as he continued, thoughtfulness and sadness took over; eventually, he set the instrument aside and sat lost in reflection.

“My heart beat rapidly; this was the moment of trial that would decide my hopes or confirm my fears. The servants had gone to a nearby fair. Everything was silent in and around the cottage; it was a perfect opportunity, yet when I moved to carry out my plan, my legs gave way and I sank to the ground. I rose again, and exerting all the resolve I could muster, I removed the planks I had placed in front of my hovel to hide my retreat. The fresh air revived me, and with renewed determination, I approached the door of their cottage.

“I knocked. ‘Who is there?’ the old man asked. ‘Come in.’

“I entered. ‘Pardon this intrusion,’ I said. ‘I am a traveler in need of a little rest; you would do me a great favor if you would allow me to stay a few minutes by the fire.’

“‘Come in,’ De Lacey said, ‘and I will try to see how I can help you; but unfortunately, my children are away, and since I am blind, I am afraid I will find it difficult to get food for you.’

“‘Do not trouble yourself, my kind host; I have food; it is only warmth and rest that I need.’

“I sat down, and a silence followed. I knew every minute was precious, yet I remained hesitant about how to begin the conversation, when the old man spoke to me.

‘Judging by your language, stranger, I assume you are my countryman; are you French?’

“‘No, but I was educated by a French family and understand only that language. I am now on my way to seek the protection of some friends whom I sincerely love, and whose favor I have some hope of gaining.’”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I remained inside my shack for the rest of the day, trapped in a daze of absolute hopelessness. My companions had left, severing the sole connection I had to humanity. For the first time, feelings of vengeance and malice flooded my heart, and rather than resisting them, I let myself be swept away, focusing my thoughts on destruction and death. Whenever I recalled my friends—the soft tone of De Lacey, the tender gaze of Agatha, and the stunning grace of the Arabian—those dark impulses faded, and a flood of tears brought me some relief. Yet, when I considered how they had rejected and abandoned me, my rage flared up again. Unable to harm any person, I directed my wrath toward lifeless things. As night fell, I arranged various flammable materials around the house, ruined every bit of the garden’s vegetation, and waited with strained patience for the moon to set before beginning my work.

As the night deepened, a violent gale blew from the forest, quickly scattering the clouds that had been drifting in the sky. The wind roared like a massive avalanche, inducing a kind of madness in my mind that shattered all limits of logic and contemplation. I ignited a dry tree branch and circled the doomed cottage in a frenzy, my gaze locked on the western horizon where the moon was nearly touching the edge. Finally, a portion of its disk disappeared, and I swung my torch; as it vanished, I let out a loud cry and set fire to the straw, heather, and brush I had gathered. The wind fueled the blaze, and the cottage was soon swallowed by flames that gripped the structure and licked at it with jagged, ruinous tongues.

Once I was certain that no help could salvage any portion of the home, I left the area and retreated into the woods.

Now, with the entire world ahead of me, where should I go? I decided to flee far from the site of my misery; yet, being loathed and shunned, every land seemed equally dreadful to me. Eventually, the idea of you occurred to me. I discovered from your documents that you were my father, my maker; and who could be more appropriate to turn to than the one who had granted me existence? Among the subjects Felix had taught Safie, geography was included; I had learned from these lessons the locations of various nations. You had identified Geneva as your home city, and I determined to travel there.

But how was I to find my way? I understood that I needed to head southwest to reach my goal, but the sun was my only compass. I did not know the names of the villages I would pass, nor could I seek guidance from any person; still, I did not lose heart. From you alone could I expect aid, even though I felt nothing but hatred toward you. Callous, unfeeling creator! You gave me senses and emotions, only to cast me out as a target for the world’s disgust and terror."
}

Instead, I let myself be carried away by the current, focusing my mind on destruction and death. When I thought of my friends—the gentle voice of De Lacey, the kind eyes of Agatha, and the exquisite beauty of the Arabian—those thoughts faded, and a flood of tears brought me some relief. But when I reflected again that they had rejected and abandoned me, my anger returned, a furious rage. Unable to hurt any human, I turned my fury toward inanimate objects. As night fell, I placed various flammable materials around the cottage, and after destroying every trace of the garden’s cultivation, I waited with forced impatience for the moon to set before beginning my work.

As the night deepened, a fierce wind rose from the woods, quickly scattering the clouds that had lingered in the sky. The gale tore through like a massive avalanche, inducing a kind of madness in my spirit that broke through all reason and reflection. I lit a dry tree branch and danced with fury around the doomed cottage, my eyes fixed on the western horizon, which the moon was nearly touching. Finally, part of its orb disappeared, and I waved my torch. It sank, and with a loud scream, I set fire to the straw, heather, and bushes I had gathered. The wind fanned the flames, and the cottage was quickly engulfed, the fire clinging to it and licking it with forked, destructive tongues.

Once I was certain that no help could save any part of the home, I left the scene and sought refuge in the woods.

Now, with the world before me, where should I go? I decided to flee far from the site of my misfortunes, but to me, hated and despised, every country seemed equally horrible. Eventually, the thought of you crossed my mind. I learned from your papers that you were my father, my creator; and to whom could I turn more appropriately than to the one who had given me life? Among the lessons Felix had taught Safie, geography was included; I had learned from these the relative locations of the world’s different countries. You had mentioned Geneva as your hometown, and I decided to head there.

But how was I to find my way? I knew I had to travel southwest to reach my destination, but the sun was my only guide. I did not know the names of the towns I would pass through, nor could I ask anyone for directions, but I did not despair. From you alone could I hope for help, even though I felt nothing toward you but hatred. Unfeeling, heartless creator! You gave me perceptions and passions, then cast me out as an object for the scorn and horror of humanity."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The guilt originated with her; let her suffer the penalty! Instructed by Felix and the bloodthirsty statutes of humanity, I had mastered the art of causing harm. I leaned down and tucked the miniature firmly into the pleats of her garment. She stirred, and I retreated.

“For several days I lingered near the location of these events, at times desiring to encounter you, at others determined to abandon this world and its suffering forever. Eventually, I drifted toward these peaks, traversing their vast depths, devoured by an intense longing that only you can fulfill. We cannot separate until you vow to fulfill my request. I am solitary and wretched; humanity rejects my company; yet a creature as hideous and grotesque as myself would not turn away from me. My partner must belong to my own kind and possess identical flaws. You are obligated to fashion this being.”

Chapter 17

The creature ceased his address and stared at me, awaiting an answer. I felt dazed and confused, incapable of organizing my thoughts to grasp the full implications of his demand. He persisted,

“You are required to fashion a female for me, one with whom I might share the emotional connections essential to my existence. You are the only one capable of this, and I claim it as a prerogative you must not deny.”

The conclusion of his narrative reignited the fury that had faded while he described his tranquil existence with the cottagers, and as he spoke, I could no longer contain the wrath smoldering within me.

“I decline,” I answered; “and no agony will ever force my agreement. You may transform me into the most wretched of men, but you shall never force me to despise myself. Should I fashion another like you, whose combined malice might lay waste to the earth? Depart! I have given my response; you may torment me, but I will never yield.”

“You are mistaken,” the monster responded; “and rather than issue threats, I prefer to argue with you. I am spiteful because I am wretched. Am I not avoided and loathed by all humanity? You, my maker, would gladly tear me apart and rejoice; keep that in mind, and explain why I should show mercy to man when he shows none to me? You would not label it homicide if you could cast me into one of these glacial chasms to shatter the form you yourself constructed. Should I honor humanity when it condemns me? Let them offer me kindness, and instead of harm, I would shower them with every favor, weeping with thankfulness for their acceptance. But that is impossible; human perceptions are impassable walls between us. Still, mine shall not be the surrender of a groveling slave. I will avenge my wrongs; if I cannot evoke affection, I will instill terror, and I vow undying animosity toward you, my primary adversary and creator. Be warned; I will labor for your ruin, and I will not cease until I have devastated your soul, leaving you to lament the moment you were born.”

A demonic fury possessed him as he spoke; his features twisted into grimaces too ghastly for any person to witness; yet he soon regained his composure and continued—

“I meant to argue.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_018",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The crime originated with her; let her bear the punishment! Thanks to the lessons from Felix and the bloody laws of humanity, I had learned how to cause harm. I leaned over her and tucked the portrait securely into the folds of her dress. She stirred again, and I ran away.

“For several days, I haunted the place where these events occurred, sometimes wanting to see you, sometimes determined to leave the world and its miseries forever. Eventually, I wandered toward these mountains and roamed through their vast depths, consumed by a burning desire that only you can satisfy. We cannot part until you promise to fulfill my request. I am alone and miserable; humanity will not associate with me, but someone as deformed and hideous as myself would not reject me. My companion must be of the same species and share the same defects. You must create this being.”

Chapter 17

The creature finished speaking and stared at me, waiting for a reply. But I was bewildered, confused, and unable to organize my thoughts enough to grasp the full scope of his proposal. He continued,

“You must create a female for me with whom I can share the sympathies necessary for my existence. You alone can do this, and I demand it of you as a right that you must not refuse to grant.”

The latter part of his story had reignited the anger in me that had faded while he described his peaceful life among the cottagers, and as he said this, I could no longer suppress the rage burning inside me.

“I refuse,” I replied; “and no torture will ever force my consent. You may make me the most miserable of men, but you will never make me despise myself. Should I create another like you, whose combined wickedness might devastate the world? Leave! I have answered you; you may torture me, but I will never agree.”

“You are mistaken,” the fiend replied; “and instead of threatening, I am willing to reason with you. I am malicious because I am miserable. Am I not shunned and hated by all of humanity? You, my creator, would tear me to pieces and celebrate; remember that, and tell me why I should pity humanity more than it pities me? You would not call it murder if you could throw me into one of those ice-rifts and destroy my body, the work of your own hands. Should I respect humanity when it condemns me? Let it live with me in an exchange of kindness, and instead of injury, I would bestow every benefit upon it with tears of gratitude for its acceptance. But that cannot be; human senses are insurmountable barriers to our union. Yet mine will not be the submission of a slave. I will avenge my injuries; if I cannot inspire love, I will cause fear, and I swear inextinguishable hatred toward you, my arch-enemy and creator. Be careful; I will work toward your destruction and will not stop until I desolate your heart, so that you will curse the hour of your birth.”

A demonic rage animated him as he said this; his face twisted into contortions too horrible for human eyes to behold; but he soon calmed himself and continued—

“I intended to reason.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "A transformation had truly occurred within me; my physical well-being, which had previously been failing, was now significantly improved, and my mood, whenever it was not suppressed by the recollection of my wretched vow, lifted in equal measure. My father observed this shift with satisfaction, and he began to consider the most effective way to remove the lingering traces of my sadness, which would occasionally return in sudden bouts, casting a consuming darkness over the emerging light. During those intervals, I sought sanctuary in absolute isolation. I spent entire days by myself on the lake in a small skiff, observing the clouds and hearing the lapping of the water, quiet and lethargic. However, the crisp air and radiant sun rarely failed to bring me back to a state of relative calm, and upon my arrival home, I greeted my companions with a more spontaneous smile and a lighter spirit.

It was following my return from one of these excursions that my father, drawing me to the side, spoke to me in this manner:

“I am pleased to observe, my beloved son, that you have taken up your previous enjoyments and appear to be becoming your old self again. Nevertheless, you remain troubled and continue to shun our company. For a while, I was puzzled regarding the reason for this, but yesterday a thought occurred to me, and if it is accurate, I implore you to admit it. Secrecy regarding such a matter would not only be futile, but would bring triple the sorrow upon us all.”

I shook intensely at his opening remarks, and my father proceeded—

“I admit, my son, that I have always anticipated your wedding to our dear Elizabeth as the foundation of our family happiness and the support of my waning years. You were fond of one another from your earliest childhood; you learned together, and seemed, in temperaments and preferences, perfectly matched. Yet human foresight is so limited that what I believed to be the greatest aids to my intention may have completely ruined it. You might, perhaps, view her as a sister, without any desire for her to be your wife. Indeed, you may have encountered someone else whom you love; and viewing yourself as obligated by honor to Elizabeth, this conflict might be causing the intense distress you seem to suffer.”

“My dear father, set your mind at ease. I love my cousin with tenderness and sincerity. I have never met any woman who inspired, as Elizabeth does, my deepest admiration and devotion. My future aspirations and outlook are entirely centered on the hope of our marriage.”

“The articulation of your feelings on this matter, my dear Victor, brings me more joy than I have felt for quite some time. If you feel this way, we shall certainly be content, regardless of how current circumstances might shadow us. Yet it is this shadow that seems to have gripped your mind so firmly that I wish to dispel it. Inform me, therefore, if you have any objection to an immediate celebration of the wedding. We have suffered misfortune, and recent occurrences have diverted us from the daily serenity suitable for my age and frailty. You are younger; yet I do not imagine, provided as you are with a sufficient inheritance, that an early marriage would in any way hinder any future ambitions for honor and usefulness that you might have devised.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_019",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "A real change had occurred in me; my health, which had previously been failing, was now much improved, and my spirits, when not held back by the memory of my unfortunate promise, rose accordingly. My father observed this shift with pleasure and began considering the best way to remove the lingering traces of my melancholy, which would occasionally return in fits, casting a consuming darkness over my brightening mood. During those moments, I sought refuge in total isolation. I spent entire days alone on the lake in a small boat, watching the clouds and listening to the rippling water, silent and listless. However, the fresh air and bright sun rarely failed to restore some sense of calm, and upon my return, I greeted my friends with a readier smile and a lighter heart.

It was after coming back from one of these outings that my father called me aside and spoke to me:

“I am happy to notice, my dear son, that you have returned to your former interests and seem to be becoming yourself again. Yet, you remain unhappy and still avoid our company. For some time, I was lost in speculation as to the cause, but yesterday an idea occurred to me, and if it is correct, I urge you to admit it. Keeping quiet on such a matter would not only be useless but would bring triple the misery upon us all.”

I shook violently at his opening remarks, and my father continued—

“I admit, my son, that I have always looked forward to your marriage with our dear Elizabeth as the foundation of our family happiness and the support of my final years. You were attached to each other from your earliest childhood; you studied together and seemed, in temperament and interests, perfectly suited to one another. But human judgment is so blind that what I thought were the best factors for my plan may have actually ruined it. Perhaps you view her as a sister, without any desire for her to be your wife. Or perhaps you have met someone else you love, and, feeling bound by honor to Elizabeth, this internal conflict is causing the intense misery you seem to feel.”

“My dear father, please set your mind at ease. I love my cousin tenderly and sincerely. I have never met any woman who inspires such deep admiration and affection in me as Elizabeth does. My future hopes and prospects are entirely tied to the expectation of our marriage.”

“Hearing your feelings on this subject, my dear Victor, gives me more pleasure than I have felt in a long time. If you feel this way, we will certainly be happy, no matter how current events may cast a shadow over us. But it is this shadow that seems to have taken such a strong hold on your mind that I wish to clear away. Tell me, therefore, if you have any objection to an immediate wedding. We have been unfortunate, and recent events have pulled us away from the everyday peace suitable for my age and health. You are younger; yet I do not imagine, possessing a comfortable fortune as you do, that an early marriage would interfere with any future plans for success or service you may have made.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "We traveled to the burial site of the renowned Hampden and the battlefield where that patriot perished. Briefly, my spirit rose above its wretched and degrading anxieties to reflect upon the noble concepts of freedom and martyrdom that these locations commemorate. For a fleeting moment, I felt bold enough to cast aside my shackles and survey my surroundings with a liberated and noble outlook, yet the metal had scarred my skin, and I collapsed once more into my pathetic state, shaking and devoid of hope.

We departed Oxford with sorrow and traveled to Matlock, our subsequent stop. The landscape surrounding this village bore a resemblance to the vistas of Switzerland, though everything appeared on a more modest level, and the verdant hills lacked the crowning distant white Alps that invariably grace the pine-covered peaks of my homeland. We toured the remarkable cavern and the small displays of natural history, where the artifacts were arranged similarly to the exhibits in Servox and Chamounix. The mention of that latter name by Henry caused me to shudder, and I hurried to leave Matlock, as it was linked to that horrific event.

Moving north from Derby, we spent two months in Cumberland and Westmorland. I could almost imagine I was back among the Swiss peaks. The small drifts of snow still clinging to the northern slopes, the lakes, and the rushing of the stony brooks were all cherished and recognizable sights to me. In this place, we also formed friendships that nearly tricked me into feeling content. Clerval’s joy was correspondingly larger than my own; his intellect flourished among gifted individuals, and he discovered within himself greater potential and depth than he could have supposed possible while in the company of those less capable. “I could spend my days here,” he remarked to me; “and among these peaks, I would hardly miss Switzerland or the Rhine.”

Yet he discovered that the existence of a wanderer involves significant hardship alongside its pleasures. One’s emotions are constantly strained; just as one begins to settle into tranquility, one is compelled to abandon the source of that comfort for something novel, which then demands attention, only to be forsaken for further new experiences.

We had barely toured the diverse lakes of Cumberland and Westmorland and grown fond of some of the local residents when the time for our meeting with our Scottish acquaintance drew near, and we departed to continue our journey. Personally, I felt no regret. I had by then ignored my vow for quite some time, and I dreaded the consequences of the demon’s frustration. He might stay in Switzerland and unleash his retribution upon my kin. This thought haunted and plagued me during every interval when I might have otherwise found rest and calm. I awaited my correspondence with frantic eagerness; if the letters were late, I was wretched and besieged by countless terrors; and when they finally appeared, bearing the handwriting of Elizabeth or my father, I scarcely possessed the courage to open them and learn my destiny. At times, I imagined the monster trailed me and might hasten my negligence by slaying my traveling companion."
}

{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_020",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "We visited the tomb of the famous Hampden and the battlefield where that patriot died. For a moment, my soul was lifted above its degrading and miserable fears to reflect on the noble concepts of liberty and self-sacrifice that these sites commemorate. For an instant, I dared to cast off my chains and look around with a free and elevated spirit, but the iron had bitten into my flesh, and I sank back into my miserable self, trembling and hopeless.

We left Oxford with regret and traveled to Matlock, our next stop. The countryside near this village resembled the scenery of Switzerland to a degree, though everything is on a smaller scale, and the green hills lack the crowning distant white Alps that always accompany the pine-covered mountains of my homeland. We visited the remarkable cave and the small natural history displays, where the curiosities are arranged just like the collections in Servox and Chamonix. The latter name made me tremble when Henry spoke it, and I hurried to leave Matlock, which was now linked to that terrifying memory.

From Derby, continuing north, we spent two months in Cumberland and Westmorland. I could almost imagine myself among the Swiss mountains. The small patches of snow still lingering on the northern slopes, the lakes, and the rushing rocky streams were all familiar and dear sights to me. Here, we also made some acquaintances who almost managed to trick me into feeling happy. Clerval’s delight was even greater than mine; his mind expanded in the company of talented people, and he discovered in himself greater abilities and resources than he could have imagined while associating with his inferiors. “I could spend my life here,” he told me, “and among these mountains, I would hardly miss Switzerland or the Rhine.”

But he discovered that a traveler’s life involves as much pain as pleasure. His emotions are constantly strained, and just as he begins to settle into rest, he is forced to leave what he enjoys for something new, which captures his attention only to be abandoned for other novelties.

We had barely visited the various lakes of Cumberland and Westmorland and grown fond of some of the residents when the time for our meeting with our Scottish friend drew near, and we left to continue our journey. For my part, I was not sorry. I had neglected my promise for some time, and I feared the consequences of the demon’s disappointment. He might stay in Switzerland and take his revenge on my relatives. This thought haunted and tormented me during every moment I might otherwise have found peace. I waited for my letters with feverish impatience; if they were delayed, I was miserable and overwhelmed by a thousand fears. When they finally arrived and I saw the handwriting of Elizabeth or my father, I hardly dared to read them and learn my fate. Sometimes I thought the fiend was following me and might punish my delay by murdering my companion."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I exited the chamber and, after turning the key, swore a serious oath to myself that I would never return to my work; then, with shaking legs, I retreated to my own quarters. I was solitary; no one was nearby to scatter the shadows or lift the nauseating weight of my most horrifying thoughts.

Many hours went by, and I stayed by my window watching the ocean; it was nearly still, as the breezes had died down, and the entire world rested beneath the gaze of the tranquil moon. Only a handful of fishing boats dotted the surface, and occasionally a soft wind carried the murmurs of the fishermen hailing each other. I sensed the quiet, though I barely grasped its absolute depth, until my hearing was suddenly caught by the splashing of oars by the coast, and a figure stepped onto the land near my dwelling.

Moments later, I caught the groan of my door, as if someone were trying to unlatch it quietly. I shook from top to bottom; I had a foreboding of who it might be and wanted to wake one of the laborers who lived in a hut nearby; yet I was seized by that feeling of paralysis, so common in terrifying nightmares, when you try in vain to escape a looming threat, and I remained fixed where I stood.

Soon I caught the noise of pacing in the hallway; the door swung wide, and the creature I feared stood there. Closing the door, he drew near and spoke in a muffled tone,

“You have ruined the task you started; what is your plan? Do you truly intend to violate your word? I have suffered labor and agony; I departed Switzerland with you; I skulked along the banks of the Rhine, through its willow-covered isles and across the peaks of its mountains. I have lived for many months on the moors of England and in the wilds of Scotland. I have suffered immense exhaustion, freezing temperatures, and starvation; do you dare to crush my expectations?”

“Leave! I am breaking my word; I will never fashion another being like you, matching your ugliness and malice.”

“Slave, I once debated with you, but you have shown yourself undeserving of my patience. Keep in mind that I possess strength; you think you are unhappy, but I can render you so miserable that the sun will be loathsome to you. You are my maker, but I am your superior; submit!”

“The time for my hesitation has ended, and the moment for your authority has passed. Your menaces cannot sway me to commit an evil deed; instead, they solidify my resolve not to build you a partner in crime. Should I, in cold blood, unleash upon the world a fiend whose joy is found in slaughter and sorrow? Go! I am resolute, and your speech will only fuel my fury.”

The creature perceived my resolve in my expression and ground his teeth in the futility of his rage. “Must every man,” he shouted, “find a spouse for his heart, and every animal have its partner, while I remain solitary? I possessed capacity for love, and it was met with loathing and contempt. Human! You may despise me, but take heed! Your days will be spent in terror and grief, and soon the strike will land that must tear your joy away forever.”"
}

“You have ruined the task you started; what is your plan? Do you truly intend to violate your word? I have suffered labor and agony; I departed Switzerland with you; I skulked along the banks of the Rhine, through its willow-covered isles and across the peaks of its mountains. I have lived for many months on the moors of England and in the wilds of Scotland. I have suffered immense exhaustion, freezing temperatures, and starvation; do you dare to crush my expectations?”

“Leave! I am breaking my word; I will never fashion another being like you, matching your ugliness and malice.”

“Slave, I once debated with you, but you have shown yourself undeserving of my patience. Keep in mind that I possess strength; you think you are unhappy, but I can render you so miserable that the sun will be loathsome to you. You are my maker, but I am your superior; submit!”

“The time for my hesitation has ended, and the moment for your authority has passed. Your menaces cannot sway me to commit an evil deed; instead, they solidify my resolve not to build you a partner in crime. Should I, in cold blood, unleash upon the world a fiend whose joy is found in slaughter and sorrow? Go! I am resolute, and your speech will only fuel my fury.”

The creature perceived my resolve in my expression and ground his teeth in the futility of his rage. “Must every man,” he shouted, “find a spouse for his heart, and every animal have its partner, while I remain solitary? I possessed capacity for love, and it was met with loathing and contempt. Human! You may despise me, but take heed! Your days will be spent in terror and grief, and soon the strike will land that must tear your joy away forever.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I recoiled in disgust from the woman who could speak so heartlessly to someone who had just been rescued from the brink of death; yet, I felt too weak and exhausted to contemplate the events that had transpired. My entire existence seemed like a phantom; at times, I questioned if it were truly factual, as it lacked the weight of reality in my mind. As the visions drifting through my thoughts sharpened, I grew feverish; a suffocating gloom surrounded me, and there was no one nearby to comfort me with loving words or a supportive hand. The doctor arrived to order treatments, which the old woman prepared; however, the former showed complete indifference, while the latter’s face bore a distinctly cruel expression. Who could possibly care about the destiny of a killer, other than the executioner waiting for his payment?

These were my initial thoughts, though I eventually discovered that Mr. Kirwin had treated me with great benevolence. He had arranged for the finest room in the jail to be readied for me—though even the best was miserable—and he was the one who had secured the doctor and the nurse. Admittedly, he rarely visited, for while he sincerely wished to alleviate the suffering of all people, he preferred not to witness the torment and frantic ravings of a murderer. Consequently, he stopped by occasionally to ensure I was not being ignored, but his stays were brief and infrequent.

One day, as I was slowly mending, I sat in a chair with my eyes partially closed and my face pale as a corpse. I was consumed by sorrow and despair, frequently pondering whether it would be better to embrace death than to persist in a world filled with such misery for me. At one point, I contemplated confessing to the crime and accepting the legal punishment, even though I was less innocent than poor Justine had been. I was lost in these musings when the door to my room swung open and Mr. Kirwin walked in. His face showed pity and kindness; he pulled a chair near mine and spoke to me in French,

“I worry that this environment is very distressing for you; is there anything I can do to improve your situation?”

“I appreciate your offer, but your suggestions mean nothing to me; there is no solace on this earth that I am able to accept.”

“I realize that the compassion of a stranger offers little relief to someone burdened by such an unusual tragedy. Nevertheless, I hope you will soon leave this dismal place, as I am sure evidence will be found to clear you of this criminal accusation.”

“That is my smallest worry; through a series of bizarre occurrences, I have become the most wretched of men. Harassed and tormented as I am and have been, how could death be a misfortune for me?”

“Indeed, nothing could be more tragic or agonizing than the strange circumstances that have recently unfolded. You were cast upon this shore, known for its kindness, by some unexpected mishap, only to be immediately apprehended and accused of homicide. The first thing you beheld was the corpse of your companion, killed in such an inexplicable fashion and positioned, as if by some demon, directly in your way.”"
}

As the images in my mind became clearer, I grew feverish. A darkness closed in around me; there was no one nearby to comfort me with a loving voice, and no dear hand to support me. The doctor came to prescribe medicine, and the old woman prepared it, but the doctor seemed completely indifferent, and the woman’s face was marked by pure brutality. Who would care about the fate of a murderer, other than the executioner waiting for his payment?

These were my initial thoughts, but I soon learned that Mr. Kirwin had shown me great kindness. He had arranged for the best room in the prison to be prepared for me—though even the best was wretched—and he was the one who had provided the doctor and the nurse. It is true that he rarely visited, for although he genuinely wanted to relieve the suffering of every human being, he did not want to witness the agony and miserable ravings of a murderer. He came occasionally to ensure I was not neglected, but his visits were brief and infrequent.

One day, as I was slowly recovering, I sat in a chair with my eyes half-open and my cheeks pale as death. I was overcome by gloom and misery, often thinking it would be better to seek death than to remain in a world filled with such wretchedness for me. “I fear this place is very distressing for you; is there anything I can do to make you more comfortable?”

“I thank you, but none of that matters to me; there is no comfort on this entire earth that I am capable of receiving.”

“I know that the sympathy of a stranger can offer little relief to someone burdened as you are by such a strange misfortune. But I hope you will soon leave this melancholy place, for surely evidence can easily be found to clear you of the criminal charge.”

“That is my least concern; through a series of strange events, I have become the most miserable of mortals. Persecuted and tortured as I am, could death be anything but a relief?”

“Indeed, nothing could be more unfortunate and agonizing than the strange circumstances that have recently occurred. You were cast by some surprising accident onto this shore, which is known for its hospitality, only to be immediately seized and charged with murder.   "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "As dawn approached, a nightmare seized me; I imagined the monster’s fingers tightening around my throat, unable to break free, while wails and lamentations echoed in my hearing. My father, observing my agitation while he kept watch, roused me. The crashing sea surrounded us, the heavens were overcast, and the creature was absent. A fleeting sense of safety, a belief that a temporary peace existed between the current moment and the inevitable, tragic future, granted me a brief, tranquil oblivion—a state to which the human spirit is naturally prone.

Chapter 22

The sea voyage concluded. Upon landing, we traveled to Paris. I quickly realized my vigor was spent and that I required rest before proceeding. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Alas! While the miserable may find resignation, the wicked find no tranquility. The torments of guilt corrupt the comfort that might otherwise be discovered in surrendering to profound sorrow.

Shortly after I reached home, my father discussed my impending wedding to Elizabeth. I stayed mute.

“Do you possess some other affection?”

“None at all. I adore Elizabeth and anticipate our marriage with joy. Set the date, therefore; and upon that day, I shall dedicate my existence, for life or death, to my cousin’s joy.”

“My dear Victor, do not talk in such a way. Great tragedies have struck us, yet we must hold tighter to the survivors and shift our love for the departed to those still with us. Our group will be limited but tightly knit by bonds of affection and shared suffering. Once time has dulled your grief, fresh and precious responsibilities will emerge to fill the void left by those we have so harshly lost.”

These were my father’s teachings. Yet, the memory of the warning haunted me; you cannot be surprised that, given the demon’s absolute power in his murderous acts, I viewed him as nearly unstoppable, and when he declared, “I shall be with you on your wedding-night,” I considered the predicted outcome inevitable. Still, death seemed no tragedy if it meant losing Elizabeth, so I agreed with a satisfied and even bright expression that if my cousin agreed, the wedding should occur in ten days, thereby, as I supposed, finalizing my destiny.

Merciful God! Had I for a moment grasped the infernal purpose of my monstrous enemy, I would have exiled myself forever from my homeland and roamed the world as a lonely wanderer rather than agreeing to this wretched union. Yet, as if wielding sorcery, the creature had obscured his true designs from me; and while I believed I was merely arranging my own demise, I was accelerating the death of a much more precious victim.

As the date for our marriage approached, whether from fear or a sense of foreboding, my spirit faltered. I hid my emotions behind a facade of cheerfulness that delighted my father, though it barely fooled the observant and sensitive gaze of Elizabeth. She anticipated our marriage with calm satisfaction, tempered by a slight anxiety, born of past tragedies, that this seemingly certain happiness might vanish like a phantom, leaving behind only profound and eternal sorrow.

Arrangements for the ceremony were underway, well-wishers visited, and everything appeared joyful. I buried the dread consuming me deep within and participated with apparent sincerity in my father’s schemes, even though they might only function as the stage dressing for my own ruin. Through my father’s efforts, a portion of Elizabeth’s legacy had been returned to her by the Austrian authorities. A modest estate on the banks of Lake Como was now hers."
}

“None at all. I love Elizabeth and look forward to our union with joy. Let the day be set; on that day, I will dedicate myself, in life or death, to my cousin’s happiness.”

“My dear Victor, do not speak like that. We have suffered heavy losses, but let us cling closer to what remains and transfer our love for those we have lost to those who still live. Our circle will be small but bound by affection and shared misfortune. When time has softened your despair, new and dear objects of care will arrive to replace those we were so cruelly deprived of.”

Such were my father’s lessons. But the memory of the threat returned to me; you cannot wonder that, as powerful as the fiend had been in his bloodthirsty deeds, I regarded him as invincible. When he declared, “I shall be with you on your wedding-night,” I viewed that fate as unavoidable. Death was no evil to me if it meant losing Elizabeth, so I agreed with my father, with a cheerful face, that if my cousin consented, the ceremony should take place in ten days, effectively sealing my fate.

Great God! If I had for one instant considered the hellish intention of my fiendish adversary, I would have banished myself forever from my country and wandered as a friendless outcast rather than consent to this miserable marriage. But, as if possessing magic powers, the monster blinded me to his true intentions. While I thought I was only preparing for my own death, I was actually hastening the death of a far dearer victim.

As the date for our marriage approached, whether from cowardice or a prophetic feeling, my heart sank. I hid my feelings behind a mask of cheerfulness that brought smiles to my father, though it hardly deceived the watchful, perceptive eyes of Elizabeth. She looked forward to our union with calm contentment, mixed with a fear born of past misfortunes that this tangible happiness might soon vanish into a dream, leaving only deep and lasting regret.

Preparations were made, congratulatory visits were received, and everything appeared bright. I locked the anxiety preying on me deep within my heart and participated with seeming earnestness in my father’s plans, even though they served only as decorations for my tragedy. Through my father’s efforts, a portion of Elizabeth’s inheritance had been restored to her by the Austrian government. A small property on the shores of Lake Como now belonged to her."
}

I indicated the location where he had vanished, and we pursued the trail using vessels; we dragged the waters with nets, yet to no avail. Following several hours of effort, we came back without hope, as the majority of my associates assumed the figure was merely a phantom born of my imagination. Once we reached the shore, they began to scour the countryside, with groups venturing in various paths through the forests and vineyards.

I tried to join them and moved a brief distance from the dwelling, but my brain spun, my gait resembled that of an intoxicated person, and I eventually collapsed in total fatigue; a haze obscured my vision, and my flesh felt scorched by the intensity of a fever. In this condition, I was transported back and laid upon a bed, barely aware of the preceding events; my gaze drifted about the chamber as if searching for something I had misplaced.

After a time, I stood up and, guided by instinct, crept into the room where the body of my beloved rested. Women were sobbing nearby; I leaned over the remains and mingled my sorrowful tears with theirs; throughout this period, no clear concept formed in my mind, as my thoughts drifted to disparate topics, pondering vaguely on my calamities and their origins. I remained perplexed, trapped in a fog of astonishment and dread. The passing of William, the hanging of Justine, the slaying of Clerval, and finally that of my wife; even then, I remained unaware if my sole surviving companions were protected from the monster's malice; my father might at that very moment be suffering in his clutches, and Ernest might lie lifeless at his feet. This thought caused me to tremble and spurred me into motion. I jumped up, determined to hasten back to Geneva with the utmost urgency.

No horses were available, so I was forced to travel by way of the lake; yet the breeze was against us, and rain poured down in heavy sheets. Nevertheless, it was barely dawn, and I could reasonably expect to reach my destination by nightfall. I engaged men to row and grasped an oar myself, for I had consistently found solace from psychological anguish through physical labor. Yet the overwhelming wretchedness I experienced, combined with the extreme turbulence of my spirit, left me unable to perform any task. I dropped the oar, and resting my head in my hands, surrendered to every dark thought that surfaced. When I glanced up, I beheld landscapes that were known to me from my more joyful days and which I had viewed only the previous afternoon alongside her who was now merely a ghost and a memory. Tears flowed down my cheeks. The downpour paused briefly, and I watched fish darting in the depths as they had only hours earlier; they had been noticed then by Elizabeth. Nothing is as agonizing to the human spirit as a profound and abrupt transformation. The sun might beam or the skies might darken, but nothing could seem to me as it had appeared the day before. A monster had stolen from me every prospect of future joy; no being had ever endured such misery as mine; such a terrifying occurrence is unique in the annals of humanity."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "To achieve this end, I shall sustain my existence; to carry out this cherished retribution, I will once more gaze upon the sun and walk upon the verdant grass of the world, which would otherwise fade from my sight forever. I invoke you, ghosts of the departed, and you, roaming agents of retribution, to assist and guide me in my undertaking. May that damned and infernal creature swallow his fill of suffering; may he experience the hopelessness that currently plagues me.

I had commenced my solemn appeal with a gravity that nearly convinced me the spirits of my slain companions listened and sanctioned my dedication, yet fury seized me as I finished, and anger stifled my speech.

My words were met in the quiet of the night by a boisterous, demonic laugh. It reverberated in my ears with weight and duration; the peaks repeated the sound, and I felt as though the entire underworld encircled me with derision and mirth. Without a doubt, I would have succumbed to madness in that instant and ended my wretched life, had my oath not been acknowledged and had I not been kept for the sake of vengeance. The laughter faded, and a familiar, loathsome voice, seemingly right beside me, spoke in a clear whisper: “I am content, pathetic creature! You have resolved to survive, and I am satisfied.”

I lunged toward the location where the noise originated, but the demon slipped from my reach. Abruptly, the wide face of the moon ascended and illuminated his hideous, twisted form as he retreated with supernatural swiftness.

I chased him, and for numerous months, this has been my mission. Following a faint trail, I tracked the twists of the Rhone, but to no avail. The azure Mediterranean emerged, and by a curious coincidence, I observed the fiend board a ship under the cover of darkness, concealing himself on a vessel headed for the Black Sea. I secured passage on that same boat, yet he vanished, I know not how.

Throughout the wilderness of Tartary and Russia, though he consistently eluded me, I have perpetually pursued his path. Occasionally, villagers, terrified by this monstrous vision, alerted me to his direction; at other times, he himself—fearing that I might lose his trail and succumb to despair—left behind signs to lead me. Snow fell upon me, and I observed the impression of his massive footprint upon the white expanse. To you who are just beginning your journey, for whom worry is novel and suffering is foreign, how could you grasp what I have endured and continue to endure? Cold, deprivation, and weariness were the minor hardships I was fated to suffer; I was plagued by some demon and bore my own private hell with me. Nevertheless, a benevolent spirit accompanied and steered my path, and when I complained the most, it would suddenly deliver me from seemingly impossible obstacles. At times, when my body, overcome by starvation, collapsed from exhaustion, a meal was provided for me in the wilderness that revived and encouraged me. The food was certainly simple, the kind eaten by local peasants, yet I do not doubt it was placed there by the spirits I had called upon for help. Frequently, when the land was parched and the sky cloudless, and I was withered by thirst, a small cloud would darken the heavens, release the few drops that restored me, and then disappear."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_027",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "For this reason, I will keep myself alive. To carry out this precious revenge, I will look upon the sun again and walk on the green grass of the earth, which otherwise would disappear from my sight forever. I call upon you, spirits of the dead, and you, wandering agents of vengeance, to assist and guide me in my mission. Let that cursed and hellish monster drink deeply of agony; let him feel the despair that now torments me.

I had begun my plea with solemnity and an awe that almost convinced me the ghosts of my murdered friends heard and approved of my dedication, but fury took hold of me as I finished, and rage choked my voice.

I was answered through the silence of the night by a loud, demonic laugh. It rang in my ears, long and heavy; the mountains echoed it, and I felt as if all of hell surrounded me with mockery and laughter. Surely, in that moment, I would have been overcome by madness and ended my miserable existence, had my vow not been heard and had I not been reserved for vengeance. The laughter faded, and a well-known, detested voice, seemingly right next to my ear, spoke in a clear whisper: “I am satisfied, miserable wretch! You have decided to live, and I am satisfied.”

I lunged toward the spot where the sound came from, but the devil slipped from my grasp. Suddenly, the broad disk of the moon rose and shone directly upon his ghastly, distorted form as he fled with more than human speed.

I pursued him, and for many months, this has been my task. Guided by a small clue, I followed the winding Rhone, but in vain. The blue Mediterranean appeared, and by a strange coincidence, I saw the fiend enter a ship bound for the Black Sea at night to hide. I booked passage on the same vessel, but he escaped, I know not how.

Across the wilds of Tartary and Russia, although he still evaded me, I have always followed his trail. Sometimes the peasants, frightened by this horrific apparition, told me of his path; sometimes he himself, fearing that I would despair and die if I lost all trace of him, left some mark to guide me. Snow fell upon my head, and I saw the print of his huge step on the white plain. To you, just beginning life, to whom worry is new and agony unknown, how can you understand what I have felt and still feel? Cold, hunger, and fatigue were the least of the pains I was destined to endure; I was cursed by some devil and carried my own eternal hell with me. Yet, a spirit of good still followed and directed my steps, and when I complained the most, it would suddenly rescue me from seemingly impossible difficulties. Sometimes, when my body, overcome by hunger, collapsed from exhaustion, a meal was prepared for me in the wilderness that restored and energized me. The food was indeed coarse, the kind the local peasants ate, but I do not doubt it was placed there by the spirits I had invoked to help me. Often, when everything was dry, the sky cloudless, and I was parched with thirst, a small cloud would dim the sky, shed the few drops that revived me, and vanish."
}

Yet, a spirit of good still followed and directed my steps, and when I complained the most, it would suddenly pull me out of seemingly impossible difficulties. Sometimes, when my body, overcome by hunger, collapsed from exhaustion, a meal was prepared for me in the wilderness that restored and energized me. The food was simple, like what the local peasants ate, but I do not doubt that it was placed there by the spirits I had called upon to help me. Often, when everything was dry, the sky was cloudless, and I was parched with thirst, a small cloud would dim the sky, shed the few drops that revived me, and then vanish."
}

parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_027",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "To achieve this, I will keep myself alive. To carry out this precious revenge, I will look at the sun again and walk on the green grass of the earth, which otherwise would disappear from my eyes forever. I call upon you, spirits of the dead, and you, wandering agents of vengeance, to help and guide me in my task. Let that cursed and hellish monster drink deeply of agony; let him feel the despair that now torments me.

I had begun my plea with solemnity and an awe that almost convinced me the ghosts of my murdered friends heard and approved of my dedication, but fury took hold of me as I finished, and rage choked my voice.

I was answered through the silence of the night by a loud and fiendish laugh. It rang in my ears long and heavily; the mountains echoed it, and I felt as if all of hell surrounded me with mockery and laughter. Surely, in that moment, I would have been driven to madness and destroyed my miserable life, if not for the fact that my vow was heard and I was saved for vengeance. The laughter died away, and then a well-known and hated voice, apparently close to my ear, spoke to me in a clear whisper, “I am satisfied, miserable wretch! You have decided to live, and I am satisfied.”

I darted toward the spot where the sound came from, but the devil escaped my grasp. Suddenly, the broad disk of the moon rose and shone directly upon his ghastly and distorted shape as he fled with more than human speed.

I pursued him, and for many months this has been my task. Guided by a small clue, I followed the winding path of the Rhone, but in vain. The blue Mediterranean appeared, and by a strange chance, I saw the fiend enter a ship at night and hide himself on a vessel bound for the Black Sea. I booked passage on the same ship, but he escaped, I do not know how.

In the wilds of Tartary and Russia, although he still evaded me, I have always followed his trail. Sometimes the peasants, scared by this horrible apparition, told me which way he went; sometimes he himself, fearing that I would despair and die if I lost his trail, left some mark to guide me. Snow fell on my head, and I saw the print of his huge foot on the white plain. To you who are just starting life, to whom worry is new and agony unknown, how can you understand what I have felt and still feel? Cold, hunger, and fatigue were the least of the pains I was destined to endure; I was cursed by some devil and carried my own personal hell with me. Yet, a spirit of good still followed and directed my steps, and when I complained the most, it would suddenly pull me out of seemingly impossible difficulties. Sometimes, when my body, overcome by hunger, collapsed from exhaustion, a meal was prepared for me in the wilderness that restored and energized me. The food was simple, like what the local peasants ate, but I do not doubt that it was placed there by the spirits I had called upon to help me. Often, when everything was dry, the sky was cloudless, and I was parched with thirst, a small cloud would dim the sky, shed the few drops that revived me, and then vanish."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "In this fashion, many dreadful hours went by; a number of my dogs perished, and I was on the verge of collapsing from the weight of my suffering when I spotted your ship anchored, offering me the prospect of rescue and survival. I had no idea that ships ever ventured this far north and was stunned by the sight. I promptly broke apart a section of my sledge to fashion oars, and through this immense exertion, I managed to propel my ice raft toward your vessel. I had resolved that if you were traveling south, I would still commit myself to the mercy of the ocean rather than give up my goal. I hoped to persuade you to provide me with a boat so I could continue chasing my foe. However, you were heading north. You rescued me when my strength was spent, and I would have soon succumbed to my many trials, meeting a death I still fear, for my mission remains incomplete.

Oh! When will my guiding spirit, in leading me to the fiend, grant me the peace I crave so deeply; or must I perish while he continues to live? If I do, promise me, Walton, that he will not get away, that you will hunt him down and fulfill my need for retribution by killing him. Do I dare request that you take up my journey and endure the miseries I have faced? No; I am not that self-centered. Still, after I am gone, if he should show himself, if the agents of justice should lead him to you, swear that he shall not survive—swear that he will not prevail over my gathered sorrows and live on to increase his tally of dark deeds. He is articulate and convincing, and at one time his speech even held sway over my heart; but do not believe him. His spirit is as demonic as his appearance, brimming with deceit and devilish spite. Do not listen to him; invoke the names of William, Justine, Clerval, Elizabeth, my father, and the miserable Victor, and drive your blade into his chest. I will linger nearby and guide the steel to its mark.

Walton, continuing.

August 26th, 17—.

You have perused this bizarre and terrifying account, Margaret; and does your blood not run cold with dread, just as mine does even now? At times, overcome by sudden torment, he could not proceed with his story; at other moments, his voice, fractured yet sharp, struggled to articulate words so filled with pain. His handsome and expressive eyes were at times ignited with fury, then softened into dejected grief and dimmed by boundless misery. Occasionally he mastered his features and tone, recounting the most ghastly events in a calm voice, hiding any sign of distress; then, like an erupting volcano, his face would abruptly shift to a look of the most intense rage as he screamed curses at his tormentor.

His narrative is coherent and delivered with the appearance of absolute sincerity, yet I admit to you that the correspondence of Felix and Safie, which he displayed to me, and the sighting of the creature from our deck, provided me with a stronger belief in the reality of his tale than his declarations, however sincere and logical they seemed. Such a monster, then, truly exists!"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Addressing the crew, he asked, "What is your intent? What do you ask of your leader? Have you so quickly abandoned your mission? Did you not label this a magnificent voyage? And why was it magnificent? Not because the path was calm like a tropical ocean, but because it was fraught with peril and dread, because each new event demanded your resilience and bravery, because you were meant to face and conquer the dangers and death that encompassed us. That is what made this a noble and honorable pursuit. You were to be celebrated as saviors of humanity, your names revered as those of courageous men who faced mortality for honor and the common good. Yet now, at the first hint of risk—or, if you prefer, the first significant test of your mettle—you retreat, satisfied to be remembered as men lacking the stamina to withstand cold and hardship; poor, shivering souls who fled back to their comfortable hearths. Such a retreat requires no such elaborate preparation; you need not have traveled this far and forced your captain to endure the humiliation of failure simply to demonstrate your cowardice. Oh! Act like men, or even better than men. Remain steadfast in your intentions and immovable as stone. This ice is not as unyielding as your hearts; it is changeable and will succumb if you command it. Do not go home to your families bearing the mark of shame. Return as victors who have battled and triumphed, never knowing the meaning of retreat." 

He delivered these words with a tone perfectly tuned to the shifting emotions of his address, his gaze filled with such noble ambition and heroism, that it is no surprise his men were stirred. They exchanged glances, speechless. I intervened, instructing them to withdraw and contemplate his words, stating that I would not force them further north if they firmly opposed it, but that I trusted their resolve would return upon reflection. 

They withdrew, and I turned to my companion, who had fallen into a state of exhaustion, appearing nearly lifeless. 

I cannot foresee the outcome, yet I would prefer death to a shameful retreat with my mission incomplete. Still, I fear that will be my destiny; the crew, lacking any sense of honor or glory, cannot be expected to endure these trials willingly. 

September 7th. 

The decision is final; I have agreed to turn back unless we are destroyed. My aspirations are shattered by their timidity and hesitation; I return empty-handed and defeated. It takes more stoicism than I possess to endure this unfairness with grace. 

September 12th. 

It is over; I am heading back to England. My dreams of service and renown are gone; I have lost my friend. Nevertheless, I will attempt to recount these painful events to you, my dear sister; and as I am carried toward England and toward you, I will not lose heart. 

On September 9th, the ice began to shift, and distant sounds like thunder echoed as the floes fractured and splintered in every direction."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_029",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Turning toward the men, he said, "What do you mean? What do you demand of your captain? Are you so easily swayed from your mission? Did you not call this a glorious expedition? And why was it glorious? Not because the path was smooth and calm like a southern sea, but because it was filled with danger and terror, because every new event demanded your fortitude and courage, because danger and death surrounded it, and these you were to face and overcome. For this reason, it was a glorious and honorable undertaking. You were to be hailed as benefactors of your species, your names adored as those of brave men who faced death for honor and the benefit of humanity. And now, look, at the first thought of danger, or, if you prefer, the first mighty and terrifying test of your courage, you shrink away and are content to be remembered as men who lacked the strength to endure cold and peril; and so, poor souls, they were chilly and returned to their warm firesides. Why, that does not require this level of preparation; you did not need to come this far and drag your captain into the shame of defeat merely to prove yourselves cowards. Oh! Be men, or be more than men. Stay true to your purposes and firm as a rock. This ice is not made of the same material as your hearts; it is changeable and cannot withstand you if you declare that it shall not. Do not return to your families with the mark of disgrace on your brows. Return as heroes who have fought and conquered and who do not know what it is to turn their backs on the enemy."

He spoke this with a voice so modulated to the different emotions expressed in his speech, with an eye so full of lofty ambition and heroism, that can you wonder these men were moved? They looked at one another and were unable to reply. I spoke; I told them to retire and consider what had been said, that I would not lead them further north if they strongly desired the contrary, but that I hoped their courage would return with reflection.

They retired and I turned toward my friend, but he was sunk in exhaustion and almost lifeless.

How all this will end, I do not know, but I would rather die than return shamefully, my purpose unfulfilled. Yet I fear such will be my fate; the men, unsupported by ideas of glory and honor, can never willingly continue to endure their present hardships.

September 7th.

The die is cast; I have consented to return if we are not destroyed. Thus are my hopes blasted by cowardice and indecision; I come back ignorant and disappointed. It requires more philosophy than I possess to bear this injustice with patience.

September 12th.

It is over; I am returning to England. I have lost my hopes of utility and glory; I have lost my friend. But I will endeavor to detail these bitter circumstances to you, my dear sister; and while I am carried toward England and toward you, I will not despair.

September 9th, the ice began to move, and roarings like thunder were heard at a distance as the islands split and cracked in every direction."
}

How all this will end, I do not know, but I would rather die than return shamefully, my purpose unfulfilled. Yet I fear such will be my fate; the men, unsupported by ideas of glory and honor, can never willingly continue to endure their present hardships.

September 7th.

The die is cast; I have consented to return if we are not destroyed. Thus are my hopes blasted by cowardice and indecision; I come back ignorant and disappointed. It requires more philosophy than I possess to bear this injustice with patience.

September 12th.

It is over; I am returning to England. I have lost my hopes of utility and glory; I have lost my friend. But I will endeavor to detail these bitter circumstances to you, my dear sister; and while I am carried toward England and toward you, I will not despair.

September 9th, the ice began to move, and roarings like thunder were heard at a distance as the islands split and cracked in every direction."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "From that point on, wickedness served as my virtue. Driven to this extreme, I had no alternative but to align my character with the path I had voluntarily taken. Finishing my devilish plan turned into an unquenchable obsession. And now it is finished; there lies my final victim!”

Initially, I felt moved by his displays of sorrow; however, remembering Frankenstein’s warnings about his gift for rhetoric and persuasion, and looking once more at the dead body of my companion, my anger flared up again. “Monster!” I exclaimed. “It is convenient for you to arrive here and weep over the destruction you have caused. You set fire to a collection of structures, and once they have burned down, you sit amidst the debris and mourn their collapse. You deceitful demon! If the man you grieve were still alive, he would remain the target, he would again become the prey, of your wicked retribution. You feel no genuine compassion; you only lament because the target of your malice is no longer within your reach.”

“Oh, that is not the case—not at all,” the creature broke in. “Though that must be the conclusion you draw from the apparent intent of my deeds. Yet I do not ask for shared feelings in my wretchedness. I can never find any empathy. When I originally looked for it, I wanted to share the love of virtue, the sensations of joy and warmth that filled my entire existence. But now that virtue has faded into a ghost, and those joys and affections have transformed into harsh and sickening despair, where should I look for understanding? I am prepared to endure my pain in solitude for as long as it lasts; when I perish, I am perfectly content that hatred and shame should burden my legacy. My imagination was once comforted by visions of goodness, renown, and pleasure. I once mistakenly trusted that I would encounter individuals who, overlooking my physical appearance, would cherish me for the noble traits I was prepared to reveal. I was sustained by lofty ideas of integrity and loyalty. But now, wrongdoing has lowered me below the lowliest beast. No sin, no harm, no malice, no suffering can be compared to mine. When I review the terrifying list of my transgressions, I cannot accept that I am the same being whose mind was once occupied by grand and elevated dreams of the beauty and splendor of righteousness. Yet it is true; the fallen angel turns into a spiteful devil. Even that adversary of God and humanity had companions and allies in his isolation; I am entirely alone.

“You, who claim to be Frankenstein’s friend, appear to understand my offenses and his tragedies. But in the account he provided, he could not capture the years and months of agony I spent languishing in helpless rage. For while I ruined his prospects, I did not fulfill my own longings. They remained forever intense and hungry; I continued to crave love and companionship, and I was continually rejected. Was there no unfairness in this? Am I to be considered the sole offender, when all of humanity committed wrongs against me? Why do you not despise Felix, who cast his acquaintance from his home with insults?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_FRANKENSTEIN_030",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "From that point on, evil became my good. Driven this far, I had no choice but to adapt my nature to the path I had willingly chosen. Completing my demonic plan became an insatiable passion. And now it is finished; there lies my final victim!”

I was initially moved by his expressions of misery; yet, when I recalled what Frankenstein had said about his powers of eloquence and persuasion, and when I looked again at the lifeless body of my friend, my indignation was rekindled. “Wretch!” I said. “It is convenient that you come here to whine over the destruction you have caused. You throw a torch into a group of buildings, and when they are consumed, you sit among the ruins and lament their fall. Hypocritical fiend! If the man you mourn were still alive, he would still be the target, once again the prey, of your cursed vengeance. It is not pity that you feel; you only lament because the victim of your malice is now beyond your reach.”

“Oh, it is not like that—not like that,” the being interrupted. “Yet that must be the impression you get from the apparent purpose of my actions. I do not seek shared feelings in my misery. I can never find sympathy. When I first sought it, it was the love of virtue, the feelings of happiness and affection with which my whole being overflowed, that I wished to share. But now that virtue has become a shadow to me, and happiness and affection have turned into bitter, loathing despair, in what should I seek sympathy? I am content to suffer alone for as long as my suffering lasts; when I die, I am satisfied that hatred and disgrace should burden my memory. Once, my imagination was soothed by dreams of virtue, fame, and enjoyment. Once, I falsely hoped to meet beings who, forgiving my outward appearance, would love me for the excellent qualities I was capable of revealing. I was nourished by high thoughts of honor and devotion. But now, crime has degraded me below the lowest animal. No guilt, no mischief, no malice, no misery can be compared to mine. When I review the frightening list of my sins, I cannot believe I am the same creature whose thoughts were once filled with sublime and transcendent visions of the beauty and majesty of goodness. But it is true; the fallen angel becomes a malignant devil. Yet even that enemy of God and man had friends and associates in his desolation; I am alone.

“You, who call Frankenstein your friend, seem to have knowledge of my crimes and his misfortunes. But in the details he gave you, he could not sum up the hours and months of misery I endured, wasting away in helpless passions. For while I destroyed his hopes, I did not satisfy my own desires. They were forever burning and craving; I still desired love and companionship, and I was still rejected. Was there no injustice in this? Am I to be considered the only criminal, when all of humanity sinned against me? Why do you not hate Felix, who drove his friend from his door with insults?”"
}
