model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "With my thoughts entirely consumed by these notions, I felt as though spellbound, lingering near the future residence of the young Earl. I observed the ongoing renovations and remained near the unloading carts as various luxury items, transported from London, were removed and carried into the estate. It was the former Queen’s intention to envelop her son in princely opulence. I witnessed luxurious rugs and silk draperies, golden decorations, intricately worked metals, ornate furnishings, and every accessory of high status being positioned so that only regal splendor would meet the eyes of one born of royal lineage. I observed these things, then shifted my attention to my own humble attire. What was the source of this disparity? It stemmed from nothing but betrayal, deceit, and a complete abandonment of noble empathy and kindness by the prince’s father. Surely, he too, whose veins carried the mingled blood of his haughty mother—he, the recognized center of the nation’s riches and aristocracy—had been instructed to utter my father’s name with contempt and to mock my legitimate requests for support. I attempted to convince myself that all this magnificence was merely a more obvious disgrace, and that by positioning his gold-threaded standard next to my worn and ragged flag, he was demonstrating not his greatness, but his moral decay. Nevertheless, I felt envious. His stable of fine horses, his expensive weaponry, the acclaim he received, the adoration, the eager attendants, his elevated status and high reputation—I viewed these as things violently stolen from me, and I envied them all with a fresh and agonizing resentment.

To add to my mental distress, Perdita, the idealistic Perdita, appeared to come alive with excitement when she informed me that the Earl of Windsor was soon to arrive.

“And this brings you joy?” I remarked, sullenly.

“It truly does, Lionel,” she answered; “I am quite eager to see him; he is the heir to our monarchs, the foremost nobleman in the country: everyone admires and cherishes him, and they claim that his status is his least significant virtue; he is noble, courageous, and kind.”

“You have mastered a fine lesson, Perdita,” I stated, “and you recite it so faithfully that you overlook the actual evidence we possess regarding the Earl’s character; his generosity toward us is evident in our abundance, his courage in the security he provides, his kindness in the attention he grants us. His status is his least significant virtue, you claim? In truth, all his virtues stem solely from his position; because he is wealthy, he is labeled generous; because he is influential, courageous; because he is well-attended, he is kind. Let them label him so, let all of England believe him to be such—we know the truth—he is our adversary—our stingy, cowardly, haughty adversary; if he possessed even a fraction of the virtues you attribute to him, he would treat us fairly, if only to demonstrate that if he must attack, it should not be against a defeated opponent. His father wronged my father—his father, secure upon his throne, dared to scorn the man who only lowered himself when he chose to associate with that royal ingrate. We, the descendants of the one and the other, are destined to be enemies as well. He shall discover that I am capable of feeling my wrongs; he shall learn to fear my retribution!”"
}

I tried to convince myself that all this grandeur was merely a more glaring disgrace, and that by planting his gold-woven flag beside my tarnished and tattered banner, he was proclaiming not his superiority, but his own degradation. Yet, I envied him. His stable of beautiful horses, his weapons of costly craftsmanship, the praise he received, the adoration, the ready servants, his high status and high esteem—I viewed them all as things forcibly stolen from me, and I envied them with a new and tormenting bitterness.

To add to my frustration, Perdita, the idealistic Perdita, seemed to wake up to reality with excitement when she told me that the Earl of Windsor was about to arrive.

“And this pleases you?” I remarked, moodily.

“It truly does, Lionel,” she replied. “I really look forward to seeing him. He is the descendant of our kings, the highest noble in the land. Everyone admires and loves him, and they say that his rank is his least significant merit; he is generous, brave, and friendly.”

“You have learned your lesson well, Perdita,” I said, “and you repeat it so word-for-word that you forget the actual evidence we have of the Earl’s virtues. His generosity to us is obvious in our abundance, his bravery in the protection he provides, and his friendliness in the attention he pays us. His rank is his least merit, you say? Why, all his virtues are derived solely from his position. Because he is rich, he is called generous; because he is powerful, brave; because he is well-served, he is friendly. Let them call him that; let all of England believe it—we know him. He is our enemy—our stingy, cowardly, arrogant enemy. If he possessed even a fraction of the virtues you attribute to him, he would treat us justly, if only to show that if he must strike, it should not be against a fallen foe. His father wronged my father—his father, secure on his throne, dared to despise the man who only lowered himself when he deigned to associate with that royal ingrate. We, the descendants of the one and the other, must be enemies as well. He will find that I can feel my injuries; he will learn to fear my revenge!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The penmanship was remarkably elegant; even I, who lacked affection for her and possessed little expertise in such things, could recognize the refinement in her stationery and how it was folded. Her tone conveyed significant warmth, appreciation, and gentleness, yet lacked romantic passion. Evadne was two years Adrian’s senior, and at eighteen, who truly falls for someone so much younger? I contrasted her calm letters with Adrian’s fervent ones. His spirit seemed to pour into his writing, the pages pulsing with the vitality of the love that defined his existence. The act of writing drained him, and he would often cry over the letters, overwhelmed by the intensity of the feelings they stirred within him.

Adrian’s character was reflected in his face, and he was far too transparent to ever practice deception. Evadne insisted that their romance remain hidden from his mother, and after some resistance, he eventually agreed. It was a futile promise; his behavior soon gave the secret away to the observant former queen. With the cautious discretion that defined her, she kept her knowledge to herself but moved quickly to distance her son from the captivating Greek woman. He was dispatched to Cumberland, yet the secret communication channel Evadne had established remained undetected. Consequently, the separation intended to drive them apart only strengthened their connection. To me, he spoke constantly of his beloved Ionian. Her homeland, its history, and its recent conflicts were all imbued with her brilliance and virtue. He accepted his exile only because she demanded it; otherwise, he would have proclaimed his devotion to all of England and defied his mother’s disapproval with unwavering resolve. Evadne’s feminine caution recognized that asserting his intentions would be pointless until he was older and more influential. Perhaps she also harbored a secret reluctance to publicly commit to someone she did not love—at least, not with the fiery intensity she believed she might one day experience with someone else. He followed her instructions and spent a year in Cumberland.

CHAPTER III.

Those months, weeks, and hours were exceptionally joyful. Friendship, accompanied by admiration, affection, and esteem, created a sanctuary of happiness within my heart, which had previously been as wild and uncultivated as a desolate landscape or a barren sea. A relentless hunger for learning and deep devotion to Adrian kept my mind and spirit fully engaged, ensuring my contentment. What joy is more genuine and serene than the exuberant, candid delight of youth? Whether we were in our boat on my home lake, near the streams and the silver poplars, or wandering through valleys and hills, I set aside my shepherd’s staff to tend to a more significant flock—a collection of emerging ideas. I would read or listen to Adrian, and his words, whether regarding his romance or his philosophies for human progress, completely captivated me."
}

{

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The handwriting was exquisite; there was something about the paper and the way it was folded that even I, who did not love her and was generally unskilled in such things, could recognize as tasteful. Her tone was full of kindness, gratitude, and sweetness, but lacked any real love. Evadne was two years older than Adrian, and who, at eighteen, ever falls for someone so much younger? I compared her calm letters with Adrian’s intense ones. His soul seemed to pour into his words; they breathed on the page, carrying a piece of the vital love that defined his existence. The act of writing often exhausted him, and he would weep over the letters simply from the overwhelming emotion they stirred in his heart.

Adrian’s soul was written on his face, and concealment or deceit were the complete opposites of his fearless, open nature. Evadne earnestly requested that their romance be kept from his mother, and after arguing for a while, he gave in. It was a futile concession; his behavior quickly revealed his secret to the sharp eyes of the former queen. With the cautious prudence that defined her character, she hid her discovery but moved quickly to remove her son from the influence of the attractive Greek woman. He was sent to Cumberland, but the plan for the lovers to correspond, which Evadne had arranged, remained hidden from her. Thus, Adrian’s absence, intended to separate them, only bound them together more tightly. He spoke to me constantly of his beloved Ionian. Her country, its ancient history, and its recent, memorable struggles were all woven into his view of her glory and excellence. He accepted being away from her only because she demanded it; otherwise, he would have declared his feelings to all of England and resisted his mother’s opposition with unwavering resolve. Evadne’s feminine caution recognized how useless any public declaration would be until he was older and had more authority. Perhaps there was also a hidden reluctance to commit herself publicly to someone she did not love—at least, not with the passionate enthusiasm she felt she might one day experience for someone else. He followed her instructions and spent a year in exile in Cumberland.

CHAPTER III.

Happy, thrice happy, were the months, weeks, and hours of that year. Friendship, joined with admiration, tenderness, and respect, built a sanctuary of delight in my heart, which had previously been as rough as an untrodden American wilderness, as wild as the wind or a barren sea. An insatiable thirst for knowledge and boundless affection for Adrian kept both my heart and mind occupied, and I was, as a result, happy. What happiness is as genuine and clear as the overflowing, talkative joy of young people? In our boat on my native lake, beside the streams and the pale poplars—in the valley and over the hills, with my shepherd’s crook cast aside, tending a nobler flock than foolish sheep, specifically a flock of new-born ideas—I read or listened to Adrian. His conversation, whether it concerned his love or his theories for the improvement of humanity, always entranced me."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Several months after I had come back to England, I left London one day to go see my sister. Her company was my primary comfort and joy, and my mood invariably lifted at the prospect of meeting her. Her talk was filled with sharp observations and insight; within her delightful garden nook, fragrant with the most aromatic blossoms and decorated with impressive plaster casts, ancient urns, and her own reproductions of masterpieces by Raphael, Correggio, and Claude, I imagined I had found an enchanted sanctuary, untouched and unreachable by the clamorous disputes of statesmen or the shallow interests of the fashionable world. On this particular visit, my sister was not by herself, and I immediately knew her guest: it was Idris, the woman who had been the unseen focus of my intense, obsessive devotion.

With what appropriate words of awe and pleasure, with what select phrasing and gentle cadence, can I introduce the most beautiful, intelligent, and virtuous of women? How can a meager collection of terms capture the radiant glow that encircled her, or the countless charms that attended her constantly? The initial impression upon seeing that lovely face was one of absolute kindness and sincerity; honesty rested on her forehead, artlessness in her gaze, and divine warmth in her smile. Her slender, tall form swayed with the grace of a poplar in a western breeze, and her movement, like that of a goddess, resembled a winged angel recently descended from the heavens; the pearly pale tone of her skin was flushed with a pure tint, and her tone of voice was like the soft, muted melody of a flute. It is perhaps simplest to explain by way of comparison. I have described my sister’s virtues, yet she was entirely different from Idris. Perdita, even when she felt affection, remained guarded and shy; Idris was open and trusting. The former withdrew into isolation to protect herself from hurt and sorrow, while the latter moved through the world in the light of day, convinced that no one would cause her harm. Wordsworth once likened a beloved woman to two beautiful things in nature, though his verses always struck me as a contrast rather than a likeness:

A violet by a mossy stone
 Half hidden from the eye,
Fair as a star when only one
 Is shining in the sky.

Such a violet was the sweet Perdita, hesitant to expose herself to the breeze, shrinking from notice yet revealed by her own merits, and rewarding those who discovered her in her secluded path with a thousand graces. Idris was like that star, positioned in solitary brilliance within the soft crown of a mild evening; prepared to illuminate and please the world below, protected from every impurity by her immense distance from all that was not, like her, related to the divine.

I discovered this vision of loveliness in Perdita’s alcove, engaged in serious discussion with its occupant. When my sister spotted me, she stood up, took my hand, and remarked, “He has arrived, just as we hoped; this is Lionel, my brother.” Idris stood as well, turned her sky-blue eyes toward me, and with a unique elegance stated, “You scarcely require an introduction; we possess a portrait, greatly treasured by my father, which identifies you at once. Verney, you will recognize this connection, and as my brother’s friend, I feel I can rely upon you.”

Then, with eyes moist with tears and a quivering voice, she went on, “Dear friends, do not consider it odd that now, upon my first visit to you, I seek your help and share my hopes and anxieties with you.”"
}

Her company was my primary comfort and joy, and my mood invariably lifted at the prospect of meeting her. Her talk was filled with sharp observations and insight; within her delightful garden nook, fragrant with the most aromatic blossoms and decorated with impressive plaster casts, ancient urns, and her own reproductions of masterpieces by Raphael, Correggio, and Claude, I imagined I had found an enchanted sanctuary, untouched and unreachable by the clamorous disputes of statesmen or the shallow interests of the fashionable world. On this particular visit, my sister was not by herself, and I immediately knew her guest: it was Idris, the woman who had been the unseen focus of my intense, obsessive devotion.

With what appropriate words of awe and pleasure, with what select phrasing and gentle cadence, can I introduce the most beautiful, intelligent, and virtuous of women? How can a meager collection of terms capture the radiant glow that encircled her, or the countless charms that attended her constantly? The initial impression upon seeing that lovely face was one of absolute kindness and sincerity; honesty rested on her forehead, artlessness in her gaze, and divine warmth in her smile. Her slender, tall form swayed with the grace of a poplar in a western breeze, and her movement, like that of a goddess, resembled a winged angel recently descended from the heavens; the pearly pale tone of her skin was flushed with a pure tint, and her tone of voice was like the soft, muted melody of a flute. It is perhaps simplest to explain by way of comparison. I have described my sister’s virtues, yet she was entirely different from Idris. Perdita, even when she felt affection, remained guarded and shy; Idris was open and trusting. The former withdrew into isolation to protect herself from hurt and sorrow, while the latter moved through the world in the light of day, convinced that no one would cause her harm. Wordsworth once likened a beloved woman to two beautiful things in nature, though his verses always struck me as a contrast rather than a likeness:

A violet by a mossy stone
 Half hidden from the eye,
Fair as a star when only one
 Is shining in the sky.

Such a violet was the sweet Perdita, hesitant to expose herself to the breeze, shrinking from notice yet revealed by her own merits, and rewarding those who discovered her in her secluded path with a thousand graces. Idris was like that star, positioned in solitary brilliance within the soft crown of a mild evening; prepared to illuminate and please the world below, protected from every impurity by her immense distance from all that was not, like her, related to the divine.

I discovered this vision of loveliness in Perdita’s alcove, engaged in serious discussion with its occupant. When my sister spotted me, she stood up, took my hand, and remarked, “He has arrived, just as we hoped; this is Lionel, my brother.” Idris stood as well, turned her sky-blue eyes toward me, and with a unique elegance stated, “You scarcely require an introduction; we possess a portrait, greatly treasured by my father, which identifies you at once. Verney, you will recognize this connection, and as my brother’s friend, I feel I can rely upon you.”

Then, with eyes moist with tears and a quivering voice, she went on, “Dear friends, do not consider it odd that now, upon my first visit to you, I seek your help and share my hopes and anxieties with you.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "His voice faltered as he spoke, then he lowered his head and covered his face with his hands, sobbing. Although I was still stung by my own setbacks, this display weighed upon me until I felt terrified, and I could not bring myself to break his outburst. Eventually, it faded; he collapsed onto the sofa, remaining quiet and still, though his shifting expressions revealed a deep inner struggle. Finally, he stood and remarked in his customary tone, “Time is pressing, Verney; I must depart. Let me not neglect my primary purpose for coming here. Will you join me at Windsor tomorrow? You will suffer no disgrace by my company, and since this is likely the final favor, or disfavor, you can perform for me, will you agree to my petition?”

He extended his hand with a somewhat shy demeanor. I reflected quickly—Yes, I shall observe the final act of this play. Furthermore, his manner overcame me, and a sense of affection for him returned to my heart; I told him to command me. “Indeed, I shall,” he replied lightheartedly, “that is my role now; meet me tomorrow morning at seven; be discreet and loyal; and you shall be my chief attendant before long.”

With those words, he hurried off, leaped onto his horse, and with a motion as if offering his hand for me to kiss, he gave me another laughing farewell. Once alone, I struggled with agonizing focus to fathom the reason for his request and to anticipate the happenings of the next day. The time slipped by unnoticed; my head throbbed from contemplation, my nerves feeling strained by the heavy burden—I pressed my scorching forehead, as if my feverish hand could soothe the ache. I arrived promptly at the designated time the next morning and discovered Lord Raymond expecting me. We entered his carriage and traveled toward Windsor. I had prepared myself, determined not to reveal my inner turmoil through any external sign.

“What an error Ryland committed,” Raymond remarked, “when he attempted to overwhelm me the other evening. He spoke effectively, quite effectively; such a speech would have been more successful if delivered to me privately, rather than to the idiots and scoundrels gathered there. Had I been by myself, I might have listened to him with a desire to be reasonable, but when he tried to defeat me on my own ground, using my own tactics, he challenged my spirit, and the outcome was exactly what anyone might have foreseen.”

I offered a skeptical smile and answered: “I share Ryland’s perspective, and if you wish, I will recount all his points; we shall see to what extent you might be persuaded by them to trade your royal style for a patriotic one.”

“Repeating them would be pointless,” Raymond stated, “since I recall them clearly, and I possess many others, of my own devising, which carry undeniable conviction.”

He offered no further explanation, and I made no comment on his response. We remained silent for several miles, until the landscape of open fields, shaded forests, and parks offered pleasant sights to behold. After a few remarks regarding the scenery and estates, Raymond observed: “Philosophers have termed man a microcosm of nature, discovering a reflection within the human mind for all the mechanisms visibly operating around us.”"
}

  "rewritten_text": "He spoke with a shaky voice, then bowed his head and, hiding his face in his hands, wept. I was still stinging from my own disappointment, yet this scene overwhelmed me to the point of terror, and I could not interrupt his outburst. It eventually subsided; he threw himself onto the couch and remained silent and motionless, though his shifting expressions revealed a deep internal struggle. At last, he rose and said in his normal tone, “Time is running out, Verney; I must go. Let me not forget my main reason for being here. Will you join me at Windsor tomorrow? You will not be disgraced by my company, and since this is likely the last favor—or disfavor—you can do for me, will you grant my request?”

He held out his hand with an almost shy air. I thought quickly—Yes, I will witness the final act of this drama. Furthermore, his demeanor won me over, and a feeling of affection for him filled my heart once more. I told him to command me. “Yes, I will,” he said cheerfully, “that is my role now; meet me tomorrow morning by seven; be discreet and loyal, and you shall be my right-hand man before long.”

With that, he hurried away, vaulted onto his horse, and with a gesture as if offering his hand for me to kiss, bid me another laughing farewell. Left alone, I struggled with painful intensity to figure out the motive for his request and to foresee the events of the coming day. The hours passed unnoticed; my head ached with thought, my nerves feeling overloaded. I pressed my burning forehead, as if my fevered hand could soothe the pain. I was punctual at the appointed hour the next day and found Lord Raymond waiting for me. We got into his carriage and headed toward Windsor. I had prepared myself, determined not to reveal my inner agitation through any outward sign.

“What a mistake Ryland made,” Raymond said, “when he thought he could overpower me the other night. He spoke well, very well; such a speech would have succeeded better if addressed to me alone, rather than to the fools and knaves gathered there. Had I been by myself, I would have listened to him with a desire to hear reason, but when he tried to defeat me on my own turf, using my own weapons, he challenged my pride, and the outcome was exactly what anyone might have expected.”

I smiled skeptically and replied, “I agree with Ryland, and if you like, I will repeat all his arguments; we shall see how far you are persuaded by them to trade your royal style for a patriotic one.”

“Repeating them would be useless,” Raymond said, “since I remember them well, and I have many others, which I thought of myself, that speak with undeniable persuasion.”

He did not explain himself, nor did I comment on his reply. We sat in silence for several miles, until the countryside, with its open fields and shady woods and parks, offered pleasant sights to view. After a few observations about the scenery and estates, Raymond said, “Philosophers have called man a microcosm of nature, finding a reflection in the human mind for all the machinery visibly at work around us.”"
}

At last, he rose and said in his normal tone, “Time is running out, Verney; I must go. Let me not forget my main reason for being here. Will you join me at Windsor tomorrow? You will not be disgraced by my company, and since this is likely the last favor—or disfavor—you can do for me, will you grant my request?”

He held out his hand with an almost shy expression. I thought quickly: Yes, I will witness the final act of this drama. Furthermore, his manner won me over, and a feeling of affection for him filled my heart once more. I told him to command me. “Yes, I will,” he said cheerfully, “that is my role now. Meet me tomorrow morning by seven; be secret and faithful, and you shall be a high-ranking official before long.”

With that, he hurried away, jumped onto his horse, and with a gesture as if offering his hand for me to kiss, he gave me another laughing goodbye. Left alone, I tried with painful intensity to guess the motive for his request and to foresee the events of the coming day. The hours passed unnoticed; my head ached with thought, and my nerves felt overwhelmed. I pressed my burning forehead, as if my fevered hand could soothe the pain. I was punctual the next morning and found Lord Raymond waiting for me. We got into his carriage and headed toward Windsor. I had prepared myself, determined not to show any outward sign of my inner agitation.

“What a mistake Ryland made,” Raymond said, “when he thought he could overpower me the other night. He spoke well, very well; such a speech would have succeeded better if addressed to me alone, rather than to the fools and knaves gathered there. Had I been alone, I would have listened to him with a desire to hear reason, but when he tried to defeat me on my own ground, using my own weapons, he challenged me, and the result was what everyone should have expected.”

I smiled skeptically and replied, “I agree with Ryland, and if you like, I will repeat all his arguments. We shall see how far they persuade you to trade your royal style for a patriotic one.”

“Repeating them would be useless,” Raymond said, “since I remember them well, and I have many others of my own that are impossible to argue against.”

He did not explain himself, and I made no comment on his reply. We remained silent for several miles, until the countryside, with its open fields and shady woods, offered pleasant sights. After a few remarks about the scenery and the estates, Raymond said, “Philosophers have called man a miniature version of nature, finding a reflection in the human mind for all the machinery visibly at work around us.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "How could I possibly suggest to this noble person, “Keep me in a state of sloth. You, who have devoted your intellectual gifts and wealth to the welfare of humanity, would you truly squander your efforts by sustaining a healthy, capable man in such unproductive idleness?”

Still, I lacked the courage to ask him to leverage his status so I might secure a respectable living for myself—for that would have necessitated my departure from Windsor. I lingered constantly near the Castle ramparts and beneath its shaded groves; my only company consisted of my volumes and my affectionate reflections. I examined the insights of the ancients and stared at the joyful walls that protected the one I adored. My intellect, however, remained stagnant. I pored over classical poetry; I explored the philosophical theories of Plato and Berkeley. I perused the chronicles of Greece, Rome, and England’s past, all while observing the movements of the woman I loved. At night, I could discern her silhouette against the walls of her room; by day, I watched her in her garden or riding through the park with her usual associates. I felt the spell would shatter if I were discovered, yet hearing the melody of her voice brought me joy. I attributed her beauty and peerless virtues to every heroine I read about—she was Antigone, leading the sightless Oedipus to the Eumenides’ grove and performing the burial rites for Polynices; she was Miranda in Prospero’s secluded cave; she was Haidee upon the shores of the Ionian island. I was delirious with intense, passionate devotion; yet a pride as fierce as flame possessed me, keeping me from revealing my feelings through any gesture or utterance.

Meanwhile, as I indulged in these opulent mental feasts, a common laborer would have scorned the meager sustenance I occasionally pilfered from the forest squirrels. I admit I was frequently tempted to return to the illicit habits of my youth and strike down the nearly tame pheasants that sat in the trees, fixing their bright gazes upon me. Yet they belonged to Adrian and were the charges of Idris; thus, although my hunger-sharpened imagination suggested they would be better suited for my kitchen spit than for the forest foliage, I nevertheless restrained my arrogant impulses and refrained from eating, choosing instead to dine on sentiment and dream fruitlessly of the sweet morsels I could not obtain while awake.

Yet, at this juncture, the entire structure of my life was on the verge of transformation. The neglected, orphaned son of Verney was about to be connected to the social order by a golden link and to participate in all the responsibilities and bonds of existence. Miracles were to be performed on my behalf, with the gears of society pushed backward through immense effort. Listen, reader, as I recount this marvelous story!

One afternoon, as Adrian and Idris rode through the woods with their mother and their usual companions, Idris pulled her brother away from the group and abruptly inquired, “What has become of your friend, Lionel Verney?”

“You can see his home from this very spot,” Adrian answered, gesturing toward my sister’s cottage."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_005",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "How could I say to this generous person, “Support me in idleness. You who have dedicated your intellect and fortune to the benefit of humanity, should you so misdirect your efforts as to maintain a strong, healthy, and capable man in uselessness?”

And yet I dared not ask him to use his influence so that I might obtain an honorable living for myself—for then I would have been forced to leave Windsor. I hovered constantly around the walls of its castle and beneath its shaded thickets; my only companions were my books and my loving thoughts. I studied the wisdom of the ancients and gazed at the happy walls that sheltered the beloved of my soul. My mind was nonetheless idle. I pored over the poetry of old times; I studied the metaphysics of Plato and Berkeley. I read the histories of Greece and Rome, and of England’s past, while watching the movements of the lady of my heart. At night I could see her shadow on the walls of her apartment; by day I watched her in her flower garden or riding in the park with her usual companions. I thought the charm would be broken if I were seen, but I heard the music of her voice and was happy. I gave each heroine I read about her beauty and matchless excellence—such was Antigone when she guided the blind Oedipus to the grove of the Eumenides and performed the funeral rites for Polynices; such was Miranda in the unvisited cave of Prospero; such was Haidee on the sands of the Ionian island. I was mad with an excess of passionate devotion, but a pride as untamable as fire defined my nature and prevented me from betraying myself by word or look.

In the meantime, while I pampered myself with such rich mental feasts, a peasant would have disdained my meager fare, which I sometimes stole from the squirrels of the forest. I admit I was often tempted to return to the lawless habits of my boyhood and knock down the almost tame pheasants that perched in the trees, fixing their bright eyes on me. But they were the property of Adrian, the nurslings of Idris; and so, although my imagination, made sensual by deprivation, suggested they would be better suited for the spit in my kitchen than the green leaves of the forest, I nevertheless checked my haughty will and did not eat, but supped upon sentiment and dreamt in vain of such sweet morsels as I could not attain while awake.

But at this period, the entire scheme of my existence was about to change. The orphan and neglected son of Verney was on the verge of being linked to the mechanism of society by a golden chain and entering into all the duties and affections of life. Miracles were to be performed in my favor, the machine of social life pushed with vast effort backward. Attend, reader, while I narrate this tale of wonders!

One day as Adrian and Idris were riding through the forest with their mother and their usual companions, Idris drew her brother aside from the rest of the group and suddenly asked him, “What has become of your friend, Lionel Verney?”

“Even from this spot,” replied Adrian, pointing to my sister’s cottage, “you can see his home.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "On other occasions, we undertook journeys lasting several days, traversing the land to see locations celebrated for their scenery or historical significance. Occasionally, we traveled to London to participate in the diversions of the bustling crowds, or our seclusion was interrupted by guests arriving from that sphere. Such shifts only heightened our appreciation for the pleasures of our private company, the serenity of our sacred woods, and the joyful nights spent within the chambers of our cherished Castle.

Idris possessed a character that was notably candid, gentle, and loving. Her disposition remained consistently pleasant; while she stood firm and determined regarding matters close to her heart, she was accommodating toward those she held dear. Perdita’s nature was perhaps less flawless, yet joy and affection refined her temperament and lessened her inherent reticence. She possessed a sharp and broad intellect alongside a lively imagination; she was honest, magnanimous, and sensible. Adrian, that incomparable kindred spirit, that delicate and noble man who loved everyone and was loved in return, appeared fated never to discover the partner meant to finalize his contentment. He frequently departed from our company to roam the forests alone or navigate his small boat, accompanied only by his volumes. He was often the most cheerful member of our group, yet he was also the sole person prone to bouts of melancholy; his fragile physique seemed burdened by the heaviness of existence, and his spirit appeared to reside within his frame rather than be truly joined to it. I was scarcely more committed to my Idris than to her brother, and she regarded him as her mentor, her companion, and the benefactor who had ensured the realization of her fondest desires. Raymond, the driven and unsettled Raymond, paused at the midpoint of life’s journey, satisfied to abandon his grand designs for power and renown to join our ranks as one of the wildflowers. His domain was Perdita’s heart, and his subjects were her thoughts; she loved him, revered him as a superior entity, and served him with obedience. No task, no act of devotion, and no period of vigilance felt burdensome to her when it concerned him. She would withdraw from our group to observe him, weeping with happiness at the thought that he belonged to her. She built a sanctuary for him within her soul, where every faculty acted as a priestess dedicated to his worship. At times she might be temperamental or fickle, yet her remorse was profound, her reconciliation complete, and even this volatility suited a man not naturally inclined to drift aimlessly through life.

Throughout the initial year of their union, Perdita bore Raymond a beautiful daughter. It was fascinating to observe in this tiny likeness the exact features of her father. She possessed the same slightly scornful mouth and triumphant expression, the same perceptive eyes, the same forehead and auburn hair; even her hands and slender fingers mirrored his. How precious she was to Perdita! As time went on, I likewise became a father, and our small children, our sources of amusement and joy, awakened a multitude of fresh and exquisite emotions.

Years unfolded in this manner—many years. Each month gave way to the next, each year resembled the one preceding it; in truth, our existences served as a living illustration of that lovely thought from Plutarch, that “our souls have a natural inclination to love, being born as much to love, as to feel, to reason, to understand and remember.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_006",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "At other times, we took trips lasting many days, traveling across the country to visit locations known for their beauty or historical significance. Occasionally, we went up to London to join in the amusements of the busy crowds, or our sanctuary was visited by guests from among them. These changes only made us appreciate more deeply the joys of the close-knit connection within our own circle, the peace of our beautiful forest, and our happy evenings in the halls of our beloved castle.

Idris had a personality that was exceptionally open, gentle, and affectionate. Her temperament was consistently sweet; while she remained firm and resolute on any matter that touched her heart, she was accommodating to those she loved. Perdita’s nature was less perfect, but tenderness and happiness improved her disposition and softened her natural reserve. Her intellect was clear and broad, her imagination vivid; she was sincere, generous, and reasonable. Adrian, the incomparable brother of my soul, the sensitive and excellent Adrian, who loved everyone and was loved by all, seemed destined never to find the partner who would complete his happiness. He often left us to wander alone in the woods or sail in his small boat, with only his books for company. He was frequently the most cheerful member of our group, yet he was also the only one struck by bouts of despondency; his slender frame seemed burdened by the weight of existence, and his soul appeared to inhabit his body rather than be truly united with it. I was hardly more devoted to my Idris than to her brother, and she loved him as her teacher, her friend, and the benefactor who had secured the fulfillment of her dearest wishes. Raymond, the ambitious and restless, rested midway along the great highway of life, content to abandon all his schemes for power and fame to become one of us, the flowers of the field. His kingdom was Perdita’s heart, his subjects her thoughts; she loved him, respected him as a superior being, obeyed him, and waited on him. No task, no devotion, no period of watching was tiresome to her when it concerned him. She would sit apart from us to watch him, weeping with joy at the thought that he was hers. She built a temple for him in the depths of her being, and every faculty was a priestess vowed to his service. Sometimes she could be wayward and moody, but her repentance was bitter, her reconciliation complete, and even this unevenness of temper suited a man who was not naturally inclined to drift idly down the stream of life.

During the first year of their marriage, Perdita gave Raymond a lovely daughter. It was fascinating to trace in this miniature version the exact traits of her father. She had the same slightly disdainful lips and triumphant smile, the same intelligent eyes, the same brow and chestnut hair; even her hands and slender fingers resembled his. How very dear she was to Perdita! In time, I also became a father, and our little darlings, our playthings and delights, brought forth a thousand new and delicious feelings.

Years passed this way—even years. Each month gave birth to its successor, each year resembled the one that had gone before; truly, our lives were a living commentary on that beautiful sentiment of Plutarch, that “our souls have a natural inclination to love, being born as much to love, as to feel, to reason, to understand and remember.”"
}

Sometimes she could be wayward and moody, but her repentance was bitter, her reconciliation complete, and even this unevenness of temper suited a man who was not naturally inclined to drift idly down the stream of life.

During the first year of their marriage, Perdita gave Raymond a lovely daughter. It was fascinating to trace in this miniature version the exact traits of her father. She had the same slightly disdainful lips and triumphant smile, the same intelligent eyes, the same brow and chestnut hair; even her hands and slender fingers resembled his. How very dear she was to Perdita! In time, I also became a father, and our little darlings, our playthings and delights, brought forth a thousand new and delicious feelings.

Years passed this way—even years. Each month gave birth to its successor, each year resembled the one that had gone before; truly, our lives were a living commentary on that beautiful sentiment of Plutarch, that “our souls have a natural inclination to love, being born as much to love, as to feel, to reason, to understand and remember.”"
}

Her temper was always sweet; while she was firm and resolute about matters close to her heart, she was always willing to compromise for those she loved. Adrian, my incomparable soul-brother, was sensitive and excellent. Though he loved everyone and was loved in return, he seemed destined never to find the partner who would complete his happiness. He often left us to wander alone in the woods or sail in his small boat, with only his books for company. He was frequently the most cheerful member of our group, yet he was also the only one who suffered from bouts of depression. His slender frame seemed burdened by the weight of existence, and his soul appeared to merely inhabit his body rather than be truly united with it. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "She found in his arms a precious certainty that as long as he held her, he would avoid despair. “This is generous,” she exclaimed; “this is noble, my dearest! Do not dread shame or humble circumstances while you have your Perdita; do not fear grief while our child survives and smiles. Let us travel wherever you choose; the love that travels with us will keep us from mourning.”

Held tightly in his arms, she spoke these words and tilted her head back, looking for agreement in his gaze—his eyes shone with indescribable joy. “Well, my small Lady Protector,” he remarked in jest, “what is this you are saying? What lovely plan have you constructed regarding exile and anonymity, when a more brilliant tapestry, a golden-threaded fabric, is actually what you ought to be considering?”

He pressed a kiss to her forehead—but the capricious girl, feeling slightly regretful of his victory and stirred by rapid shifts in mood, buried her face against his chest and cried. He consoled her, sharing his own aspirations and wishes, and soon her face glowed with shared feeling. How incredibly joyful they were that evening! How overflowing was their capacity for happiness!

CHAPTER VII.

After ensuring our friend was settled into his new position, we directed our attention toward Windsor. The proximity of this location to London served to lessen the feeling of a distressing parting when we left Raymond and Perdita. We said our goodbyes to them at the Protectoral Palace. It was quite charming to watch my sister embrace the spirit of the role, attempting to occupy her position with appropriate grace. Her inner pride and modest demeanor were now more conflicted than ever. Her shyness was genuine, stemming from that anxiety about not being valued correctly, that slight disregard for the world’s indifference, which also defined Raymond. Yet Perdita thought of others more consistently than he did; part of her reserve came from a desire to spare those around her from feeling inferior—a sentiment that never occurred to her. Given her background and upbringing, Idris would have been better suited for the rituals of ceremony, but the very naturalness that made such tasks easy for her also made them monotonous, whereas, despite every obstacle, Perdita clearly relished her role. She was too occupied with fresh ideas to feel much sorrow when we left; she bid us a warm farewell and vowed to visit soon, yet she did not lament the events that necessitated our departure. Raymond’s energy was limitless; he was unsure how to utilize his newly acquired authority; his mind teemed with schemes; he had settled on none as of yet—but he vowed to himself, his companions, and the public that the period of his Protectorship would be marked by some deed of extraordinary brilliance. Thus, we discussed them and reflected on life as we returned to Windsor Castle with a smaller party. We experienced immense relief at our departure from political chaos and pursued our seclusion with renewed enthusiasm. We were not lacking in tasks, but my restless nature was now directed solely toward intellectual pursuits, and I discovered that rigorous study served as a perfect remedy to soothe the agitation of spirit that, in idleness, would surely have plagued me."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_007",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "She felt in his embrace a comforting assurance that as long as he had her, he would not lose hope. “This is kind,” she cried; “this is noble, my own beloved! Oh, do not fear disgrace or poverty while you have your Perdita; do not fear sorrow while our child lives and smiles. Let us go wherever you wish; the love that travels with us will prevent any regrets.”

Locked in his arms, she spoke these words and tilted her head back, searching his eyes for agreement—they were sparkling with indescribable delight. “Why, my little Lady Protector,” he said playfully, “what is this you are saying? And what elaborate plan have you woven of exile and obscurity, when a brighter, gold-threaded future is what you should truly be considering?”

He kissed her forehead, but the impulsive girl, feeling a bit sorry at his triumph and agitated by a sudden shift in her thoughts, hid her face in his chest and wept. He comforted her, instilling his own hopes and desires into her, and soon her face beamed with sympathy. How incredibly happy they were that night! How full, even to the point of overflowing, was their sense of joy!

CHAPTER VII.

Having seen our friend properly settled in his new office, we turned our attention toward Windsor. The proximity of this place to London was such that it removed the idea of a painful separation when we left Raymond and Perdita. We said our goodbyes to them in the Protectoral Palace. It was charming to see my sister embrace the spirit of the role and attempt to fill her position with appropriate dignity. Her internal pride and humble manner were now more at odds than ever. Her timidity was not fake, but stemmed from that fear of not being properly appreciated—that slight disregard for the world’s opinion—which also characterized Raymond. But Perdita thought more constantly of others than he did, and part of her bashfulness arose from a wish to spare those around her from feeling inferior, a feeling that never crossed her own mind. Given her birth and upbringing, Idris would have been better suited for the formalities of ceremony, but the very ease with which she performed such actions, born of habit, made them tedious. Conversely, despite every drawback, Perdita clearly enjoyed her situation. She was too full of new ideas to feel much pain when we departed; she said an affectionate goodbye and promised to visit us soon, but she did not regret the circumstances that caused our separation. Raymond’s spirits were boundless; he did not know what to do with his newfound power. His head was full of plans, and although he had decided on none yet, he promised himself, his friends, and the world that the era of his Protectorship would be marked by some act of surpassing glory. Thus, we talked about them and reflected as we returned to Windsor Castle with our numbers diminished. We felt immense relief at our escape from political turmoil and sought our solitude with renewed enthusiasm. We did not lack for occupation, but my eager disposition was now turned solely toward intellectual pursuits, and I found that hard study was an excellent medicine to calm the fever of spirit that, in idleness, would undoubtedly have plagued me."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“I am a vessel,” she mused, “a vessel overflowing with the most bitter spirit of despair. Goodbye, Perdita! Goodbye, unfortunate girl! You shall never behold yourself in this state again; opulence and riches have abandoned you; in your profound destitution, you might even covet the lot of a wandering pauper; I am truly homeless! I dwell upon a desolate wasteland, vast and endless, yielding neither blossoms nor fruit; in its center stands a lonely crag to which you, Perdita, are tethered, watching the bleak horizon extend into the distance.”

She flung wide her casement, which faced the royal gardens. Illumination and shadow contended, and the eastern sky was marked with streaks of pink and gold. A single star flickered within the brightening expanse. The morning breeze, blowing coolly across the damp foliage, surged into the stifling chamber. “Everything continues,” Perdita reflected, “everything advances, fades, and expires! Once the midday has waned and the exhausted day has guided its steeds to their evening stables, the celestial fires emerge from the East, traveling their habitual route, climbing and descending the heavens. When their journey concludes, the sundial begins to cast a wavering shadow toward the west; the eyes of the day open, and the birds, blossoms, awakened greenery, and crisp winds stir; finally, the sun emerges, ascending the heights of the sky in grand procession. Everything progresses, transforms, and perishes, save for the awareness of agony within my shattering heart.”

“Yes, everything progresses and transforms: why should it surprise me, then, that love has traveled toward its sunset, and that the master of my existence has altered? We label the heavenly lights constant, yet they roam across that expanse, and if I gaze once more where I looked an hour prior, the aspect of the infinite firmament has shifted. The foolish moon and fickle planets alter their wandering dance each night; the sun itself, monarch of the heavens, periodically abandons his seat, surrendering his realm to darkness and winter. Nature ages, trembling in her failing members—creation has fallen into insolvency! Why wonder, then, that shadow and mortality have guided the light of your existence, O Perdita, toward ruin!”

CHAPTER IX.

Such were the melancholy and chaotic reflections of my unfortunate sister once she grew certain of Raymond’s unfaithfulness. Both her merits and her flaws ensured that the wound would remain unhealed. Her devotion to me, her brother, as well as to Adrian and Idris, was subordinate to the dominant passion of her soul; even her maternal affection derived much of its intensity from the joy she found in recognizing Raymond’s traits and expressions in her child’s face. She had been guarded and even harsh during her youth; however, love had mellowed the sharpness of her temperament, and her marriage to Raymond had allowed her gifts and emotions to blossom; with the former betrayed and the latter vanished, she reverted somewhat to her former nature. The intense pride of her character, dormant during her period of bliss, stirred, piercing her heart with its serpent’s sting; her inner humility intensified the poison’s effect; she had felt elevated in her own eyes while honored by his devotion: what value did she possess now that he had cast her from that position? She had taken pride in having captured and kept him—yet another had claimed him from her, and her triumph was as frigid as a quenched coal."
}

{

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“I am a vessel,” she thought, “a vessel filled to the brim with the most bitter essence of despair. Goodbye, Perdita! Goodbye, poor girl! You will never see yourself like this again; luxury and wealth are no longer yours. In your extreme poverty, you might even envy the homeless beggar; I am truly without a home. I live on a barren desert, vast and endless, which produces neither fruit nor flower. In the center stands a solitary rock to which you, Perdita, are chained, watching the dreary landscape stretch out into the distance.”

She threw open her window, which overlooked the palace garden. Light and darkness were battling, and the eastern horizon was streaked with rose and gold. Only one star trembled in the depths of the brightening atmosphere. The morning air, blowing freshly over the dewy plants, rushed into the heated room. “Everything continues,” Perdita thought, “everything moves forward, decays, and perishes. When noon has passed and the weary day has driven its team to their western stalls, the fires of heaven rise from the East, moving along their accustomed path, ascending and descending the sky. When their course is complete, the sundial begins to cast an uncertain shadow westward; the eyelids of day open, and birds, flowers, the startled vegetation, and the fresh breeze awaken. Finally, the sun appears and climbs the heights of heaven in a majestic procession. Everything proceeds, changes, and dies, except for the sense of misery in my bursting heart.”

“Yes, everything proceeds and changes. Why should it be a surprise, then, that love has reached its sunset and that the lord of my life has changed? We call the heavenly lights fixed, yet they wander across that plain, and if I look again where I looked an hour ago, the face of the eternal heavens has altered. The foolish moon and inconstant planets vary their erratic dance nightly; the sun itself, sovereign of the sky, occasionally deserts his throne and leaves his dominion to night and winter. Nature grows old and shakes in her decaying limbs—creation has gone bankrupt! Why wonder, then, that eclipse and death have led the light of your life to destruction, O Perdita!”

CHAPTER IX.

Such were the sad and chaotic thoughts of my poor sister when she became certain of Raymond’s infidelity. All her virtues and flaws combined to make the blow incurable. Her affection for me, her brother, and for Adrian and Idris was subject to the reigning passion of her heart; even her maternal tenderness drew half its strength from the delight she took in tracing Raymond’s features and expressions in her child’s face. She had been reserved and even stern in childhood, but love had softened the rough edges of her character, and her marriage to Raymond had allowed her talents and affections to blossom. With the former betrayed and the latter lost, she returned in some degree to her original disposition. The concentrated pride of her nature, forgotten during her blissful dream, awoke and pierced her heart with an adder’s sting. Her humility only increased the power of the venom; she had felt exalted in her own estimation while distinguished by his love. What was she worth now that he had cast her aside? She had been proud of having won and kept him, but another had taken him from her, and her sense of triumph was now as cold as a water-quenched ember."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_010",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The council chamber stood empty; the crowds that once served as agents for his various projects were ignored. Celebration, and even debauchery, became the standard of the day.

Perdita watched the growing chaos with alarm. For a moment, she believed she could hold back the tide and that Raymond might be persuaded to listen to reason—a vain hope. Her moment of influence had passed. He listened with arrogance and replied with disdain; if she truly managed to stir his conscience, the only result was that he sought an escape from the guilt through mindless excess. With her natural energy, Perdita then tried to fill his role. Their still-visible union allowed her to accomplish much, but no woman could ultimately fix the Protector’s mounting negligence. He acted as if gripped by a fit of madness, trampling on all ceremony, order, and duty to surrender himself to indulgence.

Reports of these strange events reached us, and we were unsure how to restore our friend to his senses and his country when Perdita suddenly appeared before us. She described the progression of this sorrowful change and begged Adrian and me to go to London to try and address the worsening situation. “Tell him,” she cried, “tell Lord Raymond that my presence will no longer bother him. He does not need to dive into this destructive dissipation just to disgust me and drive me away. That goal is now achieved; he will never see me again. But let me—this is my final request—let me find the choices of my youth justified by the praise of his countrymen and the prosperity of England.”

During our ride to the city, Adrian and I debated Raymond’s behavior and his failure to live up to the high expectations we once held for him. My friend and I had been raised with the same belief: that a steady commitment to principle was the only path to honor, and that a constant focus on the common good was the only worthy goal of human ambition. Though we shared these ideas, we disagreed on how to apply them. Resentment added a sting to my criticism, and I condemned Raymond’s conduct in harsh terms. Adrian was more gentle and thoughtful. He admitted that my principles were excellent, but he denied they were the only ones. Citing the idea that there are many paths to righteousness, he insisted that the ways to become good or great vary as much as human temperaments, which, like the leaves of a forest, are never identical.

We arrived in London around eleven at night. Despite what we had heard, we assumed we would find Raymond at St. Stephen’s, so we hurried there. The chamber was full, but the Protector was absent. A stern dissatisfaction was visible on the faces of the leaders, and the whispering and busy gossip among the subordinates felt equally ominous. We rushed to the Protector’s palace. We found Raymond in his dining room with six others; the bottle was being passed around merrily, and it had already significantly clouded the judgment of one or two of them."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "However, contrary breezes keep me landbound; much like Ulysses, I remain by the shore and shed tears. My weak hands, though, possess neither the strength to chop timber nor the skill to plane boards. Swayed by these gloomy reflections, she grew increasingly enamored with her own grief. Nevertheless, Adrian’s company provided some relief; he immediately shattered the unspoken rule regarding any mention of Raymond. Initially, she recoiled at the unfamiliar noise, but she soon grew accustomed to it and even cherished it, hanging on every word as he recounted his exploits. Clara also shed her reserve; having been childhood companions, she and Adrian walked or rode together while he complied with her fervent requests, retelling for the hundredth time some story of her father’s valor, generosity, or fairness.

Meanwhile, every ship delivered stirring news from Greece. Having a friend among their military and political leaders caused us to follow the specifics with great fervor, and occasional brief notes from Raymond described how he was consumed by the concerns of his chosen nation. The Greeks were deeply committed to their trade, and they would have been content with their current gains had the Turks not provoked them through an invasion. The patriots triumphed, a desire for expansion took hold, and they already viewed Constantinople as their own. Raymond’s reputation climbed steadily, yet one individual held a higher rank within their forces. He stood out for his leadership and tactical positioning during a conflict on the Thracian plains near the Hebrus River, a battle intended to determine the future of Islam. The Muslims were routed and pushed entirely out of the territory west of the river. The fighting was brutal, and the Turkish losses seemed permanent; yet, in mourning one man, the Greeks ignored the countless others scattered across the gore-stained ground, and they stopped celebrating a triumph that cost them—Raymond.

During the engagement at Makri, he had spearheaded the cavalry assault, chasing the retreating forces all the way to the Hebrus. His prized steed was discovered feeding by the quiet riverbank. It remained uncertain if he had perished among the unidentified dead, yet no shattered gear or bloodied harness revealed his destiny. It was conjectured that the Turks, having captured such a renowned prisoner, chose to indulge their malice rather than their greed, and fearing British intervention, decided to permanently hide the cold-blooded execution of the soldier they most loathed and dreaded among their foes.

Raymond remained in the thoughts of the English. His resignation from the Protectorate had triggered an unprecedented reaction, and when his grand, noble policies were compared to the limited perspectives of later leaders, his time in power was remembered with regret. The constant appearance of his name, accompanied by glowing reports in the Greek newspapers, sustained the fascination he had inspired. He appeared to be fortune’s darling, and his premature death darkened the world, leaving the remaining populace feeling diminished. They grasped desperately at the possibility that he might still be breathing."
}

{

  "rewritten_text": "However, contrary winds keep me on shore; like Ulysses, I sit by the water and weep. Yet my weak hands can neither cut down trees nor shape planks. Influenced by these gloomy thoughts, she became more enamored with sorrow than ever. Still, Adrian’s presence helped; he immediately broke the silence maintained regarding Raymond. At first, she flinched at the unfamiliar sound, but soon she grew accustomed to it and even cherished it, listening eagerly to accounts of his accomplishments. Clara also shed her reserve; she and Adrian were childhood playmates, and now, as they walked or rode together, he gave in to her earnest requests and repeated, for the hundredth time, some story of her father’s courage, generosity, or fairness.

Meanwhile, every ship brought exciting news from Greece. Having a friend in their armies and councils made us follow the details with enthusiasm, and an occasional short letter from Raymond told us how consumed he was by the interests of his adopted country. The Greeks were deeply committed to their commercial pursuits and would have been content with their current gains had the Turks not provoked them with an invasion. The patriots were victorious, a spirit of conquest took hold, and they already viewed Constantinople as their own. Raymond’s reputation grew constantly, though one man held a higher command in their armies. He was notable for his leadership and tactical positioning in a battle fought on the plains of Thrace, along the banks of the Hebrus, which was to decide the fate of Islam. The Muslims were defeated and driven entirely from the land west of the river. The battle was bloody, and the Turkish losses were seemingly irreparable; yet, in losing one man, the Greeks forgot the countless others scattered across the gory field, and they stopped celebrating a victory that cost them—Raymond.

At the battle of Makri, he had led the cavalry charge and pursued the fleeing enemy to the banks of the Hebrus. His favorite horse was found grazing by the edge of the quiet river. It remained a question whether he had fallen among the unidentified, but no broken gear or bloodstained harness revealed his fate. It was suspected that the Turks, having captured such a famous prisoner, chose to satisfy their cruelty rather than their greed; fearing interference from England, they decided to hide forever the cold-blooded murder of the soldier they most hated and feared among their enemy’s ranks.

Raymond was not forgotten in England. His resignation from the Protectorate had caused an unprecedented sensation, and when his magnificent, bold policies were contrasted with the narrow-mindedness of later politicians, his time in power was remembered with regret. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The rout was absolute; the artillery fire died away; the foot soldiers regrouped, and cavalry chased the retreating Turks across the desolate expanse. Raymond’s officers scattered in different ways to gather intelligence and deliver orders. I, too, was sent to a remote section of the battlefield.

The terrain of the conflict was a flat plain—so level that from the burial mounds one could view the undulating mountain range on the distant horizon. The intervening land lacked any variation, save for gentle swells like the surface of the ocean. This region of Thrace had been a theater of war for so long that it lay fallow, offering a bleak and sterile vista. My instructions were to observe the path taken by an enemy unit from a northern mound. The entire Turkish force, pursued by the Greeks, had surged eastward; only the fallen remained in my vicinity. From the summit of the hill, I scanned the horizon—everything was quiet and abandoned.

The final rays of the setting sun cast light from behind the peak of Mount Athos. The Sea of Marmora shimmered under the glow, while the distant Asian shore was partially obscured by a low-hanging mist. Numerous helmets, bayonets, and blades, dropped from lifeless hands, caught the fading light, scattered across the landscape. From the east, a flock of ravens, long-time dwellers of Turkish graveyards, glided toward their grim feast; the sun vanished. This moment, somber yet pleasant, has always struck me as the time when we are most inclined to connect with the divine; our human harshness fades, and a soft peace fills the spirit. Yet now, surrounded by the dying and the dead, how could a murderer feel heavenly thoughts or inner calm? Throughout the active day, my mind had been a submissive servant to the circumstances created by my peers; historical duty, animosity toward the enemy, and martial fervor had governed me. Now, I gazed at the evening star, hanging gently in the orange sunset. I looked down at the corpse-littered ground and felt mortified by my own kind. Perhaps the serene heavens felt the same, for they quickly shrouded themselves in fog, accelerating the rapid twilight typical of the south. Dense clouds drifted up from the southeast, and crimson, murky lightning flickered from their dark borders. The gusting wind stirred the clothing of the deceased, turning cold as it swept over their frozen bodies. Darkness closed in, and the surroundings grew blurred. I climbed down from my vantage point and struggled to steer my horse to avoid the fallen.

Abruptly, I caught a shrill cry; a figure appeared to emerge from the ground, rushing toward me before collapsing as it approached. The event occurred so rapidly that I struggled to pull back my horse to prevent it from trampling the fallen person. The individual wore a soldier’s uniform, but the exposed neck and arms, along with the persistent wailing, revealed a woman in disguise."
}

The ground where the battle took place was a flat plain—so level that from the burial mounds, you could see the undulating line of mountains on the distant horizon. The space between was featureless, save for swells that looked like ocean waves. This region of Thrace had been a war zone for so long that it remained uncultivated, presenting a desolate, barren sight. My orders were to observe the path a detachment of the enemy might have taken from a northern mound. The entire Turkish army, pursued by the Greeks, had surged eastward; only the dead remained in my sector. From the top of the mound, I looked all around—everything was silent and deserted.

The final rays of the setting sun shot up from behind the distant peak of Mount Athos. The Sea of Marmara still shimmered under the light, while the Asian coast beyond was half-obscured by a haze of low clouds. Many helmets, bayonets, and swords, dropped from lifeless hands, reflected the fading light; they lay scattered everywhere. From the east, a flock of ravens, long-time residents of Turkish cemeteries, came gliding toward their feast. The sun vanished. This hour, melancholy yet sweet, has always seemed to me the time when we are most naturally drawn to commune with higher powers; our human harshness fades, and a gentle peace fills the soul. But now, surrounded by the dying and the dead, how could a thought of heaven or a sense of tranquility possess one of the killers? During the busy day, my mind had been a willing slave to the circumstances created by my peers; historical context, hatred of the enemy, and military fervor had dominated me. Now, I looked at the evening star as it hung softly and calmly in the orange sunset. I turned to the corpse-strewn earth and felt ashamed of my species. Perhaps the placid skies felt the same, for they quickly veiled themselves in mist, accelerating the rapid twilight typical of the south. Heavy masses of cloud drifted up from the southeast, and red, murky lightning flashed from their dark edges. The rushing wind disturbed the clothing of the dead and turned cold as it passed over their icy bodies. Darkness gathered; the objects around me became indistinct. I descended from my post and struggled to guide my horse to avoid the slain.

Suddenly, I heard a piercing shriek. A figure seemed to rise from the ground; it flew swiftly toward me, sinking back to the earth as it drew near. It all happened so fast that I barely managed to rein in my horse to avoid trampling the prostrate person. The individual wore a soldier’s uniform, but the exposed neck and arms, along with the persistent shrieking, revealed a woman in disguise."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "In this manner shall I confront the contagion; and even if no soul joins me, I shall hoist the Greek flag upon the summit of St. Sophia.”

Logic proved useless against such intense emotions. I attempted in vain to demonstrate that the arrival of winter would clear the infected atmosphere and renew the Greeks’ resolve. “Speak not of any season but the present!” he exclaimed. “I have seen my final winter, and the year 2092 shall be inscribed upon my sepulcher. I already perceive,” he added, gazing upward with sorrow, “the boundary and the sheer precipice of my life, from which I shall fall into the dark enigma of the hereafter. I am ready, provided I leave a path of brilliance so vivid that even my harshest foes cannot obscure it. I owe this to Greece, to you, to my remaining Perdita, and to myself, the sacrifice of ambition.”

Our conversation was broken off by a servant, who reported that Raymond’s officers were gathered in the council hall. He asked me to ride through the encampment in the interim to assess and report on the soldiers’ morale; he then departed. I had been stirred to the limit by the day’s events, and now even more so by Raymond’s fervent words. How fragile is human reason! He charged the Greeks with being superstitious, yet what term would he apply to the belief he placed in Evadne’s prophecies? I traveled from the palace of Sweet Waters to the field where the camp was situated, discovering its occupants in a state of unrest. The arrival of several men bearing new accounts of wonders from the fleet, the embellishments added to existing rumors, and stories of ancient omens and terrifying reports of entire territories devastated by the plague this year, frightened and preoccupied the troops. Order vanished; the military force disintegrated. Each soldier, previously a component of a vast entity acting only in concert with others, now reverted to the solitary individual nature intended, concerned only with his own survival. They slipped away initially in ones and twos, then in larger groups, until, without interference from their commanders, entire battalions took the path toward Macedonia.

Around midnight, I went back to the palace and looked for Raymond; he was solitary and seemingly tranquil—a composure born of a firm commitment to a specific course of action. He listened to my report regarding the army’s collapse with serenity, then remarked, “You are aware, Verney, of my unwavering resolve not to abandon this location until Stamboul is openly ours in the light of day. If the men currently with me fear to accompany me, others of greater bravery can be located. Depart before dawn, deliver these messages to Karazza, and supplement them with your own pleas that he dispatch his marines and naval troops to me; if I can secure just one regiment to support me, the others would naturally follow. Let him provide this regiment. I shall await your arrival by noon tomorrow.”

I felt this was a weak strategy, yet I promised him my compliance and dedication. I left him to secure a brief period of sleep. As dawn arrived, I was prepared for my journey."
}

It was pointless to explain that when winter arrived, the cold would clear the pestilential air and restore the Greeks' courage. “Don’t talk to me about any season but this!” he shouted. “I have lived my final winter, and the year 2092 will be carved onto my tombstone. I can already see,” he continued, looking up sadly, “the boundary and the steep edge of my existence, over which I will plunge into the dark mystery of the afterlife. I am ready, provided I leave behind a trail of light so brilliant that even my worst enemies cannot dim it. I owe this to Greece, to you, to my surviving Perdita, and to myself, the victim of ambition.”

We were interrupted by an attendant who announced that Raymond’s staff had gathered in the council chamber. He asked me to ride through the camp in the meantime to observe the soldiers’ morale and report back to him; then he left. I had been extremely agitated by the day’s events, and even more so by Raymond’s passionate words. How fragile human reason is! He accused the Greeks of superstition, yet what did he call the faith he placed in Evadne’s predictions? I traveled from the palace of Sweet Waters to the plain where the camp was located and found the people in an uproar. The arrival of several men with new, marvelous stories from the fleet, the exaggerations of what was already known, and tales of ancient prophecies and terrifying accounts of entire regions devastated by the plague this year had alarmed and preoccupied the troops. Discipline vanished; the army fell apart. Each individual, previously part of a great whole that moved only in unison, now reverted to the solitary person nature had created, thinking only of himself. They slipped away at first in ones and twos, then in larger groups, until, without any interference from the officers, entire battalions headed for the road to Macedonia.

Around midnight, I returned to the palace and looked for Raymond. He was alone and appeared composed; at least, he possessed the composure that comes from a resolve to follow a specific course of action. He listened to my report about the army’s self-dissolution calmly, then said, “You know, Verney, my firm decision not to leave this place until Stamboul is clearly ours in the light of day. If the men I have with me are too cowardly to follow, others who are braver can be found. Leave before dawn, take these dispatches to Karazza, and add your own pleas for him to send me his marines and naval forces. If I can get just one regiment to back me, the rest would naturally follow. Have him send me this regiment. I will expect your return by noon tomorrow.”

I thought this was a weak plan, but I assured him of my obedience and dedication. I left him to get a few hours of rest. At daybreak, I was geared up for my ride."
}
