model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I shall remain and perish in this place!

“Lionel, you must depart for England; go back to gentle Idris and beloved Adrian. Return, and treat my orphaned daughter as your own child within your home. Consider me deceased; if death is merely a transition of existence, then I am truly dead. This realm is distinct from the one I recently occupied, and from the one that serves as your current home. Here, I commune solely with the past and the future. Travel to England, and abandon me in the only location where I can endure the wretched days I am forced to survive.”

A torrent of weeping concluded her mournful speech. I had anticipated some irrational suggestion, so I stayed quiet for a moment, gathering my resolve to better challenge her fanciful plan. “You are harboring gloomy reflections, my dear Perdita,” I remarked, “and I am not surprised that your sound judgment is temporarily clouded by intense sorrow and a troubled mind. Even I feel an attachment to this final residence of Raymond’s; yet, we are obligated to depart.”

“I foresaw this,” Perdita exclaimed; “I knew you would regard me as a foolish, irrational girl. Do not deceive yourself; I commanded that this cottage be constructed, and I will stay here until the moment comes when I may join him in his more joyful abode.”

“My dearest girl!”

“What is so peculiar about my intention? I could have misled you; I might have spoken of staying here for only a few months. In your eagerness to arrive at Windsor, you would have departed, and I could have followed my course without any argument or conflict. However, I scorned such deception; or rather, in my despair, it was my sole comfort to unburden my soul to you, my brother and only friend. You will not oppose me, will you? You understand how stubborn your miserable, grief-stricken sister can be. Take my daughter with you; distract her from scenes and memories of sadness; let youthful joy return to her heart and brighten her eyes. That could never happen if she remained near me; it is far better for all of you that you never see me again. As for me, I will not intentionally pursue death—at least, not while I retain my self-command, which I can maintain here. But if you force me away from this land, my ability to restrain myself will vanish, and I cannot be held accountable for the desperate acts my agony might drive me to commit.”

“You express your intent, Perdita,” I answered, “with forceful language, yet that intent is self-centered and beneath you. You have frequently concurred with me that there is only one answer to the complex puzzle of existence: to better ourselves and add to the welfare of others. Now, in the very height of your life, you abandon your values and seclude yourself in unproductive isolation. Will you contemplate Raymond any less at Windsor, the setting of your youthful joy? Will you commune any less with his departed spirit while you nurture and observe the rare virtues of his child? You have suffered a grievous trial, and I do not marvel that a sensation bordering on madness should push you toward harsh and illogical fantasies. Nevertheless, a home filled with affection awaits you in your native England. My care and devotion shall comfort you; the company of Raymond’s friends will provide more solace than these bleak musings.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_014",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I will live and die here!

“Go back to England, Lionel; return to sweet Idris and dearest Adrian; go, and let my orphan girl be like a child of your own in your home. Look at me as if I were dead; and truly, if death is just a change of state, then I am dead. This is a different world from the one I recently inhabited, and from the one that is now your home. Here, I commune only with what has been and what is to come. Go to England, and leave me where I alone can consent to drag out the miserable days I am forced to endure.”

A flood of tears ended her sad speech. I had expected some extreme proposal, so I remained silent for a while, gathering my thoughts so I could better challenge her fanciful plan. “You are holding onto bleak thoughts, my dear Perdita,” I said, “and I am not surprised that for a time your better judgment is being clouded by intense grief and a troubled imagination. Even I am fond of this final home of Raymond’s; nevertheless, we must leave it.”

“I expected this,” Perdita cried; “I assumed you would treat me like a mad, foolish girl. But do not fool yourself; this cottage was built by my order, and here I will remain until the hour arrives when I can share his happier dwelling.”

“My dearest girl!”

“And what is so strange about my plan? I could have deceived you; I could have spoken of staying here for only a few months; in your anxiety to reach Windsor, you would have left me, and without any argument or conflict, I could have followed through with my plan. But I disdained such trickery; or rather, in my misery, it was my only consolation to pour out my heart to you, my brother, my only friend. You will not argue with me, will you? You know how stubborn your poor, grief-stricken sister is. Take my girl with you; help her move past these sights and thoughts of sorrow; let childhood joy return to her heart and brighten her eyes. That could never happen if she were near me; it is far better for all of you that you never see me again. As for myself, I will not voluntarily seek death—that is, I will not as long as I can control myself, and I can do that here. But drag me from this country, and my self-control will vanish; I cannot be responsible for the desperate acts my agony might lead me to commit.”

“You express your meaning, Perdita,” I replied, “in powerful words, yet that meaning is selfish and beneath you. You have often agreed with me that there is only one solution to the complex riddle of life: to improve ourselves and contribute to the happiness of others. Now, in the very prime of life, you are abandoning your principles and shutting yourself away in useless solitude. Will you think of Raymond any less at Windsor, the scene of your early happiness? Will you commune any less with his departed spirit while you watch over and nurture the rare excellence of his child? You have been through a terrible ordeal, and I do not wonder that a feeling bordering on insanity is driving you to bitter and unreasonable thoughts. But a home of love awaits you in your native England. My tenderness and affection will soothe you; the company of Raymond’s friends will provide more comfort than these dreary speculations.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_016",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The terror that took hold seemed to cause more harm than the sickness and its typical symptoms. Winter was greeted as a universal and reliable healer. The darkening forests, rising rivers, evening fog, and morning ice were received with thankfulness. The restorative power of the chill was instantly apparent, and the weekly death tolls began to shrink. Many of our guests departed; those with homes far to the south fled joyfully from our northern winter, returning to their own countries, confident of abundance despite the frightening ordeal they had endured. We could breathe once more. We were uncertain what the following summer might hold, but the current months belonged to us, and our expectations for an end to the epidemic were strong.

I have delayed this long on the outer edge, the eroding sandbar extending into the river of life, playing with the specter of death. For this long, I have comforted my spirit by looking back on former joy, back when hope existed. Why not remain this way forever? I am not eternal, and the narrative of my life could be drawn out until my final days. Yet the identical feeling that initially drove me to depict scenes filled with fond memories now urges me to move forward. The same longing within this beating, fervent heart that compelled me to document my wandering youth, my peaceful maturity, and my soul’s deepest passions, now makes me shrink from further hesitation. I must finish my task.

So here I remain, as I mentioned, by the rapid currents of passing years, and now I must depart! Unfurl the canvas and pull the oars, rushing past looming, dark cliffs and down steep rapids, toward the sea of emptiness I have arrived at. Yet, one moment, a single brief pause before I leave the bank—once more, let me imagine myself as I was in 2094 at my home in Windsor, let me shut my eyes and pretend that the vast branches of its oaks still provide me shade, with its castle walls nearby. Let my imagination paint the happy scene of the twentieth of June, just as my pained heart remembers it even now.

Events had drawn me to London, where I heard reports that signs of the plague had appeared in the city’s hospitals. I traveled back to Windsor; my expression was somber, my spirit burdened. I walked into the Little Park, as I usually did, through the Frogmore gate, heading toward the Castle. Much of the land had been turned over to farming, with patches of potatoes and grain spread throughout. Rooks called out noisily in the branches overhead; mingling with their harsh sounds, I caught a cheerful melody. It was Alfred’s birthday. The youth, the Eton students, and the children of local families were holding a festive fair, to which all the local residents were welcomed. The park was dotted with tents, their bright colors and vibrant flags fluttering in the sun, contributing to the festive atmosphere. On a stage built below the terrace, several of the younger guests were dancing. I rested against a tree to watch them. The musicians performed the lively eastern melody by Weber from Abon Hassan; its spirited notes made the dancers move with ease, while the spectators tapped their feet in rhythm without realizing it."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The concept itself was magnanimous and grand, yet his modest demeanor and complete lack of self-righteousness made his deed infinitely more moving. I intended to refuse his plea, but having witnessed the benevolence he spread, I realized his intentions were immovable; thus, with a burdened spirit, I agreed to his request. He squeezed my hand with warmth, stating, “Thank you; you have freed me from a difficult predicament and remain, as always, my dearest friend. Goodbye—I must depart for a few hours. Go and speak with Ryland. Even though he is abandoning his duties in London, he could prove immensely useful in the north of England by welcoming and aiding travelers, and helping to provide the capital with sustenance. I implore you to stir him toward a sense of responsibility.”

Adrian departed, as I later discovered, to continue his routine of visiting infirmaries and examining the congested districts of London. I found Ryland significantly changed since his visit to Windsor. Constant dread had sallowed his skin and withered his frame. I informed him of the evening’s agenda, and a smile eased his tense features. He was eager to leave; every day he anticipated catching the contagion, and every day he struggled against the gentle pressure of Adrian’s insistence that he stay. Once Adrian was officially named his deputy, he intended to flee to safety. Influenced by this thought, he attended to my words, and, buoyed by the imminent prospect of his escape, he began discussing the strategies he would implement in his home county, momentarily abandoning his firm intention to isolate himself from all contact within his private estate.

That evening, Adrian and I traveled to Westminster. During the journey, he reminded me of my instructions, yet, remarkably, I arrived at the chamber without having contemplated my objective. Adrian waited in the coffee-room while I, following his wishes, took my place in St. Stephen’s. An unusual quiet hung over the hall. I had not been there since the time of Raymond’s protectorate, an era marked by high attendance, powerful oratory, and spirited debate. The benches were largely deserted; the seats traditionally held by hereditary peers were empty, though representatives from the city and commercial hubs were present, alongside a few landowners and those seeking a political career. The house first addressed a petition from the Lord Protector, requesting that they name a deputy to serve during his unavoidable absence.

A hush fell over the room until a member approached me, whispering that the Earl of Windsor had sent word that I was to propose his election, as the individual originally selected for the role was absent. Only then did I grasp the true weight of my responsibility, and I felt crushed by the situation I had accepted. Ryland had abandoned his position out of terror of the pestilence, and because of that same fear, Adrian faced no opposition."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_017",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The very idea was generous and noble, but beyond that, his modest manner and complete lack of self-righteousness made his actions ten times more moving. I wanted to refuse his request, but I had seen the good he spread; I felt that his resolve was unshakable, so with a heavy heart, I agreed to do as he asked. He grasped my hand affectionately. "Thank you," he said. "You have relieved me of a painful dilemma, and you are, as you have always been, my best friend. Farewell—I must leave you for a few hours now. Go and speak with Ryland. Although he is abandoning his post in London, he could be of great service in the north of England by receiving and assisting travelers and helping to supply the capital with food. I beg you to awaken him to some sense of duty."

Adrian left me, as I later learned, for his daily task of visiting the hospitals and inspecting the crowded districts of London. I found Ryland much changed, even from when he had visited Windsor. Constant fear had sallowed his complexion and made him look withered. I told him about the evening's business, and a smile relaxed his tense muscles. He wanted to go; every day he expected to catch the plague, and every day he was unable to resist the gentle pressure of Adrian’s insistence that he stay. The moment Adrian was legally elected his deputy, he would escape to safety. With this in mind, he listened to everything I said, and, buoyed by the near prospect of his departure, he began discussing the plans he would adopt in his own county, forgetting for the moment his cherished resolution to isolate himself from all contact within his estate.

In the evening, Adrian and I went to Westminster. As we traveled, he reminded me of what I was to say and do, yet, strangely, I entered the chamber without having once reflected on my purpose. Adrian stayed in the coffee-room, while I, following his wishes, took my seat in St. Stephen’s. An unusual silence reigned in the chamber. I had not visited it since Raymond’s protectorate, a period marked by high attendance, eloquent speakers, and heated debate. The benches were mostly empty; those usually occupied by the hereditary members were vacant. The city members were there, along with representatives from commercial towns, a few landowners, and not many of those who entered parliament to build a career. The first subject to occupy the house was an address from the Lord Protector, asking them to appoint a deputy during his necessary absence.

A silence prevailed until one of the members came to me and whispered that the Earl of Windsor had sent word that I was to move for his election, in the absence of the person originally chosen for the office. Now, for the first time, I saw the full extent of my task, and I was overwhelmed by what I had brought upon myself. Ryland had deserted his post out of fear of the plague; because of that same fear, Adrian had no competition."
}

Constant fear had made his skin look sickly and caused his whole body to wither. I told him about the evening’s business, and a smile relaxed his tense face. He wanted to go; every day he expected to catch the plague, and every day he was unable to resist Adrian’s gentle pressure to stay. The moment Adrian was legally elected as his deputy, he planned to escape to safety. With this in mind, he listened to everything I said. Feeling almost joyful at the near prospect of his departure, he began discussing the plans he would adopt in his own county, forgetting for the moment his firm resolution to shut himself away from all contact on his private estate.

In the evening, Adrian and I went to Westminster. As we traveled, he reminded me of what I was to say and do, yet, strangely, I entered the chamber without having once thought about my purpose. Adrian stayed in the coffee-room, while I, following his request, took my seat in St. Stephen’s. An unusual silence filled the chamber. I had not visited it since Raymond’s time as Protector, a period known for high attendance, eloquent speakers, and heated debates. The benches were mostly empty; the seats usually occupied by hereditary members were vacant. The city members were there, along with representatives from commercial towns, a few landowners, and not many of those who entered parliament to build a career. The first subject the house addressed was a request from the Lord Protector, asking them to appoint a deputy during his necessary absence.

A silence followed until one of the members came to me and whispered that the Earl of Windsor had sent word that I was to propose his election, since the person originally chosen for this office was absent. Now, for the first time, I saw the full extent of my task, and I was overwhelmed by what I had taken on. Ryland had deserted his post out of fear of the plague; because of that same fear, Adrian had no competition."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I proceeded to Idris, formulating believable justifications for spending the entire day at the Castle, while attempting to smooth the signs of worry from my face. Happily, she was not by herself. I discovered Merrival, the astronomer, in her company. His perspective on humanity was far too distant to notice the daily tragedies, and he existed amidst the plague oblivious to its presence. This unfortunate man, as knowledgeable as La Place yet as innocent and shortsighted as a youth, had frequently neared starvation—he, his wan wife, and their many children—all while he neither experienced hunger nor noticed their suffering. His celestial theories consumed him; he scribbled equations in coal upon the exposed walls of his attic. He would trade a hard-won coin or a piece of clothing for a book without hesitation. He neither heard his offspring weeping nor noticed his partner’s gaunt appearance, and the height of disaster was to him merely like a night of cloud cover, when he would have sacrificed much to witness a heavenly event. His wife was one of those remarkable individuals, found only among women, whose devotion could not be lessened by hardship. Her spirit was split between immense reverence for her spouse and gentle concern for their children; she attended to him, labored for them, and never uttered a grievance, even though sorrow turned her existence into a prolonged, somber reverie.

He had first approached Adrian by asking to view certain planetary movements through his telescope. His destitution was readily apparent and subsequently eased. He frequently expressed gratitude for the volumes we provided and for access to our equipment, yet he never mentioned his improved living quarters or his changed situation. His wife confirmed that he had noticed no difference, save for the absence of the children from his workspace, and to her immense astonishment, he actually lamented this unusual silence.

He arrived then to inform us that he had finished his treatise on the pericyclical movements of the Earth’s axis and the precession of the equinoctial points. If a Roman from the Republican era had been resurrected to discuss the upcoming selection of a laurel-crowned consul or the final conflict with Mithridates, his notions would not have been more detached from the present than Merrival’s discourse. Humanity, no longer possessing a desire for connection, expressed its thoughts through visible symbols, yet no readers remained. While everyone, having discarded their weapons, stood alone against their shield awaiting the pestilence, Merrival spoke of the condition of mankind six millennia into the future. He might have just as well provided us with a commentary describing the unknown and inconceivable features of the beings who would then inhabit the abandoned home of humanity. We lacked the cruelty to disillusion the elderly man, and when I entered, he was reciting sections of his manuscript to Idris, inquiring what rebuttal could be offered to this or that argument.

Idris could not suppress a grin as she heard him. She had already learned from him that his family remained living and healthy. Although she was not prone to forgetting the precarious edge of time upon which she stood, I could see that she was briefly entertained by the disparity between the narrow focus we had long maintained regarding human existence and the vast, sweeping strides with which Merrival traversed a coming eternity."
}

He was far too focused on the long-term view of humanity to pay attention to the daily tragedies, and he lived in the middle of the contagion without even realizing it existed. This poor man, as learned as La Place but as innocent and shortsighted as a child, had often been on the verge of starvation—he, his pale wife, and their many children—while he neither felt hunger nor noticed their suffering. His astronomical theories consumed him; calculations were scribbled in coal on the bare walls of his attic. A hard-earned coin or a piece of clothing would be traded for a book without a second thought. He neither heard his children crying nor noticed his wife’s emaciated frame, and the height of the disaster was to him merely like a cloudy night when he would have given his right hand to observe a celestial event. His wife was one of those remarkable beings, found only among women, whose capacity for love could not be diminished by misfortune. Her mind was split between boundless admiration for her husband and tender anxiety for her children; she waited on him, worked for them, and never complained, even though her worries made her life one long, melancholy dream.

He had introduced himself to Adrian by asking to observe some planetary motions through his telescope. His poverty was easily spotted and relieved. He often thanked us for the books we lent him and for the use of our instruments, but he never mentioned his new home or his change in circumstances. His wife assured us that he had not noticed any difference, except for the absence of the children from his study, and to her infinite surprise, he actually complained about this unusual quiet.

He had come now to announce the completion of his Essay on the Pericyclical Motions of the Earth’s Axis and the precession of the equinoctial points. If an ancient Roman from the Republic had returned to life and talked about the upcoming election of a consul or the last battle with Mithridates, his ideas would not have been more out of touch with the times than Merrival’s conversation. People, no longer having an appetite for connection, kept their thoughts to themselves; there were no readers left. While everyone else, having cast aside their weapons, waited alone for the plague, Merrival talked about the state of mankind six thousand years in the future. He might have just as well provided a commentary describing the unknown and unimaginable features of the creatures who would then occupy the abandoned home of humanity. We did not have the heart to disillusion the poor old man, and at the moment I walked in, he was reading parts of his book to Idris, asking what answer could be given to this or that argument.

Idris could not help but smile as she listened. She had already gathered from him that his family was alive and healthy. Although she was not prone to forgetting the precarious edge of time on which she stood, I could see that she was amused for a moment by the contrast between the narrow view of human life we had taken for so long and the giant leaps with which Merrival paced through a coming eternity."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "For an instant, I was incapacitated, my head drooping from a wave of intense sickness; then, regaining my senses, I leaped up, pushed the creature away, and rushed up the stairs into the room my family occupied. A faint glow revealed Alfred resting on a sofa; Clara, shaking and whiter than fresh snow, had lifted him onto her arm, offering him a drink of water. It was clear to me that no vitality remained in that broken body; his face was stiff, his eyes were vacant, and his head had slumped backward. I lifted him from her, placed him gently down, pressed a kiss to his cold lips, and attempted to speak in a futile whisper, though the loudest roar of artillery would have been unable to reach him in his spiritual dwelling.

And what of Idris? The news that she had departed to find me and had not come back was terrifying, while the wind and rain hammered against the glass and howled about the residence. Furthermore, the nauseating symptoms of the plague were intensifying within me; I had no time to waste if I hoped to find her again. I climbed onto my horse and rode out to search, imagining her voice in every breeze, burdened by fever and sharp agony.

I traveled through the dark, rainy, and winding streets of deserted London. My son was dead at home; the roots of a fatal illness had taken hold of me; I went to find my beloved Idris, now wandering by herself, while the rain poured from the sky like a waterfall to drench her head in freezing moisture and her delicate limbs in numbing cold. A woman stood on a doorstep and shouted to me as I rode by. It was not Idris, so I hurried on, until a sudden realization—a mental replay of what I had glimpsed but not registered—convinced me that another figure, slender, elegant, and tall, was standing there, clutching the person who held her up. Within moments, I was at the side of the petitioner, and in moments, I caught the collapsing Idris in my embrace. Lifting her, I set her upon the horse; she lacked the power to hold herself up, so I climbed on behind her and pulled her tightly against my chest, enveloping her in my cloak, while her companion, whose familiar but altered face—it was Juliet, the Duke of L—'s daughter—could earn from me at this moment of terror nothing more than a brief look of pity. She grabbed the loose reins and guided our compliant horse toward home. Can I admit it? That was the final instant of my joy; yet I was joyful. Idris was destined to perish, for her spirit was shattered: I was destined to perish, for I had contracted the pestilence; the world was a wasteland; optimism was insanity; existence had wedded mortality; they were unified; but, as I held my fading love, as I felt that my own end was near, I delighted in the bliss of holding her once more; repeatedly I kissed her and held her to my heart.

We reached our residence. I helped her off the horse, carried her upstairs, and entrusted her to Clara so that her soaked clothing could be replaced."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_022",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "For a moment I was overwhelmed, my head bowed by aching nausea; then, as my senses returned, I jumped up, pushed the wretch away, and dashed up the stairs, entering the room where my family usually stayed. A dim light revealed Alfred on a couch; Clara, trembling and paler than the whitest snow, had lifted him onto her arm, holding a cup of water to his lips. I saw clearly that no spark of life remained in that ruined form; his features were rigid, his eyes glazed, and his head had fallen back. I took him from her, laid him down gently, kissed his cold little mouth, and turned to speak in a whisper that would have been useless, as even the loudest thunder could not have reached him in his spiritual home.

And where was Idris? The news that she had gone out to find me and had not returned was terrifying, while the rain and driving wind rattled against the window and roared around the house. Added to this, the sickening sensation of the disease took hold of me; there was no time to lose if I ever wanted to see her again. I mounted my horse and rode out to search for her, imagining I heard her voice in every gust, weighed down by fever and aching pain.

I rode through the dark and rain in the labyrinthine streets of unpeopled London. My child lay dead at home; the seeds of a fatal disease had taken root in my chest; I went to find Idris, my beloved, now wandering alone while the rain poured from the sky like a waterfall to soak her dear head in chill damp and her fair limbs in numbing cold. A woman stood on a doorstep and called out to me as I galloped past. It was not Idris, so I rode on quickly until a kind of second sight—a realization of what I had seen but not registered—made me certain that another figure, thin, graceful, and tall, stood clinging to the person supporting her. In a minute I was beside the woman, and in a minute I received the sinking Idris into my arms. Lifting her up, I placed her on the horse; she lacked the strength to support herself, so I mounted behind her and held her close to my chest, wrapping my riding cloak around her, while her companion, whose well-known but changed face (it was Juliet, daughter of the Duke of L—) could at this moment of horror receive from me nothing more than a passing glance of pity. She took the loose rein and led our obedient horse toward home. Dare I admit it? That was the last moment of my happiness, but I was happy. Idris had to die, for her heart was broken; I had to die, for I had caught the plague; the earth was a scene of desolation; hope was madness; life had married death; they were one. Yet, while supporting my fainting love and knowing I would soon die, I reveled in the joy of holding her once more; again and again I kissed her and pressed her to my heart.

We arrived at our home. I helped her dismount, carried her upstairs, and left her in Clara’s care so her wet clothes could be changed."
}

And where was Idris? The news that she had gone out to look for me and had not returned was terrifying, especially with the rain and wind clattering against the windows and roaring around the house. On top of this, the sickening feeling of the plague was growing inside me. I knew I had to act quickly if I ever wanted to see her again. I mounted my horse and rode out to find her, imagining I heard her voice in every gust of wind, all while suffering from fever and pain.

I rode through the dark, rainy, and empty streets of London. My child was dead at home, and the seeds of the fatal disease had taken root in me. I went to find my beloved Idris, who was wandering alone while the rain poured down like a waterfall, soaking her head and chilling her limbs. A woman stood on a doorstep and called out as I galloped past. It was not Idris, so I rode on quickly. Then, a sudden realization—a memory of something I had seen but not fully registered—made me certain that another figure, thin, graceful, and tall, was standing there, clinging to the person who supported her. In a moment, I was beside them, and in another, I held the collapsing Idris in my arms. I lifted her onto the horse, but she was too weak to sit up, so I mounted behind her, held her close to my chest, and wrapped my cloak around her. Her companion, whose familiar but changed face I recognized as Juliet, the daughter of the Duke of L—, received only a brief, compassionate glance from me in that moment of horror. She took the loose reins and led our horse back home. Can I admit it? That was the last moment of my happiness, yet I was happy. Idris had to die because her heart was broken; I had to die because I had caught the plague. The earth was a wasteland, hope was insanity, and life had joined with death. Yet, as I held my fainting love and knew I would soon die, I delighted in having her with me once more. I kissed her again and again, pressing her to my heart.

We arrived home. I helped her dismount, carried her upstairs, and left her in Clara’s care so her wet clothes could be changed."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Her bravery faltered solely when she anticipated danger for her parent, or worried that mortality might hinder her from fulfilling the obligations to which she was steadfastly committed.

Upon leaving Windsor for London, as a preliminary to our ultimate departure, we called upon Lucy and coordinated the strategy for her and her mother’s relocation. Lucy felt regret at the compulsion that forced her to abandon her familiar lanes and village, and to transport a frail parent from her domestic comforts into the desolate expanse of a depopulated world; yet, she was too accustomed to hardship, and possessed too gentle a disposition, to yield to complaints regarding the unavoidable.

Later events, specifically my sickness and that of Idris, pushed her from our thoughts; we eventually recalled her only to assume that she was among the few who had traveled from Windsor to accompany the emigrants, and that she had already reached Paris. Consequently, when we reached Rochester, we were astonished to receive a letter from this model of endurance, delivered by a man who had just arrived from Slough. His report stated that, while traveling from his home and passing through Datchet, he was startled to observe smoke rising from the inn’s chimney; assuming he would find fellow travelers gathered there, he knocked and was let in. The only occupants were Lucy and her mother; the latter had lost the use of her limbs due to a bout of rheumatism, and thus, one by one, all the remaining local residents had departed, leaving them solitary. Lucy begged the man to remain with her; she believed her mother would recover in a week or two, allowing them to depart, but insisted they would surely die if abandoned in such a helpless and isolated state. The man explained that his wife and children were already among the emigrants, and he therefore felt it impossible to stay. As a final measure, Lucy gave him a letter for Idris, to be handed to her wherever he might encounter us. He fulfilled this request, and Idris received the following note with great feeling:

“HONOURED LADY,

“I am certain you will recall and sympathize with me, and I dare to hope you will aid me; what other prospect remains? Forgive my style of writing, as I am quite distraught. A month ago, my dear mother lost the use of her limbs. She is improving, and I am certain that in another month she would be capable of traveling, in the manner you were so generous as to suggest you would organize for us. But now everyone has departed—everyone—and as they left, each person remarked that perhaps my mother would be recovered before we were entirely forsaken. Three days ago, I visited Samuel Woods, who had stayed until the end due to his newborn child; having a large family, I hoped I might convince them to wait a little longer for us, but I discovered the house empty. I have not seen a single person since, until this kind man arrived. —What will become of us? My mother is unaware of our situation; she is so unwell that I have concealed it from her."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_024",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "She wanted to share her fears with her son, hoping to find comfort in his empathy or strength in his dismissal of her dark premonitions.

On the day she arrived in Dover, she walked with him along the beach. With the hesitation typical of someone prone to intense, dramatic emotions, she was gradually steering the conversation toward her concerns when a messenger arrived with my letter, announcing our sudden return to Windsor. He gave a verbal update on how he had left us, adding that despite Lady Idris’s cheerful spirit, he feared she might not survive the journey to Windsor. “Indeed,” the Countess replied, “your fears are correct; she is on the verge of death!”

As she spoke, her gaze was fixed on a tomb-like hollow in the cliff. She swore to me with absolute solemnity that she saw Idris walking slowly toward the cave. Idris was turned away from her, her head bowed, wearing the white dress she usually favored, though a thin, crape-like veil covered her golden hair, obscuring her like a faint, transparent mist. She appeared dejected, as if submissively yielding to an irresistible force; she walked into the dark opening and vanished.

“If I were prone to hallucinations,” the venerable lady continued, “I might doubt my own eyes and dismiss my belief in what I saw. But I live in a world of reality, and I have no doubt that what I witnessed existed outside of my own mind. From that moment, I could find no peace. I would have given anything to see her one last time before she died. I knew it was unlikely, yet I had to try. I left for Windsor immediately. Even though we traveled quickly, our progress felt agonizingly slow, as if every delay were designed specifically to torment me. I blamed you, heaping my burning impatience upon your head. It was no surprise, though it was an agonizing blow, when you pointed to her final resting place. Words cannot describe the hatred I felt for you at that moment—you, the triumphant obstacle to my deepest desire. I saw her, and my anger, hatred, and injustice died at her bier, replaced by a remorse—great God, that I should feel it—that will last as long as my memory and feelings endure.”

To heal such remorse, and to ensure that this newfound love and tenderness did not produce the same bitter fruit as her previous hatred and harshness, I dedicated all my efforts to soothing the elderly penitent. Our group was a somber one; everyone was consumed by regret for what could not be undone. Even young Evelyn’s natural cheerfulness was overshadowed by the absence of his mother. Added to this was the uncertainty of the future. Before committing to any major, voluntary change, the mind wavers, alternating between soothing itself with hopeful expectations and recoiling from obstacles that suddenly seem more terrifying than ever before. An involuntary tremor ran through me when I realized that, in another day, we might have crossed the sea and begun that hopeless, endless, and sorrowful wandering which, only a short time ago, I had viewed as the only relief our situation could offer."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Eventually, his voice was heard and heeded; the throng retreated, leaving only the leaders gathered near him. I had previously watched Lord Raymond traverse his ranks; his triumphant expression and regal bearing commanded universal deference and compliance. Adrian’s presence and impact were quite different. His slender frame, his passionate gaze, and his movements—suggesting entreaty rather than command—demonstrated that affection, devoid of dread, granted him authority over the spirits of a crowd that understood he never retreated from peril and was driven solely by concern for the common good. No division remained between the two factions that had recently been prepared to spill one another’s blood, for while neither would defer to the other, both readily submitted to the Earl of Windsor.

One group, however, stood apart, isolated from the rest, failing to share in the delight sparked by Adrian’s appearance or to absorb the spirit of reconciliation that descended like dew upon the softened hearts of their fellow citizens. Leading this gathering was a heavy, somber individual whose malevolent gaze observed the grim countenances of his followers with gloating satisfaction. They had remained idle until now, but noticing they were overlooked in the general celebration, they moved forward with menacing motions. Our allies had engaged in a sort of reckless dispute; they only required the realization that their interests were identical for them to unite. Their shared hostility had been a fleeting blaze compared to the smoldering resentment they both harbored toward these dissenters, who claimed a segment of the future world to fortify and entrench themselves, emerging with terrifying raids and dreadful proclamations against the ordinary inhabitants of the earth. The initial movement of this small band of the chosen reignited their fury; they seized their weapons and awaited only their commander’s sign to begin the assault, when the distinct resonance of Adrian’s voice commanded them to withdraw. With a confused rumble and a hasty retreat, much like a tide receding noisily from the shores it recently submerged, our friends complied. Adrian rode alone into the gap between the rival groups; he neared the hostile commander as if inviting him to follow his lead, but his gesture was ignored, and the chief moved forward, accompanied by his entire troop. Many women were among them, appearing even more fervent and determined than the men. They crowded around their leader as if to protect him, while loudly showering him with every holy title and term of adoration. Adrian encountered them midway; they stopped. “What,” he inquired, “are you searching for? Do you demand anything from us that we decline to provide, and that you feel compelled to seize through combat and violence?”

His inquiries were met with a collective shout, in which only the terms election, sin, and the red right arm of God were distinguishable.

Adrian gazed pointedly at their leader, remarking, “Are you unable to quiet your followers? Mine, you observe, heed my commands.”

The man responded with a glare; then, perhaps apprehensive that his people might overhear the discussion he anticipated, he ordered them to step back and moved forward alone. “I inquire once more,” Adrian stated, “what is it you demand of us?”"
}

There was no longer any distinction between the two factions that had recently been ready to shed each other’s blood, for although neither would submit to the other, both readily obeyed the Earl of Windsor.

One group, however, remained isolated, failing to share in the joy of Adrian’s arrival or to absorb the spirit of peace that settled like dew upon the softened hearts of their countrymen. Leading this assembly was a heavy, dark-featured man whose malicious eyes surveyed the stern expressions of his followers with gloating delight. They had been inactive until now, but seeing themselves ignored in the general celebration, they advanced with threatening gestures. Our friends had been fighting in a kind of reckless rivalry; they only needed to be told their cause was the same for it to become so. Their mutual anger had been like a straw fire compared to the slow-burning hatred they both felt for these separatists, who claimed a portion of the world for themselves, fortifying their position to launch fearful raids and terrifying condemnations against the ordinary people of the earth. The initial advance of this small army of the "elect" reignited their rage; they grabbed their weapons and waited only for their leader’s signal to attack, when the clear tones of Adrian’s voice were heard, ordering them to fall back. They crowded around their leader as if to shield him, while loudly bestowing upon him every sacred title and epithet of worship. Adrian met them halfway; they halted. "What," he asked, "do you seek? Is there anything you require from us that we refuse to give, and that you are forced to acquire through arms and warfare?"

They had been inactive until now, but seeing themselves ignored in the general celebration, they advanced with threatening gestures. Our friends had been fighting in a kind of reckless rivalry; they only needed to be told their cause was the same for it to become so. Their mutual anger had been like a straw fire compared to the slow-burning hatred they both felt for these separatists, who claimed a portion of the world for themselves, fortifying their position to launch fearful raids and terrifying condemnations against the ordinary people of the earth. The initial advance of this small army of the "elect" reignited their rage; they grabbed their weapons and waited only for their leader’s signal to attack, when the clear tones of Adrian’s voice were heard, ordering them to fall back. With confused murmurs and a hurried retreat—like a wave receding noisily from the sands it had just covered—our friends obeyed. Adrian rode alone into the space between the opposing groups; he approached the hostile leader as if inviting him to do the same, but his gesture was ignored, and the chief advanced, followed by his entire troop. There were many women among them who seemed more eager and resolute than their male companions. They crowded around their leader as if to shield him, while loudly bestowing upon him every sacred title and epithet of worship. Adrian met them halfway; they halted. "What," he asked, "do you seek? Is there anything you require from us that we refuse to give, and that you are forced to acquire through arms and warfare?"

His questions were answered by a general cry, in which the words "election," "sin," and "the red right arm of God" were the only ones that could be heard.

Adrian looked directly at their leader, saying, "Can you not silence your followers? Mine, you see, obey me."

The man answered with a scowl, and then, perhaps fearful that his people might overhear the debate he expected to follow, he ordered them to fall back and advanced by himself. "What, I ask again," said Adrian, "do you require of us?""
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Whenever our numbers became separated by night, the vanguard would pause at dawn for the rear guard to catch up. We were scheduled to gather at each of the previously noted major towns, where the leading officers would convene to deliberate on our collective welfare. As I had stated, I traveled in the lead, while Adrian brought up the rear. His mother stayed with him, keeping Clara and Evelyn under her care. With our arrangement settled, I set out. My intention was to travel only as far as Fontainebleau initially, where Adrian would meet me in a few days before I continued my journey further toward the east.

My friend traveled a few miles with me out of Versailles. He seemed melancholy, and with a tone of unusual hopelessness, he voiced a wish that we might reach the Alps quickly, adding a futile lament that we were not already there. I remarked that we could simply accelerate our pace if he found the current, slower schedule so disagreeable.

He answered that it was too late for such changes. A month prior, we had been in control of our own fates, but now—he turned his face away from me, and though the deepening dusk already obscured his features, he averted his gaze further as he added that a man had perished from the plague the previous night.

He spoke in a muffled tone, then suddenly wrung his hands and cried out that the final hour was approaching us with great speed. He compared its arrival to stars fading before the sun, claiming it would destroy us. He had tried his best, he said, clutching at the wheels of the plague’s chariot with weak, desperate hands, but it dragged him along as it crushed the lives of everyone scattered across the path of existence. He wished it were finished and that we had all reached the grave together.

His eyes filled with tears as he continued, lamenting that the tragedy would repeat itself again and again. He spoke of the agony of hearing the dying groan and the survivors wail, and of witnessing the final pangs that seemed to contain an eternity within a fleeting moment. He questioned why he was spared, asking why he, the infected sheep of the flock, was not struck down among the first. He found it incredibly difficult for a human being to bear what he was enduring.

Until this moment, Adrian had carried out his self-appointed duty with a brave spirit and a profound sense of purpose. I had watched him with deep respect and a vain wish to emulate him. I offered him some words of sympathy and support. He covered his face with his hands, struggling to regain his composure, and prayed that God would not let his heart fail or his courage falter for a few more months. He begged that the sights of unbearable suffering would not drive him to madness or cause his fragile heart to break. He had accepted it as his fate to lead the remnants of humanity until death ended his rule, and he bowed to that destiny.

He apologized to me, Verney, for causing me distress, promising to complain no more. He claimed to be himself again, or perhaps even better than he had been."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_026",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "If the groups were separated at night, those in the front would wait for the rear in the morning. We were all to gather at each of the major towns mentioned previously, where a council of the primary officers would meet for the common good. As I mentioned, I went first, while Adrian went last. His mother, with Clara and Evelyn under her care, stayed with him. With our order established, I set out. My plan was to travel no further than Fontainebleau at first, where Adrian would join me in a few days before I continued my journey eastward.

My friend traveled a few miles with me from Versailles. He was sad, and in a tone of unusual hopelessness, he prayed for our quick arrival in the Alps, expressing a futile regret that we were not already there. "In that case," I remarked, "we can speed up our pace; why stick to a plan whose slow progress you already dislike?"

"No," he replied, "it is too late now. A month ago, we were in control of our own fates; now—" he turned his face away from me; even though the gathering twilight had already hidden his expression, he turned it further still as he added, "a man died of the plague last night!"

He spoke in a muffled voice, then suddenly clasped his hands and exclaimed, "The final hour for us all is approaching rapidly; just as the stars disappear before the sun, so will its arrival destroy us. I have done my best; with grasping hands and weak strength, I have tried to hold back the chariot of the plague, but it drags me along as it moves like a Juggernaut, crushing the life out of everyone who litters the highway of existence. I wish it were over—I wish that once its procession was finished, we had all entered the grave together!"

Tears streamed from his eyes. "Again and again," he continued, "the tragedy will play out; I must hear the groans of the dying and the wailing of the survivors once more; I must witness the agony that, in its finality, packs an eternity into a fleeting moment. Why am I kept for this? Why, as the tainted sheep of the flock, am I not struck down among the first? It is hard, very hard, for a human being to endure all that I am enduring!"

Until now, Adrian had fulfilled his self-appointed task with an undaunted spirit and a deep sense of duty and worth. I had watched him with reverence and a useless desire to emulate him. I offered a few words of encouragement and sympathy. He hid his face in his hands, and as he struggled to calm himself, he cried out, "For a few months, just for a few more months, God, do not let my heart fail or my courage break; do not let the sights of unbearable misery drive this half-crazed brain to madness or cause this fragile heart to beat against its cage until it bursts. I have believed it to be my destiny to guide and rule the last of the human race until death ends my leadership; and to this destiny, I submit."

"Forgive me, Verney, I am causing you pain, but I will complain no longer. Now I am myself again, or rather, I am better than myself.""
}

{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_026",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "If our numbers were separated at night, those in the front would wait for those in the rear the next morning. We were all to gather at each of the large towns mentioned previously, where a group of the main officers would meet to discuss our general welfare. As I mentioned, I went first, while Adrian went last. His mother, with Clara and Evelyn under her care, stayed with him. With our order established, I set out. My plan was to travel no further than Fontainebleau at first, where Adrian would join me in a few days before I continued my journey further east.

My friend traveled with me for a few miles outside of Versailles. He was sad, and in a tone of unusual hopelessness, he prayed that we would arrive in the Alps quickly, expressing a useless regret that we were not already there. “In that case,” I remarked, “we can speed up our march; why stick to a plan whose slow pace you already dislike?”

“No,” he replied, “it is too late now. A month ago, we were in control of our own fates; now—” he turned his face away from me. Even though the gathering twilight had already hidden his expression, he turned further away as he added, “A man died of the plague last night!”

He spoke in a muffled voice, then suddenly clasped his hands and exclaimed, “The final hour for us all is approaching very quickly. Just as the stars vanish before the sun, the plague’s approach will destroy us. I have done my best; with grasping hands and weak strength, I have tried to hold back the chariot of the plague, but it drags me along with it. Like a Juggernaut, it crushes the life out of everyone who litters the road of existence. I wish it were over—I wish that once the procession was finished, we had all entered the grave together!”

Tears streamed from his eyes. “Again and again,” he continued, “this tragedy will be repeated; again I must hear the groans of the dying and the wailing of those who survive; again I must witness the suffering that, in its finality, packs an eternity into a brief moment of existence. Why am I kept for this? Why, as the tainted sheep of the flock, am I not struck down among the first? It is hard, very hard, for a person born of woman to endure all that I must endure!”

Until now, Adrian had carried out his self-imposed duty with a brave spirit and a strong sense of responsibility and worth. I had watched him with respect and a useless desire to be like him. I now offered a few words of encouragement and sympathy. He hid his face in his hands, and while he tried to calm himself, he cried out, “For a few months, just a few months more, O God, do not let my heart fail or my courage break; do not let the sights of unbearable misery drive this half-crazed brain to madness, or cause this weak heart to beat against its chest until it bursts. I have believed it to be my destiny to guide and rule the last of the human race until death ends my leadership; and to this destiny, I submit.”

“Forgive me, Verney, I am causing you pain, but I will complain no longer. Now I am myself again, or rather, I am better than myself.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Amber flashes of lightning flickered across the immense summit of Mont Blanc, as mute as the frozen stone they lit; everything appeared stark, untamed, and majestic, while the humming of the pine trees provided a soft contrast to the rugged grandeur. At times, the splitting and descent of glacial boulders pierced the atmosphere; at others, the roar of an avalanche crashed upon us. In regions of smaller scale, nature reveals her vitality through the leaves of forests, the sprouting of grass, and the quiet babbling of winding brooks; here, possessed of colossal traits, the waterfall, the tempest, and the movement of heavy currents reveal her force. Such was the cemetery, such the funeral song, and such the everlasting assembly that attended our companion’s burial.

It was not merely the human body we had laid within this permanent tomb, whose final rites we were observing. With this final casualty, the Plague departed from the world. Death had never lacked instruments to extinguish life, and we, diminished and frail as we had grown, remained vulnerable to every other arrow contained in his overflowing quiver. Yet, the pestilence was gone from our midst. For seven years, it had reigned supreme across the planet; it had traversed every corner of our vast world; it had permeated the air, which serves as a mantle for all living beings—the people of Europe, the opulent Asian, the dark-skinned African, and the independent American had all been conquered and annihilated by it. Its savage rule reached its conclusion here within the stony valley of Chamounix.

Recurring visions of suffering and agony, the results of this sickness, no longer defined our existence—the term plague ceased to echo in our hearing—the manifestation of the plague etched upon the human face no longer confronted our sight. From that instant, I witnessed the plague no more. It surrendered its dominion and cast aside its royal staff amidst the icy crags that enclosed us. It left solitude and stillness as the joint inheritors of its realm.

My current emotions are so intertwined with those of the past that I cannot determine if the awareness of this shift reached us while we stood upon that barren ground. It appears to me that it did; that a shadow seemed to lift from above us, that a burden was removed from the atmosphere; that from then on we inhaled more easily and held our heads with a measure of our former freedom. Yet we did not possess hope. We were struck by the conviction that our species had reached its end, but that the plague would not be our executioner. The future was like a powerful river, along which a bewitched vessel is carried, whose human pilot understands that the visible threat is not the one he must dread, yet that peril is near; and who drifts, filled with dread, beneath overhanging cliffs and through murky, turbulent waters—observing in the distance even more bizarre and harsh forms, toward which he is inevitably drawn. What was to be our fate? Oh, for some Delphic prophecy, or Pythian priestess, to declare the mysteries of what is to come! Oh, for some Oedipus to decipher the puzzle of the merciless Sphinx! Such an Oedipus I was destined to be—not one interpreting a verbal trick, but one whose own excruciating torments and grief-stricken existence were to serve as the tools to expose the mysteries of fate and uncover the significance of the enigma, the resolution of which concluded the chronicle of mankind."
}

The future was like a powerful river, along which a bewitched vessel is carried, whose human pilot understands that the visible threat is not the one he must dread, yet that peril is near; and who drifts, filled with dread, beneath overhanging cliffs and through murky, turbulent waters—observing in the distance even more bizarre and harsh forms, toward which he is inevitably drawn. What was to be our fate? Oh, for some Delphic prophecy, or Pythian priestess, to declare the mysteries of what is to come! Oh, for some Oedipus to decipher the puzzle of the merciless Sphinx! Such an Oedipus I was destined to be—not one interpreting a verbal trick, but one whose own excruciating torments and grief-stricken existence were to serve as the tools to expose the mysteries of fate and uncover the significance of the enigma, the resolution of which concluded the chronicle of mankind."
}

Now the splitting and falling of icy rocks tore through the air; now the thunder of an avalanche crashed into our ears. In regions with less dramatic features, nature reveals her living power through the foliage of trees, the growth of grass, and the soft bubbling of winding streams; here, gifted with giant attributes, the torrent, the thunderstorm, and the flow of massive waters display her activity. Such was the graveyard, such the requiem, and such the eternal congregation that attended our companion’s funeral!

It was not just the human form we had placed in this eternal tomb, whose funeral rites we were now observing. With this final victim, the plague vanished from the earth. Death had never lacked weapons to destroy life, and we, few and weak as we had become, were still exposed to every other arrow in his full quiver. But the pestilence was gone from among them. For seven years it had held full sway over the earth; it had walked every corner of our spacious globe; it had mingled with the atmosphere, which wraps around all our fellow creatures like a cloak—the inhabitants of native Europe, the luxurious Asiatic, the swarthy African, and the free American had all been defeated and destroyed by it. Its barbaric tyranny came to an end here in the rocky valley of Chamonix.

Recurring scenes of misery and pain, the results of this sickness, were no longer a part of our lives—the word plague no longer rang in our ears—the appearance of plague embodied in the human face no longer stood before our eyes. From this moment, I saw the plague no more. It abdicated its throne and stripped itself of its imperial scepter among the ice rocks that surrounded us. It left solitude and silence as the joint heirs of its kingdom.

My current feelings are so mixed with the past that I cannot say whether the knowledge of this change visited us as we stood on that barren spot. It seems to me that it did; that a cloud seemed to pass from over us, that a weight was lifted from the air; that from then on we breathed more freely and raised our heads with some portion of our former liberty. Yet we did not hope. We were struck by the feeling that our race was run, but that the plague would not be our destroyer. The coming time was like a mighty river, down which a charmed boat is driven, whose mortal steersman knows that the obvious peril is not the one he needs to fear, yet that danger is near; and who floats, awe-struck, under looming precipices, through dark and murky waters—seeing in the distance even stranger and harsher shapes, toward which he is irresistibly pulled. What would become of us? Oh, for some Delphic oracle or Pythian priestess to utter the secrets of the future! Oh, for some Oedipus to solve the riddle of the cruel Sphinx! Such an Oedipus I was to be—not guessing a word puzzle, but one whose agonizing pangs and sorrow-tainted life were to be the tools used to lay bare the secrets of destiny and reveal the meaning of the enigma whose explanation closed the history of the human race."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Observe us now within our fragile dwelling, surrounded by ravenous, crashing billows and lashed by gales. In the pitch-black east, two immense cloud formations, drifting in opposing directions, collided; lightning flashed, and the deep thunder rumbled. Once more in the south, the clouds answered, and the jagged bolt of fire streaking across the dark heavens revealed to us the terrifying masses of clouds, now joined and obscured by the rising swells. Merciful God! And we are solitary—we three—alone—the only inhabitants of the ocean and the world, and we three are destined to die! The immense cosmos, its countless spheres, and the stretches of infinite land we had abandoned—the vastness of the endless sea surrounding us—narrowed in my vision; they, along with everything they held, diminished to a single point, specifically our pitching vessel, which carried the remnants of noble humanity.

A spasm of hopelessness passed over Adrian’s affectionate features, while he muttered through clenched teeth, “Yet they shall be rescued!” Clara, struck by a mortal dread, pallid and shaking, moved toward him—he gazed at her with a reassuring grin—“Are you afraid, dear girl? Oh, do not be afraid, we will reach the coast soon!”

The gloom kept me from observing the shifts in her expression; however, her tone was distinct and gentle as she answered, “Why would I be afraid? Neither the ocean nor the tempest can injure us, unless powerful fate or the master of fate forbids it. Furthermore, the sharp terror of outliving either of you is absent here—a single death will embrace us together.”

In the meantime, we furled all our canvas, except for a jib; and, as soon as it was safe, we altered our direction, sailing with the gale toward the Italian coast. The black night blended all things; we barely perceived the white foam of the lethal waves, except when lightning created a fleeting midday, consuming the darkness, exposing our peril, and returning us to intensified night. We remained quiet, except when Adrian, acting as helmsman, offered a hopeful remark. Our small craft responded to the tiller remarkably well, gliding along the summits of the waves as if it were a child of the ocean, and the furious mother protected her threatened offspring.

I remained at the bow, observing our path; when abruptly I listened to the waters crashing with renewed intensity. We were surely near the coast—simultaneously I shouted, “Turn about!” and a wide flash of lightning filling the sky revealed for an instant the flat beach ahead, exposing even the sands and the stunted, mud-covered patches of reeds that grew at the high-tide line. Once more it was dark, and we caught our breath with the relief one might feel who, while pieces of volcanic rock darken the atmosphere, watches a massive boulder strike the earth directly at his feet. We were uncertain how to proceed—the surf here, there, and everywhere surrounded us—they bellowed, crashed, and cast their loathed spray upon our faces. With significant effort and hazard, we eventually managed to change our heading and steered away from the coast. I encouraged my companions to ready themselves for the destruction of our small boat and to secure themselves to an oar or spar that might serve to keep them afloat. I was personally a skilled swimmer—the mere sight of the ocean usually stirred in me feelings similar to those a huntsman experiences when he listens to a pack of hounds in full pursuit; I delighted in feeling the waves envelop me and attempt to overwhelm me, while I, master of my own movements, navigated this way or that, despite their furious assaults."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "SHELLEY_THE_LAST_MAN_029",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Look at us now in our fragile shelter, surrounded by hungry, roaring waves and battered by the wind. In the pitch-black east, two massive clouds moving in opposite directions collided; lightning flashed, and the harsh thunder rumbled. In the south, the clouds responded, and a jagged stream of fire cutting across the dark sky revealed the terrifying masses of clouds, now meeting and being swallowed by the rising waves. Great God! And we are alone—the three of us—alone—the only inhabitants of the sea and the earth, and we three are destined to perish! The vast universe, its countless worlds, the plains of the endless earth we had left behind, and the expanse of the shoreless sea around us—all contracted in my view. They, and everything they contained, shrank down to a single point: our tossing boat, carrying the last of humanity.

A spasm of despair crossed Adrian’s kind face, though he muttered through clenched teeth, “They will be saved!” Clara, struck by a human pang of terror, pale and trembling, crept toward him. He looked at her with an encouraging smile: “Are you afraid, sweet girl? Oh, don’t be afraid; we will be on shore soon!”

The darkness kept me from seeing her expression, but her voice was clear and sweet as she replied, “Why should I fear? Neither the sea nor the storm can harm us unless powerful destiny or the ruler of destiny allows it. Besides, the agonizing fear of outliving either of you is gone—one death will claim us together.”

Meanwhile, we took in all our sails except for the jib and, as soon as it was safe, changed our course, running with the wind toward the Italian coast. The dark night blurred everything; we could barely make out the white crests of the deadly swells, except when lightning created a brief moment of daylight, cutting through the darkness to reveal our danger before plunging us back into deeper night. We were all silent, except when Adrian, acting as helmsman, made an encouraging remark. Our little boat handled the rudder miraculously well, skimming the tops of the waves as if she were a child of the sea, and the angry mother was shielding her endangered offspring.

I sat at the bow, watching our path, when suddenly I heard the water breaking with renewed fury. We were definitely near the shore. I shouted, “Turn around!” and a wide flash of lightning filled the sky, revealing the flat beach ahead for a single moment, showing the sands and the stunted, mud-covered reeds that grew at the high-tide mark. Then it was dark again, and we held our breath with the relief of someone who, while volcanic rocks rain down, sees a massive boulder plow into the ground right at his feet. We didn’t know what to do—the breakers surrounded us on all sides, roaring, crashing, and flinging their bitter spray into our faces. With great difficulty and danger, we finally managed to change course and head away from the shore. I urged my companions to prepare for our boat to break apart and to tie themselves to an oar or a spar that might keep them afloat. I was an excellent swimmer myself; the mere sight of the sea usually gave me the same thrill a hunter feels when he hears a pack of hounds in full cry. I loved feeling the waves wrap around me and try to overpower me, while I, in control of myself, moved this way or that, despite their angry battering."
}

We were all silent, except when Adrian, acting as helmsman, made an encouraging remark. Our little boat handled the rudder miraculously well, riding the tops of the waves as if she were a child of the sea, and the angry mother was sheltering her endangered offspring.

I sat at the bow, watching our path, when suddenly I heard the water breaking with renewed fury. We were certainly near the shore. I shouted, “Turn around!” Just then, a wide flash of lightning filled the sky, showing us the flat beach ahead for a moment. It revealed the sand and the stunted, mud-covered reeds that grew at the high-tide mark. Then it was dark again, and we held our breath with the same relief one feels when, while volcanic rocks fall from the sky, one sees a massive boulder strike the ground right at his feet. We did not know what to do; the breakers surrounded us on all sides, roaring, crashing, and throwing their bitter spray into our faces. With great difficulty and danger, we finally managed to change our course and steer away from the shore. I urged my companions to prepare for our boat to break apart and to tie themselves to an oar or a spar that might help them float. I was an excellent swimmer; the very sight of the sea used to give me the same thrill a hunter feels when he hears a pack of hounds in full cry. I loved to feel the waves wrap around me and try to overpower me, while I, in control of myself, moved this way or that, despite their angry blows."
}
